## Part 3: Accessing Claude with the API

### 3. Accessing the API

> Type: Video | Duration: 5:18 | Video: 03 - 001 - Accessing the API.mp4

When building applications with Claude, understanding the complete request lifecycle helps you make better architectural decisions and debug problems more effectively. Let's walk through everything that happens from the moment a user clicks "send" in your chat interface to when Claude's response appears on the screen.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623274%2F03_-_001_-_Accessing_the_API_01.1748623274598.png)

## The five-step request flow

Every interaction with Claude follows a predictable pattern of five distinct stages: request to server, request to the Anthropic API, model processing, response to server, and response to client.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623275%2F03_-_001_-_Accessing_the_API_03.1748623275310.png)

## Why you need a server

You should never make requests directly to the Anthropic API from client-side code. Here's why:

- API requests require a secret API key for authentication
- Exposing this key in client-side code creates a serious security vulnerability
- Anyone could extract the key and make unauthorized requests

Instead, your web or mobile app should send requests to your own server, which then communicates with the Anthropic API using a securely stored key.

## Making an API request

When your server contacts the Anthropic API, you can use an official SDK or make a plain HTTP request. Anthropic provides SDKs for Python, TypeScript, JavaScript, Go, and Ruby.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623276%2F03_-_001_-_Accessing_the_API_05.1748623276722.png)

Every request must include these essential fields:

- **API Key** - identifies your request to Anthropic
- **Model** - the name of the model to use (such as "claude-3-sonnet")
- **Messages** - a list containing the user's input text
- **Max Tokens** - the upper limit on the number of tokens Claude can generate

## Claude's internal processing flow

Once Anthropic receives your request, Claude processes it through four main stages: tokenization, embedding, contextualization, and generation.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623277%2F03_-_001_-_Accessing_the_API_08.1748623277503.png)

### Tokenization

Claude first breaks your input text down into small chunks called tokens. These can be complete words, parts of words, spaces, or symbols. For simplicity, you can think of each word as a token.

### Embedding

Each token is converted into an embedding—a long list of numbers that represents all the possible meanings of that word. You can think of an embedding as a numerical definition that captures semantic relationships.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623278%2F03_-_001_-_Accessing_the_API_10.1748623278148.png)

Words often have multiple meanings. For example, "quantum" might refer to:

- A discrete unit of a physical quantity (physics)
- Quantum mechanics or quantum physics concepts
- Something extremely tiny or subatomic
- Quantum computing applications

### Contextualization

Claude refines each embedding based on the surrounding words to determine the most likely meaning in context. This process adjusts the numerical representation to highlight the appropriate definition.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623278%2F03_-_001_-_Accessing_the_API_11.1748623278717.png)

### Generation

The contextualized embeddings pass through an output layer that computes a probability for each possible next word. Claude doesn't always choose the highest-probability word—it combines probability with controlled randomness to create natural, varied responses.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623279%2F03_-_001_-_Accessing_the_API_13.1748623279317.png)

After selecting each word, Claude adds it to the sequence and repeats the whole process for the next word.

## When Claude stops generating

After generating each token, Claude checks several conditions to decide whether to continue:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623280%2F03_-_001_-_Accessing_the_API_15.1748623279963.png)

- **Max tokens reached** - Has it reached the limit you specified?
- **Natural ending** - Has it generated an end-of-sequence token?
- **Stop sequence** - Has it encountered a predefined stop phrase?

## API response

Once generation is complete, the API returns a structured response containing:

- **Message** - the generated text
- **Usage** - the number of input and output tokens
- **Stop Reason** - the reason generation ended

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623281%2F03_-_001_-_Accessing_the_API_17.1748623281653.png)

Your server receives this response and forwards the generated text back to your client application to display in the user interface.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623282%2F03_-_001_-_Accessing_the_API_19.1748623282180.png)

## Key takeaways

Understanding this flow helps you:

- Design a secure architecture that protects your API key
- Set appropriate token limits for your use case
- Handle different stop reasons in your application logic
- Debug problems by understanding where in the flow the issue might be occurring

You don't need to memorize every detail—the goal is to become familiar with the terminology and overall flow you'll encounter when using the Claude API.

#### Video transcript

In this module, we'll explore how to access Claude and use it to generate some text. To help you understand how it works, I'll walk you through the full lifecycle of making a request to the Anthropic API. We'll also take a brief look at what happens behind the scenes inside Claude. To start this walkthrough, we'll take a simple, standard chatbot application as an example.

Suppose you're building a web app and want to display a chat window to users in their web browser. When a user types a message and clicks send, they expect some response to magically appear. As I said, I want to explore what's happening behind the scenes here to generate this text and display it on the screen. We'll break it down into five separate steps.

I've outlined these five steps at the top of this diagram. We'll go through them one at a time. When a user types some text and clicks send, that text gets sent to a server that you, the developer, have implemented. I mention this step just to make one thing clear.

You shouldn't try to access the Anthropic API directly from a web or mobile app. Every time you make a request to the API, you need to include a secret API key. The best way to keep this key secret is to never include it in your client app, and to only make requests to the API through a server you implement. On to step two.

Once your server receives a request from the client, the server makes a request directly to the Anthropic API. Typically, you'd make this request through one of the SDKs Anthropic publishes. There are official SDK implementations for Python, TypeScript, JavaScript, Go, and Ruby. Now, if you don't want to use an SDK, you don't have to.

You can also make a plain HTTP request if you prefer. When you make this request, you need to pass along a few pieces of data. In particular, you need to include an API key, the name of the model you want to run, a list of messages (which will contain the text the user submitted), and a `max_tokens` value, which limits the length of text Claude will generate. Next comes the Anthropic API, which is where our text actually gets generated.

Here, we'll cover the text-generation process inside the language model in a bit of detail. This process is complex, so I'll give you a simplified, high-level overview. We'll break the text-generation process down into four separate stages. In the first stage, the user's input gets broken down into smaller strings.

Each of these chunks of text is called a token. These tokens can be complete words, parts of words, or even spaces or symbols. For clarity, we'll assume each word constitutes a single token. Then, each token is converted into an embedding.

An embedding is a long list of numbers; you can think of these lists as a number-based definition for a given word. Now, one interesting aspect of written language is that a word can have many possible meanings, and only the word's position in the sentence and the presence of other surrounding words can narrow the definition down to a specific meaning. For example, "quantum" is a word with many different definitions, and when we see the word, we don't know its true meaning until we see the other words around it. Likewise, each embedding can be thought of as containing all the possible meanings of each word.

To refine each embedding into a single, precise definition, we use a process called contextualization. In contextualization, each embedding is adjusted based on the other embeddings around it. This process helps highlight each embedding's most meaningful interpretation in the context of its neighbors. The final step is generation, where the text actually gets written out.

By this point, each embedding has absorbed a great deal of information from its neighbors. The final processed embedding is passed to an output layer that produces a probability for each possible next word. Now, the model doesn't automatically choose the word with the highest probability. Instead, it combines probability and randomness to select words, which helps create more natural and varied responses.

Then, the selected word is added to the end of our embedding list, and the whole process repeats. After generating each output token, the model pauses and asks itself a few questions to decide whether it has finished generating text. First, it counts the number of tokens generated and checks whether that's greater than the `max_tokens` parameter provided in the input request. This `max_tokens` parameter limits the total number of tokens the model will generate.

The model can also generate a special end-of-sequence token. This isn't a regular word. It's a special signal the model uses to indicate that it believes it has reached the natural end of its generation and should stop. Once generation is complete, the API sends a response to your server.

The response will contain a message with the generated text, along with usage and a stop reason. Usage is a count of the number of tokens you input into the model and the number of tokens generated. The stop reason tells you exactly why the model decided to stop generating text, whether it reached a natural end-of-sequence token or perhaps exceeded the allotted number of tokens. Once your server receives this response, it sends the generated text back to your web or mobile app, which you'll display on the screen.

That's the whole flow. Now, we covered a lot of topics in this video. I don't expect you to remember all of this right now. The only goal is to start getting you familiar with some of the common terminology around accessing Claude through the API.

---

### 4. Getting an API key

> This lesson is an environment configuration/installation guide; please visit the online course platform and follow the steps.

### 5. Making a request

> Type: Video | Duration: 6:02 | Video: 03 - 003 - Making a Request.mp4

Once you understand the basic setup and structure, making your first request to the Anthropic API is straightforward. This guide walks you through the essential steps needed to get Claude to respond to your prompt using Python.

## Setting up your environment

Before making any API calls, you need to install the required packages and securely configure your API key.

First, install the necessary dependencies in your Jupyter notebook:

```
%pip install anthropic python-dotenv
```

Next, create a `.env` file in the same directory as your notebook to securely store your API key:

```
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="your-api-key-here"
```

This approach keeps your API key separate from your code and prevents accidentally committing it to version control. Be sure to add `.env` to your `.gitignore` file.

Load the environment variables and create your API client:

```
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()

from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()
model = "claude-sonnet-4-0"
```

## The `create` function

The core of making an API request is the `client.messages.create()` function. This function requires three key parameters:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623269%2F03_-_003_-_Making_a_Request_09.1748623269461.png)

- **model** - the name of the Claude model you want to use
- **max_tokens** - a safety limit on response length (not a target length)
- **messages** - the conversation history you want to send to Claude

The `max_tokens` parameter acts as a safety mechanism. If you set it to 1000, Claude will stop after generating 1000 tokens, even if it has more to say. Claude doesn't try to reach this limit—it just writes what it thinks is appropriate and stops when it hits the maximum.

## Understanding `messages`

`Messages` represent the conversation between you and Claude, similar to a chat application. There are two types of messages:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623270%2F03_-_003_-_Making_a_Request_13.1748623270369.png)

- **User messages** - what you want to send to Claude (written by a human)
- **Assistant messages** - the responses Claude generates

Each message is a dictionary containing a `role` (`"user"` or `"assistant"`) and a `content` (the actual text).

## Making your first request

Here's a complete example of making a request to Claude:

```
message = client.messages.create(
    model=model,
    max_tokens=1000,
    messages=[
        {
            "role": "user",
            "content": "What is quantum computing? Answer in one sentence"
        }
    ]
)
```

When you run this code, Claude will process your request and return a response object containing the generated text along with metadata about the request.

## Extracting the response

The response object contains a lot of information, but usually you only need the generated text. Access it using:

```
message.content[0].text
```

This gives you clean, readable output, for example: "Quantum computing is a type of computation that uses qubits to process information by harnessing quantum-mechanical principles such as superposition and entanglement, with the potential to solve certain complex problems exponentially faster than classical computers can."

With these fundamentals in hand, you can start experimenting with different prompts and building more sophisticated interactions with Claude.

#### Video transcript

We've covered a lot, so in this video, we'll switch things up and get hands-on writing some code. We'll learn how to make a simple, basic request to the Anthropic API. I'll walk you through four setup steps. In the first step, we'll open a Jupyter Notebook and install the Anthropic Python SDK, along with a package called python-dotenv.

First, let me open a notebook myself. I've added some comments to my notebook to guide myself through the process. Here in step one, I'll add a magic install command, namely percent sign, pip, install, anthropic, python-dotenv. If you write notebooks in Visual Studio Code like I do, you might notice a red syntax error appearing at the percent sign here.

If you see that syntax error, that's totally fine. You can ignore it. After writing this command, I'll run it to install these packages. Then I'll clear the output so you can more easily see what's on my screen.

Next, we'll use that python-dotenv package to store and load our API key. As a reminder, I gave instructions on creating an API key in the previous lecture. So if you haven't created an API key yet, I recommend going back to the previous lecture to find those instructions. To store this key in my editor, I'll create a file with a very special name in the same directory as my notebook.

I'll name this file .env. Then, inside it, I'll put the API key I just generated. I'll write out ANTHROPIC_API_KEY exactly, followed by an equals sign. After that, inside double quotes, I'll put that key.

A quick note: the whole reason we create this file and put the key in it is so we can ignore this file when we use version control. That way we don't accidentally commit this file to, say, git, and then accidentally push it to some public repository where anyone could see this file. So again, if you're using Git or a similar version control system, I recommend making sure you ignore this file when you commit your work. Now, back in my notebook, I can safely load that environment variable.

Then, on to step three, we'll use the Anthropic package to create our API client. In this cell, I'll also declare a variable called `model`. This will be a string. It will contain the name of the model we want to run in the Anthropic API.

We'll use Claude 3.5 Sonnet. Now on to the last step, where we'll actually make a request using the client we just created. But before writing any code, I want to show you some terminology that will make the later work a bit easier. So the first thing to understand is that we'll access Claude by using the `create` function from the Anthropic SDK.

This function requires three different keyword arguments: a `model`, a `max_tokens`, and a `messages`. The `model` keyword argument is just the name of the model we want to run. We've defined that variable ahead of time in an earlier cell. The second required keyword argument is `max_tokens`.

It sets a maximum budget for the number of tokens Claude can generate. For example, if we pass in a `max_tokens` of 1000, and Claude tries to generate anything longer than that, then generation will be automatically stopped and we'll receive the first 1000 generated tokens. One thing to note here is that Claude won't try to reach your `max_tokens` count. In other words, Claude won't try to write a 1000-token response; it will just write whatever response it thinks is appropriate.

So you should really think of `max_tokens` as a safety mechanism to make sure you don't generate too much text. Finally, there's `messages`. This is the part I really want to focus on, because `messages` will be a huge focus in our upcoming videos. To understand what `messages` is, I want you to think back to the chat application we just discussed.

So a user might type some question to Claude and then expect to get an answer back. The `messages` we pass to this `create` function are meant to represent that kind of exchange. There are two types of `messages`: a user message and an assistant message. A user message contains the text we want to input to Claude.

The content in a user message is text authored by the user, or by us as developers. In other words, a user message will contain text written by some person. The second type of message is an assistant message. These messages contain text generated by the model and sent back to us.

Now, I think we have enough knowledge to at least make our first request. So let's do that, and then talk a bit more about `messages`. Back in my notebook, in the last cell, I'll declare a `message` variable that will come from `client.messages.create`. I'll pass in those different parameters we just discussed.

So I'll pass in a `model` of `model`, a `max_tokens`, which I'll set to 1000 here—definitely a safe limit, I think—and then a list of input messages. So here, I'll put in a single user message that will contain the question or query I want to send to Claude. To create a user message, we'll create a dictionary that will have a `role` of `user`, and then a `content` containing the actual string we want to send to Claude. So in this case, I'll ask Claude to define quantum computing in one sentence, like "What is quantum computing? Answer in one sentence."

Then I'll run it, which will take a minute or two, because we're actually accessing Claude. After that, in the cell below, I'll try to print out the `message` variable. Let's see what we get. Okay, inside there, we can see there's a lot of output, but notably, we get a definition of what quantum computing is around here.

So, in this `message` variable we got back, our text is nested fairly deep. We usually only want to get the text Claude generated, and we usually don't care much about any of the other attributes contained in this thing. So to access only the generated text, we'd write `message.content[0].text`. If I run that cell again, I'll now see only the generated text, nothing else.

---

### 6. Multi-Turn conversations

> Type: Video | Duration: 8:54 | Video: 03 - 004 - Multi-Turn Conversations.mp4

When working with the Anthropic API and Claude, there's a crucial concept you need to understand: Claude doesn't store any of your conversation history. Every request you make is completely independent and carries no memory of previous exchanges.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623270%2F03_-_004_-_Multi-Turn_Conversations_01.1748623269971.png)

This means that if you want to have a multi-turn conversation where Claude remembers the context of previous messages, you need to handle the conversation state yourself.

## The problem with stateless conversations

Suppose you ask Claude "What is quantum computing?" and get a great answer. Then you follow up with "Write another sentence"—Claude has no idea what you're referring to. It will write a sentence about something completely random, because it has no memory of the quantum computing discussion.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623270%2F03_-_004_-_Multi-Turn_Conversations_02.1748623270625.png)

## How multi-turn conversations work

To maintain conversation context, you need to do two things:

- Manually maintain a list of all messages in your code
- Send the full message history with every request

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623271%2F03_-_004_-_Multi-Turn_Conversations_05.1748623271251.png)

Here's the workflow that actually works:

1. Send your initial user message to Claude
2. Get Claude's response and add it to your message list as an assistant message
3. Add your follow-up as another user message
4. Send the entire conversation history to Claude

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623271%2F03_-_004_-_Multi-Turn_Conversations_08.1748623271832.png)

## Building helper functions

To make conversation management easier, you can create three helper functions:

```
def add_user_message(messages, text):
    user_message = {"role": "user", "content": text}
    messages.append(user_message)

def add_assistant_message(messages, text):
    assistant_message = {"role": "assistant", "content": text}
    messages.append(assistant_message)

def chat(messages):
    message = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=1000,
        messages=messages,
    )
    return message.content[0].text
```

## Putting it all together

Here's how to use these functions to maintain a conversation:

```
# Start with an empty message list
messages = []

# Add the initial user question
add_user_message(messages, "Define quantum computing in one sentence")

# Get Claude's response
answer = chat(messages)

# Add Claude's response to the conversation history
add_assistant_message(messages, answer)

# Add a follow-up question
add_user_message(messages, "Write another sentence")

# Get the follow-up response with full context
final_answer = chat(messages)
```

Now Claude will understand that "Write another sentence" means extending the definition of quantum computing, because you've provided the full conversation context.

These helper functions will be very useful throughout your work with Claude, making it much easier to build applications that maintain meaningful conversations across multiple turns of exchange.

#### Video transcript

Naturally, at some point we might want to continue this conversation. So we might want to be able to send a follow-up question, like "Write another sentence." Then we'd expect to get back a reply that extends the quantum computing content in some way. To have a multi-message conversation like this, you need to understand a very crucial thing about the Anthropic API and Claude itself.

That is, the Anthropic API and Claude don't store any of the messages you send to them. None of the messages you send are stored in any way, and none of the replies you get back are stored in any way. So if you want to have some kind of conversation where multiple messages can maintain context or flow, then you need to do two things. You need to manually maintain a list of all the exchanged messages in your code.

Second, you need to make sure you provide that complete message list with every subsequent request. ### 8. System prompts

> Type: Video | Duration: 6:20 | Video: 03 - 006 - System Prompts.mp4

System prompts are a powerful way to customize how Claude responds to user input. Instead of getting generic answers, you can shape Claude's tone, style, and approach to match your specific use case.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623273%2F03_-_006_-_System_Prompts_00.1748623272065.png)

## Why system prompts matter

Consider building a math tutoring chatbot. When a student asks "How do I solve for x in the equation 5x + 2 = 3?", you want Claude to behave like a real tutor rather than just giving the answer directly. A good math tutor should:

- Initially give hints rather than the complete solution
- Patiently guide the student to solve the problem step by step
- Show the solution to a similar problem as an example

You definitely don't want Claude to:

- Immediately give the direct answer
- Tell the student to just use a calculator

## How system prompts work

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623273%2F03_-_006_-_System_Prompts_05.1748623273817.png)

System prompts provide guidance for how Claude should respond. You define them as plain strings and pass them into the `create` function call. The main advantages are:

- System prompts provide guidance for how Claude should respond
- Claude will try to respond the way a person in the specified role would
- Helps keep Claude on task

Here's the basic structure:

```
system_prompt = """
You are a patient math tutor.
Do not directly answer a student's questions.
Guide them to a solution step by step.
"""

client.messages.create(
    model=model,
    messages=messages,
    max_tokens=1000,
    system=system_prompt
)
```

## Seeing the difference

Without a system prompt, Claude will immediately give a complete, step-by-step solution. This can be helpful, but it doesn't encourage the student to think through the problem themselves.

With the math tutor system prompt, Claude's responses change dramatically. Instead of providing the complete solution, Claude asks guiding questions, such as "What do you think the first step should be to isolate x? Consider what operation we might need to perform on both sides to start moving terms around."

## Building a flexible chat function

You can make your chat function more reusable by accepting the system prompt as a parameter instead of hardcoding it:

```
def chat(messages, system=None):
    params = {
        "model": model,
        "max_tokens": 1000,
        "messages": messages,
    }
    
    if system:
        params["system"] = system
    
    message = client.messages.create(**params)
    return message.content[0].text
```

This approach handles an important detail: Claude's API doesn't accept `system=None`, so you need to conditionally include it only when a system parameter is provided.

Now you can call your chat function with or without a system prompt:

```
# Without a system prompt
answer = chat(messages)

# With a system prompt
system = """
You are a patient math tutor.
Do not directly answer the student's questions.
Guide them to a solution step by step.
"""
answer = chat(messages, system=system)
```

System prompts are essential for creating AI applications that behave consistently and align with their intended purpose. They turn generic AI responses into specialized, role-appropriate interactions.

#### Video transcript

In this video, we're going to explore how to customize the tone and style of the responses Claude generates. To help you understand why this matters, I want you to imagine that we're building a math tutor chatbot. Users will use this chatbot to ask for help solving math problems. For example, a user might ask for help solving 5x + 2 = 3.

Now, there are some things we want our math tutor to do, and definitely some things we don't want it to do. For example, we might want Claude to initially just give the student some hints. Maybe just give them one or two little tips on how to start approaching the problem. Then, if the student still doesn't quite understand how to solve the problem, only then might it guide the student through the solution step by step.

We might also want Claude to show the solution to a similar problem, to give the student some inspiration for how to approach this specific problem. Likewise, there are some things we definitely don't want Claude to do. For example, we don't want Claude to just immediately respond with a complete answer. We also don't want Claude to tell the student to just go use a calculator to solve the problem or anything like that.

To address this, we're going to use a technique called "system prompting." System prompts are used to customize the style and tone of Claude's responses. We define a system prompt as a plain string, then pass it into the create function call. The first line of a system prompt usually assigns Claude a role, so we might just tell Claude that it is a patient math tutor.

This will encourage Claude to respond the way a real math tutor would. It might become patient, provide lots of explanation, but probably not directly answer the student's questions. I'm going to assign it a multi-line string. Inside, I'm going to put a system prompt I wrote ahead of time. So I'll tell Claude that it is a patient math tutor. It should not directly answer the student's questions.

Instead, give them some guidance on how to solve the problem. I'll make sure to pass the system prompt as the `system` keyword argument to the `create` function. Then I'll re-run this cell. I'll go back down and see how Claude responds now.

So now we've provided a more interactive experience for the student.

This will hopefully help them learn what's going on here better than just getting a direct answer. Okay, so clearly, using system prompts is a powerful tool for steering Claude in a specific direction in terms of how it answers a given user input. Before we continue, I want to do a little refactoring of our `chat` function. Instead of hardcoding a system prompt inside the function, I want to be able to specify a system prompt when calling the `chat` function.

In other words, I want to cut this part out and put it down in the cell below, and then I want to be able to pass in a system prompt like this. If I run this cell now, then run the cell below, everything will work fine, exactly as expected. So we're not allowed to pass in a system prompt that is `none`. So we need to assemble the parameters we're going to pass to the `create` function more dynamically. If we have a `system` that is `none`, we don't want to include this parameter at all.

So let me show you how we do this with a little refactor. First, I'm going to create a parameters dictionary above. I'm going to cut and paste `model`, `max_tokens`, and `messages`. I'm going to convert it to dictionary syntax.

That's it. Now I'll re-run that cell. I'll go back to the next one. If I call `chat` without passing in any `system` keyword argument, no problem.

Everything will work fine. If I decide I do want to provide a system prompt, yes, that'll work fine too. Okay, this looks good. So we now support system prompts in our `chat` function.

### 10. Temperature

> Type: Video | Duration: 6:07 | Video: 03 - 008 - Temperature.mp4

Temperature is a powerful parameter that controls how predictable or creative Claude's responses are. Understanding how to use it effectively can greatly improve your AI applications.

## How Claude generates text

Before diving into temperature, it's helpful to understand Claude's text generation process. When you send Claude a prompt like "What do you think?", it goes through three key steps:

- **Tokenization** - Breaking your input into smaller chunks
- **Prediction** - Calculating probabilities for possible next words
- **Sampling** - Selecting a token based on those probabilities

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623338%2F03_-_008_-_Temperature_00.1748623338635.png)

In this example, Claude might assign "about" a 30% probability, "would" 20%, "of" 10%, and so on. The model then selects a token and repeats the entire process to build the complete sentence.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623339%2F03_-_008_-_Temperature_05.1748623339740.png)

## What temperature does

Temperature is a decimal value between 0 and 1 that directly affects these selection probabilities. It's like a "creativity knob" for tuning Claude's responses.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623340%2F03_-_008_-_Temperature_06.1748623340446.png)

At low temperature (near 0), Claude becomes very deterministic—it almost always selects the token with the highest probability. At high temperature (near 1), Claude distributes probabilities more evenly across the various options, producing more diverse and creative output.

## Interactive temperature demo

You can see temperature in action through Claude's interactive demo. Watch how the probability distribution changes as you adjust the temperature slider:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623341%2F03_-_008_-_Temperature_07.1748623341049.png)

At temperature 0.0, "about" gets 100% of the probability—completely deterministic. At temperature 1.0, the probability is distributed more evenly across all possible tokens, introducing randomness and creativity.

## Choosing the right temperature

Different tasks call for different temperature ranges:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623341%2F03_-_008_-_Temperature_10.1748623341732.png)

### Low temperature (0.0 - 0.3)

- Factual answers
- Coding assistance
- Data extraction
- Content moderation

### Medium temperature (0.4 - 0.7)

- Summarization
- Educational content
- Problem solving
- Constrained creative writing

### High temperature (0.8 - 1.0)

- Brainstorming
- Creative writing
- Marketing content
- Joke generation

## Implementing temperature in code

Adding temperature support to your chat function is straightforward. Here's how to modify your existing function:

```
def chat(messages, system=None, temperature=1.0):
    params = {
        "model": model,
        "max_tokens": 1000,
        "messages": messages,
        "temperature": temperature
    }
    
    if system:
        params["system"] = system
    
    message = client.messages.create(**params)
    return message.content[0].text
```

The key changes are adding `temperature=1.0` as a parameter and including `"temperature": temperature` in the `params` dictionary.

## Testing temperature's effects

To see temperature in action, try generating movie ideas with different settings:

```
# Low temperature - more predictable
answer = chat(messages, temperature=0.0)

# High temperature - more creative  
answer = chat(messages, temperature=1.0)
```

At temperature 0.0, you might consistently get responses like "A time-traveling archaeologist must stop the theft of an ancient artifact." At temperature 1.0, you'll see more diversity in themes, characters, and plot elements.

## Key takeaways

Remember, temperature doesn't guarantee different output every time—it just changes the probability of getting them. Even at very high temperature, Claude may sometimes produce similar responses. The key is to match your temperature choice to your specific use case:

- Need consistent, factual answers? Use low temperature
- Want creative brainstorming? Turn up the temperature
- Somewhere in between? Medium temperature works well for most general tasks

Temperature is one of the most practical parameters you can adjust to fine-tune Claude's behavior to meet your specific needs.

#### Video transcript

Earlier in this course, we briefly touched on how Claude actually generates text. Remember, we feed Claude some text, like "what do you think." Claude then tokenizes this text, or breaks it down into smaller chunks. Next, Claude enters a prediction phase, where it decides which words might come next and assigns a probability to each different option.

Finally, in the sampling phase, the system actually selects a token based on these probabilities. So in this diagram on my screen, given the input "what do you think," the possible next token might be "about," "wood," and so on. Everything you see here on the right side. Each one is assigned a probability.

Then, maybe in this case, Claude decides that "about" is the best next token. So we end up with a phrase, "what do you think about?". The whole process repeats to complete the sentence or the entire message. Now, to make things very clear, the numbers I'm showing here are probabilities, that is, the percentage chance of each token being selected.

To make these probabilities clearer, I'm going to display them in a chart for the rest of this video. So the probabilities are still the same, just in a format that's easier for us to understand. You'll also notice I've sorted them from left to right. There's actually no internal ordering.

I just sorted by probability from largest to smallest to make this chart easier to understand. So now that we've reviewed how Claude generates text, I want to show you a way that we can directly influence these probabilities and control which token Claude might ultimately select. So we can use a parameter called "temperature" to control these probabilities. Temperature is a decimal value between 0 and 1 that we provide when making the model call.

So whenever we call that converse function, temperature affects the exact distribution of the probabilities. This is a bit hard to grasp. So you can look at my diagram here or these charts. Or, I made a quick little demo with plot itself to give you a better sense of what's going on.

So let me show that demo. Okay, this is the same chart we just saw. Whenever we provide a temperature value approaching zero, as you can see, I have temperature here, the highest-probability event becomes more likely to happen. So our highest probability is "about," and it increases all the way up to 100%.

So at temperature zero, we start to get what we call deterministic output, that is, we always select the token with the highest initial probability. Then, as we start increasing the temperature, the chance that we select a token with a lower initial probability also increases. So we go from maybe a 0% chance of selecting "we" as the next token, up to say 9%. So that's the theory behind temperature, but what does this actually mean in the real world?

Well, we start to use different temperature values based on the actual task we're trying to accomplish. These are some example ranges and the tasks that might fit each example range. For a task like data extraction, we really don't want too much randomness or creativity. If we give Claude a large block of text and ask it to extract very specific information, there's no need for any creativity there at all.

We just need Claude to look at the exact text we provided and extract the most relevant information. Then on the higher-temperature end, we start to become more creative. We start to see rarer tokens being used. We might want to use a higher temperature when doing any truly creativity-focused task, like brainstorming, writing, maybe doing some very creative marketing, or things like jokes, where a lot of the essence of a joke lies in using words in ways that aren't always so expected.

Now that we understand all about temperature, let's go back to our notebook and learn how to dynamically adjust temperature. I want to update our `chat` function to accept a `temperature` parameter, which we'll pass to our `create` function call. So in the parameter list, I'm going to add `temperature`, and I'm going to default my temperature to 1.0. So I want to lean toward the more creative side.

Then I'm going to receive that parameter and add it as `temperature` to the `Params` object. That's all we have to do to add support for adjusting temperature in our application. So now to test this, I'm going to re-run this cell. Then I'm going to go to the next cell, where I'm going to ask Claude to generate a one-sentence movie idea.

Initially, I'm going to provide a temperature of 0.0. So now in theory, I should get movie ideas that are essentially always somewhat similar. So I'm going to run this. The first time, I get a time-traveling archaeologist.

You'll find this is a very common pattern, at least for me. When my temperature is zero, I often get movie ideas about something involving time travel. So if I run it again, I might see another time travel thing. Yep, the same thing.

Maybe one more time. Yep, the same idea, a weary time-traveling historian. Now let's try adjusting our temperature, hopefully to encourage Claude to give me some more original or creative ideas. I'm going to try turning my temperature up to 1.0.

Now if I run it again, I'm hoping I won't get another idea about a weary time traveler or something like that. You can almost immediately see that I did. So this is something to be aware of. Just because you turn up the temperature doesn't mean you'll always get drastically different ideas.

It just increases the chance of getting a different idea. So if I run it again, I might see a more creative idea. Okay, that's definitely more creative. This time there's nothing about time travel, let's test again.

Once again, not about a time traveler or anything like that. Okay, that's temperature. Now remember, here's some general guidance. Whenever we're doing a task that requires less creativity, or we want a very deterministic output, that's when we want to use that lower temperature value.

Whenever we have a task that requires a bit more creativity, that's when we want to start thinking about turning the temperature up a bit.

### 12. Response streaming

> Type: Video | Duration: 8:23 | Video: 03 - 009 - Response Streaming.mp4

When building chat applications with Claude, there's a significant user experience challenge: response generation can take 10-30 seconds, leaving users staring at a loading spinner. The solution is "response streaming," which lets users see text appear chunk by chunk as Claude generates it, creating a much more responsive feel.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623337%2F03_-_009_-_Response_Streaming_00.1748623336822.png)

## The problem with standard responses

In a typical chat setup, your server sends the user message to Claude and waits for the complete response before sending anything back to the client. This creates an awkward delay where the user gets no feedback that anything is happening.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623338%2F03_-_009_-_Response_Streaming_02.1748623337803.png)

## How streaming works

With streaming enabled, Claude immediately sends back an initial response indicating that it has received your request and is beginning to generate text. Then you receive a series of events, each containing a small portion of the entire response.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623338%2F03_-_009_-_Response_Streaming_03.1748623338384.png)

Your server can forward these text chunks to your client application as they arrive, letting users watch the response build up word by word. All of these events are part of a single request to Claude.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623339%2F03_-_009_-_Response_Streaming_04.1748623338949.png)

## Understanding stream events

When you enable streaming, Claude sends back several types of events:

- **MessageStart** - A new message is being sent
- **ContentBlockStart** - The start of a new block containing text, tool use, or other content
- **ContentBlockDelta** - A chunk of actual generated text
- **ContentBlockStop** - The current content block is complete
- **MessageDelta** - The current message is complete
- **MessageStop** - End of information about the current message

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623339%2F03_-_009_-_Response_Streaming_11.1748623339633.png)

The `ContentBlockDelta` events contain the actual generated text you want to display to users.

## Basic streaming implementation

To enable streaming, add `stream=True` to your `messages.create` call:

```
messages = []
add_user_message(messages, "Write a 1 sentence description of a fake database")

stream = client.messages.create(
    model=model,
    max_tokens=1000,
    messages=messages,
    stream=True
)

for event in stream:
    print(event)
```

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623340%2F03_-_009_-_Response_Streaming_12.1748623340577.png)

## Simplified text streaming

Instead of manually parsing events, you can use the SDK's simplified streaming interface, which only extracts text content:

```
with client.messages.stream(
    model=model,
    max_tokens=1000,
    messages=messages
) as stream:
    for text in stream.text_stream:
        print(text, end="")
```

This approach automatically filters out everything except the actual text content, which is usually what you need when displaying responses to users.

## Getting the complete message

While streaming individual chunks is great for user experience, you'll often need the complete message for storage or further processing. After streaming completes, you can get the assembled final message:

```
with client.messages.stream(
    model=model,
    max_tokens=1000,
    messages=messages
) as stream:
    for text in stream.text_stream:
        # Send each chunk to your client
        pass
    
    # Get the complete message for database storage
    final_message = stream.get_final_message()
```

This gives you the best of both worlds: real-time streaming for users, and a complete message object for your application logic.

#### Video transcript

I want to revisit that original chat interface example we looked at earlier in this section. So, remember, the idea here is that we have a chat window running in a web app or mobile app. The user enters a question. This question gets submitted to our server.

We'll stuff this content into a user message and send it to Claude. Claude then gives us back an assistant message. We extract the text from it and send it back to our mobile app or web app. And, hopefully, this content appears on screen.

Now, this sounds pretty straightforward and easy at this point, but there's a small problem here that we haven't really solved yet. You see, the time between sending the user message to Claude and finally receiving the assistant message back is likely to be much longer than we'd expect. In some cases, depending on the size of the input user message and the output assistant message, it could take 10 seconds, or even as long as 30 seconds. Now, during the entire time the user is waiting for a response, we could display a loading spinner on the screen.

But this isn't a great user experience. Most users expect that when they enter some initial message, like "what is quantum computing," they should see some response on screen almost immediately. To get a better user experience, we're going to use a technique called "streaming." So let me tell you how streaming works.

Our server still sends an initial user message to Claude, but then Claude sends an initial response back to us almost immediately. This initial response doesn't actually contain any text content. Instead, it's more like a signal to our server that Claude has received our initial request and that Claude is about to start generating some text. Then, we'll start receiving a stream of events.

We'll dig into exactly what these events are, but for now, you just need to understand that they contain pieces of the generated response that we want to send back and ultimately display to the user. The number of events we receive depends on the amount of text we're generating. Each event will contain only a small portion of the entire generated message. So, maybe the first event only contains the text "quantum," then the second says "computing," the third is "is," and so on.

Now, each event doesn't just contain one word. It might contain many words, or even a whole sentence. It really depends on how much time it takes Claude to generate each little piece of text. Now, as I mentioned, our server will receive these events, and our server can optionally extract the text from each event and immediately send it back to our web app or mobile app, or wherever else we're using it, where we can display that little chunk of text on the screen.

Then we can repeat this process for each additional event received on our server. So the end effect is that the user will start to see some text appear chunk by chunk in the chat interface. Now let's go back to our notebook, where we'll write a bit of code to better understand how streaming works. Okay, back in my notebook, I still have these three helper functions.

Now, we're going to ignore the `chat` function in this video, because once we start using streaming, it doesn't work very well with the `chat` function we've already implemented. So I'm going to create a messages list down here and manually call the `client.messages.create` function. So I'm going to create an empty messages list. I'm going to add a user message to the messages list.

I'm going to have Claude write a one-sentence description of a fictional database. Then I'm going to call `client.messages.create`. I'm going to pass in the model name. We still need to provide a `max_tokens`.

Provide our messages list. Then finally, we'll put in an additional keyword argument `stream=True` here. This will return to us, instead of a final answer, a stream of different events. We can iterate over this thing, since it's a normal iterator.

So we can say `for event in stream:` and then print out `event`. Now, if I run this, we'll quickly see a series of different events on screen. So each of these represents a different little chunk of data Claude sent back to us. You'll notice we start with an event called `raw message store event`.

Then we get a `raw content block start`, a `raw content block delta`. We actually get several. Then, at the bottom, we end up with a `content block stop` event, a `message delt` event, and a `message stop` event. So these are all events that Claude sent back to us, all within the context of a single request.

Each of these different events has some meaning within the context of the entire response we got from Claude. However, there's one event type we usually care about more than all the others, and that's the raw `content block delta` event. This event contains the actual text that Claude is generating and sending back to us chunk by chunk. In fact, we usually get the same sequence of events repeatedly.

So when we get a response from Claude, we almost always start by receiving a `message start`, then a `content block start`, then we'll get a series of `content block deltas`. Again, these are the ones containing the actual text. So we usually want to collect all these different events and extract the text from them, then send that text back to our web app or mobile app, or whatever else we're using. Now, back in our notebook, inside this for loop, we can add a check to determine which type of event we're dealing with.

Then, if it's one of those raw content block delta events, we can dig into it and grab the text we actually care about. But this requires us to write a lot of extra code. Fortunately, the Anthropic SDK provides a different way of creating a stream than the one I just showed. This alternative method of creating a stream, which I'll show you shortly, makes it much easier to get just the text from the response.

Again, the text is usually the part of the response we really care about. So let me show you an alternative method of streaming a response. I'm going to go to the next code cell. Here, I'm going to create a messages list again, add a user message that says "write a one-sentence description of a fictional database."

Then we're going to call a slightly different function and wrap it in a `with` block. So we say `with client.messages.stream`. Inside, we'll again put in our `model`, `max_tokens`, and `messages`. But we don't need to add the `stream=True` parameter.

Then we'll say `as stream`, colon, then indent, and inside, I'll say `for text in stream.text_stream`, like this. So now `text` is just the text portion of those different events. So just the text we actually care about, which again is almost always what we really care about when streaming a response from Claude. Now to show you how this actually works, I'm going to add a `print` statement and log out that text.

I'm going to add an `end` of an empty string. The `end` of an empty string is just to make sure these print statements don't add a newline at the end of the print statement. So we'll see each piece of text logged side by side. Now I'm going to run this, and it'll happen very fast, but you'll see that we're now receiving a streaming response chunk by chunk.

Let me do it one more time. So running again. I'll see chunk, chunk, chunk. That's it.

You'll notice each chunk contains multiple different words. So again, we're not guaranteed to get just a single word in each event. We might get several. There's one last feature here I want to show you.

Now, as I mentioned, we often want to stream the response back to a mobile app or web app, so the user can see each chunk of text appear on screen as quickly as possible. But another thing we often want to do is, after streaming is complete, take the entire message out and perhaps store it in a database. That way we have a record of the entire conversation we had with a specific user. Let me show you how we can collect all these different events and assemble them all into a single final message.

I'm going to replace this print statement with `pass`, so we don't have any printing. Then, after it, I'm going to do `stream.getfinalmessage`. Now, if I run this again, we're still streaming a response, which we can print out if we want, but we'll also collect all the individual events we received and assemble them into a final message, which we can then store in a database, or do whatever we need with it.

### 13. Controlling model output

> Type: Video | Duration: 6:27 | Video: 03 - 010 - Controlling Model Output.mp4

Beyond crafting better prompts, there are two powerful techniques for controlling Claude's output: prefilling assistant messages and stop sequences. These methods let you precisely control how Claude responds and when it stops generating text.

## Prefilling assistant messages

Prefilled Assistant Messages let you provide the beginning of Claude's response, and it will continue from that starting point. This technique is very useful for steering Claude in a specific direction.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623335%2F03_-_010_-_Controlling_Model_Output_01.1748623335487.png)

Here's how it works: instead of just sending a user message, you add an assistant message at the end of your messages list. After seeing this assistant message, Claude thinks: "I've already started answering this question, so I should continue from where I left off."

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623336%2F03_-_010_-_Controlling_Model_Output_05.1748623336281.png)

For example, if you ask "Is tea or coffee better at breakfast?" without prefilling, Claude usually gives a balanced answer mentioning both options. But if you add an assistant message saying "Coffee is better because," Claude will continue from there and build an argument for coffee.

The key thing to understand is that Claude continues exactly from where your prefilled text ends. If you write "Coffee is better because," Claude won't repeat that text—it will continue right after "because" and complete the thought.

Here's the code structure:

```
messages = []
add_user_message(messages, "Is tea or coffee better at breakfast?")
add_assistant_message(messages, "Coffee is better because")
answer = chat(messages)
```

You can use this technique to steer Claude in any direction:

- Lean toward coffee: "Coffee is better because"
- Lean toward tea: "Tea is better because"
- Take the opposite stance: "Neither is very good because"

## Stop sequences

Stop Sequences force Claude to immediately end its response when it generates a specific character sequence. This is perfect for controlling the length or endpoint of a response.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623337%2F03_-_010_-_Controlling_Model_Output_15.1748623337033.png)

The concept is straightforward: you provide a list of strings, and as soon as Claude generates any one of them, it stops responding immediately. The stop sequence itself is not included in the final response.

For example, if you ask Claude to "count from 1 to 10" and set the stop sequence to "5", you'll get:

```
1, 2, 3, 4, 
```

Generation stops before "5" because that's your stop sequence.

To implement stop sequences, you need to modify your chat function to accept a `stop_sequences` parameter:

```
def chat(messages, stop_sequences=[]):
    # Add stop_sequences to your API call parameters
```

Then use it like this:

```
messages = []
add_user_message(messages, "Count from 1 to 10")
answer = chat(messages, stop_sequences=["5"])
```

You can precisely tune where the stop happens. If you want to avoid a trailing punctuation mark, you can use a more specific stop sequence, like ", 5", instead of just "5".

## Practical applications

These techniques are especially useful for:

- **Format consistency:** Use prefilling to ensure responses always start with a specific structure
- **Length control:** Use stop sequences to truncate responses at natural breakpoints
- **Biased responses:** When you need Claude to take a specific stance rather than remaining neutral
- **Structured output:** Combine both techniques to generate responses that match a specific template

Both prefilled messages and stop sequences give you fine-grained control over Claude's behavior, making them important tools for building reliable AI applications.

#### Video transcript

Beyond just changing the prompt we send to Claude, there are two other ways to strongly influence the output we get from it. So in this video, we're going to discuss two techniques. One is prefilling assistant messages, and the second is stop sequences. Let's first look at prefilling assistant messages.

Okay, let's imagine we send Claude a very hard-to-answer question. Like "Is tea or coffee better at breakfast?" I have no idea what kind of response Claude will give me. In fact, let's quickly go to our Jupyter notebook and see what kind of response we get first.

Back in my notebook, I'm creating an empty messages list. Then I'm going to add a user message with the text "Is tea or coffee better at breakfast?" Then I'm going to feed this messages list into our chat function, and then I'm going to print out the answer we get. I'm going to run this.

Then, if we look at the response, we'll find that Claude doesn't take a firm stance. Instead, Claude takes a middle-of-the-road approach, saying some people like tea and some like coffee. Overall, this response is perfectly fine, but in some cases, we might want to steer Claude's response in one direction or another. There could be a situation where we want to always steer Claude toward tea, or another where we want to steer it toward coffee.

So, we can do this by prefilling an assistant response. With message prefilling, we still assemble a messages list. We'll put our user prompt in it, but with one small additional difference. You and I will manually add an assistant message at the end, and you and I will write the content of that assistant message.

Then, in Claude, we can imagine this is what happens behind the scenes. We can imagine Claude sees the first message and says to itself, okay, the user wants to know my opinion on coffee and tea. Then it looks at the second message, which is an assistant message. Because this is an assistant message, Claude says to itself, oh, it looks like I already have some thoughts on this situation.

So I'd better continue my final response. I'll use this as the opening of my reply. So Claude will basically use this as the start of its response. Because Claude saw the sentence "Coffee is better because," this will very strongly steer it toward supporting coffee being better at breakfast.

So, very likely, Claude will give us back a final assistant message saying something like "it has a higher caffeine content," meaning it's talking about coffee. Now, one thing to pay special attention to here is that whenever we put this final assistant message in here, Claude will assume this is already-written content, and it will continue its response from the end of this sentence. So you might expect Claude to give you a complete response like this that says "Coffee is better because it has a higher caffeine content," but that's not the case. It will continue the response from the end of what you prefilled.

In other words, this isn't actually a complete sentence. If you want to use this, you might need to go back and join that text with this text. Okay, it's a bit hard to explain. But in practice, once you've seen a demo or two, it becomes very simple.

So let's just write some code and see how this actually works. Back here, if you want to steer Claude toward supporting coffee, after adding the user message, we can add another assistant message. Pass in our messages list, then we put our prefill content here. So I might say, "Coffee is better because," just like this.

We've now added two different messages to our initial messages list and sent them to Claude. Again, Claude will see the assistant message and assume it wrote this content. So the rest of the response we get next will be steered toward supporting coffee. Now let's run this and see what kind of response we get.

There it is. So now Claude has clearly taken the stance of preferring coffee at breakfast, and it provided justification for the text we wrote earlier. It justifies this by saying coffee gives you energy to start your day. If we want to change Claude's direction, having it prefer tea, we can change coffee to tea, like this, re-run, and now we'll get some reasoning for why tea is better.

Finally, we can also steer Claude in a completely different direction. So we can change the assistant message to something like "Neither is very good because," and now Claude will try to argue why neither tea nor coffee is very good at breakfast. Now that we've looked at message prefilling, let's look at the other topic of this video, which is stop sequences. Stop sequences force Claude to immediately stop generating a response when it generates a specific string you provide.

So let's imagine we provide a prompt to "count from 1 to 10," and naturally we expect to get 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 all the way to 10. We can stop generation early by providing a string "5" as the stop sequence. Then internally, whenever Claude generates the string "5," it will immediately stop responding and send back whatever it has already generated. Again, let's look at a quick example of this.

Back in my notebook, I'm going to scroll up to find our `chat` function implementation. I'm going to add an additional parameter `stop_sequences` to it, and default it to an empty list. Then I'm going to update my `premiums` dictionary to add that `stop_sequences` parameter. Then I'm going to go to the bottom of my notebook, where I'm going to add a new code cell.

I'm going to create an empty messages list again. I'm going to add a user message to the list and have Claude count from 1 to 10. Then I'm going to feed this list into the `chat` function and print out the answer. So let's run this, and as expected, we'll probably get 1 through 10.

Now let's try limiting the sequence at 5. In other words, if Claude generates the character 5, I want to immediately stop the response and not generate any more text after that. To do this, I can add a `stop_sequences` list here. In it, I'm going to put a string containing "5."

Then, if I run this cell, I should see 1, 2, 3, 4, comma and space, then 5 is generated, but because I provided it as a stop sequence, the entire generation ends here, and 5 will not be included. If I want to remove that extra space and comma, I can put comma, space, 5 in my stop sequence, like this. Now if I run this again, I'll just get 1, 2, 3, 4.

### 14. Structured data

> Type: Video | Duration: 5:59 | Video: 03 - 011 - Structured Data.mp4

When you need Claude to generate structured data like JSON, Python code, or bulleted lists, you often run into a common problem: Claude is helpful and always wants to add explanatory text around your content. While this is usually nice, sometimes you just want the raw data and nothing else.

Consider building a web app that generates AWS EventBridge rules. The user enters a description, clicks generate, and expects to see clean JSON they can copy and use immediately. If Claude returns JSON wrapped in markdown code blocks and explanatory text, the user can't simply copy the entire response—they have to manually select just the JSON part.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623326%2F03_-_011_-_Structured_Data_02.1748623325858.png)

## The problem with default responses

By default, when you ask Claude to generate JSON, you might get something like this:

```
```json
{
  "source": ["aws.ec2"],
  "detail-type": ["EC2 Instance State-change Notification"],
  "detail": {
    "state": ["running"]
  }
}
```

This rule captures EC2 instance state changes when instances start running.
```

The JSON is correct, but it's wrapped in markdown formatting and includes explanatory text. For a web app that needs users to copy the raw JSON, this creates an inconvenient user experience.

## The solution: assistant message prefilling + stop sequences

You can combine assistant message prefilling with stop sequences to get exactly the content you want. Here's how it works:

```
messages = []

add_user_message(messages, "Generate a very short event bridge rule as json")
add_assistant_message(messages, "```json")

text = chat(messages, stop_sequences=["```"])
```

This technique works as follows:

1.  The user message tells Claude what to generate
2.  The prefilled assistant message makes Claude think it has already started a markdown code block
3.  Claude continues by writing only the JSON content
4.  When Claude tries to close the code block with ```, the stop sequence immediately ends generation

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623327%2F03_-_011_-_Structured_Data_15.1748623326804.png)

The result is clean JSON without any extra formatting:

```
{
  "source": ["aws.ec2"],
  "detail-type": ["EC2 Instance State-change Notification"],
  "detail": {
    "state": ["running"]
  }
}
```

## Processing the response

You may notice some extra newlines in the response. These are easy to handle:

```
import json

# Clean up and parse the JSON
clean_json = json.loads(text.strip())
```

## Beyond JSON

This technique isn't limited to JSON generation. You can use it whenever you need structured data without any commentary:

- Python code snippets
- Bulleted lists
- CSV data
- Any formatted content where you just want the content without an explanation

The key is to recognize the structure that Claude naturally wants to use to wrap your content, then use it as your prefill and stop sequence. For code, this is usually a Markdown code block. For lists, it might be a different formatting marker.

This approach gives you precise control over the format of Claude's output, making it easier to integrate AI-generated content into applications that require clean, structured data.

#### Video transcript

Stop sequences and assistant message prefilling can be combined in a very powerful way. Any time you need to generate some kind of structured data, which you'll probably do quite often, this is useful. To help you understand how these things work together, I'm going to walk you through a quick example. Let's imagine we're building a web application like the one you see on screen.

This is a web app that will generate EventBridge rules based on user input. If you're not familiar with them, EventBridge rules are used in AWS, and they're essentially small JSON snippets. So, the user will enter some prompt like this, then click "generate." The user most likely wants to see a generated rule appear here immediately, which they can very easily select or click that little copy button and use elsewhere.

The key point here is that a critical part of the user experience is that we only want to display the JSON of the generated rule, and nothing else. So if we display a response that looks like this, it's certainly not as helpful for our users. We're still generating the rule, but now it has a header at the top and a comment footer at the bottom. So now the user can't really use this "copy all" button.

They'll have to go in and manually select the JSON there. So this is an example where we really don't want Claude to be so helpful and explain its work. We just want some very specific data and nothing else. Now to be clear, this isn't a problem only when generating JSON.

It turns out, any time you use Claude to generate any kind of structured data. So it might be JSON, it might be Python, or even just a text list of items. Claude often tries to insert a header or footer or some extra commentary. And in many of these scenarios, you don't want those extra comments.

You just want the raw content you asked Claude to create. So, to help Claude stay on track and just give us the raw content we asked for, without any extra headers, footers, comments, or anything like that, we can use our stop sequence combined with a prefilled assistant message. Let me show you how. I'm going to go back to my notebook.

I'm going to start a new cell here to continue. I'm going to create a messages list again. I'm going to add a user message. I'll say something like "Generate a very short event ridge rule in Jason format."

Then I'll pass it out like this, and let's see what we get on our first attempt. So right away, we can see we did get some JSON, but unfortunately it has that backtick, backtick, backtick, JSON part, and then a matching closing part. To be very clear, these backticks are for formatting all of this as Markdown. So if you were to render this as Markdown text, it would format very nicely.

But in our case, we don't want any of those extra characters. We just want the raw JSON itself. To do this, we can do two things. We're going to use both an assistant message and a stop sequence.

Now, let's just write the code to do this. Then I'll show you a diagram to explain how all of this works. First, I'm going to prefill an assistant message. So we say add assistant message.

My prefill message will be backtick, backtick, backtick, JSON. Then in my chat call, I'm going to add stop sequences. Any time we see backtick, backtick, backtick, I want to immediately stop generation. So now let's run this cell and see what we get.

Okay, now we just get the JSON itself. You'll notice there are some newlines here, but that's perfectly fine. We can very easily remove those extra newlines by parsing the response as JSON or by calling strip. So I can say text is chat.

I'm going to print out text, then in the next cell, I might import JSON and use JSON loads and strip on text. If I run it, yes, we do get very nicely formatted JSON that we can access in any way we expect. Okay, so what exactly is going on with the assistant message and stop sequence? Well, let me show you a diagram to break this all down and make sure it's very clear.

So again, we're doing our user message. We provide a prefilled assistant and a stop sequence. Claude will look at all the different parts of this request. It will first look at that user message content and then say, okay, clearly I need to write a complete rule.

And I should probably describe it too. So maybe put a header and a footer, because this is kind of what Claude naturally wants to do. It wants to explain the work it's doing. But then it will encounter that assistant message.

Just like we learned in the previous video, Claude will assume it has already written that content in its response. So it will say, oh, I've already started the JSON part. So now all I have to do is write out the actual JSON. It will then write out all this JSON in the response.

Then when it reaches the very end, it will naturally want to close that Markdown code block it thinks it created earlier. So Claude will want to put in a closing backtick, backtick, backtick. However, as soon as it does this, it will encounter the stop sequence, which will completely stop generation and immediately send the response back to us. So you can really imagine that what's really happening here is we're kind of saying, start from this, end at that, just give us everything in between.

And this leads to us getting just the part we really care about, which is the JSON itself. As I mentioned, this is a very powerful technique we'll use very frequently. Any time we want to generate some kind of structured data and just get that data, nothing else. And remember, this technique can be used for any kind of structured data.

It's not limited to use on JSON. So any time we have some very specific content we want to generate and just get that content without any extra commentary, we'll consider using assistant message prefilling and stop sequences.
