### 15. Structured data exercise

> Type: Video | Duration: 4:57 | Video: 03 - 012 - Structured Data Exercise.mp4

#### Video transcript

Let's do a very quick exercise, just to make sure the concepts of stop sequences and message prefilling are very clear. So in this exercise, I want you to write the exact code I'm showing here on screen. All of this code should be very familiar. If you look at the prompt, it says "Generate three different example AWS CLI commands."

Now, when you run this code, you might get some output that looks roughly like this. To make it easier to read, I've rendered it as Markdown here. This is the example starting output I got.

You'll notice it does give us three different example commands, but there's a lot of commentary around them. So I have a header, then some numbers listing each individual command. For this exercise, I want you to take this code, and using only message prefilling and stop sequences, I want you to get all three different commands in a single response, next to each other, without any extra comments or explanation or anything like that. I want you to do this using only message prefilling and stop sequences, so don't adjust this prompt at all.

So please give it a try. I've put a little hint here, just to remind you that with message prefilling, it's not limited to using characters like backticks. You can put in any prefilled response you want. I now encourage you to pause this video and give this exercise a try.

Otherwise, I'll give a solution right away. Okay, here we go. This is how we solve this. To solve this, the first thing I suggest you do is look at the output without any prefilling or stop sequences or anything like that.

So if we look at what we have here, we'll notice that each of our three commands is wrapped in a series of three backticks. So a good start might be to put in a prefill message consisting of three backticks, to tell Claude, start writing the commands immediately, skip any initial commentary. Then we might also decide to put in a stop sequence of three backticks as well. Let's see how far this gets us.

So I'm going to put in an assistant message. I'm going to start everything with three backticks. I'm going to put in a stop sequence of three backticks. Let's run this and see how far it gets us.

My initial output looks fairly reasonable, but it's not quite perfect yet. I did get three commands. This is one, two, and three. But you'll notice, I also got the word `bash` at the very beginning.

So where exactly did this word come from? Well, let me show you what Claude really wants to do. We provided an initial assistant message of three backticks. Whenever you put down three backticks, this kind of indicates you're writing some Markdown.

When you write a Markdown code block with backticks, you can optionally put a language identifier here. If you choose to put one in, then when you render this as Markdown, the content inside those backticks will be rendered with syntax highlighting for that language. So in this case, Claude decided to put `bash` in here, just to say, hey, when we render this stuff, we should use bash-style syntax highlighting. Now for us, we don't want that at all.

So one way we can solve this might be to adjust our prefill message here and include `bash` ourselves. So this now makes it very clear to Claude, yes, you're inside a Markdown code block. Inside this code block, you should write out commands in bash format. So let me try running this one more time and see how I do now.

Okay, that looks much better. Now I'll tell you, from this point, there are probably two more bugs you might want to fix. The first is that sometimes you'll get a single command, which kind of indicates Claude might want to write out three separate Markdown code blocks. Another problem you might run into is that, because we're now using a bash code block, Claude might try to insert some bash-format comments in there.

So it might look something like, `This command does XYZ`, and you might see this repeated. So we definitely don't want those comments, really just because that's one of the requirements of this exercise. So to get rid of those comments, and also to make sure we more reliably get all three commands, we can use that hint I gave you. Remember that hint was that message prefilling isn't limited to specifying characters like backticks or things like that.

We can also use message prefilling to greatly steer how Claude answers us. So in this case, we can put in something like "Here are all three commands in a single code block, without any comments." Then I'll put a colon there, then a newline, so that it starts all the Markdown content on the next line. So I'm going to try running this, and we should now get some more reliable output.

Okay, that looks good. Of course, I could run it all day, and we'd probably see the exact result we want. Okay, this looks good.

### 17. Prompt evaluation

> Type: Video | Duration: 1:48 | Video: 04 - 001 - Prompt Evaluation.mp4

When working with Claude, writing a good prompt is just the beginning. To build reliable AI applications, you need to understand two key concepts: prompt engineering and prompt evaluation. Prompt engineering gives you techniques for writing better prompts, while prompt evaluation helps you measure how well those prompts actually perform.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623381%2F04_-_001_-_Prompt_Evaluation_00.1748623381094.png)

## Prompt Engineering vs Prompt Evaluation

Prompt engineering is your toolbox for crafting effective prompts. It includes techniques like:

- Multishot prompting
- Structuring with XML tags
- Many other best practices

These techniques help Claude accurately understand what you're asking and how you want it to respond.

Prompt evaluation takes a different approach. Rather than focusing on how to write prompts, it measures their effectiveness through automated testing. You can:

- Test against expected answers
- Compare different versions of the same prompt
- Review errors in the output

## Three paths after writing a prompt

After drafting a prompt, you typically face three follow-up choices:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623382%2F04_-_001_-_Prompt_Evaluation_10.1748623382207.png)

Option 1: Test the prompt once and decide it's good enough. This carries a huge risk of breaking in production when users provide unexpected input.

Option 2: Test the prompt a few times and fine-tune it to handle one or two edge cases. While better than option 1, users will often provide very unexpected output you hadn't considered.

Option 3: Run the prompt through an evaluation pipeline to score it, then iterate on the prompt based on objective metrics. This approach takes more work and cost, but gives you much greater confidence in the prompt's reliability.

## Why most engineers fall into the testing trap

Options 1 and 2 are common traps that all engineers (including myself) fall into. It's natural to write a prompt for a serious application without testing it thoroughly enough. We tend to underestimate the number of edge cases real users will encounter.

The reality is, when you deploy a prompt to production, users will interact with it in ways you never anticipated. A prompt that looks reliable in your limited testing will quickly break down when faced with a wide range of real-world inputs.

## The evaluation-first approach

Option 3 represents a more systematic approach to prompt development. By running your prompt through an evaluation pipeline, you get objective metrics about its performance across a much broader range of test cases. This data-driven approach lets you:

- Identify weaknesses before production problems arise
- Objectively compare different versions of prompts
- Iterate with confidence based on measurable improvements
- Build more reliable AI applications

While this approach requires more up-front investment in time and testing infrastructure, it pays off handsomely in the reliability and robustness of the final application. The goal is to discover problems during development rather than after users encounter them.

#### Video transcript

Now that we understand how to access Claude, we're going to shift focus a bit and look at two new topics: prompt engineering and prompt evaluation. Both topics are about making sure the prompts we write get the best output from Claude. Prompt engineering is a set of techniques we want to use when writing or editing a prompt. These techniques will help Claude understand what we're asking and how we want it to respond.

On the other hand, prompt evaluation is where we run some automated tests on a prompt, with the goal of getting some kind of objective metric that tells us whether our prompt is effective. In this section, we'll focus primarily on prompt evaluation. After we understand how to measure the effectiveness of a prompt, we'll look at some prompt engineering techniques. So, let's get started.

The first thing I want to do is help you understand where prompt evaluation fits in the overall prompt-writing process. Whenever you first write a prompt, you usually have three different paths forward. There are three different ways you can go from there. For option one, you might take the prompt you put together, maybe test it once or twice, then decide it's good enough to use in production.

For option two, you might test the prompt a few times with your own custom inputs, and maybe fine-tune it a bit to handle one or two edge cases you noticed. I want you to understand right away that options one and two are traps that all engineers fall into, including myself. This happens to everyone. We all start writing prompts that will ultimately be used in serious applications, but we don't really test them thoroughly enough to make sure they work as expected.

So whenever you write a prompt, I strongly recommend you choose option three. Run your prompt through an evaluation pipeline to get an objective score, which will tell you how well your prompt performs. Then you can try iterating on your prompt a bit and making sure it performs as well as possible.

---

### 18. A typical eval workflow

> Type: Video | Duration: 4:36 | Video: 04 - 002 - A Typical Eval Workflow.mp4

A typical prompt evaluation workflow follows five key steps that help you systematically improve your prompts through objective measurement. While there are many different ways to assemble these workflows, and various open-source and paid tools available, understanding the core process helps you start small and scale as needed.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623388%2F04_-_002_-_A_Typical_Eval_Workflow_00.1748623388621.png)

## Step 1: Draft a prompt

Start by writing an initial prompt you want to improve. For this example, we'll use a simple prompt:

```
prompt = f"""
Please answer the user's question:

{question}
"""
```

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623390%2F04_-_002_-_A_Typical_Eval_Workflow_04.1748623389909.png)

This basic prompt will serve as our baseline for testing and improvement.

## Step 2: Create an eval dataset

Your eval dataset contains sample inputs that represent the types of questions or requests your prompt will handle in production. The dataset should contain questions that will be inserted into your prompt template.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623391%2F04_-_002_-_A_Typical_Eval_Workflow_06.1748623390933.png)

For this example, our dataset includes three questions:

- "What's 2+2?"
- "How do I make oatmeal?"
- "How far away is the moon?"

In a real eval, you might have dozens, hundreds, or even thousands of records. You can assemble these datasets manually, or use Claude to generate them for you.

## Step 3: Feed to Claude

Take each question from your dataset and merge it with your prompt template to create complete prompts. Then send each one to Claude to get a response.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623391%2F04_-_002_-_A_Typical_Eval_Workflow_08.1748623391715.png)

For example, the first question becomes:

```
Please answer the user's question:
What's 2+2?
```

For the math question, Claude might answer "2 + 2 = 4," provide cooking instructions for oatmeal for the second question, and give the distance to the moon for the third question.

## Step 4: Evaluate through a grader

A grader evaluates the quality of Claude's responses by examining the original question and Claude's answer. This step provides an objective score, typically on a scale of 1 to 10, where 10 represents a perfect answer and lower scores indicate room for improvement.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623392%2F04_-_002_-_A_Typical_Eval_Workflow_11.1748623392294.png)

In our example, the grader might give:

- Math question: 10 (perfect answer)
- Oatmeal question: 4 (needs improvement)
- Moon question: 9 (very good answer)

The average score across all questions gives you an objective measurement: (10 + 4 + 9) ÷ 3 = 7.66

## Step 5: Change the prompt and repeat

Now that you have a baseline score, you can modify your prompt and run the entire process again to see whether your changes improved performance.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623393%2F04_-_002_-_A_Typical_Eval_Workflow_15.1748623393094.png)

For example, you might add more guidance to the prompt:

```
prompt = f"""
Please answer the user's question:

{question}

Answer the question with ample detail
"""
```

After running this improved prompt through the same evaluation process, you might get a higher average score of 8.7, indicating that the additional instruction helped Claude provide better responses.

## Prompt scoring

The key benefit of this workflow is getting an objective measurement of prompt performance. You can:

- Compare different versions of prompts numerically
- Use the highest-scoring version
- Continue iterating to find better approaches

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623393%2F04_-_002_-_A_Typical_Eval_Workflow_17.1748623393804.png)

This systematic approach eliminates guesswork in prompt engineering and lets you trust that your changes are actually improvements, not just different variants.

#### Video transcript

In this video, we're going to walk through all the steps involved in a typical prompt evaluation workflow. However, before doing any of these steps, I just want you to understand two important things. First, there are many ways to build a workflow. There's no single fixed methodology that's standardized across the industry.

The second thing to understand is that there are many different open-source packages, and even online paid options, that can help you implement your own workflow. Now, in this video and this module, we're going to implement our own custom workflow from scratch in a Jupyter notebook. The reason we do this, of course, is just to help you understand how these workflows behave, but also to help you understand that you don't have to adopt a very heavyweight solution to do prompt evaluation. You can start small, just to get going and understand how everything works, and then scale up later.

Okay, so let's get started. The first step of a typical prompt evaluation. In the first step, we're going to write out an initial draft of a prompt. So you and I are going to sit down and just write out some prompt we want to improve in some way.

For this example, we'll have a very simple prompt that just says, "Please answer the user's question." Then we'll insert some user input. That is, some question provided by the user. In the second step, we're going to create an eval dataset.

This dataset will contain some possible inputs we might want to put into our prompt. So for us, our prompt has only one input, a user-provided question. So for our eval dataset, we'll have a list of different possible questions we might want to put into our prompt. My dataset contains only three different questions.

But in a real eval, your dataset might have dozens, hundreds, or even thousands of different records. Now, you can assemble these datasets manually, or of course you can use Claude to generate them for you. Once we have our eval dataset, we'll feed each of these different questions into our prompt. This gives us a complete prompt, which we can then feed into Claude.

So we might have a first prompt here, where we have "Please answer the user's question," then an example question from our dataset, like "What's two plus two?" Then we'll repeat this for all the other records in our dataset. So here are the second and third. Then we'll feed each of these into Claude and get an actual response from Claude.

So for the first one, we might get a response like "2 plus 2 is 4," then something about how to make oatmeal, then something about the distance to the moon. Once we get these actual answers from Claude, we'll score them in some way. In this scoring step, we'll take each question from our dataset, along with the answer we got from Claude. We'll pair them all up, then feed them one by one into a grader.

We can implement this grader in many different ways. We'll look at a few different approaches later. Then the grader gives us a score, maybe from 1 to 10, based on the quality of the answer Claude produced. So a 10 means we got a perfect answer, with really no possible way to improve it.

Maybe a score like 4 indicates there's definitely room for improvement there. Now, as you might guess, there's a lot of hidden complexity in the grader here, because you might be curious or wondering, how exactly do we get these scores? Again, don't worry. We'll discuss these graders in more detail later.

After we get these scores, we'll average them all together. So in this case, I'll add up the scores, divide by three, and get an average score of 7.66. So now I have an objective way to describe how well our initial prompt performed. Now that we have this score, we can change our prompt in some way and iterate or repeat the entire process.

So if I want to improve my score, I might try adding more detail to the prompt, hoping to steer Claude more and help it understand what kind of output we want. So I might add something to the end of the prompt like "Answer the question with ample detail." Once I have the second version of my prompt, I'll run it through the entire pipeline again. Then I'll have scores for prompt version one and prompt version two.

Then I can compare these two scores, whichever is greater or higher. This is an objective indicator, better than nothing, that tells me, in this case, prompt V2 is probably the better version of our prompt. So now that we have a high-level overview of the entire process, as I mentioned, we're going to start implementing our own custom evaluation framework in a Jupyter notebook. So, let's start implementing it in the next video.

### 19. Generating test datasets

> Type: Video | Duration: 4:44 | Video: 04 - 003 - Generating Test Datasets.mp4

Building a custom prompt evaluation workflow starts with creating a reliable prompt, then generating test data to see how it performs. Let's build up an evaluation system step by step to assess a prompt that helps users write AWS-specific code.

## Setting the goal

Our prompt needs to help users write three specific types of output for AWS use cases:

- Python code
- JSON configuration files
- Regular expressions

The key requirement is that when a user asks for help with a task, we return clean output in one of these three formats, without any extra explanation, header, or footer.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623393%2F04_-_003_-_Generating_Test_Datasets_01.1748623392676.png)

Here is our starting prompt (version 1):

```
prompt = f"""
Please provide a solution to the following task:
{task}
"""
```

## Creating the eval dataset

The eval dataset contains the inputs we'll feed into the prompt. For each combination of prompt and input, we'll run the prompt and analyze the result.

Our dataset will be an array of JSON objects, where each object contains a "task" property describing the task we want Claude to complete. We can create this dataset manually, or generate it automatically using Claude.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623393%2F04_-_003_-_Generating_Test_Datasets_05.1748623393811.png)

Since we're generating test data, this is a great opportunity to use a faster model like Haiku instead of the full Claude model.

## Generating test data with code

Let's create a function that automatically generates a test dataset. First, we need the helper functions for working with Claude:

```
def add_user_message(messages, text):
    user_message = {"role": "user", "content": text}
    messages.append(user_message)

def add_assistant_message(messages, text):
    assistant_message = {"role": "assistant", "content": text}
    messages.append(assistant_message)

def chat(messages, system=None, temperature=1.0, stop_sequences=[]):
    params = {
        "model": model,
        "max_tokens": 1000,
        "messages": messages,
        "temperature": temperature
    }
    if system:
        params["system"] = system
    if stop_sequences:
        params["stop_sequences"] = stop_sequences
    
    response = client.messages.create(**params)
    return response.content[0].text
```

Now we'll create our dataset generation function:

```
def generate_dataset():
    prompt = """
Generate an evaluation dataset for a prompt evaluation. The dataset will be used to evaluate prompts that generate Python, JSON, or Regex specifically for AWS-related tasks. Generate an array of JSON objects, each representing task that requires Python, JSON, or a Regex to complete.

Example output:
```json
[
  {
    "task": "Description of task",
  },
  ...additional
]
```

* Focus on tasks that can be solved by writing a single Python function, a single JSON object, or a single regex
* Focus on tasks that do not require writing much code

Please generate 3 objects.
"""
```

To correctly parse the JSON response, we'll use prefilling and stop sequences:

```
    messages = []
    add_user_message(messages, prompt)
    add_assistant_message(messages, "```json")
    text = chat(messages, stop_sequences=["```"])
    return json.loads(text)
```

## Testing dataset generation

Let's run our function and see what kind of test cases we get:

```
dataset = generate_dataset()
print(dataset)
```

This should return three different test cases covering our target outputs—Python functions, JSON configurations, and regular expressions for AWS-specific tasks.

## Saving the dataset

Once we have the dataset, we'll save it to a file so we can easily load it later during evaluation:

```
with open('dataset.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(dataset, f, indent=2)
```

This creates a `dataset.json` file in the same directory as your notebook, containing your list of tasks ready for prompt evaluation.

With this foundation, you now have a systematic way to generate test data to evaluate how well your prompt performs across different types of AWS-related coding tasks.

#### Video transcript

Let's start building our own custom prompt evaluation workflow. We're going to write out a prompt, then write some code to evaluate how it performs. So we'll first focus on making a prompt. The goal of our prompt is to help users write some specific code for AWS use cases.

So we're going to allow users to enter some kind of task they need help with. Then we'll respond with one of three output types. We'll either output Python, a JSON configuration, or a raw, pure regular expression. These are our three possible outputs.

So we need to make sure that when the user asks us to complete some kind of task, we give them some output in one of these three specific outputs, without any other kind of explanation, header, or footer or anything like that. So that's the overall goal. Now, the first step toward our goal, of course, is to write out a draft of a prompt. Now, I've already done this for us here on the right side.

I have version V1 of our prompt, which just says, "Please provide a solution to the following task," then we put the user's task in. The second step is to put together a dataset. Remember, a dataset will contain some inputs we'll feed into our prompt, then we'll run our prompt for each combination of prompt and input. For our specific case, we'll have an array of JSON objects, where each object has a task property.

These tasks will describe what we want Claude to complete, so we'll put each of these tasks into our prompt, then feed the result into Claude. Remember, when we make a dataset, we can assemble it manually, or we can generate it automatically with Claude. Now, by the way, if you're using Claude to do something like this, this would be a great opportunity to use a faster model like Haiku. That's what we'll do here.

Let's go back to Jupyter, where we'll open our notebook, then we'll write a bit of code that will generate an example dataset like the one you see on the left, using Haiku. Back here, I created a new notebook, which has a lot of the same code we've been using throughout the course. So I created a client in the top cell, and also loaded some environment variables, and I also created the same three helper functions we've been developing. Then, a bit further down, I defined a function called `generate_dataset`.

Inside, I prepared a fairly large prompt to help us get started. Now, I've attached this notebook to this lecture. So I encourage you to download this notebook and copy the prompt here, or just use this notebook to save yourself a lot of typing time. This prompt will ask Claude to go ahead and generate some different test cases for us.

### 20. Running the eval

> Type: Video | Duration: 6:42 | Video: 04 - 004 - Running the Eval.mp4

Now that our eval dataset is ready, it's time to build the core evaluation pipeline. This involves taking each test case, merging it with our prompt, feeding it to Claude, then scoring the results.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623396%2F04_-_004_-_Running_the_Eval_00.1748623396420.png)

The evaluation process follows a clear workflow: we take the dataset of test cases, combine each one with our prompt template, send it to Claude for processing, then evaluate the output using a grader system.

## Building the core functions

The evaluation pipeline consists of three main functions, each with a specific responsibility. Let's start with the simplest one—the function that handles a single prompt.

## The `run_prompt` function

This function takes a test case and merges it with our prompt template:

```
def run_prompt(test_case):
    """Merges the prompt and test case input, then returns the result"""
    prompt = f"""
Please solve the following task:

{test_case["task"]}
"""
    
    messages = []
    add_user_message(messages, prompt)
    output = chat(messages)
    return output

```

For now, we're keeping the prompt extremely simple. We haven't included any formatting instructions, so Claude might return more verbose output than we need. We'll optimize this later when we iterate on the prompt design.

## The `run_test_case` function

This function coordinates running a single test case and scoring the result:

```
def run_test_case(test_case):
    """Calls run_prompt, then grades the result"""
    output = run_prompt(test_case)
    
    # TODO - Grading
    score = 10
    
    return {
        "output": output,
        "test_case": test_case,
        "score": score
    }

```

Currently, we use a hardcoded score of 10. The grading logic is where we'll spend a lot of time in the coming sections, but this placeholder lets us test the entire pipeline.

## The `run_eval` function

This function orchestrates the entire evaluation process:

```
def run_eval(dataset):
    """Loads the dataset and calls run_test_case with each case"""
    results = []
    
    for test_case in dataset:
        result = run_test_case(test_case)
        results.append(result)
    
    return results

```

This function processes each test case in our dataset and collects all the results into a list.

## Running the eval

To execute our evaluation pipeline, we load the dataset and run it through our function:

```
with open("dataset.json", "r") as f:
    dataset = json.load(f)

results = run_eval(dataset)

```

The first time you run this, expect it to take some time—even with Claude Haiku, processing a full dataset can take around 30 seconds. We'll cover optimization techniques later.

## Inspecting the results

The evaluation returns a structured JSON array, where each object represents the result of one test case:

```
print(json.dumps(results, indent=2))

```

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623397%2F04_-_004_-_Running_the_Eval_18.1748623397308.png)

Each result contains three key pieces of information:

- **output**: The complete response from Claude
- **test_case**: The original test case that was processed
- **score**: The evaluation score (currently hardcoded)

As you can see in the output, since we haven't yet provided specific formatting instructions, Claude generated a fairly verbose response. This is exactly what we'll address when we optimize the prompt.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623397%2F04_-_004_-_Running_the_Eval_01.1748623397839.png)

## What we've accomplished

At this point, we've successfully built the core evaluation pipeline. We can take a dataset, process it through Claude, and collect structured results. The main missing piece is the scoring system—the hardcoded score of 10 needs to be replaced with actual evaluation logic.

This pipeline represents the foundation of most AI evaluation systems. While it may look simple, you've just built the bulk of what an evaluation pipeline actually does. The complexity is in the details—better prompts, sophisticated grading, and performance optimization.

Next, we'll dive into the critical topic of graders, which will transform our hardcoded score into a meaningful assessment of Claude's performance.

### 21. Model-based grading

> Type: Video | Duration: 10:01 | Video: 04 - 005 - Model Based Grading.mp4

When building a prompt evaluation workflow, the scoring system provides an objective signal for output quality. A grader takes a model output and returns some kind of measurable feedback—usually a number between 1 and 10, where 10 represents high quality and 1 represents low quality.

## Types of graders

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623451%2F04_-_005_-_Model_Based_Grading_03.1748623451557.png)

There are three main approaches to grading model outputs:

- **Code graders** - Use custom logic to programmatically evaluate outputs
- **Model graders** - Use another AI model to assess quality
- **Human graders** - Have people manually review and score outputs

### Code graders

Code graders let you implement any programmatic check you can imagine. Common uses include:

- Checking output length
- Verifying whether the output contains/does not contain certain words
- Syntax validation for JSON, Python, or regex
- Readability scores

The only requirement is that your code returns some usable signal—typically a number between 1 and 10.

### Model graders

Model graders feed your raw output into another API call for evaluation. This approach offers tremendous flexibility in assessing things like:

- Response quality
- Quality of instruction following
- Completeness
- Helpfulness
- Safety

### Human graders

Human graders offer the most flexibility, but are time-consuming and tedious. They can be used to evaluate:

- Overall response quality
- Comprehensiveness
- Depth
- Conciseness
- Relevance

## Defining evaluation criteria

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623452%2F04_-_005_-_Model_Based_Grading_06.1748623452682.png)

Before implementing any grader, you need clear evaluation criteria. For a code generation prompt, you might care about:

- **Format** - Should return only Python, JSON, or regex, with no explanation
- **Valid syntax** - The generated code should have valid syntax
- **Task following** - The response should directly solve the user's task with accurate code

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623453%2F04_-_005_-_Model_Based_Grading_07.1748623453532.png)

The first two criteria are well-suited to a code grader, while task following is better suited to a model grader due to its flexibility.

## Implementing a model grader

Here's how to build a model grader function:

```
def grade_by_model(test_case, output):
    # Create evaluation prompt
    eval_prompt = """
    You are an expert code reviewer. Evaluate this AI-generated solution.
    
    Task: {task}
    Solution: {solution}
    
    Provide your evaluation as a structured JSON object with:
    - "strengths": An array of 1-3 key strengths
    - "weaknesses": An array of 1-3 key areas for improvement  
    - "reasoning": A concise explanation of your assessment
    - "score": A number between 1-10
    """
    
    messages = []
    add_user_message(messages, eval_prompt)
    add_assistant_message(messages, "```json")
    
    eval_text = chat(messages, stop_sequences=["```"])
    return json.loads(eval_text)

```

The key insight is to ask for strengths, weaknesses, and reasoning in addition to the score. Without this context, the model tends to default to a mediocre score, around 6.

## Integrating grading into your workflow

Update your test case runner to call the grader:

```
def run_test_case(test_case):
    output = run_prompt(test_case)
    
    # Grade the output
    model_grade = grade_by_model(test_case, output)
    score = model_grade["score"]
    reasoning = model_grade["reasoning"]
    
    return {
        "output": output, 
        "test_case": test_case, 
        "score": score,
        "reasoning": reasoning
    }

```

Finally, calculate the average score across all test cases:

```
from statistics import mean

def run_eval(dataset):
    results = []
    
    for test_case in dataset:
        result = run_test_case(test_case)
        results.append(result)
    
    average_score = mean([result["score"] for result in results])
    print(f"Average score: {average_score}")
    
    return results

```

This way, you have an objective metric to track as you iterate on the prompt. While model-based graders can be somewhat fickle, they provide a consistent baseline for measuring improvement.

#### Video transcript

The next thing we're going to implement in our prompt evaluation workflow is a grading system. As a reminder, a grader takes in the output of our model. Then we want the grader to give us some kind of objective signal. This signal could be a number, or it could be a boolean (true/false).

It can be anything, but what you'll most often see is a number output between 1 and 10. Where 10 means we got a very high quality output, and 1 means we got a very low quality output. Again, this is not a requirement. We don't necessarily have to get numbers from these graders, but it's a very common practice that you'll see often.

In this video, we'll discuss three different types of graders: code-based, model-based, and human. Let's first look at what a code-based grader is. For a code-based grader, we feed the model's output into a piece of code that we write.

In this code, we can do almost any programmatic check you can think of. For example, we can verify that the model's output isn't too long or too short. We can ensure the output contains or doesn't contain certain words. If JSON or code is returned, we can programmatically validate the syntax, and we can even do more complex checks, like implementing a readability score to ensure the generated text is at a reading level appropriate for our specific use case.

The only requirement here is that when we run this code, it returns some kind of actual signal that we can use. Again, this is usually a number between 1 and 10, but it's not mandatory. The next type of grader you'll often see is the model-based grader. With this approach, we feed the output of the original model call (the one we already made) into another model.

So this is another API request. When we use a model grader, we gain tremendous flexibility. We can ask the model to evaluate a response based on its overall quality, how well it follows the prompt's instructions, the completeness of the response, and just about anything else you can think of.

Again, the only real requirement here is that the model returns some kind of clear objective signal, usually a number between 1 and 10.

Finally, there's human grading. For human grading, we present all the model's outputs to a real person. Then it's up to that person to evaluate the responses in some particular way. As you can imagine, humans are very flexible, so we can ask them to evaluate responses in just about any way you can imagine or against any metric.

A major drawback of human grading is that it usually takes a lot of time and is undoubtedly very tedious work. Now, no matter which grading approach you use, you need to decide your evaluation criteria up front. In other words, which aspects of the response will you focus on? For our specific use case, I've identified three different evaluation criteria.

First, I think we should evaluate the response to ensure we only get Python, JSON, or regular expression, without any additional explanation from Claude. Finally, we should probably do some general task-following checks to ensure the model clearly solves the user's task and answers with code that's basically accurate, without any major errors or logical mistakes.

So for these three different evaluation criteria, I think we can evaluate the first two with a code grader. We can evaluate the format, ensuring we get actual Python, JSON, or regex code. We can also validate the syntax of that code with additional code. Finally, for the general response and ensuring the user's question is clearly solved, given its flexibility, that's better addressed with a model grader.

OK, let's start by implementing the model grader, because believe it or not, it's probably the easiest one to implement. I'll start by going back to my notebook. Then, inside here, we'll basically call a model and ask it to grade the output. To do this, we usually write a fairly long prompt. Again, to save a little time, I'll copy and paste a prompt in. This one.

I'll paste it in. Yes, it's a bit long, but this is basically the minimum we want. This prompt sets a role. Then it very explicitly asks the model to evaluate an AI-generated solution.

Then we'll print out the task. Next, we'll list the solution the model generated. After that, we'll provide specific guidance on how to respond. In this particular example, I'm asking the model to give me a list of strengths and weaknesses of the AI-generated solution, along with the reasoning behind it and an actual score.

Now, we could just ask for a score, but if you do that, you'll often find you tend to just get a 6. So if you don't ask for any additional strengths, weaknesses, or reasoning, you'll usually just get very mediocre scores, because the model thinks, well, it could be better, it could be worse, let's just give it a 6. ### 22. Code-based grading

> Type: Video | Duration: 7:26 | Video: 04 - 006 - Code Based Grading.mp4

When evaluating an AI model that generates code, you need more than just checking whether the response makes sense. You also need to verify that the generated code actually has valid syntax and follows the correct format. This is where code-based grading comes into play.

## How code grading works

Code grading validates two key aspects of an AI-generated response:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623444%2F04_-_006_-_Code_Based_Grading_00.1748623444116.png)

- **Format** - The response should return only the requested type of code (Python, JSON, or Regex), with no explanation
- **Valid Syntax** - The generated code should parse correctly as the intended language
- **Task Following** - The response should directly solve the question asked and be accurate

The first two criteria are handled by a code grader, while task following is evaluated by a model grader. Together, they provide a comprehensive evaluation.

## Syntax validation functions

To check whether the generated code has valid syntax, you can create three helper functions that attempt to parse the output:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623445%2F04_-_006_-_Code_Based_Grading_02.1748623445106.png)

```
def validate_json(text):
    try:
        json.loads(text.strip())
        return 10
    except json.JSONDecodeError:
        return 0

def validate_python(text):
    try:
        ast.parse(text.strip())
        return 10
    except SyntaxError:
        return 0

def validate_regex(text):
    try:
        re.compile(text.strip())
        return 10
    except re.error:
        return 0
```

Each function attempts to parse the text in its respective format. If parsing succeeds, it returns a full score of 10. If parsing fails with an error, the syntax is invalid and it returns 0.

## Dataset format requirements

For the code grader to know which validator to use, your test cases need to specify the expected output format:

```
{
    "task": "Create a Python function to validate an AWS IAM username",
    "format": "python"
}
```

You can update your dataset generation prompt to automatically include this format field by adding it to the example output structure.

## Improving prompt clarity

To get better results from your AI model, make your prompt instructions more specific about the expected output format:

```
* Respond only with Python, JSON, or a plain Regex
* Do not add any comments or commentary or explanation
```

You can also use a pre-filled assistant message with a code block to encourage the model to return only raw code:

```
add_assistant_message(messages, "```code")
```

This tells Claude to start generating code content without needing to specify in advance whether it's Python, JSON, or Regex.

## Combining scores

The final step is to combine the model grader's score with the code grader's score. A simple approach is to take the average:

```
model_grade = grade_by_model(test_case, output)
model_score = model_grade["score"]
syntax_score = grade_syntax(output, test_case)

score = (model_score + syntax_score) / 2
```

This gives equal weight to content quality and technical correctness. You can adjust these weights based on what matters more in your specific use case.

## Testing your implementation

Once you've implemented code grading, run your evaluation to get a baseline score. The score itself isn't inherently good or bad—what matters is whether you can improve it by optimizing your prompt. This gives you a way to quantitatively measure your prompt engineering progress, rather than relying on subjective assessment.

#### Video transcript

Next, we need to implement our code grader. Our code grader will take in the model's output and ensure we only get pure Python, JSON, or regular expression, without any form of explanation. Additionally, we should ensure that any type of code we actually get has valid syntax. You might be wondering, how exactly are we going to validate, say, Python syntax?

Well, we solve this with a little trick. We'll define three helper functions, one called `ValidateJSON`, another called `ValidatePython`, and another called `ValidateRegX`. Then inside each function, we'll take whatever output we get from the model and try to parse it as JSON, or parse it as a Python Abstract Syntax Tree (AST), or try to compile it as a regular expression. For any of these operations, if we successfully parse or load, we'll return a full score of 10.

Otherwise, if an error occurs during this parsing operation, we'll assume the syntax check failed completely and return zero. There's one more thing to note here. To know which of these different validators or grading functions to run, we need our test case dataset to include the expected format for each output. In other words, in our dataset, for my first task, I expect to get a Python function, then JSON, then a regular expression.

So we need to update our dataset to include a key like `format` that says, hey, this output should be Python. This one should be JSON, and this one is a regular expression. Of course, I could manually edit this file, but we'll update the prompt that generates our dataset so we can eventually generate really large test datasets. Now, to get all this done, we need to go through a few different steps.

So, to help you understand the code side and keep everything organized, I've put together a quick checklist of the items we're going to do. So our first item is to add functions to validate JSON, Python, and regular expressions. For this, I'll go back to my notebook. Find the `run_test_case` cell.

I'll add a new cell above it. Then inside, I'll add the three functions you just saw in that diagram. Again, to save a little time, I'll paste them in here. You can always copy the full code from the completed version of this notebook.

So, at the top of the cell, I imported these two helper modules. Then the three different validator functions I just saw. At the bottom, I have a generic function to decide which validator to use. So I have the `grade_syntax` function here. It looks at the test case. It pays specific attention to the format. So we need to make sure our test case has that `format` attribute I just mentioned. Then based on this attribute, we'll call the corresponding formatting function.

OK, that's the first step. Now for the second step, we need to update our dataset to make sure we include that `format` key. For this, we'll scroll up a bit and find our dataset. This one.

So to generate the dataset, I'll add content to the example output. On the task, I'll add a comma at the end, then I'll add a `format` key, where we'll simply say JSON or Python or RegX. That's basically all we need to do. So now if I rerun that cell, and rerun the cell below it, which is the cell that actually generates the dataset, there we go.

Now we should go back to our dataset file. I'll see that, yes, I did get the format in there. And it looks like it matches the task perfectly. So the first task to create a JSON configuration got JSON, write Python got Python, then write a regular expression, and I got regx.

OK, on to step three. Now, we'll update our draft prompt template just to make sure it's very clear that we only want JSON, Python, or regular expression. Because right now, our draft prompt just says, yeah, try to solve the task. So inevitably, we'll get some content that's not JSON or not Python, and we'll always fail the actual validation checks.

So we're just going to give our prompt a little help, give it some of the work we know it needs. So for step three, we'll go back to our `run_prompt`, which is where our draft prompt is. I'll update the prompt a little. I'll add some comments and ask it to respond only with Python, JSON, or plain regular expression.

And don't add any comments, commentary, or explanation. Next, I'll make sure we only get the raw content we actually care about. Again, for this, we'll use a prefilled assistant message and a stop sequence. So I'll add an assistant message here.

In my assistant message, in this case, I can put in three backticks, and then usually, as we saw before, we might put something like JSON or Bash or Python here. But in this particular case, we don't really know in advance the specific format we expect to get. We don't know whether we'll get Python, JSON, or RegX. So here's a little trick, a little workaround, we can just put in `code`, which prefills the assistant message and tells Claude, hey, you're going to put some code in here, without us needing to specify whether it'll be Python, JSON, or reg X.

Then I'll add the closing stop sequence like this, with backticks. Finally, we need to combine the model grader's and code grader's scores together. For this, going back here, I'll scroll down again and find our `run_test_case` function. This is where we run the model grader, right below it.

I'll put the syntax grader or code grader, whatever you want to call it, together. So I'll say my syntax score is `grade_syntax`. We need to pass in the output and our test case.

Finally, we'll combine the syntax score and model score. I'll first rename `score` to `model_score` here, just to be clearer. I'll take the average of these two scores. So I'll say `score` will be `model_score` plus `syntax_score` divided by two.

That's it. That's all we need to do to add a little code grading. So the last thing is to test all this. For this, we'll go down a bit.

So right below the `runeval` function is where we actually call `runeval` and compute our overall average score. So I'll rerun this, remembering it usually takes quite a while to complete. After a brief pause, I got a final score of 8.166. Now the question is, is this good or bad?

Well, the real answer is we have no idea. The only way we can find out is to now try changing our prompt, hopefully getting a better score. So, let's try an exercise in the next video, where we'll try to change our prompt a little, and hopefully improve our score.

### 25. Prompt engineering

> Type: Video | Duration: 10:50 | Video: 05 - 001 - Prompt Engineering.mp4

Prompt engineering is the process of taking a prompt you've already written and refining it to get more reliable, higher-quality outputs. This process involves iterative optimization—starting with a basic prompt, evaluating its performance, then systematically applying engineering techniques to improve it.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623584%2F05_-_001_-_Prompt_Engineering_00.1748623584369.png)

## The iterative improvement process

This approach follows a clear cycle that you can repeat until you achieve your desired results:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623585%2F05_-_001_-_Prompt_Engineering_01.1748623585186.png)

1.  **Set a goal** - Define what you want your prompt to accomplish
2.  **Write an initial prompt** - Create a basic first draft
3.  **Evaluate the prompt** - Test it against your criteria
4.  **Apply prompt engineering techniques** - Use specific methods to improve performance
5.  **Re-evaluate** - Verify that your changes actually improved the results

You repeat the last two steps until you're satisfied with the performance. Each iteration should show a measurable improvement in your evaluation score.

## Setting up your evaluation pipeline

To demonstrate this process, we'll use a practical example: creating a prompt that generates a one-day meal plan for an athlete. The prompt needs to consider the athlete's height, weight, goal, and dietary restrictions, then generate a comprehensive meal plan.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623586%2F05_-_001_-_Prompt_Engineering_04.1748623585977.png)

The evaluation setup uses a `PromptEvaluator` class that handles dataset generation and model grading. When creating your evaluator instance, you can use the `max_concurrent_tasks` parameter to control concurrency:

```
evaluator = PromptEvaluator(max_concurrent_tasks=5)
```

Start with a low concurrency value (like 3) to avoid rate limit errors. If your API quota allows, you can increase it to speed up processing.

## Generating test data

The evaluation system can automatically generate test cases based on your prompt requirements. You define what inputs your prompt needs:

```
dataset = evaluator.generate_dataset(
    task_description="Write a compact, concise 1 day meal plan for a single athlete",
    prompt_inputs_spec={
        "height": "Athlete's height in cm",
        "weight": "Athlete's weight in kg", 
        "goal": "Goal of the athlete",
        "restrictions": "Dietary restrictions of the athlete"
    },
    output_file="dataset.json",
    num_cases=3
)
```

During development, keep the number of test cases small (2-3) to speed up your iteration cycle. You can increase this number for final validation.

## Writing your initial prompt

Start with a simple, naive prompt to establish a baseline. Here's an example of a deliberately basic first draft:

```
def run_prompt(prompt_inputs):
    prompt = f"""
What should this person eat?

- Height: {prompt_inputs["height"]}
- Weight: {prompt_inputs["weight"]}
- Goal: {prompt_inputs["goal"]}
- Dietary restrictions: {prompt_inputs["restrictions"]}
"""
    
    messages = []
    add_user_message(messages, prompt)
    return chat(messages)
```

This basic prompt will likely produce poor results, but it gives you a starting point against which to measure improvement.

## Adding evaluation criteria

When running the evaluation, you can specify additional criteria that the grading model should consider:

```
results = evaluator.run_evaluation(
    run_prompt_function=run_prompt,
    dataset_file="dataset.json",
    extra_criteria="""
The output should include:
- Daily caloric total
- Macronutrient breakdown  
- Meals with exact foods, portions, and timing
"""
)
```

This helps ensure your prompt is evaluated against the specific requirements that matter for your use case.

## Analyzing results

After running the evaluation, you'll get a numerical score and a detailed HTML report. The report shows exactly how each test case performed, including the model's reasoning for each score.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623586%2F05_-_001_-_Prompt_Engineering_18.1748623586518.png)

Don't be discouraged by a low initial score—for a first attempt, getting 2.3 out of 10 is typical. The goal is to see consistent improvement as you apply engineering techniques.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623587%2F05_-_001_-_Prompt_Engineering_19.1748623587369.png)

The detailed evaluation report helps you understand exactly where your prompt is failing and what improvements need to be made. Use this feedback to guide your next iteration.

## Next steps

With a baseline in place, you can start applying specific prompt engineering techniques. Each technique you learn should bring a measurable improvement to your evaluation score, gradually transforming your basic prompt into a reliable, high-performing tool.

Remember, prompt engineering is an iterative process. The key is to make one change at a time, evaluate its impact, and continue building on what works. This systematic approach ensures you understand which techniques are most valuable for your specific use case.

### 26. Being clear and direct

> Type: Video | Duration: 2:05 | Video: 05 - 002 - Being Clear and Direct.mp4

The first line of your prompt is the most important part of your entire request. This is where you lay the foundation for everything that follows, and getting it right can dramatically improve your results.

## Being clear and direct

When writing that crucial first line, you should focus on two key principles: being clear and being direct. This means using simple language and leaving Claude no room for ambiguity about what you want to do.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623557%2F05_-_002_-_Being_Clear_and_Direct_02.1748623557175.png)

## Clear communication

"Clear" means:

- Use simple language that anyone can understand
- State exactly what you want, without beating around the bush
- Open with a direct statement of Claude's task

Instead of writing something vague like "I need to learn about those things people put on their roofs to harness solar energy—solar panels, I think they're called," write directly: "Write three paragraphs about how solar panels work."

## Direct instructions

"Direct" focuses on how you structure your request:

- Use instructions, not questions
- Begin with a direct verb like "write," "create," or "generate"

Instead of asking "I'm reading articles about renewable energy, and geothermal energy sounds great. Which countries use it?", try: "Identify three countries that use geothermal energy. Include power generation statistics for each."

## Putting it into practice

Let's see this technique in action. Starting from a weak prompt that simply asks "What should this person eat?", we can apply our clear and direct approach.

The improved version becomes: Generate a one-day meal plan for an athlete that meets their dietary restrictions.

This revision immediately tells Claude:

- What action to take (generate)
- What to create (a meal plan)
- The key constraints (one day, for an athlete, meeting dietary restrictions)

## Results matter

This simple change can have a major impact on performance. In our example, the evaluation score jumped from 2.32 to 3.92—substantial progress just from restructuring that opening sentence.

The key takeaway is that Claude responds best when you treat it as a capable assistant that needs clear instructions, rather than as a person who has to guess what you want. Open strongly with a direct verb, be specific about the task, and you'll see better results right away.

#### Video transcript

With a starting score of 2.32, we can definitely only go up. So let's look at the first prompt engineering technique we'll use to improve the prompt. OK, we're going to discuss the concept of being clear and direct. These two rules are actually about the first line of your prompt.

The first line of your prompt is often the most important. In that first line, you should use simple, direct language and tell Claude exactly what its task is with a verb-like word. For example, we might want the first line of the prompt to be something like: Write three paragraphs about how solar panels work. This tells Claude that it has a task, that it needs to write, generate, or create something.

It also clarifies some information about the expected output, and what that output should contain. So we've actually set an action and provided a task in the very first line. Another good example is: Identify three countries that use geothermal energy, and provide power generation data for each. Again, we're telling Claude to do something, or giving it a direct task and some information about the expected output.

Let's put this idea of being clear and direct in the first line of the prompt into practice, and see if we can use it to improve the results of the prompt we're currently working on. Applying the rule we just learned, I might update this line to say something like: Generate a one-day meal plan for an athlete that meets their dietary restrictions. So, again, I keep it direct by using a verb at the beginning, and then with very simple language, I provide Claude with a direct task to complete.

Now let's rerun this cell to get the updated prompt, then rerun the evaluation itself. Let's see if this does anything to improve our score. I bet, as you've guessed, yes, we'll probably do a little better than before. So I remember I got 2.32 before, and now I'm at 3.92. Definitely an improvement, but still not good enough.

So let's move on to the next video and look at our next prompt engineering topic to further improve our prompt.

### 27. Being specific

> Type: Video | Duration: 5:14 | Video: 05 - 003 - Being Specific.mp4

When working with Claude, one of the most effective ways to improve results is to be specific about what you want. Rather than leaving everything to the model's interpretation, you can provide clear guidelines or steps to guide Claude toward the output you expect.

Think about it this way: if you ask Claude to "write a short story about a character who discovers a hidden talent," Claude could write it in countless ways. The story might be 200 words or 2000 words. It might have one character or five characters. It could focus on any kind of talent-discovery scenario.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623570%2F05_-_003_-_Being_Specific_00.1748623570613.png)

By adding specific guidelines, you give Claude a clearer target. This dramatically improves the consistency and quality of the output.

## Two kinds of guidelines

There are two main ways to be specific in a prompt, and you'll often see them used together in professional applications.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623571%2F05_-_003_-_Being_Specific_05.1748623571451.png)

### Output quality guidelines

The first type focuses on listing the qualities you want the output to have. These guidelines help you control:

- The length of the response
- Structure and format
- Specific attributes or elements to include
- Tone or style requirements

For example, you might specify that a story should be under 1000 words, include a clear action that reveals the character's talent, and have at least one supporting character.

### Process steps

The second type provides Claude with specific steps to follow. This approach is especially useful when you want Claude to think through a problem systematically or consider multiple angles before arriving at a final answer.

Instead of having Claude start writing right away, you can ask it to:

1.  Brainstorm three talents that could create dramatic tension
2.  Choose the most interesting talent
3.  Outline the key scene that reveals the talent
4.  Brainstorm the types of supporting characters that could increase the impact

## Real-world impact

The difference that being specific makes is enormous. When testing a meal plan prompt, adding guidelines raised the evaluation score from 3.92 to 7.86—more than doubling the output quality just by telling Claude exactly which elements to include.

```
Guidelines:
1. Include accurate daily calorie amount
2. Show protein, fat, and carb amounts  
3. Specify when to eat each meal
4. Use only foods that fit restrictions
5. List all portion sizes in grams
6. Keep budget-friendly if mentioned
```

## When to use each approach

Here's a practical guide for when to use each method of being specific:

### Always use output guidelines

You should include quality guidelines in nearly every prompt you write. They're a safety net for getting consistent, useful results.

### Use process steps for complex problems

Add step-by-step instructions when you're dealing with:

- Solving complex problems
- Decision-making scenarios
- Critical thinking tasks
- Any situation where you want Claude to consider multiple angles

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623572%2F05_-_003_-_Being_Specific_18.1748623572246.png)

For example, if you ask Claude to analyze why a sales team's performance is declining, you'd want to guide it to examine market metrics, industry changes, individual performance, organizational changes, and customer feedback—rather than letting it focus on just one possible cause.

## Combining both approaches

In professional prompting, you'll often see these two techniques used together. You might have guidelines that control the output format and content, plus steps that ensure Claude thinks through the problem thoroughly before responding.

This combination both guarantees consistency in the results and gives you confidence that Claude has considered all the important factors in reaching its conclusion.

#### Video transcript

The next topic we're going to discuss in the area of prompt engineering is the concept of being specific. To be specific, we want our prompt to list some kind of guidelines or steps that steer our model in a particular direction in some way. For example, consider the prompt on the left side of the screen. In this prompt, I'm asking Claude to write a short story about a character who discovers a hidden talent.

If I just fed that prompt to Claude on its own, Claude could write it in countless ways. It could decide the story length varies greatly. It could decide to add or remove elements in the story. It might have just one character, or it might introduce five different characters.

If I want to make sure I get a specific type of output, I might decide to add a list of guidelines, like you see on the right. These guidelines will provide some high-level direction, or steer Claude in a particular way as it starts generating its response. For example, I might decide to add some guidelines, like keeping the story under a thousand words, adding some rising action, and including at least one supporting character. Now I've provided a little guidance to steer Claude toward writing a specific type of short story.

Now, in prompts, you'll often see two kinds of guidelines. Type A on the left side is kind of like what I just showed you in the previous image. You might decide to put in a list of guidelines that list out some qualities you want the output to have. So you might try to control the length or structure of the output, or list some different attributes the output should have.

On the right, the second type of guideline we can provide is to provide some actual steps the model should follow, with the goal of getting the model to think about specific things or choose between different directions, thereby hopefully improving the quality of our output. For example, we might instruct Claude to first brainstorm three special talents that would be very interesting, then pick the most interesting one. We might then ask Claude to try to outline or think about some interesting scenes that could reveal that talent, then think about different types of supporting characters who could make the story a bit more interesting. So on the left, we're actually directing the attributes of the output.

On the right, we're trying to be more specific about how Claude arrives at the final product. You absolutely can, and you'll often see this in professional prompts, you'll often see these two techniques mixed together. So you might have a list of guidelines aimed at controlling certain attributes of the output, and then also a list of steps the model should follow. Both of these are examples of being specific in your prompting.

So now we have a better understanding of what being specific means. Let's go back to the prompt we're working on and see if we can incorporate this idea of being specific. OK, back here, I'm looking at my `run_prompt` function. To save a little time, I'll first paste in a list of guidelines.

This is kind of like the first way of being specific, where I list out some attributes I really want to see in the output. I'll run that cell, then run the evaluation below. Let's see what we got. After a quick pause, I'll get a final score of 7.86.

This is an amazing improvement over our previous 3.92, just by adding some guidance and telling Claude exactly which things we want to see in the output. Now I'll try a small variation of this approach, where I'll use the second variation of being specific. So I'll provide some steps Claude should follow when deciding how to structure this meal plan. So now in this case, I tell Claude to first do a calculation, then consider this, then do a bit of planning.

You get the idea. I've provided some steps Claude should follow. I'll run this cell again. Then run down here, and I'll remember that 7.86 score, which is really very high, possibly a bit of a statistical anomaly.

Now we get 7.3. So still a significant improvement, but not as good as listing out the attributes we want to see in the output. So for me, I'll revert and go back to listing some guidelines. So when would you want to use one technique versus the other?

Well, I'd generally recommend almost always listing out the qualities the output should have, as I showed on the left, on just about any prompt you work on. And you'll usually want to provide the steps the model should follow, as shown on the right, any time you're asking Claude to handle a more complex problem, where you want to force Claude to consider a broader perspective or some additional topics it might not naturally consider. For example, consider the prompt on the right side of the screen, where I ask Claude to find out why a sales team's performance declined in the last quarter. In this case, we might want to force Claude to consider some additional perspectives or additional data that it might not immediately consider.

OK, now that we have a better understanding of what being specific really means, and the idea of adding guidelines or steps depending on the situation, let's take a little break here, then continue with our next prompt engineering topic later.

---

### 28. Structuring with XML tags

> Type: Video | Duration: 4:00 | Video: 05 - 004 - Structure with XML Tags.mp4

When you build a prompt that contains a lot of content, Claude can sometimes struggle to understand which pieces of text belong together, or what the different sections are supposed to represent. XML tags provide a simple way to add structure and clarity to your prompt, especially when you're inserting large amounts of data.
## Why structure matters

Imagine a prompt where you need to analyze 20 pages of sales records. Without clear boundaries, Claude may struggle to distinguish your instructions from the actual data you want analyzed.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623568%2F05_-_004_-_Structure_with_XML_Tags_00.1748623567902.png)

The example above shows how unclear boundaries can make it hard for Claude to parse your intent. By wrapping the sales records in XML tags like `<sales_records>` and `</sales_records>`, you create clear delimiters that help Claude understand the structure of the prompt.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623569%2F05_-_004_-_Structure_with_XML_Tags_06.1748623569098.png)

## Practical example: code and documentation

Here's a more dramatic example of why XML tags matter. If you ask Claude to debug code using provided documentation, mixing everything together causes confusion:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623570%2F05_-_004_-_Structure_with_XML_Tags_10.1748623569882.png)

The "worse" version makes it nearly impossible to distinguish what's code from what's documentation. The "better" version uses `<my_code>` and `<docs>` tags to create clear boundaries.

## Custom tag names

You don't have to use official XML tags. You can create descriptive names that make sense for your content:

- `<sales_records>` is better than `<data>`
- `<athlete_information>` clearly identifies the user details
- `<my_code>` and `<docs>` separate different types of content

The more specific and descriptive your tag names, the better Claude can understand the purpose of each section.

## When to use XML tags

XML tags are most useful when:

- Including large amounts of context or data
- Mixing different types of content (code, documentation, data)
- You want content boundaries to be exceptionally clear
- Working with complex prompts that interpolate multiple variables

Even for shorter content, XML tags can serve as delimiters that make the structure of your prompt more obvious to Claude.

## Real-world application

In practice, you might structure a prompt like this:

```
<athlete_information>
- Height: 6'2"
- Weight: 180 lbs
- Goal: Build muscle
- Dietary restrictions: Vegetarian
</athlete_information>

Generate a meal plan based on the athlete information above.

```

This makes it crystal clear that the height, weight, goal, and restrictions are all related athlete data that should be considered together when generating the meal plan.

While you may not see a significant improvement for simple prompts, the value of XML tags becomes increasingly apparent as your prompts grow more complex and contain more varied content.

#### Video transcript

The next topic we'll explore is using XML tags in prompts to provide structure. Let me first give you a bit of background. A lot of times, when we write a prompt, we insert some content into it. We've already done this before.

In our example, we've already inserted some height, weight, goal, and restrictions. Now, these values are relatively small, but it's entirely possible that we'll eventually write a prompt that requires putting a large amount of content into the prompt. For example, look at the prompt on the right. We might decide to paste in 20 pages of sales records and try to get Claude to analyze them in some way.

When we dump a large amount of content into a prompt, Claude can have a bit of trouble figuring out what each piece of text really means, or how the text fits together. One way to make our prompt structure clearer is to wrap different pieces of content in XML tags. So, for example, I could provide more structure for this prompt on the right by wrapping the sales records here in a `sales_records` XML tag. Like this.

Now, there isn't an official XML tag called `sales_records`. I just made up this tag name, and it might give Claude a little insight into the nature of the content that exists within these tags. I could have called it `records`, or even `data`. But of course, being a bit more specific here is definitely better.

So, providing a tag like `sales_records` will probably get us the best output. Now, I want to make sure you're clear on why XML tags like this are necessary. So let me show you a slightly exaggerated example. In the prompt on the left, I have a leading sentence: "Debug my code using the documentation provided below."

This implies two things. It implies that below this header sentence, there's some buggy code I wrote, as well as some documentation. If you just look at the code listed here, you absolutely can't tell which content is code and which content is the actual documentation. One way to make this clear to Claude is to wrap each block of code in appropriate XML tags.

For example, on the right, I can wrap my code in some XML tags, simply writing `my_code`, very direct and obvious, and then put the code representing the documentation in a `docs` tag, again, very clear and obvious. Now it's much easier for Claude to understand which part of the code it's debugging, and which part of the code provides the documentation source. Let's use this idea of providing structure with XML tags to improve the prompt we're working on in our notebook. Now, unfortunately, in this particular case, we don't have a big block of content that really needs to be delimited in any way.

All the content we're inserting, like height, weight, goal, and restrictions, is short enough that Claude probably won't be confused by them in any way. Either way, we can still use some XML tags to make it clear that this is an external input, or perhaps information about the athlete that should be considered when generating the meal plan. So we might decide to wrap the entire block in some XML tags, just to make it clear that this is information about the athlete. So I could put a tag like `athlete information`.

Then put a closing tag on the other side. Now let's try to measure this and see if it has any impact on the output quality. So I'll rerun this cell, then go to my evaluation cell and run this. Then you might remember, before adding those XML tags, my score was 7.3.

So let's see whether we go up or down. It turns out I went up a lot. Now, you might not see this big a jump. As a reminder, I'm using a simpler, more basic model, just to get some exaggerated returns from these prompt improvements.

So if you don't see a big leap in quality, that's completely fine.
