### 29. Providing examples

> Type: Video | Duration: 6:44 | Video: 05 - 005 - Providing Examples.mp4

Providing examples in a prompt is one of the most effective prompt engineering techniques you'll use. This approach is called "one-shot" or "multi-shot" prompting, and it guides Claude's response by giving it example input/output pairs.

## How examples work

Let's look at a sentiment analysis example. Suppose you want Claude to classify a tweet as positive or negative:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623645%2F05_-_005_-_Providing_Examples_00.1748623645304.png)

The challenge here is sarcasm. A tweet like "Yeah, sure, this is the best movie I've seen since Plan 9 from Outer Space" looks positive on the surface, but is actually sarcastic and negative (Plan 9 from Outer Space is notoriously one of the worst movies ever made).

## Adding examples to handle edge cases

To solve this, you can add some examples that show Claude how to handle tricky cases:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623646%2F05_-_005_-_Providing_Examples_04.1748623646206.png)

The improved prompt includes:

- A clear positive example: "Tonight's game was amazing!" → "Positive"
- A sarcastic example: "Oh yeah, I really needed my flight to be delayed tonight! Awesome!" → "Negative"
- Context explaining why sarcasm should be handled carefully

Notice how these examples are wrapped in XML tags like `<sample_input>` and `<ideal_output>`. This structure lets Claude understand at a glance what each section represents.

## When to use examples

Examples are especially useful when:

- Capturing edge cases or corner scenarios
- Defining a complex output format (like a specific JSON structure)
- Demonstrating the exact style or tone you want
- Showing how to handle ambiguous input

## One-shot vs. multi-shot

One-Shot: Provide a single example to establish the pattern

Multi-Shot: Provide multiple examples to cover different scenarios

Use multi-shot when you need to handle a variety of edge cases or want to demonstrate different types of valid responses.

## Finding good examples from your evaluations

When running prompt evaluations, look for the highest-scoring outputs to use as examples:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623647%2F05_-_005_-_Providing_Examples_12.1748623647015.png)

Find responses that score 10 (or the highest you can get), and use those input/output pairs as examples in your prompt. This helps Claude understand what a "perfect" output looks like for your specific use case.

## Adding context to your examples

Don't just provide input/output pairs—also explain why this output is good:

```
<ideal_output>
[Fill in your example output here]
</ideal_output>

This example is well-structured, provides detailed information about food choices and portions,
and meets the athlete's goals and restrictions.

```

This additional context helps Claude understand the logic behind a good response, not just the format.

## Best practices

- Always use XML tags to clearly structure your examples
- Be explicit about what you're showing: "Here is a sample input with an ideal response"
- Include examples that address your most common failure cases
- Explain why your example output is considered ideal
- Keep examples relevant to your specific task

Examples are especially powerful because they "show" rather than "tell." Instead of trying to describe exactly what you want in words, you demonstrate it directly. This makes your prompt more reliable and helps Claude understand nuanced requirements that may be hard to express through instructions alone.

#### Video transcript

I'm really excited about this prompt engineering technique we're about to discuss, because it might be one of the most effective techniques you'll find. This is the idea of providing examples in your prompt. This is often called one-shot or multi-shot prompting, depending on whether you provide one example or multiple examples. The easiest way to understand this concept is undoubtedly to look at an example, so let's get started right away.

Consider the prompt on the right. I'm asking Claude to classify the sentiment of a tweet. To be clear, when we say sentiment, we mean, does this tweet seem happy or positive, or unhappy or negative? In this particular example, I provided an input tweet: "Yeah, sure, this is the best movie I've seen since Plan 9 from Outer Space."

If you don't know, Plan 9 from Outer Space is a notoriously bad movie. So if someone posted a tweet like this, they're very likely being sarcastic, and they probably didn't like the movie they just watched at all. So we might want to classify this tweet as negative. But Claude might have some trouble with the classification.

One way to solve this is to use multi-shot prompting. So here's how we'd solve it. We'd take the starting prompt on the left, and add some examples, which I've already added in the lower half of the prompt. To add an example, we'll very directly tell Claude that we're about to give it some sample input and an ideal, perfect response.

We'll almost always wrap these inputs and outputs in some XML tags, just to better organize our prompt and make it super clear to Claude what the purpose of the input and output pairs is. So in this particular example, I gave a sample input of "Tonight's game was great," which I think is definitely positive. So right after it, I'll put a simple "positive" ideal output. This gives Claude a concrete, objective example of how to handle a certain kind of input.

So now Claude knows that if it sees an input like this in the future, well, it should label it as positive, because that's what it did in the past. We can use multi-shot prompting, which is when we provide multiple different examples when we want to handle edge cases. Handling a sarcastic tweet like this is definitely an edge case we want to highlight for Claude. When adding examples that highlight edge cases, give Claude some context, telling it to pay special attention to certain scenarios.

So, for example, we might say, "Be especially careful with tweets that contain sarcasm," and then immediately provide an example. So now, in this case, I have a sample tweet or sample input: "Oh yeah, I really needed my flight to be delayed tonight, awesome." If you don't understand the concept of sarcasm, this looks like a positive sentiment tweet. But of course, we might be able to understand this is sarcasm, so it might actually be negative.

Again, Claude can reference this example when evaluating the original input we provided above, and Claude will have a better chance of recognizing, "Oh yeah, this also looks like sarcasm, this might also be negative." Now, multi-shot prompting like this can be used not only to capture edge cases or give Claude more clarity, but also to help Claude understand more complex output formats. So if you need to generate a fairly complex JSON object, you can provide a sample input and a sample output, and show Claude that complex JSON structure. Now it'll have a better understanding of the exact output structure it's aiming for.

Providing examples is especially effective when you're doing the prompt evaluation you're currently working on. Remember, whenever you run a prompt evaluation with the little framework in our notebook, it creates an HTML file in the same directory. So we can search this file until we find a perfect 10, or hopefully just a test case that scored fairly high. If I scroll through, I'll find a 10 here.

Now, you might not have any 10s in your output. If you don't, that's completely fine. Just try to find the record with the highest score. So here's an example where we have some input and output that our model grader rated as nearly the best we can get.

So we might decide to use this as an example in our prompt. Hopefully this will guide Claude to produce outputs like this more often. Let's try it. I'll copy this input.

Back to my prompt. I'll scroll down below the guidelines section. Then I'll explicitly tell Claude that I'm going to provide an example with a sample input and ideal output. So I say, here's an example with a sample input and ideal output.

Then I'll put my sample input inside XML tags. And an ideal output. Inside these tags, I'll go back to where I was just now and copy the output here. Then I'll paste it in and fix some indentation.

Before we rerun the evaluation, I want to show you one last thing. This last step is completely optional, but I've personally had great success with it. It's often very beneficial to help Claude understand exactly why this is the ideal output. If we go back to our report, remember we have a last column on this side, which explains exactly why the grader thought this was the ideal output.

So we can copy the first half of one of the messages here, which lists why this is a good response. Bring it back, then below the closing ideal output tag, we can paste that reasoning. Then maybe modify the grammar a bit, saying, "This sample meal plan is well-structured, etc." So now Claude has a better understanding of why this is considered the ideal output.

And it'll better reinforce that Claude needs to return a well-structured output, with detailed information about food choices and portions, and most importantly, that meets the athlete's goals and restrictions. OK, now let's run that cell, then rerun our evaluation and see how we did. So I'll rerun this, are we going to go up or down? We ended up going up just a little bit, to 7.96.

OK, let's wrap up. As a reminder, this technique is usually called one-shot or multi-shot prompting. One-shot is when you provide one example, multi-shot is when you provide multiple examples. This is a technique you'll use very often, whenever you want to make sure Claude handles edge cases, or especially when you want Claude to make sure it matches a certain complex output format.

### 32. Introduction to tool use

> Type: Video | Duration: 2:54 | Video: 06 - 001 - Introducing Tool Use.mp4

Tools allow Claude to access information from the external world, extending its capabilities beyond its training data. By default, Claude only knows information from its training data and cannot access current events, real-time data, or external systems. Tool use solves this limitation by creating a structured way for Claude to request and receive up-to-date information.

## The problem without tools

When a user asks Claude for current information, it hits a wall. For example, if someone asks "What's the weather in San Francisco, California?" Claude has to respond by saying "I'm sorry, but I don't have access to up-to-date weather information."

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623642%2F06_-_001_-_Introducing_Tool_Use_05.1748623642311.png)

This creates a frustrating user experience when people need real-time data, and Claude could theoretically help if only it had access to current information.

## How tool use works

Tool use follows a specific back-and-forth pattern between your application and Claude. Here's the complete flow:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623643%2F06_-_001_-_Introducing_Tool_Use_07.1748623643055.png)

1.  **Initial request:** You send Claude a question, along with instructions on how to get additional data from an external source
2.  **Tool request:** Claude analyzes the question, decides it needs additional information, then requests specific details about what data it needs
3.  **Data retrieval:** Your server runs code to fetch the requested information from an external API or database
4.  **Final response:** You send the retrieved data back to Claude, which then combines the original question with the up-to-date data to generate a complete response

## The weather example in practice

Let's see how this works with a weather question. The process becomes more concrete:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623644%2F06_-_001_-_Introducing_Tool_Use_14.1748623643863.png)

When a user asks about the current weather, you include instructions in the prompt on how to retrieve weather data. Claude recognizes that it needs current information and requests weather data for a specific location. Your server then calls a weather API to get real-time conditions and sends that data back to Claude. Finally, Claude combines the up-to-date weather data with the user's question to provide an accurate, current response.

## Key benefits

- **Real-time information:** Access current data that wasn't available during Claude's training
- **External system integration:** Connect Claude to databases, APIs, and other services
- **Dynamic responses:** Provide answers based on the latest available information
- **Structured interaction:** Claude knows exactly what information it needs and how to request it

Tool use transforms Claude from a static knowledge base into a dynamic assistant capable of working with real-time data. This opens up possibilities for building applications that need current information, whether that's weather data, stock prices, database queries, or any other real-time information your users might need.

#### Video transcript

In this module, we're going to discuss tool use. Tools allow Claude to access information from the external world. Now, understanding tool use can be a bit challenging, so in this video, I'll give you a very gentle introduction. We'll walk through the entire flow of tool use step by step, and understand what it's all about.

I want to start by giving you an example of a limitation Claude unfortunately sometimes hits. Remember, by default, Claude can only access the information it was actually trained on. So in general, it doesn't have much information about recent current events. For example, if we have a user using our chatbot, asking a question like, "What's the weather in San Francisco, California right now?"

If we send this question to Claude, we might get a response like this: "I'm sorry, but I don't have access to up-to-date weather information like that." To solve this problem and give the user a better response, we can use tools. Let me show you a diagram that breaks down tools in very simple terms. I'll also show you another diagram that gives you an example of how we'd solve this specific weather problem.

OK, so this is the entire flow we'll ultimately implement when we start using tools. We'll send Claude an initial request. We'll ask a question or give Claude a task. At the same time, we'll include instructions on how Claude can get some additional data from the external world.

Claude will look at the question asked or the task given, then might decide it needs to request some additional data. So it'll send us a response requesting some additional data, and it'll give us some details about exactly what information it needs. Then, on our server, we'll run a little bit of code to fetch the information Claude requested, then use it to respond in a follow-up request to Claude. Now, theoretically, Claude has all the information it needs to respond to us.

So it'll generate a final response, hopefully enhanced or improved by that additional data. Now I'll show you the exact same flow, but I'll customize each little step for the situation where the user asks about the current weather in a specific location. So here's the situation. We'll send Claude an initial query, which will include a prompt where the user asks about the weather.

In that initial request, we'll include details on how to specifically retrieve current weather data. Claude will look at the prompt and decide, "Hey, to answer this question, I need to fetch some current weather data." It'll send us a response, then we'll run some code, possibly contacting some third-party weather API, and actually fetch the real-time details of the current weather conditions in a specific location. Once we get those details from that external API, we'll make a follow-up request to Claude with that current weather data.

Now, Claude has all the information it needs. It has the original prompt and the up-to-date weather data, so it can generate a final response and send it to us.

### 33. Project overview

> Type: Video | Duration: 2:23 | Video: 06 - 002 - Project Overview.mp4

We're going to build a hands-on project that teaches Claude how to set reminders for future dates. This might sound simple, but it reveals several interesting challenges that we'll solve using custom tools.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623630%2F06_-_002_-_Project_Overview_00.1748623629884.png)

The goal is straightforward: we want to be able to tell Claude "Set a reminder for my doctor's appointment. It's a week from next Thursday," and have Claude respond with "OK, I'll remind you." But to make this happen, we need to solve some limitations in how Claude handles time and reminders.

## Why this is challenging

While Claude knows the current date, we need to solve three specific problems:

- **Limited time awareness:** Claude may know the current date, but doesn't know the exact time
- **Date calculation issues:** Claude isn't always great at time-based addition, especially when looking many days into the future
- **No reminder functionality:** Claude doesn't know how to set reminders—it has no built-in reminder mechanism

Each of these limitations represents a gap between Claude's natural abilities and the functionality our reminder system requires. Tools are how we bridge these gaps.

## The tools we need

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623630%2F06_-_002_-_Project_Overview_17.1748623630589.png)

We'll create three separate tools to address each challenge:

- **Get current datetime:** Claude needs to know the current date and time precisely
- **Add a duration to a datetime:** Claude isn't perfect at datetime addition, so we'll give it a reliable tool to do this
- **Set a reminder:** We need a way to actually set a reminder in the system

We'll implement these tools one at a time, starting with the simplest. This approach lets us understand how tool calling works before we build more complex functionality. By the end, Claude will be able to handle natural language requests like "remind me in a week" by combining these tools to calculate the exact time and set the reminder.

This project demonstrates a key principle of working with AI: when a model has limitations, we extend its capabilities through tools, rather than trying to work around those limitations in the prompt.

#### Video transcript

To learn more about tools, we're going to set a little goal for ourselves. This will be a little project we implement in a Jupyter notebook. We're going to try to teach Claude how to set reminders that happen at some point in the future. This will require us to implement several different tools.

Now, we'll just focus on one tool for now, but know that we'll eventually need to handle multiple tools. So I want to eventually send Claude a message like this: "Set a reminder for my doctor's appointment, it's a week from next Thursday." I want Claude to respond, "OK, I'll remind you at that point." When you first see this task, you might think it looks simple, but it turns out there are actually several different challenges we need to solve, all of which we'll solve by using tools.

Specifically, Claude does know the current date. In other words, if you open a prompt right now, you can ask it what the current date is, and it'll give you a completely correct answer. However, Claude doesn't always know the exact time. So if we ask Claude to do something like "set a reminder for 24 hours from now," and expect it to be exactly 24 hours, Claude actually doesn't know what time it'll be 24 hours from now, because it doesn't know the current time.

Second, Claude isn't always perfect at time-based addition. So if I ask it, "What day is it 379 days from January 13, 1973?" Claude will often give you the correct answer, but sometimes it'll calculate it wrong. Finally, Claude doesn't even know what it means to set a reminder, it has no concept of it.

It conceptually knows what setting a reminder is, but there's absolutely no mechanism inside Claude to set a reminder in the future. To solve these problems, we'll make a dedicated tool. So we have three problems here. We'll make three separate tools, one at a time.

Each tool will do the following. We'll start with a very simple tool. It will help us understand what tool calling actually is. Its only job is to get the current datetime.

That is, the current date plus the time. The second tool we'll make will add a duration to a datetime. This will allow us to say something like "take the current date and add 20 days, what day will that be?" Then finally, we'll also make a reminder-setting tool.

---

### 34. Tool functions

> Type: Video | Duration: 5:22 | Video: 06 - 003 - Tool Functions.mp4

When building AI applications with Claude, you often need to give it access to real-time information or the ability to perform actions. This is where tool functions come into play—they're Python functions that Claude can call when it needs additional data to help the user.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623640%2F06_-_003_-_Tool_Functions_00.1748623639986.png)

The diagram above shows the three basic tools we'll implement: get the current date/time, add a duration to a date, and set a reminder. Let's start with the first one.

## What is a tool function?

A tool function is a regular Python function that's automatically executed when Claude decides it needs additional information to help the user. For example, if someone asks "What time is it now?", Claude will call your date/time tool to get the current time.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623641%2F06_-_003_-_Tool_Functions_06.1748623640850.png)

Here's an example of a weather tool function. Note how it validates the input and provides clear error messages—these are important best practices.

## Best practices for tool functions

When writing tool functions, follow these guidelines:

- **Use descriptive names:** Both your function name and parameter names should clearly indicate their purpose
- **Validate inputs:** Check whether required parameters are empty or invalid, and raise an error when something goes wrong
- **Provide meaningful error messages:** Claude can see the error message and may retry the function call with corrected parameters

Validation is especially important because Claude learns from errors. If you raise a clear error like "location cannot be empty," Claude may try calling the function again with an appropriate location value.

## Building your first tool function

Let's create a function to get the current date and time. This function will take a date format parameter, so Claude can request the time in different formats:

```
def get_current_datetime(date_format="%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"):
    if not date_format:
        raise ValueError("date_format cannot be empty")
    return datetime.now().strftime(date_format)
```

This function uses Python's `datetime` module to get the current time and format it according to the provided format string. The default format gives us year-month-day hour:minute:second.

You can test it with different formats:

```
# Default format: "2024-01-15 14:30:25"
get_current_datetime()


# Just hour and minute: "14:30"
get_current_datetime("%H:%M")
```

The validation check ensures that Claude cannot pass an empty string for the date format. While this particular error is unlikely to occur, it demonstrates the pattern of validating inputs and providing helpful error messages that Claude can learn from.

## Next Steps

Creating the function is just the first step. Next, you need to write a JSON schema to describe the function to Claude, and then integrate it into your chat system. This approach of tool functions gives Claude powerful capabilities while keeping your code organized and maintainable.

#### Video Transcript

Let's get started on our first tool, which will allow Claude to retrieve the current datetime. Before we continue, I want to let you know that I've created a new notebook for you. This notebook is titled `001 Tools` and is attached to this lecture. In this notebook, you'll find a lot of the same code we've already written throughout this course.

However, I've also added a new cell below, titled "Tools and Schemas." In this cell, I've placed a lot of boilerplate code, just to save us a little time later. In particular, you'll find the `add duration to date time` function, which you'll use later. So, again, I encourage you to download this notebook attached to this lecture and use it as a starting point.

All right. So, again, we're going to focus on implementing the first tool, which is getting the current datetime. I'll walk you through the entire process step by step. We'll write a lot of code in our notebook, and we won't use any of the helper functions we set up there.

Such as `add user message` and `add assistant message` and so on. The reason for this is that you'll see we need to refactor those functions a bit to accommodate tools. So, rather than trying to mix updating those helper functions and learning about tools at the same time, which would be very confusing. So, instead, we'll just focus on making tool calls without using any helper functions as much as possible.

OK, so let's get started. I've broken the whole process down into several distinct steps. The first step, when we add a tool to our implementation, is to write a tool function. A tool function is an ordinary Python function that will automatically execute at some point when Claude decides it needs to retrieve some additional information in order to help the user in some way.

I have an example of a possible tool function on the right, called `get weather`. Claude might be able to use this tool function to retrieve the current weather for some particular location in the world. Now, there are a few best practices for tool functions. First, we always want to use well-named and descriptive parameters.

So the actual function itself and the parameters it receives should be reasonably well named, at least giving us a hint of what they are. Second, we want to validate these inputs and raise an error if there's anything wrong with the inputs themselves. So, for example, if we didn't receive a location, or the location is an empty string, we'd want to immediately raise an error. Finally, whenever we do raise an error, we want to make sure it includes some kind of meaningful error message.

In some cases, if Claude tries to call a tool function and it results in an error, Claude will see the exact error message. Claude might decide to try calling your tool function again, and call it in a slightly different way, trying to correct that error. So, for example, you can imagine that if Claude tries to call this `get weather` function, and it passes in an empty string, the validation check at the very top would fail, and we'd raise an error. Claude would see the error message "location cannot be empty."

Claude might decide to try calling this tool function again, making sure that what it passes in is no longer an empty string. OK, let's go back to our notebook, and we'll put together our first tool function. Remember, the goal of the tool we're putting together is to get the current datetime. So back here, I'll add a new cell at the very bottom, and I'll define a new function, named `get current date time`.

This will take a parameter that I'll call `date format`. I'll give it a default value. This will be a somewhat complicated string. I'll type percent uppercase Y, percent lowercase m, percent lowercase d, then a space, a percent H colon, percent M colon, percent S.

Now this string is a bit complicated, so I encourage you to pause the video here and carefully check the string you've typed to make sure it matches mine exactly. Then inside this function, I'll use that date format to get the current datetime and format it in a way that matches this date format string. So I'll return `datetime.now.strftime`, and I'll pass in that `date format`. So now, as a quick example of how we'd actually use this function, we can call `get current date time`.

If we just run it like this, we get a datetime in year, month, day, hour, minute, second format. Or, I can type in a custom date format string, like percent H, colon, percent, uppercase M. This will just print out the hour and minute of my current time. A good improvement to this function is to add some validation to the `date format` parameter.

Unfortunately, we can't easily validate the exact structure. To make sure this thing is a valid string formatting time formatter. But we can at least check and make sure that we're not passing in an empty string. So I might decide to add a bit of validation here, with an `if not date format`.

Then if we don't pass that validation check, I might raise a `value error` and say something like "date format cannot be empty." So now if I try to call `get current date time` with an empty string, I'll end up getting an error message that tells me "date format cannot be empty." Now honestly, it's unlikely that Claude would make the mistake of passing in an empty string like this, but at least just in case it does, we're providing Claude with some signal, we're telling it how to fix the error. It can try calling `get current date time` again and make sure the date format it passes in is not empty.

### 35. Tool Schemas

> Type: Video | Duration: 4:39 | Video: 06 - 004 - Tool Schemas.mp4

After writing the tool function, the next step is to create a JSON schema that tells Claude which parameters your function needs and how to use it. This schema is like a piece of documentation that Claude reads to understand when and how to call your tool.

## Understanding JSON Schema

JSON Schema isn't specific to AI or tool calling—it's a widely used data validation specification that has existed for years. The AI community adopted it because it's a convenient way to describe function parameters and validate data.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623699%2F06_-_004_-_Tool_Schemas_01.1748623699583.png)

A complete tool specification has three main parts:

- **name** - A clear, descriptive tool name (like "get_weather")
- **description** - What the tool does, when to use it, and what it returns
- **input_schema** - The actual JSON schema describing the function's parameters

## Writing Effective Descriptions

Your tool description is crucial for helping Claude understand when to use your function. Best practices include:

- Aim to explain what the tool does in 3-4 sentences
- Describe when Claude should use it
- Explain what kind of data it returns
- Provide a detailed description for each parameter

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623701%2F06_-_004_-_Tool_Schemas_02.1748623700746.png)

## An Easy Way to Generate Schemas

Rather than writing JSON schemas from scratch, you can use Claude itself to generate them. The process is as follows:

1.  Copy your tool function code
2.  Go to Claude and ask it to write a JSON schema for tool calling
3.  Include Anthropic's documentation on tool use as context
4.  Have Claude generate a properly formatted schema following best practices

The prompt should be something like: "Write a valid JSON schema specification for tool calling for this function. Follow the best practices outlined in the attached documentation."

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623702%2F06_-_004_-_Tool_Schemas_13.1748623701923.png)

## Implementing the Schema in Code

Once Claude generates your schema, copy it into your code file. Here's a good naming pattern to follow:

```
def get_current_datetime(date_format="%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"):
    if not date_format:
        raise ValueError("date_format cannot be empty")
    return datetime.now().strftime(date_format)

get_current_datetime_schema = {
    "name": "get_current_datetime",
    "description": "Returns the current date and time formatted according to the specified format",
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "date_format": {
                "type": "string",
                "description": "A string specifying the format of the returned datetime. Uses Python's strftime format codes.",
                "default": "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"
            }
        },
        "required": []
    }
}
```

Using the pattern of `function_name` followed by `function_name_schema` keeps your schemas organized and easy to match with their corresponding functions.

## Adding Type Safety

For better type checking, import and use the `ToolParam` type from the Anthropic library:

```
from anthropic.types import ToolParam

get_current_datetime_schema = ToolParam({
    "name": "get_current_datetime",
    "description": "Returns the current date and time formatted according to the specified format",
    # ... rest of schema
})
```

While this isn't strictly necessary for functionality, it can prevent type errors when you use the schema with Claude's API, and makes your code more robust.

#### Video Transcript

Now that we've put together our tool function, we're going to move on to the second step, which is writing a JSON schema. We'll eventually send all of this configuration to Claude. Claude will use it to understand the different available tool functions and the different parameters that must be provided to those tool functions. The first thing I want you to understand is what exactly a JSON schema is.

So the entire object you see on the right is not, strictly speaking, itself a JSON schema. Instead, at the very top, there's a name and a description. I'll tell you later what these do. Below that, there's a key for `input schema`.

And then what's assigned to it is that dictionary. The part I'm highlighting now is technically the JSON schema. So, let me give you some more background. The concept of a JSON schema isn't specific to language models or tool calling or anything like that.

JSON schema is a data validation specification. So it's a set of rules that can be used to validate any kind of JSON data. So again, it's not specific to language models or tools or anything like that. The language model community decided at some point that JSON schema is just a very convenient way to connect and handle tool calling.

This is a widely understood technology that has been around for many years. Now, so far, I haven't really explained what the JSON schema specification is about. So, in general, this thing is used to inform Claude about which tools it has available. We'll provide a name for the tool, so in this case it might be GetWeather, and then a description of the tool.

This description is meant to tell Claude what this tool does, when to use it, and what kind of data it will return. The best practice is to make sure you have a description that's about three to four sentences long. So even though I'm only showing "retrieve the current weather" here, in reality, I'd definitely want a much longer description than this. Then, below this `input schema` key would be the actual JSON schema specification.

This will describe the different parameters that should be passed to our function. So back to this example, if I had a git weather function that takes a location, I'd type in `input schema` and location here, which needs to be a string, and here a description of what that parameter is for. Again, we want this parameter's description to also be about three to four sentences long, and help Claude understand exactly what this parameter controls and how it affects the entire function call. Now, like I mentioned, you might find it a bit intimidating to have to write out all of this configuration yourself.

Fortunately, I have a trick that can help you write a nearly perfect JSON schema specification for every tool you put together. Let me show you this trick. First, I'll go back to my editor and find our tool function. For us, it's the `get current date time` function.

Then I'll bring it over to a Claude window. I'm here at Claude.ai. I'll write a very simple prompt. I'll ask Claude to write a valid JSON schema specification for tool calling for this function.

Then I'll also ask Claude to follow the best practices outlined in the attached documentation. Then I'll type in my tool function, like this. Then, here's the real trick. I'll go to the Anthropic API documentation.

In the user guide section, there's an entire page about Claude tool use. The whole page has a lot of different best practices, as well as examples of good tool descriptions and bad tool descriptions. So I'll copy all the text here, go back to my Claude window, paste it in as an attachment, and run it. Claude will probably respond with a very strong JSON schema specification.

So I'm going to copy this now, bring it back to my editor, and I'll paste it just below my existing `get current date time` function. So I'll say, `get current date time schema`, like this. Now, as a little naming pattern I like to use, I'll name my tool function whatever I want, and then the matching schema will be `_schema`. So the same name plus `_schema`.

This makes it easier to keep track of my different schemas. I have one last thing to do. At the top of the cell, I'll add an import from `anthropic.types`. I'll import `tool program`.

I'll wrap the entire dictionary with this `tool param`. So I'll type `tool param` here, an open parenthesis, and then a close parenthesis here at the bottom. Adding this `tool param` isn't strictly necessary. In other words, our code will still work without it, but it will prevent type errors later when we end up using this schema and making use of it.

### 36. Handling Message Blocks

> Type: Video | Duration: 5:44 | Video: 06 - 005 - Handling Message Blocks.mp4

When using Claude's tool capabilities, you'll encounter a new response structure that differs from the simple text responses you've seen before. Claude can now return multi-block messages that contain both text and tool use information, not just a single text block.

## Making a Tool-Enabled API Call

To enable Claude to use tools, you need to include a `tools` parameter in the API call. Here's how to build the request:

```
messages = []
messages.append({
    "role": "user",
    "content": "What is the exact time, formatted as HH:MM:SS?"
})

response = client.messages.create(
    model=model,
    max_tokens=1000,
    messages=messages,
    tools=[get_current_datetime_schema],
)
```

The `tools` parameter accepts a list of JSON schemas describing the available functions that Claude can call.

## Understanding Multi-Block Messages

When Claude decides to use a tool, it returns an assistant message containing multiple blocks in the `content` list. This is quite different from the plain text responses you've handled before.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623695%2F06_-_005_-_Handling_Message_Blocks_07.1748623695372.png)

A multi-block message typically contains:

- **Text Block** - Human-readable text explaining what Claude is doing (for example "I can help you find the current time. Let me look up that information for you")
- **ToolUse Block** - Instructions to your code about which tool to call and what parameters to use

The ToolUse block includes:

- An ID for tracking the tool call
- The name of the function to call (for example "get_current_datetime")
- The input parameters formatted as a dictionary
- A type specifying "tool_use"

## Managing Conversation History with Multi-Block Messages

Remember, Claude doesn't store conversation history—you need to manage it manually. When handling tool responses, you must preserve the entire content structure, including all blocks.

Here's how to correctly append a multi-block assistant message to your conversation history:

```
messages.append({
    "role": "assistant",
    "content": response.content
})
```

This preserves both the text block and the tool use block, which is essential for maintaining conversation context when you make subsequent API calls.

## The Complete Tool Use Flow

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623696%2F06_-_005_-_Handling_Message_Blocks_15.1748623696291.png)

The tool use process follows this pattern:

1.  Send a user message with the tool schema to Claude
2.  Receive an assistant message with a text block and a tool use block
3.  Extract the tool information and execute the actual function
4.  Send the tool result back to Claude along with the complete conversation history
5.  Receive the final response from Claude

Each step requires careful handling of the message structure to ensure Claude has the complete context it needs to provide an accurate response.

## Updating Helper Functions

If you've been using helper functions like `add_user_message()` and `add_assistant_message()`, you'll need to update them to handle multi-block content. The current versions probably only support a single text block, but now they need to accommodate the more complex content structure that includes tool use blocks.

This multi-block message handling is essential for building robust applications that can seamlessly integrate Claude's tool capabilities while maintaining a normal conversation flow.

#### Video Transcript

In the third step, we're going to call Claude using its JSON schema and some user messages. So, from our server, we'll make a request to Claude as before, but now we'll also include this tool schema. This helps Claude understand that it has a tool available. Let's go back to our notebook, and we'll try to make this request manually, without using any of the helper functions we built before, like the `chat` function.

OK, back here, I'll create a new cell below. I'll create an empty messages list. I'll manually append a new message to it, with a `role` of `user` and `content` of "what is the exact time formatted as our minute seconds." Then, below it, I'll call the `client.messages.create` function.

So I get a response from `client.messages.create`, and I'll specify my model, my `max_tokens`, my messages list, and now we also want to include this JSON schema, which tells Claude that it has a tool available. To do this, we'll include a `tools` keyword argument. This will be a list containing all the different JSON schema specifications we've created. So far, you and I have only created one, which is called `get_current_datetime_schema`.

So we'll put it here. Then I'll print out `response` at the very bottom, and let's run it and see what happens. We'll get a response message here, with a structure we've never seen before. All the messages we've received before had a `content` field with a list, and in the list there was a text block.

As I've mentioned several times before, inside the text block is the text we actually want to display to the user. But now that we're using tools, this `content` list will be a bit different. So you may notice that in the `content` list, there's a second block called a `tool use block`. So this is its entire structure.

Let me show you a diagram to make sure this is very clear. OK, this is our first encounter with a `multi-block message`. Remember, a message is either an assistant message or a user message. We typically store some text in a message, which is what we've been seeing before.

But besides plain text, there are other types of data that can be stored in a message. When Claude decides to use a tool, it typically returns an assistant message to us that contains both a `text block` and a `tool use block`. The text block is meant to display some text to the user to help them understand what's happening. So in this case, the text block might contain something like "I can help you look up the current time. Let me find that information for you." Then, in addition to this text block, there's the tool use block. This tool use block is a signal to you and me as developers that Claude wants to use a tool. The tool use block will list the name of the tool function it wants to call.

So in this case, Claude wants to call the `get_current_datetime` function we built. Then it also provides some input, or the parameters we need to pass to that function. So part of the whole process we're going to do next is to find the appropriate tool and actually run it. But before that, there's a very critical thing we need to handle, which is the idea that the `content` list contains multiple blocks.

OK, here's a quick reminder for you. At this point, we've made a request from our server to Claude, in which we had a single user message containing the tool schema. We've now received a response. In this response, there's the assistant message, and its `content` list has two separate blocks, a text block and a tool use block.

Now, I want to remind you of something about Claude. Remember, Claude doesn't store any message history or any information about the conversation you're having. If you want to maintain a conversation or history with Claude, you have to manage it manually. This means that when we eventually take this tool use block and end up calling some actual function, we'll eventually need to respond back to Claude.

When we do that, here comes the key part, we need to make sure we include the entire conversation history, just like we've been doing throughout this course. So we already know how to do this. The only difference is that this time we need to make sure we handle messages that may contain multiple blocks. So let me show you how we can do this manually.

Then, later in this section, we'll come back to our helper functions, specifically `add_user_message` and `add_assistant_message`, and make sure they can support messages containing multiple blocks. Because right now they only support a single text block. OK, to manage these messages, I'll go back to the code cell at the bottom where we're getting the response. I'll take our response and make sure to append a new assistant message to our messages list.

So I'll delete the `response` there. We'll say `messages.append`. I'll put in a new `role` of `assistant`. Then our `content` will be the exact list of `content` blocks we just got from the response.

So all we have to do is add `response.content`. That's it. So now if I print out `messages` and run that cell again, we should see that we end up with our user message. That's our original message.

We now have our assistant message. It has a text block and a tool use block in it. So now we're properly building our conversation history by including all the different blocks from all the different messages we've collected. So again, I just want to remind you that we'll eventually have to come back to the `add_user_message` and `add_assistant_message` helper functions and update them to handle multiple blocks like this.

---

### 37. Sending Tool Results

> Type: Video | Duration: 9:22 | Video: 06 - 006 - Sending Tool Results.mp4

After Claude requests a tool call, you need to execute the function and send the result back. This completes the tool use workflow by providing Claude with the information it requested.

## Running the Tool Function

When Claude responds with a `tool use block`, you extract the input parameters and call your function. Here's how to access the tool parameters:

```
response.content[1].input
```

This gives you a dictionary of the parameters Claude wants to pass to your function. Since your function expects keyword arguments rather than a dictionary, you can use Python's unpacking syntax:

```
get_current_datetime(**response.content[1].input)
```

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623700%2F06_-_006_-_Sending_Tool_Results_03.1748623700363.png)

## Tool Result Blocks

After running the tool function, you need to send the result back to Claude using a `tool result block`. This block lives inside a user message and tells Claude what happened when you executed the tool.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623701%2F06_-_006_-_Sending_Tool_Results_05.1748623701131.png)

A tool result block has several important properties:

- **tool_use_id** - Must match the id of the `ToolUse` block this `ToolResult` corresponds to
- **content** - The output of running your tool, serialized as a string
- **is_error** - True if an error occurred

## Handling Multiple Tool Calls

Claude can request multiple tool calls in a single response. For example, if the user asks "What is 10 + 10, and what is 30 + 30?", Claude might respond with two separate `ToolUse` blocks.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623702%2F06_-_006_-_Sending_Tool_Results_07.1748623702036.png)

Each tool call has a unique ID, and you must match these IDs when sending the results back. This ensures Claude knows which result corresponds to which request, even if the results arrive in a different order.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623703%2F06_-_006_-_Sending_Tool_Results_08.1748623703510.png)

## Building the Follow-Up Request

The follow-up request you make to Claude must include the complete conversation history along with the new tool result. The structure is as follows:

```
messages.append({
    "role": "user",
    "content": [{
        "type": "tool_result",
        "tool_use_id": response.content[1].id,
        "content": "15:04:22",
        "is_error": False
    }]
})
```

The complete message history now contains:

- The original user message
- The assistant message with the tool use block
- The user message with the tool result block

## Making the Final Request

When sending the follow-up request, you must still include the tool schema, even if you don't expect Claude to call a tool again. Claude needs this schema to understand the tool references in your conversation history.

```
client.messages.create(
    model=model,
    max_tokens=1000,
    messages=messages,
    tools=[get_current_datetime_schema]
)
```

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623704%2F06_-_006_-_Sending_Tool_Results_04.1748623704156.png)

Claude will then respond with a final message that integrates the tool result into a natural response for the user. At this point, the tool use workflow is complete—you have successfully enabled Claude to access real-time information through your custom function.

Moving on to the fourth step, we're going to run the tool function that Claude asked us to run. Remember, in the previous step, we got a response from Claude containing a `tool use block`. Let's quickly print it out in our notebook. Back here, remember we have this `response` variable, I'll create a new cell below and print out `response`.

Now, inside it, we get a message with a `content` attribute, and the second block in that list is the tool use block. So to access it, I'll use `response.content[1]`. Then, inside this, we get an `input` field. This is the input or parameters Claude is requesting we pass to the `get_current_datetime` function.

So to get that dictionary, we can chain `.input` after it. Now, when we add `.input`, you may get a type error here. In this video, we'll ignore the type error, and we'll come back to fix all the type errors shortly. But just so you know, if you see any type errors in this video, it's completely fine.

Ignore them for now. So now our goal is to take this dictionary and provide it to our `get_current_datetime` function. There's one thing to note here. Remember our `get_current_datetime` function.

When we defined it above, it doesn't accept a dictionary. It accepts a keyword argument called `date_format`. So to convert that dictionary into a list of keyword arguments and apply it to the function, we can do this. I'll call `get_current_datetime`, using `**` and `response.content[1].input`.

Now if I run it, I get my real-world current datetime. For me, it's 241 or 1441. Aside from the type errors we need to fix, this was actually pretty easy. So now let's move on to the fifth step.

In the fifth step, we're now going to send a follow-up request to Claude. This request will include our complete conversation history. So we'll have the user message from the beginning. It'll have the assistant message with the tool use block that we just handled.

Now, we'll append another user message at the end. This user message will contain a new type of block we haven't seen before, called a `tool result block`. Let me tell you how this block works. The tool result block will be placed inside a user message.

The tool result will contain the result of running a tool. So we're basically feeding whatever we just got from the tool function directly back to Claude. There will be several different keys in the tool result block. It's important for you to understand what these keys do.

The first one is probably the hardest to understand, `tool_use_id`. Let me quickly tell you what `tool_use_id` is about. I want you to imagine that we created a new tool called `calculator`. This tool is used to evaluate a math expression.

Then, maybe a user sends a message like "What is 10 plus 10? Also, what is 30 plus 30?" In this case, Claude might want to call the `calculator` tool twice. One call to solve 10 plus 10, and a second to solve 30 plus 30.

To do this, Claude will respond with an assistant message containing multiple tool use blocks. So we might have a first tool use block, where the expression we need to compute is 10 plus 10. Then in the second block, we might have an expression of 30 plus 30. Then we'd execute our calculator tool twice, once for this expression, and once for that expression.

Then we'd send a follow-up request to Claude. In our follow-up request, we'd add a user message containing two separate tool result blocks. So we'd get result one and result two. Now, when we send these results back to Claude, Claude needs to be able to figure out which result belongs to which request.

So we have request one and request two, and then result one and result two. Claude doesn't want to rely solely on whether these things are in the same order. Instead, it will use IDs. So in the original tool use block above, we have an ID of AB3.

Then in the second one, we have an ID of PO9. In our follow-up request, which we send back to Claude. We need to make sure the ID we put here matches the output. In other words, PO9.

Is associated with 30 plus 30, so we want to make sure we link P09 with the 60 below. Similarly, AB3 with 10 plus 10, we want to make sure it's linked with the AB3 whose output is 20. So that's what `tool_use_id` does. It helps us connect the tool use request with the tool result output.

The other property you need to know is `content`, which asks for whatever output you got from the tool function. Even if your tool function returns something like a number, a dictionary, or a list, you'll convert it to a string, and usually just converting it to plain JSON works. Then, finally, we can also optionally include an `is-air` field. If anything went wrong while running your tool function, you'd set this to true.

By default, it's always false. Now that we have a better understanding of this tool result block, I want to quickly remind you of what we need to do next. We just got an assistant message from Claude asking us to run a tool. We know it asked us to run a tool because it has a tool use block in it.

Then we executed our tool with the provided parameters. So now that we have the result of the tool function, we need to send a follow-up request to Claude. The goal of the tool schema is to help Claude understand the different tools available and how to actually call those different tools. ### 38. Multi-Turn Conversations with Tools

> Type: Video | Duration: 9:11 | Video: 06 - 007 - Multi-Turn Conversations with Tools.mp4

When building applications with multiple tools, you'll need to handle scenarios where Claude may need to call multiple tools in sequence to answer a single user question. For example, if the user asks "What day is 103 days from today?", Claude needs to first get the current date, then add 103 days to it.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623702%2F06_-_007_-_Multi-Turn_Conversations_with_Tools_02.1748623702753.png)

This creates a `multi-turn conversation pattern`, where Claude makes multiple tool requests before providing the final answer. Your application needs to be able to handle this automatically.

## The Multi-Turn Tool Pattern

When Claude needs multiple tools, here's what happens behind the scenes:

1. The user asks: "What day is 103 days from today?"
2. Claude responds with a `tool use block` requesting a call to `get_current_datetime`
3. Your server calls the function and returns the result
4. Claude realizes it needs more information and requests `add_duration_to_datetime`
5. Your server calls the function and returns the result
6. Claude now has enough information to provide the final answer

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623703%2F06_-_007_-_Multi-Turn_Conversations_with_Tools_03.1748623703654.png)

## Building the Conversation Loop

To handle this pattern, you need a conversation loop that keeps going until Claude stops requesting tools:

```
def run_conversation(messages):
    while True:
        response = chat(messages)
        
        add_user_message(messages, response)
        
        # Pseudo code
        if response isn't asking for a tool:
            break
            
        tool_result_blocks = run_tools(response)
        add_user_message(tool_result_blocks)
        
    return messages
```

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623704%2F06_-_007_-_Multi-Turn_Conversations_with_Tools_05.1748623704383.png)

## Refactoring the Helper Functions

Before implementing the conversation loop, you need to update your helper functions to properly handle multiple message blocks.

### Updating the Message Handlers

Your `add_user_message` and `add_assistant_message` functions currently assume you're always dealing with plain text. Update them to handle complete message objects:

```
from anthropic.types import Message

def add_user_message(messages, message):
    user_message = {
        "role": "user",
        "content": message.content if isinstance(message, Message) else message
    }
    messages.append(user_message)
```

This allows you to pass in a string, a list of blocks, or a complete message object.

### Updating the Chat Function

Modify your `chat` function to accept a list of tools, and to return the complete message rather than just the text:

```
def chat(messages, system=None, temperature=1.0, stop_sequences=[], tools=None):
    params = {
        "model": model,
        "max_tokens": 1000,
        "messages": messages,
        "temperature": temperature,
        "stop_sequences": stop_sequences,
    }
    
    if tools:
        params["tools"] = tools
        
    if system:
        params["system"] = system
        
    message = client.messages.create(**params)
    return message
```

### Extracting Text from a Message

Since you're now returning a complete message object, create a helper function to extract the text when you need it:

```
def text_from_message(message):
    return "\n".join(
        [block.text for block in message.content if block.type == "text"]
    )
```

This function finds all the text blocks in a message and joins them together, which is very useful when you need to display the final response to the user.

## Key Improvements

These refactoring steps prepare your code for robust tool handling:

- **Flexible Message Handling** - Your helper functions can now handle different message formats
- **Tool Support in Chat** - The `chat` function can receive and pass tool schemas
- **Complete Message Return** - You get the complete message object rather than just the text, preserving all blocks
- **Text Extraction Utility** - A way to easily get readable text from complex messages

With these foundations in place, you're ready to implement a conversation loop that automatically handles multiple tool calls, creating a seamless experience where Claude can use any number of tools as needed to answer the user's questions.

#### Video Transcript

So we want to have a total of three different tools. I want to think about what happens to our code when we connect three different tools to Claude.

So let's look at a quick example. Suppose we submit a user message to Claude asking what day it is 103 days from today. Now to answer this question, Claude needs to use two separate tools. First, it needs to use `Get Current Date Time` to figure out what the current date is, and then it needs to use `Add duration to date time` to add 103 days to that date.

So here's what happens behind the scenes. At the beginning, Claude will immediately respond to us with a `tool use block`, asking us to call `Get Current Date Time`. We'll call that function, then respond to Claude, telling it what the current date is. After that, Claude will realize it doesn't have enough information to answer the user's original question.

It now needs to take the current date and add 103 days. So Claude will respond to us with another separate tool use block, asking us to call `add_duration_to_datetime`. We'll call it, then pass the response back to Claude. Now Claude has enough information to respond to our actual query.

Now the reason I'm specifically showing you this example is because. If we get the input for our original user message from a real user, like a real person, we can't always predict what kind of question they'll ask Claude. Otherwise, if Claude does want to use a tool, we can run the tool, get the resulting tool result blocks, add them to a user message, then run Claude again, still inside the `while` loop. In the first step, we'll upgrade our `add_user_message` and `add_assistant_message` helper functions so they can better handle multiple message blocks. Remember, whenever we start using tools, we'll get responses from Claude that may contain multiple different blocks.

Currently, our `add_user_message` and `add_assistant_message` helper functions are set up entirely based on the assumption that we're always dealing with a plain text block and nothing else. So let's handle this right away in our notebook. Back here, I'm still in the same notebook we've been using. I just deleted some cells at the bottom that contained some example tool calls, so you can better see what I'm doing on the screen.

So we'll do this. At the top of the cell, I'll add an import from `Anthropic.types`. I'll import `Message`. Then I'll rename the second parameter here.

If I run it, we'll see we get a regular message like this. It has a `content` attribute, which has both a text block and a tool use block. That's it. My entire message history is being built up. Another way I could run this is to put in `response.content` like this. This works just as well.

So if I do that and then print out `messages`, I still get the right thing. Then, of course, if I want, I can also put a plain string here. Then print out `messages`. We'll see that, yes, it's still building up that response history.

So now we have a more flexible helper function in `add_user_message` and `add_assistant_message`. We can put in a plain string, a list of blocks, or an entire message, and it'll deconstruct those things for us. This will make it much easier to handle messages containing some tool use blocks later. Moving on to the second step of our refactoring.

We're going to update the `chat` function to receive a list of tool schemas. We'll pass this list of schemas to the `client.messages.create` function call. Additionally, from the `chat` function, we'll no longer return plain text from the first block inside the assistant message. Instead, we'll return the entire message we get from Claude.

Again, this is because we now expect to get responses from Claude that contain multiple blocks. And currently, our `chat` function always assumes we only get one block, just a text block and nothing else. So back here, this is the `chat` function. I'll add a `tools`.

They'll be defaulted to `none`. Then we'll wire it up the same way we handle `system`. So we'll say, if any tools are passed in, we'll add them as the `tools` parameter. Next, I'll move to the `return` statement.

Right here. As I just mentioned, our `chat` function is currently set up to assume we always get a single block from Claude, and that block is always a text block. That's why we have this code. We're making a very big assumption that we always get that single block, and that it always contains some text.

Because we're now using tools, this is no longer the case. We might get a message containing multiple blocks, so multiple entries in this `content` list. One of them might be a text block, but we're not necessarily guaranteed it. So, rather than always making this assumption, I'll now return the entire message.

This will be a bit inconvenient for us, because now if we want to get the text, we have to do a bit more work, but it's definitely much safer, because this does reflect reality. OK, that's it. So now, in the same context, we're now going to add a small helper function. Let's call it `text_from_message`.

The goal here is to look at a message, look at all the blocks, find all the text blocks, then just extract the text from them. So this somewhat replaces the functionality we just removed. It'll make it much easier to extract all the text from a given message. So I'll add that helper function below `chat`.

I'll call it `text_from_message`. It'll take a message. Inside this, I'll return a new `log new line` that joins a comprehension and `block.text.for block in message.content if block.type is equal to text`. So this will look at all the different blocks in a message.

If a block is a text block, then we just extract the block's text, then join all the blocks' text together and return it. So, this is just a helper function to make it a bit easier to get all the text from a specific message. OK, we've finished most of the refactoring. So now the last step, which we'll handle right away, is to add support for multiple tool calls in a single conversation.

So, basically, we need to implement a function like this. We need to create a function that takes a messages list, then keeps calling Claude until we get a signal that Claude no longer wants to call tools.

### 39. Implementing Multiple Turns

> Type: Video | Duration: 16:25 | Video: 06 - 008 - Implementing Multiple Turns.mp4

Building a conversation system with tools requires implementing a loop that keeps calling Claude until it stops requesting tool use. When Claude no longer requests tools, this signals that it has a final response ready for the user.

## Detecting Tool Requests

The key to knowing whether Claude wants to use a tool is the `stop_reason` field of the response message. When Claude decides it needs to call a tool, this field is set to "tool_use". This gives us a clear way to check whether we need to continue the conversation loop:

```
if response.stop_reason != "tool_use":
    break  # Claude is done, no more tools needed
```

## The Conversation Loop

The main conversation function follows a simple pattern:

```
def run_conversation(messages):
    while True:
        response = chat(messages, tools=[get_current_datetime_schema])
        add_assistant_message(messages, response)
        print(text_from_message(response))
        
        if response.stop_reason != "tool_use":
            break
            
        tool_results = run_tools(response)
        add_user_message(messages, tool_results)
    
    return messages
```

This loop keeps going until Claude provides a final answer without requesting any more tools.

## Handling Multiple Tool Calls

Claude can request multiple tools in a single response. The message content contains a list of blocks, and we need to handle each `tool use block` separately:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623771%2F06_-_008_-_Implementing_Multiple_Turns_05.1748623771473.png)

The `run_tools` function solves this by filtering for tool use blocks and processing each one:

```
def run_tools(message):
    tool_requests = [
        block for block in message.content if block.type == "tool_use"
    ]
    tool_result_blocks = []
    
    for tool_request in tool_requests:
        # Process each tool request...
```

## Tool Result Blocks

Each tool use block must be responded to with a corresponding `tool result block`. The connection between them is maintained through matching IDs:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623772%2F06_-_008_-_Implementing_Multiple_Turns_10.1748623772471.png)

The structure of a tool result block includes:

```
tool_result_block = {
    "type": "tool_result",
    "tool_use_id": tool_request.id,
    "content": json.dumps(tool_output),
    "is_error": False
}
```

## Error Handling

Robust tool execution requires handling potential errors. When a tool fails, we still need to provide a result block to Claude:

```
try:
    tool_output = run_tool(tool_request.name, tool_request.input)
    tool_result_block = {
        "type": "tool_result",
        "tool_use_id": tool_request.id,
        "content": json.dumps(tool_output),
        "is_error": False
    }
except Exception as e:
    tool_result_block = {
        "type": "tool_result", 
        "tool_use_id": tool_request.id,
        "content": f"Error: {e}",
        "is_error": True
    }
```

## Extensible Tool Routing

To support multiple tools, create a routing function that maps tool names to their implementations:

```
def run_tool(tool_name, tool_input):
    if tool_name == "get_current_datetime":
        return get_current_datetime(**tool_input)
    elif tool_name == "another_tool":
        return another_tool(**tool_input)
    # Add more tools as needed
```

This approach makes it easy to add new tools without modifying the core conversation logic.

## The Complete Workflow

The complete multi-turn conversation workflow is as follows:

- Send a user message with the available tools to Claude
- Claude responds with text and/or tool requests
- Execute all requested tools and create result blocks
- Send the tool results back as a user message
- Repeat this process until Claude provides a final answer

This creates a seamless experience where Claude can use multiple tools across multiple turns to fully answer complex user requests. The conversation history maintains the complete context, allowing Claude to build on previous tool results to provide comprehensive responses.

#### Video Transcript

Now that we've finished the refactoring, we're going to start implementing a function like `run conversation`. In fact, our actual implementation will be almost exactly the same as what you see here on the right. Remember, the entire goal of this function is to keep calling Claude until it no longer requests to use a tool. When it no longer requests to use a tool, that's a signal to us that Claude has a final response ready to send back to our user.

The first thing we really need to understand is how we know whether Claude wants to use a tool. We could just look at the response message and see if there's a `tool use block` in it. But there's a more convenient way. Back in my notebook, I'll run that little example again, this time using `client messages, create` directly, rather than our `chat` function.

I can run it, here's my message response. Now, inside it, you'll notice there's a field called `Stop Reason`, which is set to a string "tool_underscore_use". Let me tell you a bit about that field. This field tells us why Claude decided to stop generating any more text.

Our current tool use value is a signal to us that Claude has decided it needs to call a tool. So if our response message, the assistant message we get from Claude, has a tool use stop reason, that's a very clear and direct signal that Claude wants to use a tool. There are also some other possible stop reason values. We could certainly check those, but the one you'll check most often will definitely be tool use.

So this is how we'll implement that little if statement. OK, let's go back to our notebook. We'll start implementing this `run conversation` function. Back here.

I'll clear that cell. It was just for demonstration purposes. I'll define my `run conversation` function. It'll take a messages list, then we'll set up our loop.

Inside this, we'll get a response by calling Claude through our newly upgraded, now tool-supporting `chat` function. So I'll pass in my messages list, and some tools that Claude can call. In this case, we currently only have one tool. So I'll add it here.

This is our `get_current_datetime_schema`. Then I'll take that response I got, and use the newly upgraded `add_assistant_message` function to add it to my message conversation history. So we'll say, if `response.stop_underscore_reason` is not equal to `tool_underscore_use`, then that indicates Claude is done, it doesn't need to use any more tools. So we'll immediately `break`. If we get past that `if` statement, then we know Claude wants to call a tool. So we're going to put together a new function shortly.

We'll call it `Run Tools`. We'll pass in the message we just got from Claude. The goal of `Run Tools` is to look at all the `tool use blocks` in this message and run the corresponding tool for each one. I'll define the `Run Tools` function in a new cell above `Run Conversation`.

So here, I'll put together a new function called `Run Tools`, which will take a message. Inside it, we might have a text block telling us what Claude is currently thinking or doing. We don't really care much about that text block, so I just removed it from this diagram. Then we're just left with two separate, or possibly more, tool use blocks. So inside this `RunTools` function, we'll iterate over all the different tool use blocks we got.

For each one, we'll run the specified tool with the given input. So we'll look at this `name` field. We'll find the appropriate tool function to run, then run it with the given input. Then we'll take all the output from each tool run, and assemble it into separate `tool result blocks`.

Remember, the `tool result block` is how we communicate the result of running a tool to Claude. Once we've assembled all these different tool result blocks, we'll put them all into a list, then return them from the `Run Tools` function. OK, let's try to implement this. I know this is confusing.

I know there's a lot going on here, but the code itself is actually not as bad as it initially seems. So back here, inside `Run Tools`, the first thing I'll do is look at this message's `content` attribute. That's a list of blocks. I'll just extract the tool use blocks.

So I'll say `tool request. is block for block in message.content if block.type is equal to tool use`. So, there's a bit of filtering going on here. We're only getting the `tool use` blocks, because those are the only ones we care about. I'm specifically calling them `tool request`, because I think that's more meaningful than `tool use`. These are Claude's requests for us to use a tool.

Then I'll create an empty list called `tool_result_blocks`. It will eventually contain all the different tool results we create. Then I'll iterate over all these different tool requests. So `for tool_request in tool_request`. So now we're iterating over each individual tool request.

So here's where we now need to run a specified tool with the given input. We know which tool we want to run based on this `name` attribute. That'll give me some tool output. I'll now use this tool output to assemble a brand-new tool result block. We haven't used tool result blocks for a while, so let me give you a quick reminder of what these things are. OK, on the left-hand side is a tool use block.

This is what we're currently handling. This is Claude requesting to use a tool. On the right-hand side is a `tool result block`. So this is what we put together to respond to Claude's request to run a tool.

Remember, the tool result block has several different properties that we need to assign. First, it'll have a `tool use ID`. This needs to be exactly equal to the ID in the tool use block that caused us to run the specific tool. Note the ID field on the left-hand side here, it's called ID, and then on the tool result block, it's a completely different property.
