### 40. Using Multiple Tools

> Type: Video | Duration: 3:38 | Video: 06 - 009 - Using Multiple Tools.mp4

Once you have the core tool handling infrastructure, adding multiple tools to your Claude implementation becomes straightforward. This tutorial shows how to integrate additional tools by following a simple pattern.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623759%2F06_-_009_-_Using_Multiple_Tools_00.1748623759640.png)

## The Tools We're Adding

Our reminder system needs three main functions:

- **Get Current Datetime** - Claude needs to know the current date and time
- **Add Duration to Datetime** - Claude isn't perfect at datetime arithmetic
- **Set Reminder** - We need a way to set reminders

The good news is that most of the implementation work is already done. The `add_duration_to_datetime` function and the `set_reminder` function are provided, along with their corresponding schemas.

## Adding Tools to the Conversation

First, update the `run_conversation` function to include the new tool schemas in the `tools` list:

```
response = chat(messages, tools=[
    get_current_datetime_schema,
    add_duration_to_datetime_schema,
    set_reminder_schema
])
```

This tells Claude that it can use all three available tools during the conversation.

## Updating the Tool Router

Next, modify the `run_tool` function to handle the new tool calls. Add an `elif` branch for each new tool:

```
def run_tool(tool_name, tool_input):
    if tool_name == "get_current_datetime":
        return get_current_datetime(**tool_input)
    elif tool_name == "add_duration_to_datetime":
        return add_duration_to_datetime(**tool_input)
    elif tool_name == "set_reminder":
        return set_reminder(**tool_input)
```

This pattern is simple: check the tool name, call the corresponding function with the provided input, then return the result.

## Testing the Use of Multiple Tools

To test the system, try a request that requires multiple tools: "Set a reminder for my doctor's appointment. The time is 177 days after January 1, 2050."

This request forces Claude to:

1.  Calculate the date (using `add_duration_to_datetime`)
2.  Set the reminder (using `set_reminder`)

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623760%2F06_-_009_-_Using_Multiple_Tools_15.1748623760531.png)

Claude will first explain what it needs to do, then make the corresponding Tool Calls in sequence. The conversation shows Claude calculating the target date as June 27, 2050, then setting a reminder for that date.

## Understanding the Message Flow

When you examine the conversation history, you'll see the complete message structure:

-   A user message containing the request
-   An Assistant message containing both text and tool use blocks
-   Tool result messages
-   A subsequent Assistant message

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623761%2F06_-_009_-_Using_Multiple_Tools_18.1748623761688.png)

This demonstrates how Claude can include multiple blocks in a single message—combining explanatory text with tool use requests.

## The Simple Pattern for Adding Tools

Once you have the core tool infrastructure, adding new tools follows this pattern:

1.  Create the implementation of the tool function
2.  Define the tool's schema
3.  Add the schema to the tools list in `run_conversation`
4.  Add a case for the tool in `run_tool`

This modular approach makes it easy to expand the AI Assistant's capabilities without refactoring existing code. Each new tool integrates seamlessly with the existing conversation flow and tool handling logic.

This part will be a bit anticlimactic, because the previous video was quite challenging, and this video will be very simple, short, and direct. You'll find that I've already implemented an `add_duration_to_datetime` here. If you scroll down, you'll see I've also prepared the `set_reminder` function. `set_reminder` doesn't actually set a reminder or anything like that, it just prints a statement saying "Hey, we set a reminder at this time with the given content."

I've also provided the `add_duration_to_date time` schema and the `set_reminder` schema for us. So all we really need to do is pass these two schemas to Claude, and we also need to make sure that if Claude requests to use either tool, we call the corresponding tool function. Check whether the tool name is `add_duration_to_date time`. If it is, we'll return a call to `add_duration_to_date time` with `**tool_input`. Then I'll repeat this for the other tool. If the tool name is `set_reminder`, return `set_reminder` with `**tool_input`.

That's it. So `set_reminder` with `tool_input`. That's it. Once you've put together the `run_tool` function and the `run_conversation` function, after the initial difficulty, everything about tool use becomes very simple and straightforward, because adding additional tools is super easy.

Just update `run_tool`, add a tool schema, add an implementation for the actual tool function itself, and you're done. You've got it all set up. Now let's test it. I'll make sure to rerun that cell.

I'll rerun `run_conversation`. Then at the very bottom, let's update the query. I'll ask Claude to set a reminder. I'll say the reminder needs to be set for 177 days after January 1, 2050.

This will definitely result in more than one tool call. Claude first has to call `add_duration_to_datetime`, then set the reminder. Let's run it and see how it goes. Claude initially tells us it needs to calculate what day is 177 days after January 1.

Once it's calculated, it'll try to set the reminder. Then we see a log statement here. This log statement comes from the `set_reminder` function. Remember, `set_reminder` doesn't actually do anything, it just prints out the parameters given to it.

Finally, we get a response from Claude, telling us that our appointment has been set for the correct date of Monday, June 27, 2050. We can also look at the message conversation history. Again, we have the user message, and the Assistant message here has a text block. In addition to the text block, again, there's a tool use block.

So once again, we've seen an example of a message containing multiple different blocks. Then we respond with a tool result. We get some follow-up information. From here, I think you get the process.

### 41. The batch tool

> Type: Video | Duration: 8:26 | Video: 06 - 010 - The Batch Tool.mp4

When using Claude's tool calling capabilities, you may notice that Claude can include multiple tool use blocks in a single Assistant message. This allows Claude to run multiple tools in parallel, rather than making a separate request for each tool. However, in practice, getting Claude to consistently do this can be challenging.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623764%2F06_-_010_-_The_Batch_Tool_00.1748623764137.png)

For example, if you ask Claude to set two reminders for the same day, ideally it would return a response containing two tool use blocks. But often, Claude will make the first tool call, wait for your response, then make the second tool call in a separate round trip.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623765%2F06_-_010_-_The_Batch_Tool_04.1748623765474.png)

This creates unnecessary back-and-forth communication when these operations could be performed simultaneously. The solution is to implement a `batch tool` to encourage Claude to request multiple tool calls at once.

## How the Batch Tool Works

The batch tool is an additional tool that accepts a list of calls to other tools. Instead of calling multiple tools directly, Claude calls the batch tool, providing parameters that describe which tools it wants to run.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623767%2F06_-_010_-_The_Batch_Tool_05.1748623766803.png)

The batch tool receives a list of `invocations`, where each invocation specifies:

-   The name of the tool to invoke
-   The arguments to pass to that tool

When you receive a batch tool call, your code iterates over the list of invocations and executes each requested tool call.

## Implementing the Batch Tool Schema

First, you need to define the schema for the batch tool. The structure is as follows:

```
batch_tool_schema = {
    "name": "batch_tool",
    "description": "Invoke multiple other tool calls simultaneously",
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "invocations": {
                "type": "array",
                "description": "The tool calls to invoke",
                "items": {
                    "type": "object",
                    "properties": {
                        "name": {
                            "type": "string",
                            "description": "The name of the tool to invoke"
                        },
                        "arguments": {
                            "type": "object",
                            "description": "The arguments to pass to the tool"
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }
    }
}
```

## Implementing the Batch Processing Function

Next, you need to create the function that handles batch tool calls:

```
def run_batch(invocations=[]):
    batch_output = []
    
    for invocation in invocations:
        name = invocation["name"]
        args = json.loads(invocation["arguments"])
        
        tool_output = run_tool(name, args)
        
        batch_output.append({
            "tool_name": name,
            "output": tool_output
        })
    
    return batch_output
```

This function:

1.  Iterates over each invocation in the list
2.  Extracts the tool name and arguments
3.  Calls the corresponding tool using your existing `run_tool` function
4.  Collects all results into a batch output list

You'll also need to update your main tool routing function to handle the batch tool:

```
def run_tool(tool_name, tool_input):
    if tool_name == "get_current_datetime":
        return get_current_datetime(**tool_input)
    elif tool_name == "add_duration_to_datetime":
        return add_duration_to_datetime(**tool_input)
    elif tool_name == "set_reminder":
        return set_reminder(**tool_input)
    elif tool_name == "batch_tool":
        return run_batch(**tool_input)
```

## The Result

After implementation, when Claude recognizes that multiple operations can be performed in parallel, it will be more likely to use the batch tool. You'll see Claude call the batch tool with a list of all the operations it wants to perform, rather than making separate requests.

The batch tool is essentially a workaround that provides Claude with a higher-level abstraction for parallel tool execution. While this may seem like an unusual approach, it's an effective technique for encouraging Claude to batch related operations, thereby reducing API round trips and improving overall efficiency.

#### Video Transcript

I've repeated several times that a single Assistant message can contain multiple tool use blocks. For example, if we ask Claude to compute 10 plus 10 and 30 plus 30, Claude might return an Assistant message containing two separate tool use blocks. Claude has recognized that these two operations can be run in parallel. So we can make both tool calls simultaneously, then send a single follow-up reply to Claude containing the results of both evaluations.

However, we'll notice that, in practice, getting Claude to do this can sometimes be a bit challenging. Let me tell you why. Back in my notebook, I've updated my prompt. I'm now asking Claude to perform two tasks in parallel.

I'm asking it to set two separate reminders for the same day. The first reminder will have that text, and the second reminder is there. Now, there's no reason at all why Claude can't return a single response containing two tool use blocks. But if I run this cell, we'll quickly see that this isn't actually the case.

If I look at the output messages, I'll notice that the initial Assistant message I received actually only has one tool use block. Then I made another round of requests here, and the second Assistant message I received has another tool use block. Let me show you in diagram form what's happening here. OK, here's the situation.

We sent Claude an initial user message asking it to set two separate reminders. Then in the first response we received, there was only one tool use block. We then responded with a tool result, and then we separately received another tool use block. This extra round of requests here is completely unnecessary.

Ideally, what should happen is this. We send the initial user message, then we'd receive an Assistant message containing two separate tool use blocks. One for setting the tax due appointment, and another for the doctor's appointment. While Claude is capable of returning a response containing multiple tool use blocks, it doesn't do so as often as we'd like.

However, there's a little trick that can greatly increase Claude's chances of returning multiple tool uses in a single message. This trick is to implement a batch tool. Let me tell you exactly what a batch tool is. The batch tool is surprisingly simple.

This is another tool, just like `get current date time` or `add duration to date time`, which are tools we've already created. You and I will define the schema and a function for this tool. Then we'll pass it to Claude just like all the other tools we've already created.

The batch tool schema tells Claude that it can run multiple other tools in parallel. If Claude decides it does want to run multiple other tools, it won't call those tools directly, but instead will call the batch tool. And it'll provide some parameters, just like you see here. It'll be a list of objects, where each object represents some other tool Claude wants to call.

So we might have one object representing setting a reminder for a doctor's appointment, then another object representing setting a reminder for taxes due. Whenever we receive a response containing a batch tool use, you and I will write some code to handle this array of objects. We'll iterate over it, and call each tool listed here. So you can think of this as an abstraction on top of these tool use blocks.

The problem here is that Claude doesn't really want to respond with multiple tool use blocks, so we sort of give it a higher-level abstraction that does exactly the same thing as having multiple tool use blocks. The only difference is that you and I handle it manually by implementing this batch tool. This might sound crazy, and I think it is a bit crazy, but like I said, this is largely a trick. We're sort of "tricking" Claude into calling multiple tools in parallel.

So, let's go back to our notebook and try to implement this batch tool. OK, back here, to help us out, at the bottom of the `Tools and Schema` section, I've provided a batch tool schema for us. When Claude decides to call the batch tool, it'll provide a list of invocations. This list describes all the other tools Claude wants to call.

Inside this `invocations` list, there will be the name of the other tool Claude wants to call, along with some arguments to provide to that tool. I want to give you a quick demonstration of how this thing works. So I'll collapse that cell. I'll go to our `run conversation` function here, then I'll add that batch tool schema.

Then I'll make sure I run that cell. Then I'll go to my demonstration cell below, where I ask Claude to do two things, hopefully in parallel. Now I'll run this again. Before I look at the result, there's a very important thing I want to point out.

We've now added the batch tool schema, but we haven't yet provided an implementation for it. In other words, there's no code to actually handle that `invocations` list that Claude wants to do and actually run them. So this is something we still need to implement. Let's look at the messages list and see what Claude decided to do.

OK, we have the initial user message, then a subsequent Assistant message. Inside that message, there's a text block, then a single tool use block. If you look carefully, Claude did decide to use the batch tool. So Claude wants to run two separate tools.

We can see them listed in the `invocations` list. So we'll make two separate calls, one calling `set reminder` with the content `I have a doctor's appointment`, then a second call with the content `taxes are due`. So clearly, Claude has decided to use the batch tool to parallelize these two separate `set reminder` calls. So now all we have to do is provide an actual implementation for the batch tool.

Let me tell you how we do this. First, I'll scroll up to our `run tool` function. Right here. I'll add an additional `else if` condition.

So if Claude wants to run the batch tool, I'll return a call to a new function we're going to implement called `run batch`. And I'll again use `star star tool input`. Then I'll implement the `run batch` function right above this function. So we'll add a `run batch`.

It'll take an `invocations` list. I'll default it to an empty list. Inside the function, we'll write some logic very similar to what we did inside `run tools`. So we'll iterate over all the different invocations Claude requested.

We'll run the corresponding tool. Then we'll build some kind of response, add all these different responses to a list, then return a list at the end. So here's how we do it. First, I'll create a list to hold the output of all these different tool calls we make.

I'll name it `batch output`. I'll make sure to return it at the bottom of the function. In the middle, I'll iterate over all the different invocations Claude requested. So for `invocation` in `invocations`, I'll extract the name of the tool Claude wants to run and its arguments.

Now, the arguments passed in here are encoded in JSON format. So we need to use the JSON module we've already imported to parse that JSON, right there. So I can wrap it with `JSON load string`. Then we'll run the requested tool by using the `run tool` function here.

`run tool` already takes a tool name and some arguments, and calls the corresponding tool. So we can take advantage of this to make sure we call the corresponding tool. I'll get some tool output by calling `run tool` with `name` and `arcs`. Then I'll add a new record to our `batch output` list, describing this invocation and the result it produced.

So I'll add `batch output`, and I'll append a `tool_name` of `name` and an `output` of `tool_output` to it. Now let's test it. I'll make sure I run this cell.

Then I'll go to my test cell at the very bottom. I'll run this again. Now that we've provided an actual implementation for the invocations, we should quickly see two separate reminders being set completely in parallel. Very nice.

It definitely worked. If we look at the listed messages, we again get our Assistant block. It has a tool use block, trying to call the batch tool with two separate invocations. Then we respond with a `two result`.

Inside it, we can see the results of our two separate `set reminder` calls. The output is `null`, which is expected, because right now the `set reminder` tool doesn't actually return anything.

---

### 42. Tools for Structured Data

> Type: Video | Duration: 7:56 | Video: 06 - 011 - Tools for Structured Data.mp4

When you need to get `structured data` from Claude, there are two main approaches: prompt-based techniques using `message prefills` and `stop sequences`, or a more reliable method—tools. While the prompt-based approach is simpler to set up, tools provide more reliable output at the cost of added complexity.

## Tools for Structured Data

The tool-based approach works by creating a JSON schema that defines the exact structure of the data you want to extract. Instead of hoping Claude will format its response correctly, you essentially give Claude a function to call, whose specific parameters match the output structure you expect.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623754%2F06_-_011_-_Tools_for_Structured_Data_03.1748623754770.png)

Here's how the process works:

-   Write a schema describing the data structure you're looking for
-   Use the `tool_choice` parameter to force Claude to use a tool
-   Extract the structured data from the tool use response
-   No need to provide a follow-up response—once you have the data, the task is complete

For example, if you want to extract financial balances and key insights from a financial statement, your schema would define them as an integer and an array of strings, respectively.

## Controlling Tool Use

A key part of this technique is ensuring that Claude actually calls your tool. You can use the `tool_choice` parameter to control this behavior:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623755%2F06_-_011_-_Tools_for_Structured_Data_09.1748623755582.png)

-   `{"type": "auto"}` - The model decides whether it needs to use a tool (default)
-   `{"type": "any"}` - The model must use a tool, but can choose which one
-   `{"type": "tool", "name": "TOOL_NAME"}` - The model must use the specified tool

For structured data extraction, you'll typically choose the third option, to guarantee that Claude calls your specific schema tool.

## Implementation Example

Suppose you want to extract the title, author, and key insights from an article. First, you'd create a tool schema:

```
article_summary_schema = {
    "name": "article_summary",
    "description": "Extracts structured data from articles",
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "title": {"type": "string"},
            "author": {"type": "string"}, 
            "key_insights": {
                "type": "array",
                "items": {"type": "string"}
            }
        }
    }
}
```

Then you'd call Claude, forcing it to use that tool:

```
response = chat(
    messages,
    tools=[article_summary_schema],
    tool_choice={"type": "tool", "name": "article_summary"}
)
```

The response will contain a tool use block with your structured data in the `input` field. You can access it directly:

```
structured_data = response.content[0].input
```

## When to Use Which Approach

Choose prompt-based structured output when you need quick and simple output. Use tools when you need guaranteed reliability and can handle the additional setup complexity. Both techniques are valuable, depending on your specific use case and requirements.

#### Video Transcript

Earlier in this course, we discussed how to get structured output from Claude through some clever tricks. In particular, we discussed using message prefills and stop sequences, along with a carefully designed prompt, to get some JSON from Claude. Now, this approach does work well, and it's very simple to set up, but we can get more reliable output by using tools very cleverly. So we're going to look at how to achieve structured output using just tools, without worrying about any message prefills or stop sequences.

I anticipate that one of your first questions will be, since we've been able to use tools all along, why learn this way of doing structured output? Well, the answer is simple. Using tools for structured output requires more setup and has more complexity.

So, having both abilities to get structured data at the same time is very valuable, because in some cases, you might just want to use this prompt-based structured output. In other cases, you might want to use tools. OK, let's look at how to leverage tools to generate structured data. As I just mentioned, this is an alternative method for extracting structured data (mainly JSON) from some data source.

This is more reliable than the technique we looked at before, but again, it's much more complex to set up. The general idea here is that we'll write a JSON schema specification for a tool, whose input will be the data structure we're looking for. So back when we looked at this prompt earlier, we can imagine asking Claude to look at a financial statement and extract a balance of type integer and key insights of type list of strings. So, if we want to handle this with tool-based structured output, we'd write out a JSON schema specification, where the input will be a balance of type integer and key insights of type list of strings.

Then we'd provide this JSON schema specification to Claude, along with the financial data we might want to get some information from. Now, at this point, let me show you a flowchart, because it's easier to understand in diagram format. OK, so as before, we'll write out a reasonable prompt for Claude. So we might say, analyze a financial statement, then call some provided tool.

In addition to this prompt containing all the statement data, we'll also provide that schema. So these will be sent to Claude. Claude will look at the prompt. It'll see the available tools, then it'll say, ah, great.

I'll call this financial analysis tool, and make sure the parameters I provide match this structure. So Claude will then respond to us with a tool use block. In that block, it'll request to call the financial analysis tool. Its input will be the exact input we specified in this financial analysis schema.

So we'll get a balance of type integer and key insights of type list of strings. Once we get this data, we're done. So unlike a typical tool call where we'd usually respond with a tool result block, here we don't need to do that, because our only goal is to get the JSON extracted here. That's it.

So once we receive the Assistant message in response, we essentially say to Claude, thanks for the JSON, we're done, we won't have any follow-up requests. The last thing I want to mention is that a key part of this technique is making sure Claude calls the tool we provide it. To force Claude to call a specific tool, we can provide a `tool_choice` parameter when calling the `client.messages.create` function. Specifically, we can provide a `tool_choice`, which is a dictionary with a type of `tool` and a name of the tool we want to force Claude to call.

So in this case, if we provide a schema called `analyze_financial_statement`, we'd put `analyze_financial_statement` here, like this. Now that we have an idea of how all this works, let's go back to our notebook and do some hands-on practice. Back here, I'm in a new notebook called `002 Structured Data`, which should be attached to this lesson. It contains all the same code from the notebook we used before.

The only difference is that I've added this extra cell at the bottom. This cell will ask Claude to write a one-paragraph academic article about computer science, including a title and an author name. I'll quickly run this, then look at the generated article. This article has a title and author name, then some accompanying text.

So I want to try to extract some JSON data from this text. Specifically, I want to extract the title as a string, the author as a string, then I want to extract a list of strings containing some key insights from the article. To do this, we'll write a schema definition for a new tool. Our schema will clearly indicate to Claude that, in order to call that tool, it must provide a title of type string, an author of type string, and a key insights list parameter.

These will also be a list of strings. Remember, the easiest way to create a new tool definition is to leverage Claude itself. So I've again opened my browser. I've opened the documentation on tool use here.

In response, I'll get my `article summary` tool definition. So now I'll copy this, bring it back to my notebook. I'll go to the cell containing all our other tools and schemas. I'll paste it here and call it `article_summary_schema`.

That's it. I'll also make sure to run that cell. The next thing I want to do is update our `chat` function implementation. So in the cell above `helper_functions`, I'll find the `chat` function.

Remember, a key aspect of this JSON extraction operation is ensuring that Claude always calls the specific tool you made. So here we have the title, the author, then `key_insights`, which will be a list of different strings. So if we just want to access that data individually, we can assign the result of calling `chat` to `response`, then print out `response .message[0].tool_calls[0].function.arguments`, like this. Let me run it one more time, and now we have our structured data.

### 43. Fine-Grained Tool Calling

> Type: Video | Duration: 10:56 | Video: 011.1 - Fine Grained Tool Calling.mp4

When you combine Tool Use with `streaming` in Claude, you get real-time updates as the AI generates the tool parameters. This creates a more responsive user experience, but there are some important details to understand about how it works behind the scenes.

## Basic Tool Streaming

With streaming enabled, Claude sends back different types of events as it processes your request. You're already familiar with events like `ContentBlockDelta` for regular text generation. For tool use, you'll also need to handle a new event type called `InputJsonEvent`.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1752775508%2F06_-_011.1_-_Fine_Grained_Tool_Calling_01.1752775507859.png)

Each `InputJsonEvent` contains two key properties:

-   **`partial_json`** - A chunk representing part of the tool parameters as JSON
-   **`snapshot`** - The JSON accumulated and built from all chunks received so far

Here's how you handle these events in your streaming pipeline:

```
for chunk in stream:
    if chunk.type == "input_json":
        # Process the partial JSON chunk
        print(chunk.partial_json)
        # Or use the complete snapshot so far
        current_args = chunk.snapshot

```

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1752775508%2F06_-_011.1_-_Fine_Grained_Tool_Calling_02.1752775508676.png)

## How JSON Validation Works

Here's where it gets interesting. The Anthropic API doesn't immediately send you every block Claude generates. Instead, it first buffers these blocks and validates them.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1752775509%2F06_-_011.1_-_Fine_Grained_Tool_Calling_08.1752775509598.png)

The API waits for a complete top-level key-value pair to finish before sending anything. For example, if your tool expects this structure:

```
{
  "abstract": "This paper presents a novel...",
  "meta": {
    "word_count": 847,
    "review": "This paper introduces QuanNet..."
  }
}
```

The API will:

1.  Wait until the entire `abstract` value is complete
2.  Validate that key-value pair against your schema
3.  Send all the blocks buffered for `abstract` at once
4.  Repeat this process for the `meta` object

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1752775510%2F06_-_011.1_-_Fine_Grained_Tool_Calling_10.1752775510417.png)

This validation process explains why, even with streaming enabled, you see a delay followed by a burst of text. The blocks are held back until a complete, valid top-level key-value pair is ready.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1752775511%2F06_-_011.1_-_Fine_Grained_Tool_Calling_11.1752775511555.png)

## Fine-Grained Tool Calling

If you need faster, more granular streaming—perhaps to show users immediate updates or to start processing partial results quickly—you can enable `fine-grained tool calling`.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1752775512%2F06_-_011.1_-_Fine_Grained_Tool_Calling_13.1752775512126.png)

Fine-grained tool calling does primarily one thing: it disables JSON validation on the API side. This means:

-   You receive blocks as soon as Claude generates them
-   There's no buffering delay between top-level keys
-   More traditional streaming behavior
-   **Key point:** JSON validation is disabled—your code must handle invalid JSON

Enable it by adding `fine_grained=True` to your API call:

```
run_conversation(
    messages, 
    tools=[save_article_schema], 
    fine_grained=True
)
```

With fine-grained tool calling, you might receive a `word_count` value earlier in the stream without waiting for the entire `meta` object to complete.

## Handling Invalid JSON

When using fine-grained tool calling, Claude might generate invalid JSON like `"word_count": undefined` instead of a proper number. Your application needs to handle these cases gracefully:

```
try:
    parsed_args = json.loads(chunk.snapshot)
except json.JSONDecodeError:
    # Handle invalid JSON appropriately
    print("Received invalid JSON, continuing...")

```

Without fine-grained tool calling, the API's validation would catch this error and might wrap the problematic value in a string, which may not match the schema you expect.

## When to Use Fine-Grained Tool Calling

Consider enabling fine-grained tool calling when:

-   You need to show users real-time progress of tool argument generation
-   You want to start processing partial tool results as soon as possible
-   Buffering delays negatively impact your user experience
-   You're willing to implement robust JSON error handling

For most applications, the default behavior with validation is perfectly sufficient. But when you need extra responsiveness, fine-grained tool calling lets you get blocks at the speed Claude generates them.

#### Video Transcript

In this video, we'll explore how to combine tool use with streaming. As you may recall, we discussed streaming from the API earlier. Streaming lets us do a better job of showing users what's happening in our application. So when we have streaming enabled and make a request to Claude, we receive some initial responses, followed by a series of different events.

Each event will have some additional text that we might want to show the user. As you may recall, in our application, we may need to handle many different types of events, and one very common event that we need to watch out for is the Content Block Delta. To use tools with streaming, we'll be adding one additional type of event we need to handle, called the input JSON event.

So now, if we turn on streaming and try to use a tool, Claude might send us a new kind of event called an input JSON event. This object will have two important properties. First, there's a partial JSON, which is a piece of JSON representing the partial arguments Claude wants to send to our tool. We'll also get something called a snapshot, which is the cumulative sum of all the different partial JSON pieces we've received so far.

To help you better understand how this works, I've prepared a new notebook called 003 tool streaming. It's attached to this lecture. This notebook contains a modified `run conversation` function. It will use a new function called `chat stream` to open a response stream from the Anthropic API.

Then for each chunk in that stream, we'll handle it based on the chunk's type. In the case of receiving an input JSON chunk, all we're going to do for now is print it out. So I'll go to the very bottom, where I've prepared a prompt along with a new tool called `save article schema`. I'll run this notebook, and let's see what happens.

Pretty quickly, we'll see each chunk of the response come in, including the arguments to the tool call. You'll notice that this notebook is also generating an academic article, just with slightly different properties than what we had before. Now, honestly, there's not much more to tool streaming beyond this. We really just need to make sure we handle that extra event type.

That said, there's another aspect of tool streaming I want to tell you about, but this is about a very, very special feature you may never need to use. So let me give you a very brief overview, and then you can decide whether you want to learn about this very specialized feature. If I rerun this notebook, you might notice that we seem to be getting streaming behavior. Text comes in chunk by chunk.

But specifically for our tool call arguments, you'll notice we have to sit and wait for a few seconds. Then suddenly, a big chunk of text appears. This very large delay, and then the fact that we get a big chunk of text, might not be a big deal to you at all. However, you might be developing an application where showing users very precise content updates, and displaying them as quickly as possible, is very important.

Or, maybe you want to receive parts of a tool call as soon as possible so you can start doing some processing work quickly. So for the rest of this video, I'll help you understand why there's such a big delay followed by a big chunk of text appearing. I'll show you a feature in the Anthropic API to eliminate that delay. So, let's get started.

The first thing I want to do is clarify the tool I added to this notebook. So I tweaked our earlier tool for generating academic articles a bit. We're still generating an article, but now it has a slightly different input structure. I have an example of the expected input in the bottom left.

So we expect to receive an `abstract` key, which is sort of like the abstract of an academic paper, as well as a `meta` object. The `meta` object will have a word count and a review. In theory, this review could be any lengthy review of the specific paper that was generated. Now, in this example input, there are a few things I just want to check or point out very quickly.

In this large object we get, we'll have two top-level keys. In other words, the top-level keys in the entire large object. There's `abstract` and `meta`. So we'll call those the top-level keys.

`meta` will point to an object that has some additional key-value pairs inside it, but again, the top-level keys are `abstract` and `meta`. So keep that in mind for a moment. Now I want to help you understand what's actually happening inside the Anthropic API when we generate some arguments for a tool call in streaming mode. Its behavior might not be what you'd expect.

In the top left of this diagram, I've sent our call to the Anthropic API. Once it receives our request, Claude will start generating some JSON, which will be the input to our tool call. The JSON isn't generated all at once. Instead, it's created chunk by chunk.

Now, at this point, something surprising happens. The Anthropic API doesn't immediately send these blocks right back to our server. Instead, it holds onto them for a while. The reason for holding onto these blocks is that Claude sometimes generates invalid JSON.

So instead of immediately sending each block to us, the API will try to perform a validation step to make sure that what it sends us is valid JSON. Let me show you how the validation step works. First, as a reminder, our tool call has two top-level keys, `abstract` and `meta`. So the API won't wait for the entire object to finish generating before validating.

Instead, it waits for a single top-level key-value pair to finish generating. So in the case of the `abstract` key, the Anthropic API will wait until it sees the closing quote of the `abstract` value string. Once it sees that closing quote on the fourth block all the way on the right, the API knows it has generated at least one key-value pair. In this case, the `abstract` key-value pair.

Then the API will try to validate that key-value pair on its own. It will compare it against the JSON schema we provided and ensure valid JSON was generated. If it's valid, then every block that was originally generated will be sent back to our server. Note that we do receive each individual block, rather than the entire key-value pair as a single block.

If you were to print out all the different blocks we received in our notebook, you'd find that we really do receive a large number of different individual blocks. They just happen to arrive at our server at almost the same time, because these blocks were buffered on the API so it could do this validation step. Then this generation process will continue on to the `meta` top-level key. So again, the API will wait until the entire `meta` top-level key-value pair finishes generating.

Then it will validate it, and then send each individual block back to our server. So this is why, even with streaming enabled, we end up with these very large pauses while blocks are buffered on the API. Then suddenly, we see a large group of blocks appear, or a big chunk of text appear all at once. Now, again, this behavior might be fine for you.

You might not mind this buffering and validation step. But if you're building a UI where you want to show users updates as quickly as possible, or if you want to do some tool processing as quickly as possible, then this big pause might be a bit annoying. Now, if that's the case, I have good news. There's a feature in the API called Fine-Grained Tool Calling.

At its core, fine-grained tool calling really does just one thing. It disables the JSON validation step. So if you enable fine-grained tool calling, the API will wait for Claude to generate a few blocks. It will concatenate them together, and then send that big chunk to your server.

So with this feature, you'll see more traditional streaming output while generating tool inputs. I do want to repeat that with fine-grained tool calling, JSON validation on the API is disabled. So the code running on your server should now really assume that you might receive invalid JSON, and you should implement some appropriate error handling. The notebook we're using is already set up for fine-grained tool calling.

So let's see what happens to our response when we turn it on. I'll go to the `run conversation` call here, and then I'll add a `fine-grained tool`, like this. I'll run this. Now we'll see that when we finally get to the tool call, we'll get a more classic streaming experience, where we get just a little bit of text at a time, chunk by chunk.

So again, this will allow us to process certain parts of the tool call more quickly. Let's say the word count value here is very important to us. Without fine-grained tool calling, we'd have to wait for all this text to finish generating before we could access the word count. But if I run this again with fine-grained tool calling, we'll see that now we can get that word count value much more quickly.

We don't have to wait for all this extra text to finish generating. Now, you might be a little curious about what exactly happens when we get some invalid JSON. Well, I've prepared a prompt that almost guarantees we'll get some invalid JSON. I'll copy and paste it in quickly.

Then I'll also add an extra argument to the `run conversation` function. Just to make sure we really do get that invalid JSON. The extra argument I'm adding here is simply a forced tool call. I'm forcing the model to always call the `save article` model we've already created.

So I'll run this. We'll see that initially everything goes smoothly, but very quickly we'll get an error. The reason we get an error, and what this prompt really does, we just saw that word count value. So the word count should be a number.

This prompt will force an `undefined` word count value. By the way, `undefined` is not a valid value in JSON. The equivalent JSON value would be `null`. So if we get an `undefined` JSON value, that would be invalid JSON.

And we'll cause a parsing error. So that's exactly what we see here. If I scroll down a bit more, I'll see an error about... yes, we got some invalid JSON. Specifically because we got an `undefined` word count.

You might be a little curious about what would happen here if we don't use fine-grained tool calling. You might just be a little curious. So if I comment it out, and then run this again.

So now the validation step will happen. So let's see how this gets handled. So what the API ends up doing is, it still puts in that `meta` object. But it won't actually be an object; instead, it will wrap the whole thing in a string.

So now, technically, we're really not following the JSON specification in our response here. Our JSON schema, the one we provided, says `meta` must be an object that has a number-type word count and a string-type review. So now `meta` is a string instead of an object. Okay, let's wrap up.

Again, tool streaming itself isn't too crazy. We can easily add it to any kind of streaming pipeline you may have already created. With default tool streaming, if you're generating a large top-level key-value pair, then there will be a delay during generation because the API performs some validation steps. If this is a big problem for you, you can always turn on fine-grained tool streaming, which will give you a more classic streaming experience, but at the cost of losing that validation step.

---

### 44. The text edit tool

> Type: Video | Duration: 8:41 | Video: 06 - 012 - The Text Edit Tool.mp4

Important note: The tool version strings for all model versions can be found here: https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/agents-and-tools/tool-use/text-editor-tool

Claude has a built-in tool you don't need to create from scratch: the `text editor tool`. This tool gives Claude the ability to work with files and directories, just as you would in a standard text editor.

## What the Text Editor Tool Can Do

The text editor tool gives Claude a comprehensive set of file operation capabilities:

-   View the contents of a file or directory
-   View the contents of a specific range of lines in a file
-   Replace text in a file
-   Create new files
-   Insert text at a specific line in a file
-   Undo the most recent edit to a file

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623830%2F06_-_012_-_The_Text_Edit_Tool_00.1748623830120.png)

This greatly expands Claude's capabilities and essentially gives it the abilities of a software engineer right out of the box.

## Understanding the Implementation Requirements

The situation here is a bit confusing: while the tool's schema is built into Claude, you still need to provide the actual implementation. Think of it this way—Claude knows how to request file operations, but you need to write the code that actually performs them.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623831%2F06_-_012_-_The_Text_Edit_Tool_04.1748623831083.png)

When you use other tools, you write both the JSON schema and the function implementation. For the text editor tool, Claude provides knowledge of the schema, but you must write the functions to handle Claude's requests to create files, read directories, replace text, and so on.

## Schema Versions

While the main schema is built into Claude, you do need to include a small schema stub when making a request. The exact schema depends on the Claude model you're using:

```
def get_text_edit_schema(model):
    if model.startswith("claude-3-7-sonnet"):
        return {
            "type": "text_editor_20250124",
            "name": "str_replace_editor",
        }
    elif model.startswith("claude-3-5-sonnet"):
        return {
            "type": "text_editor_20241022", 
            "name": "str_replace_editor",
        }
```

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748623832%2F06_-_012_-_The_Text_Edit_Tool_12.1748623832064.png)

After Claude sees this small schema, it automatically expands it behind the scenes into the full text editor tool specification.

## A Practical Example

Let's see the text editor tool in action. When you ask Claude to work with files, it will use the tool to read, modify, and create files as needed.

For example, if you ask Claude to "open the ./main.py file and summarize its contents," Claude will:

1.  Use the text editor tool to view the file
2.  Read the contents
3.  Provide you with a summary

You can go a step further and ask Claude to modify a file. For example: "Open the ./main.py file and write a function to calculate pi to the 5th decimal place. Then create a ./test.py file to test your implementation."

Claude will:

1.  View the existing `main.py` file
2.  Replace its contents with a new implementation containing the pi calculation function
3.  Create a new `test.py` file with appropriate unit tests

### Why Use the Text Editor Tool?

You might wonder why you'd need this tool when modern code editors already have AI assistants built in. The text editor tool becomes valuable in scenarios like:

- You're building applications that need to edit files programmatically
- You're working in an environment without a full-featured code editor
- You want to integrate file editing capabilities directly into a Claude-powered application

In essence, the text editor tool lets you replicate many of the features of advanced AI code editors within your own application, giving you fine-grained control over how Claude interacts with your file system.

#### Video Transcript

As we saw earlier in this module, normally, as developers, we write all the different tools we want to pass to Claude. But there's one tool that Claude has access to by default. This tool is called the Text Editor tool, and it's built directly into Claude. This tool gives Claude all sorts of capabilities related to nearly everything you can do in a standard text editor.

For example, this tool lets Claude open a file or directory and read its contents. It can view a specific range of text in a file. It can add or replace text in a file. It can create new files.

It can perform undo operations. Basically, everything you can do in an ordinary text editor, it can do. As a result, this greatly expands Claude's capabilities, almost giving Claude the ability to act as a software engineer right from the start. Now, understanding the text editor tool is a little complicated, so I want to walk you through a few diagrams to clarify what this tool does for you, and what you and I need to do to actually use it in a project.

The first thing to understand is that only the JSON schema part is actually built into Claude. Let me clarify what I mean. Remember, when we want to use a tool, we actually need to write two separate things. First, on the left, we have to write out that JSON schema specification.

This is provided to Claude, telling Claude about a certain tool it can use and all the different arguments that tool requires. Then on the right, you and I have to write a tool function implementation to pair with that JSON schema. These are the actual functions implemented in our codebase that get called at some point when Claude wants to use our tool. So we actually need to write both sides.

We do both the JSON schema and the tool function implementation. So when we use the text editor tool, the only thing actually provided for us or built into Claude is the JSON schema. That set of instructions tells Claude how to use this tool. You have to provide an actual implementation to handle all of Claude's requests to use the text editor tool.

That implementation doesn't exist. It's something you have to write in our codebase. In other words, whenever Claude decides to, say, create a new file, it sends us back a `tool_use` section saying "I want to create a new file," and we must provide an actual function that actually creates a new file somewhere on our hard drive. So using the text editor tool isn't "free."

It requires some effort on our part, because we have to write several different functions. Now, let's go to a Jupyter notebook, where we'll demonstrate the tool's usage and see how to write these different functions. Back here, I'm in a new notebook called `005 Text Editor Tool`. As usual, you can find this notebook in the attachments for this lecture.

In this notebook, there's a lot of the same exact helper code we've been using so far. But if you look at the third cell, which has a comment at the top "implementation of the text editor tool," you'll see there's a large amount of code inside. This is a class I put together in advance. It contains all the different functions needed to use the text editor tool.

In other words, this class provides this piece of the puzzle. Remember, I just told you that we have to write some code to handle all of Claude's requests to use the text editor tool. I've written that code for you in this particular class. In this class, you'll notice, if you scroll down a bit, there are some methods, like `view`, that can be used to view the contents of a file or directory.

There's also a `string_replace` function, which will replace a string in a file. There's also a function to create a file, and so on. So everything is already provided for you in this class. I'll collapse this cell.

The next thing I want to point out is the cell with the comment "make the text edit schema based upon the model version being used." Now, things get a little complicated here. So let me show you a diagram very quickly to help you understand what's happening here. Now, I've repeated several times that the schema for this text editor tool is already built into Claude, and we don't need to include it.

This is basically true, but there's a small caveat here too, just to make things a little confusing. When we make a request to Claude, and we want to use this text editor tool, we do need to include a very, very small schema. So we need to send a schema that looks like this. The exact type string we put in here, notice it has a date, will change based on the exact version of Claude we're using.

So in this function, I'm checking whether you're using Claude 3.7 Sonnet. If so, I'll return that schema with that date. If you're using Claude 3.5, its date will be slightly different. So depending on the exact model version, your date will be slightly different.

When we send this very small schema to Claude, it automatically expands into a much larger schema. It looks something like this. So Claude will see that we included this very small stub schema, whose name is `string_replace_editor`. It will also notice the exact type we put in there.

Then behind the scenes, we can imagine this very small schema being replaced with this much larger schema. It lays out a large amount of information for Claude about how to use the text editor tool. Now that we have a slightly better understanding of what's happening here, I want to give you a quick demo of what the text editor tool can do. In the same directory as my notebook, I'll create a new file called `main.py`.

Inside, I'll create a very simple function called `greeting`. It will print out `hi there`, like this. Then I'll make sure I save that file. Then I'll go back to my notebook, and at the very bottom, I've added a cell that will add an empty user message and then send the message list to `run_conversation`.

I'll ask Claude to open the `main.py` file and tell me what's inside it. So I'll ask Claude to open the `./main.py` file and summarize its contents. Then I'll run it. Then in the response, we can see that Claude did indeed get the contents of that file.

It will be a summary of what's inside the file. If we look at the message list, we'll see there's a `tool_use` block here, where Claude requests to view the contents of the `main.py` file. The code in that text editor tool class I just showed you will receive that command. It will automatically open the file and then send the contents back to Claude.

We can actually see the contents here. Now, at this point, you might be a little curious about why the text edit tool even exists. In other words, what exactly does it do for us? Beyond the obvious fact that it can somehow work with files on the file system, what functionality does it really provide?

Well, chances are you're currently using a code editor with a very advanced AI assistant built in. You can ask that assistant to refactor files, create files, or do anything else. It turns out we can largely replicate all the features of your advanced code editor by using this text edit tool. So, for example, I'll update the prompt I send to Claude.

I'll ask Claude to open that file and write a function to calculate pi to the fifth decimal place. After it's done, I'll ask Claude to create a `./test.py` file to test your implementation. I'll run this.

After the request completes, I'll scroll down to the list of exchanged messages. So here, I have my initial user message, then our assistant message, in which Claude decides to use the text edit tool, specifically wanting to try to view a file. Then we send back the contents of that file. Claude then says, okay, great.

I know what's in this file. I'm now going to try to replace its contents with some new content. So we can see here is the actual implementation for calculating pi. Then if we look a bit further down, we respond saying we successfully updated the file.

Claude then tries to create the `test.py` file and write some text in it, specifically some tests to verify the implementation it just finished. We can verify that all this happened by looking at the newly updated `main.py` file. So we'll now see an implementation for calculating pi, along with an accompanying `test.py` file with some tests in it. So again, using this tool, we can very easily emulate a fairly advanced code editor.

You might be thinking, okay, why don't I just use my code editor? Well, in some scenarios of various applications you might work on, you may want to edit some files in some file system, or something like that, and you don't really have access to a native, full-featured code editor. That would be a scenario where you'd want to use the text edit tool.
