### 57. Extended thinking

> Type: Video | Duration: 7:01 | Video: 08 - 001 - Extended Thinking.mp4

Important note: Extended Thinking is incompatible with certain other features, particularly message pre-filling and the temperature parameter. Please see the full list of restrictions here: https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/extended-thinking#feature-compatibility

Extended thinking is an advanced reasoning feature of Claude that gives the model time to work through complex problems before generating its final response. Think of it as Claude's "scratchpad"—you can see the reasoning process used to arrive at the answer, which helps improve transparency and generally leads to higher-quality responses.

## How Extended Thinking Works

When extended thinking is enabled, Claude's response changes from a simple text block into a structured response with two parts:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542429%2F08_-_001_-_Extended_Thinking_04.1748542429342.jpg)

With thinking enabled, you get both the reasoning process and the final answer:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542429%2F08_-_001_-_Extended_Thinking_05.1748542429732.jpg)

The main benefits include:

-   Better reasoning ability on complex tasks
-   Higher accuracy on difficult problems
-   Transparency into Claude's thinking process

However, there are also important trade-offs:

-   Higher cost (you pay for the tokens produced by the thinking process)
-   Longer latency (thinking takes time)
-   More complex handling of the response in your code

## When to Use Extended Thinking

The decision is simple: use your prompt evaluations. First run your prompt without thinking enabled, and if you've already optimized your prompt but accuracy still isn't meeting your requirements, then consider enabling extended thinking. It's a tool to reach for when a standard prompt isn't enough to solve the problem.

## Response Structure and Security

The extended thinking response includes a special signature system to ensure security:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542430%2F08_-_001_-_Extended_Thinking_06.1748542430208.jpg)

The signature is a cryptographic token that ensures you have not modified the thinking text. This prevents developers from tampering with Claude's reasoning process, since doing so could steer the model in an unsafe direction.

## Redacted Thinking

Sometimes you'll receive a redacted thinking block instead of readable reasoning text:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542430%2F08_-_001_-_Extended_Thinking_08.1748542430775.jpg)

This happens when Claude's thinking process is flagged by an internal safety system. The redacted content includes the actual thinking process in encrypted form, allowing you to pass the full message back to Claude in future conversations without losing context.

## Implementation

To enable extended thinking in your code, you need to add two parameters to the chat function:

```
def chat(
    messages,
    system=None,
    temperature=1.0,
    stop_sequences=[],
    tools=None,
    thinking=False,
    thinking_budget=1024
):
```

`thinking_budget` sets the maximum number of tokens Claude can use for reasoning. The minimum value is 1024 tokens, and your `max_tokens` parameter must be greater than your `thinking_budget`.

Add the thinking configuration to your API parameters:

```
if thinking:
    params["thinking"] = {
        "type": "enabled",
        "budget": thinking_budget
    }
```

Then call it with thinking enabled:

```
chat(messages, thinking=True)
```

## Testing Redacted Responses

For testing purposes, you can force Claude to return a redacted thinking block by sending a special trigger string. This helps you ensure that your application handles redacted responses gracefully without crashing.

Extended thinking is a powerful feature for when you need Claude to handle complex reasoning tasks, but use it carefully given the cost and latency implications. Start with a standard prompt, optimize it thoroughly, and then add thinking when you need the extra reasoning ability.

#### Video Transcript

Let's study a more advanced feature of Claude called extended thinking. Extended thinking gives Claude time to reason about a user's query before generating the final response. In many chat UIs, this will appear as a separate thinking process that users can optionally view, to better understand how Claude is approaching their question. Now in general, enabling extended thinking will allow Claude to handle more complex tasks with greater accuracy, but there are some significant trade-offs to this.

You have to pay for the tokens Claude generates during the thinking phase, and this phase itself takes some time to complete. So the boost in intelligence comes with an increase in cost, and latency increases too. Now, a common question about extended thinking is deciding when to enable it. The answer is actually simple.

You'll rely on your prompt evaluations. So you'll write a prompt and evaluate it. If the accuracy doesn't meet your requirements and you've already put a fair amount of effort into improving your prompt, then that's when you should consider enabling extended thinking.

Using extended thinking is fairly straightforward. Remember, when we typically use Claude, we send a user message that might contain text blocks. Then we receive an assistant message that also contains text blocks. When we start enabling extended thinking, the response we get will contain a new block type we haven't seen before, called a thinking block.

Inside this thinking block will be the text Claude generated as it thought. There's one very interesting thing about this thinking block that I want to tell you right away, because once you start writing some code and making requests with extended thinking enabled, you'll see it quickly. On the right, I have an example of an assistant message containing a thinking block and a text block. Inside the thinking block, you'll notice there's something called a signature.

The signature is a cryptographic token, and its purpose is this. If you want to send this message back to Claude as part of a conversation in the future, Claude wants to make sure you haven't modified the text in the thinking block in any way. The signature is used to ensure you haven't changed the text. Claude absolutely does not want you to change that text, because it was heavily relied upon when generating the response.

If developers were allowed to modify that text, they could steer Claude in an unsafe direction. There's another aspect related to thinking. In some cases, you may get a thinking block that only has a `redacted` content field without any thinking text. This happens when some of the thinking text Claude generated is flagged by an internal safety system.

The redacted content is the actual thinking text, but in a fully encrypted form. It's provided to you so that you can return this full message to Claude in the future as part of a conversation, and Claude won't lose any context about its previous thinking. To really understand extended thinking, we need to write a bit of code. So let's go back to our Jupyter notebooks.

I've created a new notebook called 001_thinking. It again has much of the same code we've been using throughout this entire course, but I've added one or two very special things. So I encourage you to make sure you download this notebook. It's attached to this lecture.

To enable thinking, we need to find our `chat` function. We'll provide some additional arguments to the `chat` function, which ultimately get added to this `params` object. So I'll add `thinking`, with a default value of `false`, and then `thinking_budget`, with a value of `1024`.

`thinking_budget` is the number of tokens we want to allow Claude to use when generating the thinking portion of the response. The minimum here is 1024, so our `thinking_budget` cannot be less than 1024. It's entirely possible that Claude won't spend 1024 tokens on thinking, but again, this is the lowest budget we can specify. There's another very important fact about `thinking_budget` you need to understand.

That is, `max_tokens` must be greater than your `thinking_budget`. So, for example, if our `thinking_budget` is 1024, `max_tokens` must be at least 1025. And that would only leave a single token to actually apply toward generating some text. So usually you'll want the value of `max_tokens` to be much larger than your `thinking_budget`.

So in my case, I'll bump `max_tokens` here up to 4000. Now I have a fairly large buffer. This means I can generate a response with 1000 tokens allocated to thinking, and then the remaining 3000 tokens can be allocated to actually generating some text. Once we've added these two keyword arguments, we'll add some additional parameters to the `params` dictionary.

So scrolling down a bit, right here. I'll say, if `thinking` is enabled, then I want to add a new key to my `params` dictionary, specifically `thinking`. This will be a nested dictionary whose `type` is `enabled` and whose `budget` tokens is whatever value we passed in as `thinking_budget`, like this. There we go.

So I'll run this cell, and then I'll scroll to the bottom so we can test it out. I'll ask Claude to write a one-paragraph guide on recursion. Then I'll make sure I update my `chat` function call here, enabling thinking by passing in `thinking=True`. Then I run it, and let's see how we did.

Here's our response. So in the response, I got two separate blocks. First, I have my thinking block, and then a little further down, right there, is the beginning of my text block. Inside the thinking block, I do have a signature.

Along with it, I have my thinking text. Remember, the purpose of the signature is to ensure we don't tamper with the thinking text. And of course, our text block here contains the actual guide we asked Claude to write. Now the last thing I want to show you is something you might use when you initially build and test your application.

As a reminder, in some cases, Claude may send back a redacted thinking block. As you build your application, you may want to ensure your code works correctly when it receives a redacted thinking block. So we can actually force Claude to send us back a redacted thinking block. All we have to do is send a message containing a very, very specially formatted string.

So if you scroll to the very top of the second cell here, you'll notice I put in a `thinking_test_string`. And its value is Anthropic, magic string triggered redacted thinking, followed by a bunch of special numbers and letters. If you send this exact string to Claude, you're guaranteed to get a redacted thinking block. Again, we're only doing this for testing purposes, to make sure we can handle that kind of response.

So let me show you very quickly. I'll go back to the cell at the very bottom. I'll add a user message containing only that `thinking_test_string`. Then I send this.

Now we should get a response with a redacted thinking block in it. There it is. So I got my redacted thinking block, which has nothing but a data field and a type of `redacted_thinking`. So now, like I said, we can use this to make sure our application doesn't crash when it receives this kind of unexpected thinking block response.

---

### 58. Image support

> Type: Video | Duration: 10:16 | Video: 08 - 002 - Image Support.mp4

Claude's vision capabilities let you include images in your messages and ask Claude to analyze them in countless ways. You can ask Claude to describe what's in an image, compare multiple images, count objects, or perform complex visual analysis tasks.

## Image Processing Basics

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542455%2F08_-_002_-_Image_Support_01.1748542455758.jpg)

There are several important limitations to keep in mind when working with images:

-   A maximum of 100 images can be included across all messages in a single request
-   Each image can be at most 5MB
-   When sending a single image: maximum height/width is 8000px
-   When sending multiple images: maximum height/width is 2000px
-   Images can be included as base64-encoded data or as an image URL
-   Each image is counted in tokens based on its dimensions: `tokens = (width px × height px) / 750`

To send an image to Claude, you need to include an image block and a text block in your user message. The structure looks like this:

```
with open("image.png", "rb") as f:
    image_bytes = base64.standard_b64encode(f.read()).decode("utf-8")

add_user_message(messages, [
    # Image Block
    {
        "type": "image",
        "source": {
            "type": "base64",
            "media_type": "image/png",
            "data": image_bytes,
        }
    },
    # Text Block
    {
        "type": "text",
        "text": "What do you see in this image?"
    }
])
```

## Message Flow

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542456%2F08_-_002_-_Image_Support_02.1748542456229.jpg)

The conversation process works just like a plain-text interaction. Your server sends Claude a user message containing image and text blocks, and Claude responds with a text block containing its analysis.

## Prompting Tips

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542457%2F08_-_002_-_Image_Support_04.1748542456885.jpg)

To get good results from images, the key is to apply the same prompt engineering techniques you would use when working with text. Simple prompts often lead to poor results. For example, asking "How many marbles are in this image?" might return an incorrect count.

You can significantly improve Claude's accuracy by:

-   Providing detailed guidance and analysis steps
-   Using one-shot or multi-shot examples
-   Breaking complex tasks into smaller steps

### Step-by-Step Analysis

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542457%2F08_-_002_-_Image_Support_05.1748542457338.jpg)

Instead of just asking a simple question, give Claude a methodology:

```
Please analyze this marble image and determine the exact count using the following method:
1. First, identify one distinct marble at a time. Number each marble as you identify it.
2. Verify your result by counting using a different method. Start from the bottom-left corner and work row by row from left to right.

What is the exact, verified number of marbles in this image?
```

### One-Shot Example

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542458%2F08_-_002_-_Image_Support_07.1748542457815.jpg)

You can also improve accuracy by providing an example in the message. Include an image with a known count, state the correct answer, and then ask about your target image. This gives Claude a reference point for the kind of analysis you want.

## Real-World Case: Fire Risk Assessment

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542458%2F08_-_002_-_Image_Support_08.1748542458276.jpg)

Here's a practical application: automating fire risk assessment for home insurance. Rather than sending an inspector to every property, an insurance company can leverage satellite imagery and Claude's analysis capabilities.

The system analyzes satellite imagery to identify:

- Dense, tightly packed trees near the residence
- Access routes that are difficult for emergency service vehicles to reach
- Tree branches overhanging the residence

Compared to a simple prompt like "Provide a fire risk score," a well-structured prompt breaks the analysis into specific steps:

```
Analyze the attached satellite image of a property with these specific steps:

1. Residence identification: Locate the primary residence on the property by looking for:
   - The largest roofed structure
   - Typical residential features (driveway connection, regular geometry)
   - Distinction from other structures (garages, sheds, pools)

2. Tree overhang analysis: Examine all trees near the primary residence:
   - Identify any trees whose canopy extends directly over any portion of the roof
   - Estimate the percentage of roof covered by overhanging branches (0-25%, 25-50%, 50-75%, 75%+)
   - Note particularly dense areas of overhang

3. Fire risk assessment: For any overhanging trees, evaluate:
   - Potential wildfire vulnerability (ember catch points, continuous fuel paths to structure)
   - Proximity to chimneys, vents, or other roof openings if visible
   - Areas where branches create a "bridge" between wildland vegetation and the structure

4. Defensible space identification: Assess the property's overall vegetative structure:
   - Identify if trees connect to form a continuous canopy over or near the home
   - Note any obvious fuel ladders (vegetation that can carry fire from ground to tree to roof)

5. Fire risk rating: Based on your analysis, assign a Fire Risk Rating from 1-4:
   - Rating 1 (Low Risk): No tree branches overhanging the roof, good defensible space around the home
   - Rating 2 (Moderate Risk): Minimal overhang (<25% of roof), some separation between tree canopies
   - Rating 3 (High Risk): Significant overhang (25-50% of roof), connected tree canopies, multiple vulnerability points
   - Rating 4 (Severe Risk): Extensive overhang (>50% of roof), dense vegetation against structure

For each item above (1-5), write one sentence summarizing your findings, with your final response being the numerical rating.
```

This detailed prompt guides Claude through a systematic analysis, producing a more accurate and useful assessment than a simple request would.

Remember: the prompting techniques that work for text apply equally to images. If you want reliable results, take the time to craft a detailed, structured prompt rather than relying on a simple question.

Inside this image block, we can attach the raw image data, as shown in this diagram on the right, or we can provide an image URL hosted somewhere on the web. Whenever engineers start using Claude with images, I often notice they use very simple prompts, even somewhat like the prompt in my example here. Here's how it works. In my user message, I can alternate between image parts and text parts. So in this case, I have an image part above and a text part below, then another image, then another text part. In the initial pair, I provided an image with 11 marbles, and then very plainly stated, the image above has 11 marbles in it.

Providing an example like this can easily improve Claude's accuracy when it later processes your image. As usual, I want to test this feature in a Jupyter notebook. But this time, our example will be a bit more complex. I want you to get a sense in advance of the scenario we'll walk through in the notebook, through a brief illustration.

Okay, here's an example use case for how we might use Claude's image support feature. In case you don't know, in many parts of the United States, we have a very serious wildfire problem, where a wildfire, once it starts, sweeps through an area and burns down a large number of homes. Because this is a very common risk, many people want to buy fire insurance in case their home burns down. But these insurance companies are very well aware that a house could entirely possibly burn down tomorrow, next year, or very soon.
