### 59. PDF Support

> Type: Video | Duration: 1:29 | Video: 08 - 003 - PDF Support.mp4

Claude can read and analyze PDF files directly, making it a powerful document processing tool. This feature works similarly to image processing, but there are a few key differences in how you structure your code.

## Setting Up PDF Processing

To process PDF files with Claude, you'll use almost exactly the same code as for processing images. The main differences are the file type specification and variable names for clarity.

Here's how to modify your existing image processing code to handle a PDF:

```
with open("earth.pdf", "rb") as f:
    file_bytes = base64.standard_b64encode(f.read()).decode("utf-8")

messages = []

add_user_message(
    messages,
    [
        {
            "type": "document",
            "source": {
                "type": "base64",
                "media_type": "application/pdf",
                "data": file_bytes,
            },
        },
        {"type": "text", "text": "Summarize the document in one sentence"},
    ],
)

chat(messages)
```

## Key Differences from Image Processing

When adapting image processing code to handle PDFs, you need to update several elements:

- Change the file extension from .png to .pdf
- Update the variable name from image_bytes to file_bytes for clarity
- Set the type to "document" instead of "image"
- Change the media type to "application/pdf" instead of "image/png"

## What Claude Can Extract from PDFs

Claude's PDF processing capabilities go beyond simple text extraction. It can analyze and understand:

- The text content of the entire document
- Images and charts embedded in the PDF
- Tables and their data relationships
- Document structure and formatting

This essentially makes Claude a one-stop solution for extracting any kind of information from PDF documents, whether you need a summary, data analysis, or specific content extraction.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542484%2F08_-_003_-_PDF_Support_02.1748542484779.jpg)

The image above shows Claude successfully processing a Wikipedia article about Earth saved as a PDF, demonstrating how it can understand and summarize complex document content in a single sentence.

#### Video Transcript

In addition to images, Claude can also read content directly from PDF files. I'll show you how in this video. This video comes with a document called earth.pdf. If you open it, you'll see it's just a few pages from the Wikipedia article about Earth.

So make sure you download this PDF file and place it in the same directory as your notebook. To read a PDF file, we use almost exactly the same code as reading an image and feeding it to Claude. So I'll find where we currently open the image, and then I'll change it to earth.pdf. I'll change this variable name from image bytes to file bytes, since we're no longer reading an image.

I'll make sure to also update the variable name below. I'll change the type here from image to document, and then the media type will go from image/png to application/pdf. Finally, when we ask Claude a question about this document, instead of typing in that long prompt from the previous cell, I'll ask Claude to summarize the document in one sentence. Let's see what Claude returns.

So I'll run this. Here's the summary. It looks like it successfully read the contents of that PDF file. Now, Claude can not only read text from a PDF, it can also read images or charts, tables, and so on.

So you should think of Claude as a one-stop shop for extracting just about any kind of information from a PDF document.

---

### 60. Citations

> Type: Video | Duration: 5:19 | Video: 08 - 004 - Citations.mp4

When Claude answers a question based on a document you provided, users might assume it's just using its training data. But what if Claude could explicitly point to where it found specific information? That's what the citations feature does—a powerful feature that lets Claude reference specific parts of your source documents and show users exactly where each piece of information comes from.

## Why Citations Matter

Imagine you ask Claude how Earth's atmosphere formed and get a detailed answer. Without citations, users can't verify the information, nor can they understand that Claude is actually citing a specific document you provided. Citations solve this transparency problem by establishing a clear traceable path between Claude's response and the source material you provided.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542499%2F08_-_004_-_Citations_00.1748542499688.jpg)

## Enabling Citations

To enable citations, you need to modify your document message structure. Add two new fields to your document block:

```
{
    "type": "document",
    "source": {
        "type": "base64",
        "media_type": "application/pdf",
        "data": file_bytes,
    },
    "title": "earth.pdf",
    "citations": { "enabled": True }
}
```

The `title` field provides a readable name for your document, while `citations: {"enabled": True}` tells Claude to track where it found information.

## Understanding the Citation Structure

With citations enabled, Claude's response becomes more complex. Instead of simple text, you get structured data containing citation information for each claim.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542500%2F08_-_004_-_Citations_08.1748542500257.jpg)

Each citation contains several key pieces of information:

- **cited_text** - the exact text from the source document that supports Claude's statement
- **document_index** - which document Claude cited (useful when you provide multiple documents)
- **document_title** - the title you specified for the document
- **start_page_number** - the page number where the cited text begins
- **end_page_number** - the page number where the cited text ends

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542500%2F08_-_004_-_Citations_09.1748542500755.jpg)

## Building a User Interface with Citations

The real power of citations lies in building user interfaces that make this information easily accessible. You can create interactive elements where users hover over a citation marker to see the specific source of the information.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542501%2F08_-_004_-_Citations_11.1748542501163.jpg)

This creates a transparent experience where users can:

- See that Claude's answer is based on real source material
- Verify the information by examining the original document
- Understand the context surrounding each cited piece of information

## Using Citations with Plain Text

Citations aren't limited to PDF documents. You can also use them with plain-text sources. When working with text, modify your document structure like this:

```
{
    "type": "document", 
    "source": {
        "type": "text",
        "media_type": "text/plain",
        "data": article_text,
    },
    "title": "earth_article",
    "citations": { "enabled": True }
}
```

For plain-text sources, you'll get character positions instead of page numbers, pinpointing exactly where in the text Claude found each piece of information.

## When to Use Citations

Citations are especially valuable in the following situations:

- Users need to verify the accuracy of information
- You're working with authoritative documents and want users to be able to reference them
- Transparency about information sources is critical for your application
- Users may want to explore the broader context of a specific fact

By implementing citations, you transform Claude from a "black box" that provides answers into a transparent research assistant that shows its work. This builds user trust and enables them to dig deeper into your source material when needed.

#### Video Transcript

In the PDF file we just worked with, I'll scroll to the last page. At the bottom, you'll notice it mentions that Earth's atmosphere and oceans formed through volcanic activity and outgassing. Now, as a small exercise, I want to try asking Claude a very simple question. I want to ask it how Earth's atmosphere and oceans formed.

I'd roughly expect to see an answer like "volcanic activity and outgassing." So, I'll copy this text, then put it into my prompt and ask Claude. How did Earth's atmosphere and oceans form. Now, I'll run this quickly.

At least for me, I notice that in the first sentence, I got a very appropriate answer. I'm told these were formed through volcanic activity and outgassing. So that's exactly what I expected. But I want you to imagine getting this answer from the user's perspective.

When a user sees this generated text, they might think Claude just said it directly from memory. The user may not really understand that we're actually citing some kind of source. In this case, the source isn't perfect. It's Wikipedia, but at least it's something.

So it would be great if there were some way to inform the user, or tell them how we got this information. Fortunately, Claude has a feature called Citations. Citations allow Claude to directly cite some external information source and indicate that it got its answer by looking at some other document or other text source. Now, let me demonstrate how citations work, because once you see it in action, I think you'll get a good sense of what it does.

I'll scroll up to our prompt here, then make a small modification to the first block we put in this message. After the `source` field, I'll add a `title` of `earth.pdf`, since that's the name of the PDF file we're opening, and a `citations` field, which will be a dictionary containing `enabled: true`, like this. Now I'll send this request again. Let's see what we get now.

Now we'll see our response is much more complex than before. Our `content` field is now a list containing some text blocks, and some of those text blocks have a `citations` list with something called `citation_page_location` in it. So, let's take a moment to focus on what `citation_page_location` actually is. Let me show you a diagram.

`citation_page_location` is exactly how Claude tells us where it got a certain fact or piece of information. In our case, we get a structure containing `cited_text`, `document_index`, `document_title`, `start_page`, and `end_page`. `cited_text` is the text from the source document (in our case, `earth.pdf`) that somehow supports Claude's statement. `document_index` and `document_title` tell us exactly where this statement came from, while `start_page` and `end_page` tell us the exact location of the statement in that document.

Now, the real intent of giving you these citations is to let you build a user interface like this based on Claude's response. So I took the response we just got from Claude. I fed it back into Claude and asked it to present the entire response as a beautifully formatted document, and to give me some popups to represent all the different citations. But now if I hover over 1, 2, or 3, I'll see a nicely formatted popup appear.

This popup contains all the information from that `citation_page_location` object. It's designed to inform the user that Claude's response, specifically this sentence, was actually inspired by some external document. So in this case, this sentence comes from `earth.pdf`, specifically some text on pages 4 to 5, and the actual text we cited is "Earth's atmosphere is, etc., etc." So this entire citations feature lets you build interfaces like this, where users can be confident that the information Claude provides comes from some actual external source.

Users can then go reference that source and make sure Claude correctly interpreted the information in that external document. This citations feature isn't limited to use with PDF documents. You can also use it with plain text. As a very simple example, in the cell above, I manually copied and pasted some text from the PDF document and assigned it to a variable called `article_text`.

Now I can go back down to where I make the request, and I'm going to make a big update to this block. I'll keep the `document` type. I'll keep the `source`, but I'll change the `type` to `text`. I'll change the `media_type` to `text/plain`, and then `data`.

Change to `article`. That's the text. Oh, sorry, it's `article_text`. There we go.

That's the text I assigned to that variable. Then I'll change the title to something like `earth_article`, since it's no longer directly a PDF file. Then I can keep the `enabled: true` in `citations`. So now if I run this again and look at the response, we'll see that instead of `citation_page_location`, it's `citation_chart_location`.

So this will give us a position within that large block of text Claude is citing. We can now use this to build an interface very similar to the one I just showed in the browser. So again, you can cite from plain text or PDF documents. Either way, I really recommend you always use the citations feature, just make sure users can somehow investigate how Claude constructed its response, and ensure Claude is pulling information from some source document, whether PDF or plain text. This is critically important.

---

### 61. Prompt caching

> Type: Video | Duration: 3:35 | Video: 08 - 005 - Prompt Caching.mp4

Prompt caching is a feature that speeds up Claude's responses and lowers the cost of text generation by reusing computational work from previous requests. Instead of discarding all of its processing work after each request, Claude saves and reuses it when you send similar content again.

## How Claude Normally Processes a Request

To understand prompt caching, let's first look at what happens during a typical request without caching enabled.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542537%2F08_-_005_-_Prompt_Caching_01.1748542536808.jpg)

When you send a message to Claude, it doesn't immediately start generating a response. Instead, Claude does a lot of preprocessing work on your input:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542537%2F08_-_005_-_Prompt_Caching_04.1748542537345.jpg)

- Tokenizing the prompt into smaller pieces
- Creating embeddings for each token
- Adding context based on the surrounding text
- Only then generating the actual output text

After sending you the response, Claude discards all of this computational work—the tokenization, embeddings, and context analysis are all thrown away.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542537%2F08_-_005_-_Prompt_Caching_07.1748542537838.jpg)

## The Problem with Discarding Work

This becomes inefficient when you make follow-up requests that contain the same content. For example, in a conversation where you ask Claude to refine a summary of the same long text:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542538%2F08_-_005_-_Prompt_Caching_09.1748542538396.jpg)

Claude has to repeat all the same preprocessing work on content it just analyzed. As Claude might think to itself: "I just processed that message and threw away all the work I did—I could have reused it!"

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542539%2F08_-_005_-_Prompt_Caching_11.1748542538911.jpg)

## How Prompt Caching Solves This

Prompt caching changes this workflow by saving the preprocessing work instead of discarding it:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542539%2F08_-_005_-_Prompt_Caching_15.1748542539641.jpg)

When you make the initial request, Claude does all the usual preprocessing, but stores the results in a cache instead of discarding them. The cache is like a lookup table that says, "If I see this message again, I'll reuse the work I've already done."

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542540%2F08_-_005_-_Prompt_Caching_17.1748542540035.jpg)

## Main Benefits and Limitations

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542540%2F08_-_005_-_Prompt_Caching_19.1748542540458.jpg)

Prompt caching offers several advantages:

- **Faster responses:** requests using cached content execute faster
- **Lower cost:** you pay less for the cached portion of a request
- **Automatic optimization:** the initial request writes to the cache, and subsequent requests read from it

However, there are some important limitations to keep in mind:

- **Cache duration:** cached content only lives for one hour
- **Limited use cases:** it's only effective when you repeatedly send the same content
- **High frequency requirement:** it's most effective when the same content appears extremely frequently in your requests

Prompt caching is best suited for scenarios like document analysis workflows, where you ask multiple questions about the same large document, or iterative editing tasks, where the base content stays the same and you're only refining specific aspects.

#### Video Transcript

The next feature we're going to focus on is prompt caching. Prompt caching is used to speed up Claude's responses and lower the cost of text generation. To help you understand how prompt caching works, we'll walk through what happens inside Claude when we make a typical request without any prompt caching feature. So, this is just a normal request.

We've already touched a little on the whole flow of a normal request. But don't worry, this time I'll go into detail about what happens inside Claude. As usual, everything starts with us sending a message to Claude. When Claude receives this message, before actually generating any output text, it does a lot of processing work on the input message.

In other words, Claude internally creates a lot of internal data structures and does a lot of computation on just the input text. Then it ultimately uses all the work it did earlier to generate the output text, and then sends the response back to us as some assistant message. After the response is sent to us, Claude throws all the output text and all the results of the earlier computations done on the input message into the trash. Everything vanishes, and we see all that work disappear into nothing.

Once Claude has finished all the cleanup, it announces to the world: I'm ready to handle the next request. Now, let's imagine that after making this initial request, we make a follow-up request. In this follow-up request, let's assume we're continuing this conversation. So we'll attach a series of messages.

The first will be the exact same message we just sent, then the assistant message response we received, then some new user message we attach to continue the conversation. So we'll send all of these messages to Claude. Internally, when Claude sees the first message, it might be a bit frustrated. Because Claude will see that message and think, of course, this isn't what actually happens.

We can imagine this is what happens behind the scenes. Claude will see that message and say, I just saw this message. I just did so much work to process it. Then I threw all the computation results away.

Claude would think, I really wish I could reuse all the work I did 10 seconds ago and then threw away. If Claude had saved the work it just threw away, it could return a response to us faster, because it wouldn't need to repeat all that work. So, now that we've seen this problem, let's think about some possible ways to solve it. Well, here's one possible way to handle it.

Maybe we could say that whenever we make an initial request to Claude, and after Claude has done all the initial processing work on the user message we sent, instead of throwing all the analysis results into the trash, we could cache all that work, or put it into some temporary data store. Then, if we make a follow-up request later and include the exact same input user message, Claude could go to its cache and say, hey, I just saw this exact same message, and I saved all the analysis work around that particular message. So, instead of re-analyzing that message again, it can reuse all the work it did before. Hopefully this greatly speeds up the process of generating some text output, because we're reusing some work we've already done.

This idea of saving the work of certain requests for future use is exactly the core of prompt caching. So, later we'll come back and walk through some implementation details of prompt caching, and really understand how Claude implements it.

---

### 62. Rules of Prompt Caching

> Type: Video | Duration: 6:41 | Video: 08 - 006 - Rules of Prompt Caching.mp4

Prompt caching in Claude works by storing the computational work done on your messages, so it can be reused in subsequent requests. This makes subsequent requests execute faster and cost less, but only if you repeatedly send the exact same content.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542546%2F08_-_006_-_Rules_of_Prompt_Caching_00.1748542546287.jpg)

The process is simple: your initial request writes the processing work to the cache, and subsequent requests can read from that cache instead of reprocessing the same content. The cache has a lifetime of one hour, so this feature is only useful when you repeatedly send the same content within that time window.

## Cache Breakpoints

Caching isn't enabled automatically—you need to manually add a cache breakpoint to a specific block in your message. Here's how it works:

- Work done on a message is **not cached automatically**
- You must manually add a "cache breakpoint" to a block
- All work **before** the breakpoint is cached
- The cache is only used when the content up to and including the breakpoint is exactly the same in a subsequent request

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542547%2F08_-_006_-_Rules_of_Prompt_Caching_04.1748542546894.jpg)

To add a cache breakpoint, you need to use the longhand form of writing a text block instead of the shorthand:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542547%2F08_-_006_-_Rules_of_Prompt_Caching_06.1748542547409.jpg)

The shorthand form has nowhere to add a `cache_control` field, so you must use the expanded form and set the `cache_control` field to `{"type": "ephemeral"}`.

## How Cache Breakpoints Work

When you place a cache breakpoint in a message, Claude caches all processing work up to and including that breakpoint. Content after the breakpoint is processed normally and not cached.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542547%2F08_-_006_-_Rules_of_Prompt_Caching_08.1748542547846.jpg)

For the cache to work in a subsequent request, the content up to the breakpoint must be exactly the same. Even a tiny change, like adding the word "please," will invalidate the cache and force Claude to reprocess everything.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542548%2F08_-_006_-_Rules_of_Prompt_Caching_10.1748542548220.jpg)

## Caching Across Messages

Cache breakpoints can span multiple messages and message types. If you place a breakpoint in a later message, all the previous messages (user, assistant, etc.) will be included in the cached content.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542548%2F08_-_006_-_Rules_of_Prompt_Caching_11.1748542548748.jpg)

This is especially useful for conversation scenarios where you want to cache the entire conversation context up to a certain point.

## System Prompts and Tools

You're not limited to text blocks—cache breakpoints can be added to:

- System prompts
- Tool definitions
- Image blocks
- Tool use and tool result blocks

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542549%2F08_-_006_-_Rules_of_Prompt_Caching_13.1748542549124.jpg)

System prompts and tool definitions are excellent candidates for caching, because they rarely change between requests. This is usually where you get the most benefit from prompt caching.

## Caching Order

Behind the scenes, Claude processes the components of your request in a specific order: first tools, then the system prompt, and finally messages. Understanding this order helps you place breakpoints effectively.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542550%2F08_-_006_-_Rules_of_Prompt_Caching_15.1748542549957.jpg)

You can add up to four cache breakpoints total. For example, you might cache your tools, and then add another breakpoint in the middle of your conversation history. This gives you the flexibility to decide what to cache when different parts of the request change.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542550%2F08_-_006_-_Rules_of_Prompt_Caching_17.1748542550452.jpg)

## Minimum Content Length

The cache has a minimum threshold: content must be at least 1024 tokens long to be cached. This is the sum of all messages and blocks you're trying to cache, not a single block.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542550%2F08_-_006_-_Rules_of_Prompt_Caching_19.1748542550839.jpg)

A simple "Hi there!" message won't reach this threshold, but if you duplicate that content 500 times (or have a really long prompt), it'll exceed 1024 tokens and qualify for caching.

The key to using prompt caching effectively is identifying which parts of your request stay consistent across multiple calls, and strategically placing breakpoints to maximize reuse and minimize cache invalidation.

#### Video Transcript

Now that we understand the theory, we're going to explore how prompt caching actually works in Claude. The core idea of prompt caching is exactly the same as what we discussed in the previous video. We'll make an initial request to Claude. Claude will do some processing on that initial message, and then Claude will save all that work to a temporary cache.

Then, if at some future point we make a follow-up request and include the exact same message, instead of processing that message again, Claude will look at the cache, find the work it already saved, and load it. To be clear, the work saved in the cache doesn't last forever. It's only stored there for one hour. You'll find that prompt caching is most useful whenever you repeatedly send the same content to Claude, because it really is a two-stage process.

We have to make that initial request to write some data to the cache, and then only subsequent requests can take advantage of that pre-completed work. This cache system isn't enabled by default in Claude. Instead, to turn on caching, we must manually add a cache breakpoint to a block of one of our different messages. I'm showing an example in this sample user message on the right.

So, we just need to add that little `cache_control` field to turn on caching. There are a lot of rules about what `cache_control` actually does. But before we dive into those rules and really explain what's going on, I want to give you a small tip that will make your life easier. Throughout this course, we've frequently used a shorthand way of writing text blocks.

I'm showing this on the left. This is a user message that defines a text block using the shorthand form. If our user message has only a short bit of text, we can directly assign a string to the `content` field. There's another way to write text blocks, which I'm showing on the right as an alternative.

The other way is to write out the `content` field, assign it a list, and then put a dictionary inside, where this dictionary has a `type` of `text`, and then a `text` field containing your actual text. We haven't really used this longhand form before, but if you want to use `cache_control`, if you want to turn on this prompt caching feature, you must write out these text fields in the longhand form. That way you can actually add that `cache_control` field. In other words, we have to write out `cache_control` somewhere, and on the right, we have a place to put it.

If we use the shorthand form, we have nowhere to put it. When we add a breakpoint to a block, everything in our entire request will be cached, up to and including that breakpoint. So, in this case, if we send a request like this, where the first block has a breakpoint, Claude will do some work to process this text here. This will produce some amount of work.

Because this text block has a breakpoint, this work will be stored in the cache. But the later work is after the breakpoint, so it won't be cached. If we later send a follow-up request, Claude will look at the cache and find that some work has already been done to process this first block. So it will retrieve that work and use it to save some effort.

One thing to keep in mind is that our follow-up request must contain exactly the same content up to that breakpoint. So, for example, if our initial text block with the breakpoint, we just add the word "please" to it. Then this content is no longer the same, so this work won't be used from the cache. Instead, Claude will reprocess the entire block and everything before it.

Cache breakpoints can span multiple different messages and multiple different blocks. So, for example, if we send a user message, then an assistant message, then another user message, and the last message here has a block with a breakpoint, everything will be cached, up to and including this block. So we can imagine that the work done to process all three of these messages will be stored in the cache. Then, when we later make a follow-up request, as long as everything up to and including that breakpoint is the same, the work will be retrieved from the cache.

Again, we'll save some effort. We're not limited to adding cache points on text blocks. We can also add them to almost any other type of block, like image blocks, tool use, or tool results. We can also add them to tool schemas and system prompts.

I'm showing examples of both cases on the right. You'll very likely enable caching for your tools and your system prompt. Because it turns out that for most applications—not all, but for many applications—your system prompt and your tool list end up not changing. So these are excellent places to put cache breakpoints.

Overall, we can apply breakpoints to tool schemas, system prompts, and message blocks. Now, these aren't three separate caching systems. Let me tell you exactly what I mean. Whenever you include tools, a system prompt, and messages, behind the scenes, when they're fed to Claude, they're all concatenated together, and they're concatenated in a specific order.

First the tools, then the system prompt, then your list of messages. So, if you place a cache breakpoint on your last tool, everything up to and including that last tool will be cached. But the system prompt and your message list will not be cached. So, if we then make a follow-up request and we change the assistant message there, that's fine.

We'll still save some work, because the tool list was already cached ahead of time. Finally, I want to quickly mention that we can add multiple different cache breakpoints, up to four. So I might decide to add a cache breakpoint on the last tool schema I pass in, and then maybe I'll add a cache breakpoint on this assistant message below. If I then make a follow-up request and I change the user message below, no problem.

We'll save the work of reprocessing the entire tool list, system prompt, and user message. Similarly, if we change the first user message, then we'll invalidate the cache for everything below it, but we'll still have our cached work for the tool list. So, if appropriate, we'll very likely add multiple different cache breakpoints. We might decide to cache our entire tool list and system prompt, and maybe some messages too.

Exactly where you put these different breakpoints really just depends on your particular application. The last thing I want to share is that the cache has a minimum content length. So, to cache some content, we must cache at least 1024 tokens. So in the top-right example, I set a cache breakpoint on a message with only the text "high there".

This is definitely not 1024 tokens long, so this content won't be written to the cache. But if I duplicate that text block 500 times, now I might have over 1024 tokens, so this entire list of different blocks would be cached.

---

### 63. Prompt Caching in Action

> Type: Video | Duration: 7:21 | Video: 08 - 007 - Prompt Caching in Action.mp4

Prompt caching is a powerful optimization feature that makes your API requests faster and cheaper when you repeatedly send the same content to Claude. Let's explore how to implement it effectively in your application.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542595%2F08_-_007_-_Prompt_Caching_in_Action_19.1748542594923.jpg)

## How Prompt Caching Works

When you enable prompt caching, the first request writes content to a cache that lives for one hour. Subsequent requests can read from this cache instead of processing the same content again. This is especially valuable when you send:

- Large system prompts (like a 6K-token coding assistant prompt)
- Complex tool schemas (multiple tools, around 1.7K tokens)
- Repeated message content

The key point is that caching is only useful when you repeatedly send exactly the same content—but in many applications, this happens very frequently.

## Setting Up Tool Schema Caching

To cache your tool schemas, you need to add a `cache_control` field to the last tool in the list. Here's how to do it correctly, without modifying your original tool definitions:

```
if tools:
    tools_clone = tools.copy()
    last_tool = tools_clone[-1].copy()
    last_tool["cache_control"] = {"type": "ephemeral"}
    tools_clone[-1] = last_tool
    params["tools"] = tools_clone
```

This approach creates a copy of the `tools` list and the last tool schema before adding the `cache_control` field. While you could directly modify `tools[-1]["cache_control"]`, the copying approach prevents problems if you later reorder your tools.

## System Prompt Caching

For system prompts, you need to structure them as a text block with cache control:

```
if system:
    params["system"] = [
        {
            "type": "text",
            "text": system,
            "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}
        }
    ]
```

This converts your system prompt from a simple string into a structured format that supports caching.

## Understanding Cache Behavior

When you run a request with caching enabled, you'll see different usage patterns in the response:

- **First request:** `cache_creation_input_tokens=1772` - Claude writes to the cache
- **Subsequent requests:** `cache_read_input_tokens=1772` - Claude reads from the cache
- **Content changes:** new cache creation tokens appear

The cache is very sensitive—even changing a single character in your tools or system prompt will invalidate that component's entire cache.

## Cache Order and Breakpoints

You can set multiple cache breakpoints in a single request. The order matters:

1. Tools (if provided)
2. System prompt (if provided)
3. Messages

If you change the system prompt but keep the tools the same, you'll see partial cache reads (for the tools) and a cache write (for the new system prompt). This fine-grained caching means you only pay to process the parts that actually changed.

## Practical Considerations

Prompt caching is most effective when:

-   Tool schemas stay consistent across requests
-   System prompts are stable
-   Your application makes multiple requests with similar context

Remember, the cache only lasts one hour, so it's suited for applications with relatively frequent API usage, rather than long-term storage.

#### Video Transcript

It's time to get hands-on with prompt caching. I've created a new notebook called `003 caching`. You can of course find it in the attachments to this lecture. Here, you'll find a section called "a prompt with 6K tokens".

This can serve as a system prompt. We'll use it later. There's also a tool schema section. Several different tool schemas are defined in it.

All these different tool schemas add up to about 1.7K tokens. We're going to update our `chat` function, which is in the cell above named `helper functions`. We want to make sure our `chat` function always enables prompt caching for our tool schemas and system prompt by default. Let me demonstrate how to do that.

In the `helper function` cell, I'll scroll down to our `chat` function. Right here. I'll scroll down a bit more, and you'll notice I added two to-dos. First, if a tool list is provided, I want to always cache this tool list.

Second, if a system prompt is provided, I also want to cache it. Remember, we can have multiple different cache breakpoints in a single request. So if we end up passing both a system prompt and a tool list, we'll set up two different cache breakpoints. Let's first handle caching the tool list.

To do this, remember, we need to modify the last tool schema we pass to Claude. Specifically, we need to add the `cache_control` field. But we can do it this way. We can directly write `tools[-1]`, and then set a `cache_control` field on it, with type `ephemeral`.

Just like this. This will definitely work, but it's not the best coding practice. You see, this will actually modify our tool schema, adding the `cache_control` field to it. In our application, there might be some scenario where we later decide to change the order of the tool schemas we pass in.

If we do that, we might end up setting multiple different cache breakpoints in our tool schemas, which probably isn't what we want. So, a better approach is to first create a copy of our tool list. Then we'll clone the last tool schema in it, and add the `cache_control` field to it. Let me see how to do this.

I'll create a new variable called `tools_clone`, obtained by calling `tools.copy()`. This will create a complete copy of the list. Next, I'll copy the last tool in it. Then I'll add the `cache_control` field to the last tool schema.

So the type will be `ephemeral`. Then I'll overwrite the last element in `tools_clone` with the `tool_schema_copy` I just created. The last element of `tools_clone` will be `tools.clone` at index `-1`. I'll assign my `tools_clone` to `tools`.

Quickly repeating again, all this copying logic of mine here isn't strictly necessary. It's just good practice, in case we decide to change our tool list at some point in the future. Next, we'll handle the second to-do. So if we pass in a system prompt, I want to make sure we always set a cache breakpoint on it.

To do this, I'll remove the comment. I'll replace `system` with a list. We'll put a text block inside here. So it'll be a dictionary with a `type` of `text`, the content of `text` being `system`, and finally a `cache_control` with type `ephemeral`.

That's it. Okay, now let's run this cell. We'll go to the very bottom to test out the caching feature we set up. So down here at the bottom, I've already defined a tool list.

These are the tool schemas defined in the cell above. We also have that very large system prompt here, called `code_prompt`. So, let's first try passing nothing. So, no tool list, and no prompt.

We just want to see how many tokens it takes to process the message "what is 1+1" and generate a response. So if I run this, I'll get an output, and we can notice there's a `usage` field in it. It looks like we input 14 tokens and output 11. Now let's try adding our tool list.

So when I add it and run this, we'll now see a very different `usage` field. Now our usage has a `cache_creation_input_tokens`, with a value of 1700. This means Claude has seen that we want to cache our schemas. So it wrote a total of about 1700 tokens to the cache.

So now if we immediately make a follow-up request without changing anything, we'll see that we'll now read a certain number of tokens from the cache. So now we have a `cache_read` of 1700. This means we've successfully stored our schemas in the cache and retrieved them at a future point. Now, if we change our user message in any way, like deleting that question mark at the end, then re-run, we'll still read from the cache, because remember, the cache order is the tool list, then our system prompt, then our different messages.

So we'll still read from the cache like this. However, if we change any of our tools in any way, we'll invalidate the cache. So if I go to my tool list and change the description of the first tool. I'll remove the `s` from the word `ads`.

So now it's just `add a specified duration`. Now if I re-run the cell, I've changed my tool schema. This means the cache breakpoint we applied to all the different tools no longer applies. If I run the cell at the very bottom again, we'll see an updated `usage` value.

So now we'll do a cache write again. So no longer a read. We're now back to a write, because we sent what looks to Claude like a completely different tool list. Okay, now let's try adding our system prompt.

I'll go to our `chat` function and add `system=code_prompt`. So now when we make this request, remember the cache order is tools, then system prompt, then our message list. Because we're keeping the tool list exactly the same but changing the system prompt, I expect to see both a partial cache read and a cache write. The cache read we'll see is because we're using the same tool list.

And the cache write is because we're setting a new cache breakpoint by sending this new prompt. So I'll run this. Now we should see a, there it is, a cache read of 1700 and a cache write of 6.3K. Now, just like our tool list, if we go to our system prompt and change this prompt in any way, maybe just deleting the word `builder` here at the end, then re-run that cell.

Now, once again, to Claude, if we send another request, this will be a completely different system prompt. So we'll lose all the previous cache data around the system prompt. So now I send it again, and we'll once again see a cache read of about 1.7K, there it is. Then we again write this brand-new system prompt, so that's 6.3K again.

Okay, my friends, that's prompt caching. Again, anytime you send the same content, whether it's the same message list, the same tool schemas, or the same system prompt, you'll use prompt caching very frequently.

---

### 64. Code Execution and the Files API

> Type: Video | Duration: 11:07 | Video: 08 - 008 - Code Execution and the Files API.mp4

The Anthropic API offers two powerful features that work very well together: the Files API and Code Execution. Although they may seem independent at first glance, combining them opens up some very interesting possibilities for delegating complex tasks to Claude.

## Files API

The Files API provides an alternative way to handle file uploads. You can upload files ahead of time and reference them later, without having to encode images or PDFs directly as base64 data in your messages.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542605%2F08_-_008_-_Code_Execution_and_the_Files_API_01.1748542605372.jpg)

Here's how it works:

-   Upload your file (image, PDF, text, etc.) to Claude using a separate API call
-   Receive a file metadata object containing a unique file ID
-   Reference that file ID in future messages instead of including the raw file data

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542606%2F08_-_008_-_Code_Execution_and_the_Files_API_02.1748542606050.jpg)

This approach is especially useful when you want to reference the same file multiple times, or when working with large files that would be cumbersome to include in every request.

## Code Execution Tool

Code execution is a server-based tool that doesn't require you to provide an implementation. You simply include a predefined tool schema in your request, and Claude can optionally execute Python code in an isolated Docker container.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542607%2F08_-_008_-_Code_Execution_and_the_Files_API_04.1748542607054.jpg)

Key characteristics of the code execution environment:

-   Runs in an isolated Docker container
-   No network access (cannot make external API calls)
-   Claude can execute code multiple times within a single conversation
-   Results are captured and interpreted by Claude for the final response

## Combining the Files API and Code Execution

The real power comes from combining these features. Since the Docker container has no network access, the Files API becomes the primary way to move data into and out of the execution environment.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542607%2F08_-_008_-_Code_Execution_and_the_Files_API_06.1748542607578.jpg)

Here's a typical workflow:

1.  Upload your data file (like a CSV) using the Files API
2.  Include a `container_upload` block with the file ID in your message
3.  Ask Claude to analyze the data
4.  Claude writes and executes code to process your file
5.  Claude can generate output (like a chart) for you to download

## Practical Example

Let's look at a real example using streaming service data. This CSV file contains user information, including subscription tier, viewing habits, and whether they have churned (canceled their subscription).

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542608%2F08_-_008_-_Code_Execution_and_the_Files_API_08.1748542608112.jpg)

First, upload the file using a helper function:

```
file_metadata = upload('streaming.csv')
```

Then create a message that includes both the uploaded file and the analysis request:

```
messages = []
add_user_message(
    messages,
    [
        {
            "type": "text",
            "text": """Run a detailed analysis to determine major drivers of churn.
            Your final output should include at least one detailed plot summarizing your findings."""
        },
        {"type": "container_upload", "file_id": file_metadata.id},
    ],
)

chat(
    messages,
    tools=[{"type": "code_execution_20250522", "name": "code_execution"}]
)
```

## Understanding the Response

When Claude uses code execution, the response contains several types of blocks:

-   **Text blocks** - Claude's analysis and explanation
-   **Server tool use blocks** - the actual code Claude decided to run
-   **Code execution tool result blocks** - the output of running the code

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542608%2F08_-_008_-_Code_Execution_and_the_Files_API_13.1748542608585.jpg)

Claude may execute code multiple times within a single response, iteratively building up its analysis. Each execution cycle includes the code and its result.

## Downloading Generated Files

One of the most powerful features is Claude's ability to generate files (like charts or reports) and make them available for download. When Claude creates a visualization, it's stored in the container, and you can download it using the Files API.

Look in the response for blocks of type `code_execution_output`—these blocks contain the file ID of the generated content:

```
download_file("file_id_from_response")
```

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542609%2F08_-_008_-_Code_Execution_and_the_Files_API_18.1748542609010.jpg)

The result is a comprehensive analysis with professional visualizations that would have taken a significant amount of time to code by hand.

## Beyond Data Analysis

While data analysis is a natural fit, the combination of the Files API and code execution opens up many possibilities:

-   Image processing and manipulation
-   Document parsing and conversion
-   Mathematical computation and modeling
-   Generating reports with custom formatting

The key is that you can delegate complex computational tasks to Claude while controlling the input and output through the Files API. This creates a powerful workflow where Claude becomes your coding assistant, able to actually execute and iterate on solutions.

#### Video Transcript

In this video, we'll explore two features offered by the Anthropic API. These two features may seem somewhat different and somewhat independent, but it turns out they can be combined in very interesting ways. So, let's get started. We'll first understand what the Files API actually is.

Earlier in this course, we discussed how to pass images to Claude and ask Claude to interpret the images themselves. I showed you how we can include an image block, which can contain the raw image data encoded as base64. We also saw a very similar process used to upload PDF documents. The Files API brings a slight change to the whole system.

With the Files API, we can make a separate request ahead of time to upload a specific document, whether it's a PDF, an image, a text file, or anything else. So we might first make an initial request to Claude to upload that file. Then we'll get something called a file metadata object. This file metadata object contains several different pieces of information, but the most interesting or important property for us is the file ID.

This ID lets us reference that uploaded file at some future point. So, later at some future point, a user can submit a message like "what do you see in this image". In our image block, instead of including the raw file data of the image itself, we just include the file ID. So we can put the file ID in there.

This will make Claude go find the image we uploaded earlier. Then Claude will do its best to interpret this image. So the Files API allows us to upload a file ahead of time, then include some data about the original file to Claude in a later request. So you can really think of this as another way for us to provide an image or PDF to Claude.

Now that we understand the basic principle behind the Files API, which is that it lets us submit a file and then reference it at some future point, we'll switch topics and discuss the other focus of this video, which is code execution. Code execution is a server-based tool. So we don't have to provide an actual implementation for this tool. All we have to do is provide a predefined tool schema.

In the initial request we make to Claude, we'll include this specially defined tool schema, along with any user-submitted messages we want to include. Then behind the scenes, Claude can optionally execute a certain amount of Python code in an isolated Docker container. Claude can run code multiple times in this container. Anything Claude prints out from these code executions will be sent back to Claude, and then Claude can interpret the results and write us a final response.

These Docker containers have no network access at all. This means Claude can't write any code that would make network requests or try to access any external API. Instead, to get information into and out of the Docker container, we rely on combining the Files API we just discussed with this code execution tool. Let me show you how this works.

Let's imagine we have a CSV file called maybemydata.csv, which contains a lot of tabular information that we want Claude to analyze. Instead of going through a very complicated code setup, where we have Claude write some code that we then manually execute ourselves, we can leverage this Files API and code execution tool to have Claude automatically analyze the file and generate some results for us. To do this, we'll first upload our CSV file using the Files API. So we'll initially upload our CSV file here, with some data in it.

This will return a file ID to us. Then we'll include that file ID in a follow-up request to Claude. So we'll add something called a container upload block. A container upload block just means we want to take a file we previously uploaded to Claude, and inject or place it into the container in some way.

So we'll add this very specially crafted block, whose type is `container_upload`, with a file ID property of whatever ID we got when we uploaded the original file. Then in a separate text block, we'll ask Claude to do some analysis, possibly something as simple as "analyze the data in this file". Then behind the scenes, Claude will leverage the code execution tool. Claude will have access to that uploaded file in the Docker container.

So Claude can write some code to analyze the file, process the results, and then give us a complete report about all the data in that file. Now let's look at an example of this complete flow. I've prepared a notebook called 005 code execution. I also created a separate CSV file called streaming.csv.

In here is a bunch of fake data from a video streaming service. This file contains information about specific users, the subscription tier they belong to—that is, the level of access they have—and a lot of statistics about that particular user. So there's the total number of hours they watched, popular genres, and so on, and then at the end, the last column is called `churned`. `churned` is a flag indicating whether the user canceled their subscription.

So `0` means they didn't cancel their subscription, and `1` means they have canceled their subscription. Now I could write a lot of code to analyze the data in this file and figure out whether there's some correlation between these different features and whether users canceled their subscriptions. But instead of doing all this myself, I might decide to hand off this entire task to Claude. I can first upload this streaming.csv file, then ask Claude to leverage its code execution tool to analyze all the data in here.

Let me demonstrate how we'd do this. First, back in the notebook, I want you to look at the helper functions cell. If you scroll down a bit, you'll notice I added several different functions. I added an `upload` function, which automatically uploads a specific file given a file path.

I added a `list_files` function, which lists all the different files we've uploaded to Claude. We can also delete files, download files, and get information about a specific file. We'll use these functions shortly. So I'll collapse that cell, and of course, make sure I run it.

Then in the next cell, I'll try to upload the `streaming.csv` file. So I'll run that cell right away. We'll get our file metadata object. Inside it is our ID.

This is the unique ID that identifies this file to Claude. If we want to include it in a conversation in the future, we'll reference that specific ID. Then in the next cell, I have a short prompt asking Claude to do a detailed analysis to find out why customers cancel their subscriptions. I also ask Claude to print out a chart summarizing all its findings.

After that, I have a container upload block, which will actually include that uploaded file in our request. So now I want to run this cell, and note that whenever you use code execution, it sometimes actually takes some time to complete. The response we get will contain a lot of text. In this message will be all the code Claude decided to write, along with all the print statements and output it got, plus some final analysis.

Claude can decide to run code multiple times inside the container, so we might actually see multiple code blocks and multiple execution results in this message. Now to help you understand what's happening in the message, I took all of this content and formatted it a bit more nicely, so we can understand what's going on. So this is that message. I got my content list, with all kinds of different blocks in it.

The first block is a text block, which will contain some text, just Claude framing the initial question. Then Claude will provide a server tool use block. This will contain some code Claude wants to run inside the container. Then this is our code execution tool result.

It will contain some information about the actual code execution. So there's data from stdout, stderr, a return code in case any error handling is needed, and so on. Then it looks like in this case, Claude decided to run more code after that. So it did some further analysis here, got some more results, then did some more analysis, and so on.

So in this case, Claude ran code several times in a row to do a thorough analysis. Now, whether you decide to show all this content to your users is entirely up to you and the particular application you're developing. If you want, you can build a very nice report, maybe like this. I took all the information we just saw and used Claude to format it very nicely.

So now I can see the initial response. This is the exact information from the initial text block we got. Then this is my code execution tool. This is the code Claude decided to run, and the output of executing it.

We can see this is repeated several times. So now I have more text here, another code execution, and so on. Finally, I want to show you one of the most interesting aspects of code execution. You might recall that in this prompt I made, I asked Claude to include a detailed chart summarizing its findings.

Claude did indeed generate a chart in an image file behind the scenes, and it was stored in our Docker container. We can use the Files API to download that chart generated in the Docker container. Let me demonstrate. First, I'll take a close look at the message I formatted here.

If I scroll through it, I might eventually see a text block with an extra nested `content` property. Inside that, I might see a `code_execution_output` type. Mine is right here. If you don't see it in your response, try searching for `code_execution_output`.

Below that is a file ID. So I can use this file ID to download the file. I'll copy it, go back to my notebook, and I'll go to the very bottom and add a new cell. Okay.

I'll call `download_file`, which is one of the predefined functions we saw at the beginning of this video. I'll paste the file ID I just found in the response. I'll run this. It will run successfully.

Now if I look in the same directory where my notebook is, I'll find a PNG or JPEG file there. Their names are random. It's not truly random. It'll be whatever name Claude decided to give it.

If I then open that file, if I then open that file, I'll see a bunch of information Claude extracted from that CSV file. So this is a great visualization that tells me everything I need to know. So churn rate by hours watched, churn rate by monthly fee range, and so on, Claude did a very thorough analysis here, helping me understand the contents of this file. So as you can see, based on this demonstration, combining the Files API and the code execution tool lets us delegate fairly complex tasks to Claude.

Of course, you're not limited to doing data analysis. You can use the combination of code execution and the Files API to perform all kinds of different tasks. How you integrate this into your application is really up to you.
