### 66. Introducing MCP

> Type: Video | Duration: 4:40 | Video: 09 - 001 - Introducing MCP.mp4

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is a communication layer that provides Claude with context and tools, without requiring you to write a lot of tedious integration code. Think of it as a way to shift the burden of defining and executing tools from your server to dedicated MCP servers.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542645%2F09_-_001_-_Introducing_MCP_01.1748542645351.jpg)

When you first encounter MCP, you'll see diagrams showing the basic architecture: an MCP Client connecting to MCP Servers that contain tools, prompts, and resources. Each MCP server acts as an interface to some external service.

## Understanding MCP Through a Real Example

Suppose you're building a chat interface where users can ask Claude about their GitHub data. A user might ask: "What open pull requests do I have across all my repositories?" To answer this question, Claude needs tools to access GitHub's API.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542646%2F09_-_001_-_Introducing_MCP_04.1748542645841.jpg)

Without MCP, you'd need to create all the GitHub integration tools yourself. That means writing schemas and functions for every GitHub feature you want to support.

## The Problem with Tool Functions

GitHub's functionality is incredibly rich—repositories, pull requests, issues, projects, and much more. To build a complete GitHub chatbot, you'd need to write a staggering number of tools:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542646%2F09_-_001_-_Introducing_MCP_05.1748542646307.jpg)

Each tool requires a schema definition and a function implementation. This represents a large amount of code that you, as the developer, must write, test, and maintain.

## How MCP Solves This

MCP shifts the burden of defining and executing tools from your server to MCP servers. Instead of you writing those GitHub tools, they're written and executed inside a dedicated MCP server.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542646%2F09_-_001_-_Introducing_MCP_08.1748542646653.jpg)

The MCP server acts as a wrapper around GitHub's functionality, providing pre-built tools you can use without implementing them yourself.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542647%2F09_-_001_-_Introducing_MCP_09.1748542647009.jpg)

MCP servers provide access to data or functionality implemented by an external service. They package complex integrations into reusable components that any application can connect to.

## Common Questions About MCP

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542647%2F09_-_001_-_Introducing_MCP_12.1748542647366.jpg)

### Who writes MCP servers?

Anyone can create an MCP server implementation. Often, service providers themselves create their own official MCP implementations. For example, AWS might publish an official MCP server containing tools for their various services.

### How is MCP different from calling an API directly?

MCP servers provide you with already-defined tool schemas and functions. If you call an API directly, you're responsible for writing those tool definitions yourself. MCP saves you that implementation work.

### Isn't MCP just Tool Use?

This is a common misconception. MCP servers and tool use are complementary but distinct concepts. MCP is about who does the work of creating and maintaining the tools. With MCP, someone has already written the tool functions and schemas for you—they're packaged inside an MCP server.

The key insight is that MCP servers provide you with already-defined tool schemas and functions, eliminating the need for you to build and maintain complex integrations yourself.

#### Video Transcript

In this module, we'll focus on the Model Context Protocol. MCP is a communication layer designed to provide Claude with context and tools, without requiring you as the developer to write a lot of tedious code. When you first start using MCP, you'll often see a diagram like this. It shows the two main elements of MCP, namely the client and the server.

The server usually contains many internal components, called tools, resources, and prompts. There's a lot of terminology here. So to help you understand all of this, we'll imagine we're building a small application and see how MCP fits into it. Our example application will be another chat interface.

It will allow users to chat with Claude about their GitHub data. So if a user asks a question, like what open pull requests do I have across all my different repositories, the expectation is that Claude might leverage a tool to access GitHub, access the user's account, and look at what open pull requests they have, maybe open repositories or anything else. The key point here is that we might accomplish this by using a set of tools. Now, I want to mention something very quickly, which is that GitHub has enormous functionality.

There are repositories, pull requests, issues, projects, and a ton of other things. So to have a complete GitHub chatbot, we really have to write a lot of tools. If we want to build that example application, we'd have to be responsible for writing all these schemas and all these functions. And these are all code that you and I, as developers, have to write, test, and maintain.

That's a lot of effort, a lot of burden for us. This challenge of having developers maintain a whole bunch of integrations is one of the main difficulties that the Model Context Protocol is designed to solve. MCP shifts the burden of defining and running tools from your server to something else called an MCP server. So you and I will no longer need to write this tool.

Instead, it will be written and executed elsewhere, inside this MCP server. These MCP servers can really be thought of as an interface to some external service. So I might have a GitHub MCP server that provides access to specific data and functionality provided by GitHub, and we're basically wrapping up a lot of functionality around GitHub and putting it into this MCP server as a set of tools. So at this point, we have a very basic understanding of what an MCP server is.

It gives us access to a set of tools that expose functionality related to some external service. The benefit of this is that you and I don't need to write all these different tool schemas and functions and so on. Now that we have this basic understanding, I want to talk about some very common questions many people have when they first learn about MCP servers. So, there always seem to be three common questions that come up.

The first common question is, who writes these MCP servers? The answer is anyone. Anyone can create an MCP server implementation. But many times, you'll find that service providers create their own official implementations.

So for example, AWS might decide to publish their own official MCP server implementation, in which it might have all kinds of different tools for you to use. The second common question is, how is using an MCP server different from calling the service's API directly? Well, as we just saw, if we want to call an API directly, like GitHub, then we have to write this tool ourselves. Now we can call GitHub directly.

So what did we gain here? Well, what really changed is just that we now have to write the schemas and function implementations ourselves. So just by adding an MCP server, we save ourselves a bit of time. The last common question is more of a common criticism you'll see people raise about MCP.

And this criticism most often comes from people who don't really understand what MCP is. So you'll often see people say MCP and tool use are the same thing. Well, as I just explained to you, MCP servers and tool use, they're complementary. They're different things, but they're complementary.

The idea behind MCP is that you don't need to write the tool functions and tool schemas. That's something someone else does for you, and it's packaged inside this MCP server. So in a way, yes, they're somewhat similar, because we're talking about tool use in both cases, but what MCP servers are really about is who does the actual work. So if you see this criticism, again, it's usually because people don't really understand what MCP is all about.

---

### 67. MCP Clients

> Type: Video | Duration: 4:56 | Video: 09 - 002 - MCP Clients.mp4

An MCP client acts as the communication bridge between your server and an MCP server. Think of it as your access point to all the tools provided by the MCP server. When you need to use external tools or services, the client handles all the message passing and protocol details for you.

## Transport-Agnostic Communication

One of the key advantages of MCP is that it's transport-agnostic—which is a fancy way of saying that the client and server can communicate using different methods. The most common setup is to run the MCP client and server on the same machine, communicating through standard input/output.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542644%2F09_-_002_-_MCP_Clients_01.1748542644766.jpg)

But you're not limited to this approach. MCP clients and servers can also connect via:

-   HTTP
-   WebSockets
-   Various other network protocols

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542645%2F09_-_002_-_MCP_Clients_03.1748542645442.jpg)

## Message Types

Once connected, the client and server exchange specific message types defined in the MCP specification. The message types you'll primarily work with are:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542645%2F09_-_002_-_MCP_Clients_04.1748542645814.jpg)

`ListToolsRequest`/`ListToolsResult`: the client asks the server "what tools do you provide?", and gets a list of available tools.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542646%2F09_-_002_-_MCP_Clients_05.1748542646228.jpg)

`CallToolRequest`/`CallToolResult`: the client requests that the server run a specific tool with specific parameters, then receives the result.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542646%2F09_-_002_-_MCP_Clients_06.1748542646636.jpg)

## Complete Flow Example

Here's how all the parts work together in a real scenario. Suppose a user asks "What repositories do I have?"—here's the complete communication flow:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542647%2F09_-_002_-_MCP_Clients_08.1748542647060.jpg)

The flow begins when a user submits a query to your server. Your server realizes that, before making the request, it needs to provide Claude with a list of available tools.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542648%2F09_-_002_-_MCP_Clients_09.1748542647956.jpg)

Your server requests the tools from the MCP client, and the MCP client sends a `ListToolsRequest` to the MCP server and receives a `ListToolsResult`.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542648%2F09_-_002_-_MCP_Clients_11.1748542648353.jpg)

Now your server has everything it needs to make the initial request to Claude—both the user's question and the available tools.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542649%2F09_-_002_-_MCP_Clients_12.1748542648890.jpg)

Claude examines these tools and decides it needs to call one of them to answer the question. It responds with a tool use request.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542649%2F09_-_002_-_MCP_Clients_14.1748542649299.jpg)

Your server asks the MCP client to execute the tool Claude requested. The MCP client sends a `CallToolRequest` to the MCP server, which in turn makes the actual request to GitHub.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542649%2F09_-_002_-_MCP_Clients_15.1748542649806.jpg)

GitHub returns the repository data, which flows back as a `CallToolResult` through the MCP server, then to the MCP client, and finally to your server.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542650%2F09_-_002_-_MCP_Clients_17.1748542650158.jpg)

Your server sends the tool result back to Claude in a follow-up message. Claude now has all the information it needs to formulate a complete response.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542651%2F09_-_002_-_MCP_Clients_18.1748542650970.jpg)

Finally, Claude responds with a formatted answer, which your server relays back to the user.

Yes, this flow involves many steps, but each component has a clear responsibility. The MCP client abstracts away the complexity of server communication, letting you focus on building your application logic. When we implement our own MCP client and server, you'll see how each piece fits together in practice.

#### Video Transcript

The next part of the Model Context Protocol we're going to study is the client. The purpose of the client is to provide a way of communicating between your server and an MCP server. This client is going to be the entry point through which you access all the tools that the server implements. Now, MCP is transport agnostic.

That's a fancy term that just says the client and the server can communicate over a variety of different protocols. Right now, a very common way to run an MCP server is on the same physical machine as the MCP client. If these two things are running on the same machine, then they can communicate over standard input/output. That's what we're going to set up later in this section.

However, we have other ways we can connect an MCP client and an MCP server. They can also be connected over HTTP or Web Sockets or a variety of other technologies or methods. Once a connection is established between a client and a server, they communicate by exchanging messages. The exact messages that are allowed are all defined in the MCP specification.

Some of the message types we're about to focus on are `list tools request` and `list tools result`. As you might guess, a `list tools request` is sent from the client to the server, and asks the server to list out all the different tools it provides. The server then responds with a `list tools result` message containing a list of all the different tools it can offer. Two other common message types we're going to see are `call tool request` and `call tool result`.

The first will ask the server to run a tool with some specific arguments, and the second will contain the result of the tool running. Now, at this point, we have a notion of a server and a client. But I suspect it might not be entirely clear how all this stuff works together. So, for the remainder of this video, we're going to do the following.

We're going to walk through an example invocation that involves a lot of different things. So this is going to be a somewhat complicated process. But we're going to imagine the communication between the user or the server we're building, an MCP client, an MCP server, GitHub as the provider of some data we're trying to access, and Claude.

So, let's get started. Again, I'm Stephen Grider, and the first thing we expect to happen is that a user submits some kind of query or question to our server, like "What repositories do I have?" At this point, it's going to be up to our server to make a request to Claude. But in that request, we want to list out all the different tools that Claude can access.

So before our server makes a request to Claude, it's first going to take a little detour through the MCP client and server. So here's how it goes. The server is going to realize that it needs to see a list of tools to send along to Claude with the user's query. So it's going to ask the MCP client to get a list of tools.

The MCP client, in turn, is going to send a `list tools request` to the server, and the server is going to respond with a `list tools result`. Now our MCP client has a list of tools, and it's going to return that list of tools back to the server. Now our server has everything it needs to make the initial request to Claude. It has both the original message from the user and a list of tools to include.

So our server can make a request to Claude with that query and that set of tools. Claude is going to take a look at the tools and then realize, you know what, in order to answer the user's original question, I really want to call a tool. So Claude is going to respond with some tool use message parts. At this point, our server is going to realize that Claude wants to run a tool.

But our server is no longer really responsible for executing any tools. Instead, our tools are going to be executed by the MCP server. So to run the tool that Claude is requesting, our server is going to ask the MCP client to run a tool with some specific arguments provided by Claude. However, the MCP client doesn't actually run the tool.

It's going to send a `call tool request` to the MCP server. The MCP server is going to receive that request and make a follow-up request to GitHub. So this is where we're actually going to get a list of repositories belonging to this particular user. GitHub is going to respond with that list of repositories.

Then the MCP server is going to wrap that data in a `call tool result` and send it back to the MCP client. Then the MCP client, in turn, is going to hand the result to our server. Now our server has the list of repositories, and it can make a follow-up request to Claude, including the tool result part in a user message. So this tool result is going to contain the list of repositories that Claude is requesting.

Now Claude has all the information it needs to formulate a final response. So it's going to write out some text, like "Your repositories are," and then send it back to our server, and our server is going to send it back to our user. Okay, so this flow, yes, it's pretty complicated. The reason I wanted to show you this is that when we start to implement our own custom MCP client and MCP server, we're going to see all these different pieces.

---

### 68. Project Setup

> Type: Video | Duration: 3:09 | Video: 09 - 003 - Project Setup.mp4

We're going to build a CLI-based chatbot to better understand how MCP clients and servers work together. This hands-on project will give you practical experience with both ends of the MCP architecture.

## What We're Going to Build

Our chatbot will allow users to interact with a document collection through a command-line interface. The system consists of two main parts:

-   An MCP client that handles user interaction
-   A custom MCP server that manages document operations

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542682%2F09_-_003_-_Project_Setup_03.1748542682857.jpg)

The server will provide two basic tools: one for reading document contents, and another for updating them. For simplicity, all documents will be stored in memory—no database required.

## Important Architectural Note

In real projects, you typically implement only an MCP client or an MCP server, not both. You might create:

-   An MCP server that exposes your service to other developers
-   An MCP client that connects to an existing MCP server

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542683%2F09_-_003_-_Project_Setup_07.1748542683236.jpg)

We're building both components in this project purely for educational purposes—to understand how they communicate and work together.

## Project Setup

Download the `cli_project.zip` file from the resources attached to this lesson, and unzip it into a development directory of your choice. Open your code editor in the project folder.

The project includes a comprehensive README file with setup instructions. Please follow these steps:

1.  Add your Anthropic API key to the `.env` file
2.  Install dependencies using UV (recommended) or pip
3.  Run the starter application to verify everything works

## Running the Application

Navigate to your project directory in the terminal. You'll see the main project files, including `main.py`, `mcp_client.py`, and `mcp_server.py`.

To start the application, use one of the following commands:

```
# If using UV (recommended)
uv run main.py

# If using standard Python
python main.py
```

When the application starts successfully, you'll see a chat prompt. Test it by asking a simple question, like "What is 1+1?"—you should get a response from Claude quickly.

With the basic setup complete, we're ready to start implementing MCP functionality and exploring how clients and servers communicate via the Model Context Protocol.

### 69. Defining Tools with MCP

> Type: Video | Duration: 7:03 | Video: 09 - 004 - Defining Tools with MCP.mp4

Building an MCP server becomes much simpler when you use the official Python SDK. The SDK handles all the complexity for you through decorators and type hints, so you don't have to manually write complex JSON schemas for your tools.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542694%2F09_-_004_-_Defining_Tools_with_MCP_00.1748542693957.jpg)

In this example, we're creating an MCP server for managing documents stored in memory. The server will provide two basic tools: one for reading document contents, and another for updating them via find-and-replace operations.

## Setting Up the MCP Server

The Python MCP SDK makes server creation remarkably simple. You can initialize a complete MCP server with just one line of code:

```python
from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP

mcp = FastMCP("DocumentMCP", log_level="ERROR")
```

For this implementation, documents are stored in a simple Python dictionary, where the keys are document IDs and the values contain the document contents:

```python
docs = {
    "deposition.md": "This deposition covers the testimony of Angela Smith, P.E.",
    "report.pdf": "The report details the state of a 20m condenser tower.",
    "financials.docx": "These financials outline the project's budget and expenditure",
    "outlook.pdf": "This document presents the projected future performance of the",
    "plan.md": "The plan outlines the steps for the project's implementation.",
    "spec.txt": "These specifications define the technical requirements for the equipment"
}
```

## Defining Tools with Decorators

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542694%2F09_-_004_-_Defining_Tools_with_MCP_05.1748542694369.jpg)

The SDK turns tool creation from a verbose process into a clean and readable one. Instead of writing lengthy JSON schemas, you use Python decorators and type hints.

## Creating the Document Reading Tool

The first tool allows Claude to read any document by its ID. Here's the complete implementation:

```python
@mcp.tool(
    name="read_doc_contents",
    description="Read the contents of a document and return it as a string."
)
def read_document(
    doc_id: str = Field(description="Id of the document to read")
):
    if doc_id not in docs:
        raise ValueError(f"Doc with id {doc_id} not found")
    
    return docs[doc_id]
```

The `@mcp.tool` decorator automatically generates the JSON schema that Claude needs. The `Field` class from Pydantic provides parameter descriptions that help Claude understand what each parameter expects.

## Building the Document Editing Tool

The second tool performs a simple find-and-replace operation on a document:

```python
@mcp.tool(
    name="edit_document",
    description="Edit a document by replacing a string in the documents content with a new string."
)
def edit_document(
    doc_id: str = Field(description="Id of the document that will be edited"),
    old_str: str = Field(description="The text to replace. Must match exactly, including whitespace."),
    new_str: str = Field(description="The new text to insert in place of the old text.")
):
    if doc_id not in docs:
        raise ValueError(f"Doc with id {doc_id} not found")
    
    docs[doc_id] = docs[doc_id].replace(old_str, new_str)
```

This tool accepts three parameters: the document ID, the text to find, and the replacement text. For simplicity, the implementation uses Python's built-in string `replace()` method.

## Error Handling

Both tools include basic error handling to manage cases where Claude requests a document that doesn't exist. When provided with an invalid document ID, the tools raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message that Claude can understand and potentially act upon.

## Key Advantages of the SDK Approach

- Automatic JSON schema generation from Python type hints
- Clean, readable code that's easy to maintain
- Built-in parameter validation through Pydantic
- Reduced boilerplate compared to writing schemas manually
- Type safety and IDE support for a better development experience

The MCP Python SDK turns what used to be a complex process of writing tool definitions into something that feels natural for Python developers. You just focus on the business logic, and the SDK handles the protocol details.

### 70. The Server Inspector

> Type: Video | Duration: 3:52 | Video: 09 - 005 - The Server Inspector.mp4

When building an MCP server, you need a way to test functionality without connecting it to a full application. The Python MCP SDK includes a built-in, browser-based inspector that lets you debug and test your server in real time.

## Launching the Inspector

First, make sure your Python environment is activated (check the README in the project for the exact command). Then run the inspector with the following command:

```
mcp dev mcp_server.py
```

This starts a development server on port 6277 and gives you a local URL to open in your browser. The inspector interface will load, showing the MCP Inspector dashboard.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542727%2F09_-_005_-_The_Server_Inspector_05.1748542726831.jpg)

## Important Note About the Interface

The MCP Inspector is under active development, so the interface you see may differ from the current screenshots. However, the core functionality for testing tools, resources, and prompts should remain similar.

## Connecting and Testing Tools

Click the "Connect" button on the left to start your MCP server. Once connected, you'll see a navigation bar with sections for Resources, Prompts, Tools, and other features.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542727%2F09_-_005_-_The_Server_Inspector_07.1748542727369.jpg)

To test your tools:

- Navigate to the Tools section
- Click "List Tools" to see all available tools
- Select a tool to open its testing interface
- Fill in the required parameters
- Click "Run Tool" to execute and view the results

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542728%2F09_-_005_-_The_Server_Inspector_09.1748542727826.jpg)

## Testing Document Operations

For example, to test a document reading tool, you'd enter a document ID (like "deposition.md") and run the tool. The inspector will display the results, including any returned content or success message.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542728%2F09_-_005_-_The_Server_Inspector_17.1748542728249.jpg)

You can chain operations to verify functionality. For example, after editing a document by replacing text, you can immediately run the read tool again to confirm that the change was applied correctly.

## Development Workflow

The inspector creates an efficient development loop:

- Make changes to your MCP server code
- Test individual tools through the inspector
- Verify results without a full application setup
- Debug issues in isolation

This tool becomes essential as you build more complex MCP servers. It eliminates the need to connect your server to Claude or another application just to test basic functionality, making development faster and more focused.

### 71. Implementing a Client

> Type: Video | Duration: 7:26 | Video: 09 - 006 - Implementing a Client.mp4

Now that our MCP server is working, it's time to build the client. The client is the component that lets our application communicate with the MCP server and access its functionality.

## Understanding the Client Architecture

In most real-world projects, you implement either an MCP client or an MCP server—not both. We're building both in this project just so you can see how they work together.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542735%2F09_-_006_-_Implementing_a_Client_01.1748542735212.jpg)

The MCP client consists of two main parts:

- **MCP Client** - A custom class we create to simplify using the session
- **Client Session** - The actual connection to the server (part of the MCP Python SDK)

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542735%2F09_-_006_-_Implementing_a_Client_02.1748542735805.jpg)

The client session requires proper resource cleanup once we're done using it. This is why we wrap it in a custom MCP Client class—to handle all that cleanup automatically.

## How the Client Fits into Our Application

Remember our application flow? Our CLI code needs to do two main things with the MCP server:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542736%2F09_-_006_-_Implementing_a_Client_06.1748542736441.jpg)

1.  Get a list of available tools to send to Claude
2.  Execute tools when Claude requests them

The MCP client provides this functionality through simple method calls that our application code can use.

## Implementing the Core Methods

We need to implement two key methods in our client: `list_tools()` and `call_tool()`.

### List Tools Method

This method gets all available tools from the server:

```
async def list_tools(self) -> list[types.Tool]:
    result = await self.session().list_tools()
    return result.tools
```

This is straightforward—we access our session (the connection to the server), call the built-in `list_tools()` function, and return the tools from the result.

### Call Tool Method

This method executes a specific tool on the server:

```
async def call_tool(
    self, tool_name: str, tool_input: dict
) -> types.CallToolResult | None:
    return await self.session().call_tool(tool_name, tool_input)
```

We pass the tool name and input parameters (provided by Claude) to the server and return the result.

## Testing the Client

To test our implementation, we can run the client directly. The file includes a test utility that connects to our MCP server and calls our methods:

```
async with MCPClient(
    command="uv", args=["run", "mcp_server.py"]
) as client:
    result = await client.list_tools()
    print(result)
```

When we run this test, we should see our tool definitions printed out, including the `read_doc_contents` and `edit_document` tools we created earlier.

## Putting It All Together

Now that our client can list tools and call them, we can test the full flow. When we run the main application and ask Claude a question about documents:

1.  Our code uses the client to get the available tools
2.  These tools are sent to Claude along with the user's question
3.  Claude decides to use the `read_doc_contents` tool
4.  Our code uses the client to execute the tool
5.  The result is sent back to Claude, and then Claude responds to the user

For example, asking "What are the contents of the report.pdf document?" will trigger Claude to use our document reading tool, and we'll get information about the 20m condenser tower document we set up in the server.

The client acts as the bridge between our application logic and the MCP server, making it easy to access the server's functionality without worrying about the low-level connection details.

#### Video Transcript

Now that our server is ready, we're going to shift focus a little and start developing our MCP client. The client can be found in the `MCP client.py` file in the root project directory. Before we do anything in that file, I just want to give you a very quick reminder. Remember what I told you before.

Usually in a typical project, we're either using a client or implementing a server. It's just in this particular project we're working on that we're doing both. Again, just so you can see both sides of the puzzle. Now, the MCP client itself in this file consists of a single class.

You'll notice there's a lot of code in here, and it doesn't look as pretty as some of the code we just wrote in the server. So let me tell you exactly what's going on in this file and why it's so big. Okay, so in this file, we're creating the `MCP client` class. This class is going to wrap something called a `client session`.

The `client session` is the actual connection to our MCP server. This `client session` is part of the Anthropic Python SDK. So again, this `session` is what gives us the connection to an external server. The `session` itself requires some resource cleanup.

In other words, whenever we close the program or decide we no longer need the server, we have to go through a little cleanup process. And I've already written a lot of cleanup code in the `MCP client` class. So that's really the entire reason this class exists, just to make the cleanup a little easier. You can see some cleanup code in the `Connect` function, and a bit further down.

You can also see it in the `cleanup`, `async enter`, and `async exit` functions. So, rather than using this `client session` directly, it's a very common practice to wrap it in a larger class to manage these different resource transactions. The next thing I want to clarify is why this client exists. In other words, what exactly does this client do for us?

Well, remember this whole flow we looked at a little while ago? So we have our code here. At certain points in time, we need, say, a list of tools to send to Claude. Then after that, we also need to run a tool requested by Claude.

To contact our MCP server and get this list of tools or run a tool, we use the MCP client. So we can imagine that this client is exposing some of the server's functionality to the rest of our codebase. So, inside our codebase, specifically in the `core` directory of this project, there's already a lot of code I've put together that uses this class. So there's some other code that calls some of the different functions you see here, like `list tools`, `call tool`, `list prompts`, `get prompt`, and so on.

For now, in this video, we're going to focus on implementing two functions, `list tools` and `call tool`. As you just saw in the diagram, these two functions are going to be used in different parts of our codebase, to get a list of tools to provide to Claude, and then ultimately to call a tool when Claude requests calling one. Implementing these two functions is going to be very straightforward. Let me show you how we'll do it.

We're going to start with `list tools` first. I'm going to delete the to-do inside, and replace it with `result is await self.session`. I'm going to call `list_tools` like a function. Then I'm going to return `result.tools`.

That's it. So this is going to access our session, which is our actual connection to the MCP server. It's going to call a built-in function to get the definitions, or list, of all the different tools that server implements. I'll get `result`, then just return `tools`, and that's it.

Then we can implement `call tool` here in a very similar way. So this is going to be `return await self.session, call tool, tool_name, and tool_input`. Again, getting access to the session, which is our connection to the server, I'm going to try to call a very specific tool, namely the tool name we pass in, along with the input arguments, or input parameters, provided by Claude. Now, at this point, I want to quickly test these two functions.

To do this, we're going to go to the bottom of this file, where I've prepared a very small test utility for us. So here, you'll notice I've put a test block, so we can run this `MCP client.py` file directly. If we do this, we'll establish a connection to our MCP server, and then we can run some commands against it to see what we get. Note that in your version of the code, there's a comment about changing the command and arguments here, in case you're not using UV.

So if you're not using UV, make sure to take a look at that comment. Inside this `with` block, I'm going to add some test code. So I'll say `result is await_client list tools`. Then I'm going to print out the result we get.

So this should start up a copy of our MCP server, then try to get a list of all the different tools it defines, and then just print out the result. To test this, I'm going to switch back to my terminal and run `uv run, MCP_client.py`. As usual, if you're not using UV, you just run `python MCP client.py`. Okay, I run it, and here's our list of tool definitions.

I can see here that I have the `read.contents` tool, which we put together a little while ago, and our `edit document` tool. Each one has a description and an input schema. So these are our tool definitions, which will ultimately be passed to Claude. Before we continue, there's one more thing I want to test.

Remember, we just implemented that function that lets us list out some tools and pass them to Claude, as well as that function that lets us call a tool implemented by the MCP server and pass the result to Claude too. I've already implemented the code that calls `list tools` and `call tool` elsewhere in this project. So now that we've added this functionality, now that we've defined these tools and the ability to call a specific tool, we can now run our CLI again and try to get Claude to use these tools. In other words, we can ask Claude to check the contents of a specific document, or even edit a document.

Let me show you how to do this. In my MCP server, I just want to remind you, there's a document with the ID `report.pdf`, which has some content about a 20-meter condenser tower or something. I'm going to go back to my terminal and run my project with `uv run main.py`. Then I'm going to ask Claude what the contents of the `report.pdf` document are.

Make sure you type `report.pdf` here. When we run this command, we'll send our list of tools along with the request. Claude is going to decide to use the tool for reading documents. Then it's going to get the contents of the document.

Then we'll see that, yes, Claude was able to get the contents of that document. We're told that the report is about the contents of a 20-meter condenser tower. Okay, at this point, we've added some functionality to our client. Remember, the client is the component that lets us access some functionality implemented inside the MCP server.

At this stage, we've been able to list some tools created by the server, and execute a tool implemented by the server.

### 72. Defining Resources

> Type: Video | Duration: 9:45 | Video: 09 - 007 - Defining Resources.mp4

Resources in an MCP server allow you to expose data to clients, similar to GET request handlers in a typical HTTP server. They're ideal for scenarios where you need to fetch information rather than perform an action.

## Understanding Resources Through an Example

Suppose you want to build a document mention feature, where users can type `@document_name` to reference a file. This requires two operations:

- Get a list of all available documents (for autocomplete)
- Get the contents of a specific document (when it's mentioned)

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542781%2F09_-_007_-_Defining_Resources_01.1748542781089.jpg)

When the user types @, you need to display the available documents. When they submit a message with a mention, you automatically inject that document's contents into the prompt sent to Claude.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542782%2F09_-_007_-_Defining_Resources_03.1748542782123.jpg)

## How Resources Work

Resources follow a request-response pattern. Your client sends a `ReadResourceRequest` with a URI, and the MCP server responds with data. The URI (Uniform Resource Identifier) is like the address of the resource you want to access.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542783%2F09_-_007_-_Defining_Resources_04.1748542783045.jpg)

## Resource Types

There are two types of resources:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542783%2F09_-_007_-_Defining_Resources_07.1748542783391.jpg)

- **Direct Resources:** Static URIs that don't change, like `docs://documents`
- **Templated Resources:** URIs with parameters, like `docs://documents/{doc_id}`

For templated resources, the Python SDK automatically parses parameters from the URI and passes them as keyword arguments to your function.

## Implementing Resources

Resources are defined using the `@mcp.resource()` decorator. Here's how to create both types:

### Direct Resource (List Documents)

```
@mcp.resource(
    "docs://documents",
    mime_type="application/json"
)
def list_docs() -> list[str]:
    return list(docs.keys())
```

### Templated Resource (Fetch Document)

```
@mcp.resource(
    "docs://documents/{doc_id}",
    mime_type="text/plain"
)
def fetch_doc(doc_id: str) -> str:
    if doc_id not in docs:
        raise ValueError(f"Doc with id {doc_id} not found")
    return docs[doc_id]
```

## MIME Types

Resources can return any type of data—strings, JSON, binary, etc. The `mime_type` parameter hints to the client what kind of data you're returning:

- `application/json` - Structured JSON data
- `text/plain` - Plain text content
- Any other valid MIME type appropriate for different data formats

The MCP Python SDK automatically serializes your return value. You don't need to manually convert it to a JSON string.

## Testing Resources

You can test your resources using the MCP Inspector. Run your server with the following command:

```
uv run mcp dev mcp_server.py
```

Then connect to the inspector in your browser. You'll see:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542784%2F09_-_007_-_Defining_Resources_17.1748542783889.jpg)

- **Resources:** Lists your direct/static resources
- **Resource Templates:** Shows templated resources that accept parameters

Click any resource to test it, and view the exact response structure your client will receive.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542784%2F09_-_007_-_Defining_Resources_18.1748542784293.jpg)

## Key Points

- Resources expose data, tools perform actions
- Use direct resources for static data, and templated resources for parameterized queries
- MIME types help the client understand the response format
- The SDK handles serialization automatically
- Parameter names in a templated URI become function parameters

Resources provide a clean way to supply data to MCP clients, enabling scenarios like document mentions, file browsing, or any situation where you need to fetch information from the server.

#### Video Transcript

In this video, we're going to continue exploring the next major feature in MCP servers—resources. To help you understand resources, we're going to implement another feature in our project. Here's what we're going to add. I want to allow users to mention a document by typing the at symbol (@) followed by the document name.

When they do this, I want to automatically fetch the contents of that document and insert it into the prompt we send to Claude. So overall, this feature has two aspects. When a user types the at symbol in a message, we'll automatically show a list of all the documents that can be mentioned, in a little autocomplete window. Then, when the user submits a message containing a mention, we'll automatically fetch the contents of that document and insert it into the prompt we send to Claude.

For example, if a user says "what's in @report.pdf", I want to build a prompt like this and send it to Claude. So, the prompt would have the user's query, and then we'd also tell Claude that the user may have referenced a document, and here are the contents of that document. So the approach or idea here is that we don't have to rely on Claude using some tool to figure out what's in the report.pdf file. Instead, the user can just mention the file up front, and we'll automatically insert some context ahead of time.

Now, I want to clarify one thing—we're actually talking about two different features. The first feature is that when the user types the at symbol, we do need the MCP server to give us a list of all the documents the user might mention. Then the second aspect is that when the user submits a message containing a mention, we need the MCP server to give us the contents of a single document. To get this information from our MCP server, we're going to use resources.

Resources allow our MCP server to expose some data to the client. We typically define one resource for each different read operation. So in our case, we need to fetch a list of documents and read the contents of a single document. Therefore, we'll probably create two separate resources.

One resource will be responsible for just returning a list of document names, so we can put them into the autocomplete list, and then we'll probably create another resource that will expose the contents of a single document based on a document ID. When we define these resources, they'll be accessed through our MCP client. So the entire flow we'll ultimately build is that when the user types something like "what's in the app," and then presumably they'll type something there, once they type that app character, we need to show a list of document names to put into the autocomplete. So our code will contact the MCP client, and the client in turn will send a read resource request to the MCP server.

In that read resource request, we're going to include something called a URI. This is essentially the address of the resource we want to read. This URI is defined when we initially create the resource. So the URI is that.

When we send this read resource request, the MCP server is going to look at the exact URI we put into it, and then run the function we placed there. It gets the result and sends it back to us in a read resource result message. Then we can take the data inside it and display it in our autocomplete, or do whatever we need to with it. There are two different types of resources, direct resources and templated resources.

You'll also sometimes see direct resources called static resources. A direct resource has only one static URI, so it's always exactly the same thing, like `docs, colon slash slash documents`. A templated resource will have one or more parameters in its URI. For example, we might have `documents slash`, and then there's a wildcard here.

So we can enter any document ID we want. When we request this resource, that document ID in the URI is automatically parsed by the Python MCP SDK and provided to our function as a keyword argument. The name of the keyword argument will be exactly the same as the string you enter here. So this `Doc ID` here would be the `Doc ID` there.

As you may have guessed, whenever we want to provide more choice, variety, or customization in what the user requests from our MCP server, we use a templated resource. Implementing resources is very straightforward. So, let's go back to our editor, and we're about to add some resources to the server. Okay, back in my editor, I'm going to find the `MCP server.py` file.

Then I'm going to scroll down a bit and find some comments about writing a resource that returns all document IDs and writing a resource that returns the contents of a specific document. Now, for the first one here, I wrote "document IDs" in the comment. Keep in mind that for us, our document IDs are essentially the names of the documents. So for us, we're really just returning these IDs.

They'll function as names. This means we can put them directly into that autocomplete element. Okay, to create our resource, I'm going to delete that to-do. Then I'm going to add an `MCP.resource`.

The first argument will be the URI to access this thing. Again, this is a bit like a route handler. So I'm going to use `docs, colon slash slash documents`, and I'm also going to add a MIME type of `application slash JSON`. A resource can return any type of data, so it could be plain text, it could be JSON, it could be binary data, anything.

It's up to us to give our client a hint about what kind of data we're returning. To do this, we'll define this MIME type. The `application slash JSON` MIME type is a hint to our client, which will ultimately request this resource, telling it that we'll be sending back a string containing structured JSON data. So it's up to our client to deserialize this data, or in other words, convert it into a usable data structure.

Under that decorator, I'm going to write out my `list_docs` function, which will return a list of strings. Then inside it, I'm going to return `list(docs.keys())`. So just take all the keys from that dictionary, convert them into a list, and return it. Now, you'll notice we're not returning explicit JSON.

In other words, we're not actually returning a string. The MCP Python SDK will automatically convert whatever we return into a string. Okay, let's handle our second resource. So I'm going to delete that comment, and replace it with `MCP resource, docs, colon slash slash documents`.

This time I want a templated resource, because I've put a wildcard here. Then for my `mind type` this time, just for some variety, I'm going to return plain text, because it's just going to be the contents of the document. I'm not going to wrap it in any structure. Now, I want you to know that in a real application, like reading a document, I might return a complete document record.

That is, a dictionary containing the ID, contents, author name, author ID, and so on. But just for the sake of example, I'm going to return only the document's text, to show you how we usually return plain text. So in this case, my MIME type will be `text plane`, and then I'm going to create `fetch doc`. I'm going to take in a `doc ID` of type string, and return a string.

Again, any word you type here will show up as a keyword argument in your function. If we added some extra parameters here, like `doc type` or something, it would show up as an additional keyword argument like this. Then in here, I first want to make sure the ID this person requested actually exists. So if `doc ID not in docs`, I'm going to raise a `ValueError` with an f-string that says "Doc with ID not found".

Then if we pass that check, I'm going to return `docs[doc ID]`. That's it. Now let's test these things again in our MCP Inspector. Remember, in our terminal, we can run the command `UVRun, MCPDev, MCPServer.py`.

This will start a web server on port 6277, or 6274 by default. So I'm going to make sure I open it in my browser. Okay. I click Connect.

Then I find Resources. Then I should be able to list all the available resources. Now, when I list resources, this will be the specific static or direct resources. So I'll only see `docs/documents`.

Then I can separately list all my different resource templates. So I'll see that I have a resource template for `FetchDoc`. I can first try running `slash documents` here. Let's see what we get.

So this is the actual message, the exact structure returned from our MCP server. You'll notice it has a `text` property, inside which is all the data we're going to return, serialized as a JSON string. So again, it's going to be up to us, in our CLI application, to take this text and deserialize it from this JSON string into a usable list of strings. Then we can also test `fetch doc`.

So I click it. I have to enter a `doc ID`. So I'm going to enter it. I want to read the `report.pdf` file.

I'm going to read the resource. Now I should see the contents of that specific document. You'll notice this time, I got a `text plain` again. So this hints to me that this is plain text. I shouldn't try to deserialize it from JSON in any way.

### 73. Accessing Resources

> Type: Video | Duration: 4:38 | Video: 09 - 008 - Accessing Resources.mp4

Resources in MCP allow your server to expose data that can be included directly in prompts, rather than requiring a tool call to access the information. This creates a more efficient way to provide context to AI models like Claude.

## Understanding Resource Requests

Once you've defined resources on your MCP server, your client needs a way to request and use them. The client acts as the bridge between your application and the MCP server, automatically handling the communication and data parsing.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542773%2F09_-_008_-_Accessing_Resources_00.1748542772895.jpg)

The flow is simple: when a user wants to reference a document (such as typing "@report.pdf"), your application uses the MCP client to fetch that resource from the server, and includes its contents directly in the prompt sent to Claude.

## Implementing Resource Reading

The core functionality requires a `read_resource` function in your MCP client. This function takes a `uri` parameter that identifies which resource to fetch:

```
async def read_resource(self, uri: str) -> Any:
    result = await self.session().read_resource(AnyUrl(uri))
    resource = result.contents[0]
```

The response from the MCP server contains a `contents` list. You typically only need the first element, which holds the actual resource data along with metadata like the MIME type.

## Handling Different Content Types

Resources can return different types of content, so your client needs to parse them appropriately. The MIME type tells you how to handle the data:

```
if isinstance(resource, types.TextResourceContents):
    if resource.mimeType == "application/json":
        return json.loads(resource.text)
    
    return resource.text
```

This approach ensures that JSON resources are correctly parsed into Python objects, while plain text resources are returned as strings. The MIME type is your hint for deciding the correct parsing strategy.

## Required Imports

For this functionality to work, you need these imports in your MCP client:

```
import json
from pydantic import AnyUrl
```

The `json` module handles parsing JSON responses, while `AnyUrl` ensures the `uri` parameter's type is handled correctly.

## Testing Resource Access

After implementation, you can test the functionality through your CLI application. When you type something like "What's in the @report.pdf document?", the system should:

- Display available resources in an autocomplete list
- Allow you to select a resource
- Automatically fetch the resource contents
- Include that content in the prompt to Claude

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542774%2F09_-_008_-_Accessing_Resources_09.1748542773725.jpg)

The key advantage is that Claude receives the document contents directly in the prompt, without needing to access the information through a tool call. This makes the interaction faster and more efficient.

## Integrating with Your Application

Remember, the MCP client code you write is used by other parts of your application. The `read_resource` function becomes a building block that other components can call to fetch document contents, list available resources, or integrate resource data into prompts.

This separation of concerns keeps your code clean: the MCP client handles communication with the server, while your application logic focuses on how to use that data effectively.

#### Video Transcript

Keep in mind, this MCP client will contain some functionality we're putting together that will be used by the rest of our application.

And I've already put that part of the code together. So elsewhere in this project, something is going to try to use this function we're about to add to the MCP client. To get started, I'm going to open the MCP client file again. I'm going to scroll down and find the `read resource` here.

Our goal here is to read a specific resource by making a request to our MCP server, then parse the returned content based on its mime type, and finally just return whatever data we get. You'll notice that one of its parameters is the URI. This will be the URI of the resource we want to fetch from the server. I'm going to add an `if isinstance(resource.contents[0], ResourceTypeText)` and `resource.mime_type == "application/json":` # This is our hint. If the server tells us it's returning some JSON, we need to make sure we parse the text content as JSON. So I'm going to return `json.loads(resource.text)` in that case. So elsewhere in this codebase, we're going to call the function we just put together to get a list of document names, and then ultimately get document contents to put into a prompt. So at this point, everything should work, because the rest of the work has already been done for us.

With that in mind, let's go back to our terminal, and we're going to test our CLI application again to see if this mention feature works. Okay, back here, I'm going to run `uvicorn main:app --reload`, and now I should be able to say something like "what's in the at," and then, I see my list of resources, and I can scroll through them with the arrow keys. Once I've selected a resource I like, I just press the space bar, and we insert that resource. So, what's in the "report.pdf" document?

Now, I can tell you everything works as expected here. In other words, the contents of this document are being sent into Claude's prompt. So if I submit this, I should immediately get a response that tells me what's inside the report PDF. So this time, Claude doesn't have to use a tool to read the contents of the document.

### 74. Defining Prompts

> Type: Video | Duration: 7:44 | Video: 09 - 009 - Defining Prompts.mp4

Prompts in an MCP server allow you to define pre-built, high-quality instructions that clients can use without having to write their own prompts from scratch. Think of them as carefully crafted templates that produce better results than what a user might come up with on their own.

## Why Use Prompts?

Suppose you want Claude to reformat a document into markdown. A user could just type "convert report.pdf to markdown," and that would work too. But they'd probably get much better results using a well-tested prompt that includes specific instructions about formatting, structure, and output requirements.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542823%2F09_-_009_-_Defining_Prompts_07.1748542822992.jpg)

The key insight is that while users can do these tasks themselves, they'll get more consistent, higher-quality results when using prompts that have been carefully developed and tested by the MCP server author.

## How Prompts Work

A prompt defines a set of user and assistant messages that the client can use directly. When a client requests a prompt, your server returns a list of messages that can be sent directly to Claude.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542823%2F09_-_009_-_Defining_Prompts_08.1748542823609.jpg)

The basic structure is as follows:

- Define a prompt using the `@mcp.prompt()` decorator
- Add a name and description to each prompt
- Return a list of messages that make up the complete prompt
- These prompts should be high-quality, well-tested, and relevant to the purpose of your MCP server

## Building a Formatting Command

Here's how to implement a document formatting prompt. First, you need to import the base message type:

```
from mcp.server.fastmcp import base
```

Then define your prompt function:

```
@mcp.prompt(
    name="format",
    description="Rewrites the contents of the document in Markdown format."
)
def format_document(
    doc_id: str = Field(description="Id of the document to format")
) -> list[base.Message]:
    prompt = f"""
Your goal is to reformat a document to be written with markdown syntax.

The id of the document you need to reformat is:

{doc_id}


Add in headers, bullet points, tables, etc as necessary. Feel free to add in extra formatting.
Use the 'edit_document' tool to edit the document. After the document has been reformatted...
"""
    
    return [
        base.UserMessage(prompt)
    ]
```

## Testing Your Prompt

You can test prompts using the MCP Inspector. Navigate to the Prompts section, select your prompt, and provide any required arguments. The inspector will show you the generated messages that will be sent to Claude.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542824%2F09_-_009_-_Defining_Prompts_18.1748542824116.jpg)

This lets you verify that your prompt correctly interpolates variables and produces the expected message structure before using it in a real application.

## Best Practices

When creating prompts for your MCP server:

- Focus on your server's core tasks
- Write detailed, specific instructions rather than vague requests
- Test your prompts thoroughly with different inputs
- Provide clear descriptions so users understand what each prompt does
- Consider how the prompt works together with your server's tools and resources

Remember, prompts are meant to provide value that users can't easily get on their own—they should represent your expertise in the domain your MCP server covers.

#### Video Transcript

The last major focus we're going to look at in our MCP server is prompts. Just like we did with resources a moment ago, we're going to implement a small feature in the project again. We're going to use this feature to understand what prompts are all about. Let me tell you about the feature we're going to add to the program.

We're going to add support for slash commands. For example, I want a `format` command. I have some screenshots here about how it works. When the user types a slash, we'll list out some commands that our application supports.

For now, we only have one command called `format`. So if I just type a slash, I should see a little autocomplete prompt, and the only option should be `format`. If I select `Format`, the system should prompt me to add a document ID after it. Like one of our document names, such as `report.pdf` or something.

Then, when the user runs this command, the goal is for Claude to reformat this document using Markdown syntax. In other words, to take the plain string—which currently has no special formatting—out of each of our documents. Remember, in our MCP server, the current document contents are just plain text. We want to feed this to Claude and have Claude rewrite it using Markdown syntax.

So I'd expect to see output like this, saying "I'll help you reformat the document." Then Claude is going to use a tool to read the document contents. Finally, in the final response, I want to see the contents of that document rewritten in Markdown syntax. Now, there are some interesting things I want to point out about this feature.

The real core of this feature, the real goal, is to allow users to reformat a document into Markdown syntax. And this operation actually doesn't require you or me, as developers, to write any code to implement it. What does that mean? Well, the user can already launch our CLI and say something like "reformat the report.pdf file in markdown syntax."

The user can already do this. No problem at all. Claude will do a pretty good job. It will take the contents of our document and reformat it into markdown.

As you can see here, it does the job perfectly. So what exactly are we doing this feature for? Well, the idea here is that if we let the user manually type something like "convert this to Markdown" entirely on their own, they might get a so-so result, but if they had a really powerful prompt tailored to this specific scenario (converting a document to Markdown), they could get a much better result. So, if we, as the MCP server authors, can sit down and write, test, evaluate, and complete the whole process of crafting a really thoughtful, excellent prompt, just like the one you see on the right, then the user will probably be happier.

So, again, yes, the user can perform the entire workflow themselves, but if they use this carefully designed prompt instead, I think they'll get a better result. This is the real goal of the prompts feature in an MCP server. The idea here is that we can predefine a set of prompts in our server, tailored to whatever domain our server excels at. In our case, our server is focused on managing documents, reading documents, editing documents, and so on.

So we might decide to add a set of high-quality prompts that have been evaluated and tested, and we know work well in a variety of different scenarios. Then we can open up these prompts to any client application to use, like this CLI app we're now developing. Now, I want to point out that we could develop this prompt and put it directly into our CLI codebase. That works perfectly fine.

We could certainly do that. But again, the idea here is that your MCP server might focus on a particular task, and might open up some prompts that people can use directly, without having to worry about developing them up front. To define a prompt in our MCP server, we're going to write some syntax very similar to the tools and resources we've already created. We're going to use the `prompt` decorator.

We'll add a name to the prompt, and optionally a description. Then, when a client requests this prompt, we'll return a list of messages. These are the actual user and assistant messages. So we can send these messages directly to Claude.

Okay, so let's go back to our server and try to create a prompt of our own. As you can see here, it's going to be about taking the contents of a document and rewriting it in Markdown format somehow. Okay, back in my editor, I'm going to find my MCP server file. I'm going to scroll down to the comment about rewriting a document in Markdown format.

I'm going to delete that to-do, then add an MCP prompt, with the name `format` and the description "Rewrites the contents of the document in Markdown format." Then I'm going to add an actual implementation. So it's `format_document`, I can take in a `doc_id` as a parameter, and then we can also add a field description here, just like we did with tools before. So I can optionally add a field with the description "Id of the document to format."

I'm also going to add a type annotation of `string`. Just to make sure this is very clear too. From this function, we're going to return a list of messages. I want to make sure to import `base` for this at the top right away.

So below the existing MCP server imports, I'm going to add `from mcp_server.fast_mcp.prompts import base`. Then back down below. Here, we're going to define that well-tested and well-evaluated prompt of ours. I've pre-written a prompt.

I'm going to paste it in. So this prompt just asks Claude to take in a document ID. We implicitly ask Claude to use the `redocument` tool to get the contents of that document ID. Then after getting that document, rewrite it directly in Markdown syntax.

Finally, after rewriting, also edit the document to save these updates in our server. Now, after defining this prompt, we're going to return a list of messages. So below, I'm going to return a list containing `base.user_message`, and then I'm going to pass in the prompt we just wrote, like this. Now I'm going to save this file, and let's start up the MCP dev inspector and test this prompt from that interface.

So in my terminal, I'm going to run the same command again, then navigate to that address in my browser. I'm going to make sure to connect to my server. Then I'm going to find the prompts section. I'm going to list all the available prompts.

At this point, we only have one prompt, which is `format`. So I click `format`, and then I have to enter a document ID here. This time, maybe we'll enter a document ID like `outlook.pdf`. So I enter that.

Then click `get prompt`. Then here's our list of messages. These are pre-assembled. I have one message part here.

So it's a text part containing our complete prompt. We can see that the document ID has already been inserted into it. Now that we have these messages, we can send them to Claude. Hopefully we'll get some appropriate response.

So, again, the whole idea behind these prompts we might implement in an MCP server is that the prompts we define will be well-tested, well-evaluated, and really focused on a particular use case.

---

### 75. Prompts in the Client

> Type: Video | Duration: 3:01 | Video: 09 - 010 - Prompts in the Client.mp4

Prompts in MCP define a set of user and assistant messages that the client can use. These prompts should be high-quality, well-tested, and relevant to the overall goal of the MCP server.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542819%2F09_-_010_-_Prompts_in_the_Client_17.1748542819284.jpg)

## Implementing List Prompts

The first step is to implement the `list_prompts` method in your MCP client. This method retrieves all available prompts from the server:

```
async def list_prompts(self) -> list[types.Prompt]:
    result = await self.session().list_prompts()
    return result.prompts
```

This simple implementation calls the session's `list_prompts` method, and returns the prompts array from the result.

## Getting a Single Prompt

The `get_prompt` method retrieves a specific prompt and interpolates arguments into it. When you request a prompt, the arguments you provide are passed as keyword arguments to the prompt function:

```
async def get_prompt(self, prompt_name, args: dict[str, str]):
    result = await self.session().get_prompt(prompt_name, args)
    return result.messages
```

This method returns the `messages` from the result, which make up a conversation that can be fed directly to Claude.

## How Prompt Arguments Work

When you define a prompt function on the server side, it can accept arguments. For example, a document formatting prompt might expect a `doc_id` argument:

```
def format_document(doc_id: str):
    # The doc_id gets interpolated into the prompt
```

When the client calls `get_prompt`, the `arguments` dictionary should contain the expected keys. The MCP server passes these as keyword arguments to the prompt function, allowing dynamic content to be inserted into the prompt template.

## Testing Prompts in the CLI

After implementation, you can test prompts through the command-line interface. When you type a forward slash, the available prompts appear as commands. Selecting a prompt may prompt you to choose from available options (like a document ID), and then the complete prompt is sent to Claude.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542819%2F09_-_010_-_Prompts_in_the_Client_11.1748542819808.jpg)

The workflow is as follows:

1.  The user selects a prompt (like "format")
2.  The system prompts for required arguments (like the document to format)
3.  The prompt with interpolated values is sent to Claude
4.  Claude can then use tools to fetch additional data and complete the task

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542820%2F09_-_010_-_Prompts_in_the_Client_15.1748542820224.jpg)

## Prompt Best Practices

When creating prompts for your MCP server:

-   Make them relevant to your server's goals
-   Test thoroughly before deployment
-   Use clear, specific instructions
-   Design them to work well with your available tools
-   Consider what arguments the user needs to provide

Prompts bridge the gap between predefined functionality and dynamic user needs, providing a structured starting point for Claude to perform complex tasks while remaining flexible through parameterization.

#### Video Transcript

Our final task is to implement some functionality in the MCP client that allows us to list out all the prompts defined in the MCP server, and get a specific prompt with some variables interpolated. So, let's first implement `list_prompts`. I'm going to delete the comment and replace it with `result = await self.session.list_prompts()`, then I'm going to return `result.prompts`. That's basically it.

Then there's `get_prompt`. To be clear, when we get a single prompt, we get some arguments. These arguments will ultimately end up in our prompt function. For example, in `format_document`, we expect to receive a document ID.

In this `args` dictionary, there's expected to be a `document_id` key. This key will be passed into the corresponding function. Then we'll interpolate that value into the prompt itself. So in the `get_prompt` function, I'm going to get a result from `self.session.get_prompt()`.

I'm going to pass in the name of the prompt. This is the name of the prompt I want to retrieve. Then I'm going to pass in the arguments. Then I'm going to return `result.messages`.

So these are the returned messages. They make up some kind of conversation that we want to feed directly to Claude. That's it. That's all we need to do in the client.

So now we can test this functionality in the CLI itself. I'm going to switch back and run the project again. Now if I type a slash here, I'll see that I can access this `format` command. Now `format` is really just the name of the prompt we're going to call.

So if I select it and press space, the system will ask me to choose a document, and I'll select `plan.md`. I press enter. Then we feed the entire prompt, which is really just that user message, directly to Claude. So Claude has now received the instruction to reformat the document into Markdown syntax.

It also got the ID of the document we want to reformat. So the first thing it needs to do is go get the contents of that document. It will do this by using the `get_document` tool. Then finally, Claude will respond with the markdown version of this document.

So here's the document containing lots of markdown syntax. Okay, since this looks good, let's quickly review prompts to make sure we understand what they're all about. We first write and evaluate a prompt relevant to our MCP server's goal. In our case, we're making a document server.

So having some functionality around rewriting documents in different styles makes sense, I think. Once we've put the prompt together, we define a prompt in the MCP server. Then our client can request that prompt at any time. When we request the prompt, we pass in some arguments, which are provided as keyword arguments to this prompting function.

Then our function can use these keyword arguments in the prompt itself.
