### 76. MCP Review

> Type: Video | Duration: 4:12 | Video: 09 - 011 - MCP Review.mp4

#### Video Transcript

We've finished our project, but before we continue, I want to quickly review the three server primitives we learned about. That is, tools, resources, and prompts. I want to specifically highlight something interesting about each of them. That is, which part of an application is really responsible for running them?

In other words, in a typical application, who exactly runs these things, and who benefits from them? Well, we'd say tools are `model controlled`. This means that only Claude is responsible for deciding when to run a particular tool. Resources are `app controlled`.

In other words, some code running in your app will decide that it needs some data provided by a resource. It will be up to your app code to execute a resource and use the returned data in some way, perhaps by using that data in the UI or something similar. In our case, we fetch a resource, then use that data in the UI to provide a list of autocomplete options. We also fetch a resource to enhance a prompt.

Both of these things are done by the application-related code that you and I write. Finally, prompts are actually `user-controlled`. So the user decides when a prompt runs. The user might initiate the invocation of a prompt by clicking some UI element (like a button or menu option), or they might use a slash command like the one we made.

The reason I emphasize who controls each part is to give you a sense of what they're used for. So, if you need to add capabilities to Claude, you might want to consider implementing some tools in your MCP server, or using some server's tools through your MCP client. If you want to fetch some data for your app to display content in the UI or for similar purposes, then you might want to use a resource. And if you want to implement some kind of predefined workflow, you might want to consider using prompts.

You can see examples of all these ideas in Claude's official interface, claude.ai. So, this is what it looks like currently. You'll notice there are some buttons below the main chat input box. If I click one of them, then click one of these examples, you'll see I immediately enter a chat.

This is a user-controlled action. As the user, I decided to launch this particular workflow, and I'm using a prompt that may have been pre-written and optimized. So, to implement that list of buttons there, we might need to create a series of different prompts in the MCP server. Similarly, if I go back, maybe click this little label, the plus button, you'll notice I have an "Add from Google Drive" button.

Now, I'm not going to click it, because it would show some of my internal files. But if I clicked that button, I'd see some documents I could add to this chat as context. Knowing which documents to actually render in that list, and then automatically injecting their contents into this chat's context when I click one of them, is entirely application-related code. So it's entirely up to the application to know the list of documents to render here.

And this is, again, a UI-specific element. So, to implement the functionality of listing documents from Google Drive, I might consider implementing a resource in the MCP server. Finally, if I type a message into this chat, like, "What's the square of 3? Compute the value with JavaScript and then send it," I obviously expect Claude to execute some JavaScript code somehow, which is most likely done by using a tool. In this case, the decision to use the tool is 100% model-controlled.

It's the model that decides to use some JavaScript tool execution. To implement something like this in the MCP server, we'd most likely need, you guessed it, to provide a tool. So, all in all, these are our three different server primitives. Each primitive is really meant to be used by a different part of your overall application.

So we have tools, which usually serve your model; resources, which usually serve your app; and prompts, which usually serve your user. Again, these are high-level guidelines. I mention them just to give you a rough sense of when to use these primitives, based on what you're trying to build.

### 78. Anthropic Apps

> Type: Video | Duration: 0:43 | Video: 10 - 001 - Anthropic Apps.mp4

In this module, we're going to explore two powerful applications built by Anthropic: Claude Code and Computer Use. Not only are they useful tools in their own right, but they're also excellent examples of AI agents. By understanding how they work, you'll build a solid foundation for building your own agents down the road.

## Our Plan

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542875%2F10_-_001_-_Anthropic_Apps_02.1748542875572.jpg)

We're going to proceed step by step, gradually deepening your understanding:

-   **Claude Code** - Start with this agentic coding assistant that runs in your terminal
-   **Computer Use** - Explore this set of tools that lets Claude interact with desktop applications
-   **Agents** - Understand what makes these applications successful as agents

## Claude Code

Claude Code is a terminal-based coding assistant that can help you with a wide variety of programming tasks. You can think of it as Claude living right in your command line, ready to:

-   Edit files and fix bugs
-   Answer coding questions
-   Assist with development workflows

We're going to walk you through the complete setup process, then use Claude Code on a small example project, so you can see exactly how it works in practice.

## Computer Use

Computer Use takes Claude's capabilities to a new level. It's a collection of tools that allow Claude to interact with a full desktop computer environment. This means Claude can:

-   Access websites and browse the internet
-   Interact with desktop applications
-   Perform tasks that require navigating a visual interface

This dramatically expands the possibilities compared to text-only interactions.

## Why These Matter for Agents

Both Claude Code and Computer Use are great case studies for understanding agents. They demonstrate the key principles that make agents effective:

-   Tool integration and usage
-   Multi-step task execution
-   Environmental interaction
-   Autonomous problem-solving

By studying these real-world implementations, you'll gain insight into what makes Claude Code and Computer Use so successful, which will inform your own agent development efforts.

Let's get started with the Claude Code setup process in the next section.

### 79. Claude Code Setup

> Type: Video | Duration: 1:33 | Video: 10 - 002 - Claude Code Setup.mp4

Claude Code is a terminal-based coding assistant that runs directly in your command line. You can think of it as Claude living right in your terminal, ready to help you with whatever coding task you're working on.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542849%2F10_-_002_-_Claude_Code_Setup_00.1748542849392.jpg)

## What Claude Code Can Do

Claude Code comes with a comprehensive set of tools to help your development workflow:

-   **File operations** - Search, read, and edit files in your project
-   **Terminal access** - Run commands directly from the conversation
-   **Web access** - Search documentation, fetch code examples, and more
-   **MCP Server support** - Add additional tools by connecting MCP servers

The MCP integration is especially powerful, because it means you can extend Claude Code's capabilities by adding dedicated tools for databases, APIs, or any other services you use.

Claude Code runs on MacOS, Windows WSL, and Linux, so you can use it regardless of your development environment.

## Installation

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542850%2F10_-_002_-_Claude_Code_Setup_10.1748542849909.jpg)

Setting up Claude Code takes just three steps:

1.  **Install Node.js** from nodejs.org/en/download (check whether it's already installed by running `npm help` in your terminal)
2.  **Install Claude Code** using the command `npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code`
3.  **Launch and log in** by running `claude` in your terminal

When you run the `claude` command for the first time, it will prompt you to log into your Anthropic account. If you need more detailed instructions, the complete setup guide is available at docs.anthropic.com.

Once setup is complete, you'll be able to use Claude directly in your terminal, ready to help you with any coding project or task.

#### Video Transcript

Let's take a look at Claude Code. We're going to do some setup, learn how it works, and look at some advanced use cases. Claude Code is a terminal-based coding assistant. It's a program that runs in your terminal and can help with a variety of code-related tasks.

To help you complete coding projects, Claude Code has access to many different tools. It has many basic tools, like the ability to search, read, and edit files. But it also has many advanced tools, like web scraping and terminal access. Finally, Claude Code can act as an MCP client.

You know what that means. It means it can consume tools provided by MCP servers. So we can easily extend Claude Code's capabilities by adding some additional MCP servers. Now let's do some setup to install Claude Code on your machine.

Setup is simple. We first need to install a copy of Node.js. You may already have Node installed on your machine, and to find out whether it's installed, open your terminal and run the command `NPM help`. If you see something returned, that means you probably already have Node installed.

Once you have Node installed, you'll run an `NPM install` command to install Claude Code itself. After installation, run the `Claude` command in your terminal. This will prompt you to log into your Anthropic account. The complete setup guide can be found in the official Anthropic documentation at docs.anthropic.com.

Now I'm going to let you complete this setup process on your own—again, it's just these three steps. Once you're done, we'll look at a small project together to see exactly what Claude Code can do for us.

---

### 80. Claude Code in Action

> Type: Video | Duration: 10:29 | Video: 10 - 003 - Claude Code in Action.mp4

Claude Code isn't just a tool for writing code—it's designed to work alongside you at every stage of a software project. You can think of it as another engineer on your team, capable of handling everything from initial setup to deployment and support.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542928%2F10_-_003_-_Claude_Code_in_Action_01.1748542928114.jpg)

## The /init Command

When you start using Claude Code on a project, the first thing you'll do is run the `/init` command. This tells Claude to scan your entire codebase to understand your project's structure, dependencies, coding style, and architecture.

Claude will summarize everything it learns in a special file called `CLAUDE.md`. This file is automatically included as context in all future conversations, so Claude remembers important details about your project.

You can set up multiple `CLAUDE.md` files for different scopes:

-   **Project** - Shared among all engineers working on the project
-   **Local** - Your personal notes, not checked into git
-   **User** - Common across all your projects

When running `/init`, you can add special instructions for areas you want Claude to focus on. The generated file will include build commands, coding guidelines, and project-specific patterns that Claude should follow.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542928%2F10_-_003_-_Claude_Code_in_Action_05.1748542928626.jpg)

You can also use the `#` command to quickly add notes to your `CLAUDE.md` file. For example, typing `# Always use descriptive variable names` will prompt you to add this guideline to your project, local, or user memory.

## Common Workflows

Claude works best when you treat it as a force multiplier. The more context and structure you provide, the better the results you'll get. Here are the most effective workflows:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542929%2F10_-_003_-_Claude_Code_in_Action_11.1748542928969.jpg)

### Step 1: Provide Context to Claude

Before asking Claude to build something, identify the files in your codebase relevant to the feature you want to create. First, ask Claude to read and analyze these files. This gives Claude examples of your coding patterns and existing functionality it can draw from.

### Step 2: Have Claude Plan the Solution

Instead of jumping straight to implementation, ask Claude to think carefully about the problem and develop a plan. Explicitly tell Claude not to write any code yet—just focus on the approach and the steps required.

### Step 3: Ask Claude to Implement the Solution

Once you have a solid plan, ask Claude to implement it. Claude will write the code based on the context and planning work you've done together.

## Test-Driven Development Workflow

For even better results, you can use the `Test-Driven Development` approach:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542929%2F10_-_003_-_Claude_Code_in_Action_17.1748542929416.jpg)

1.  **Provide context to Claude** - As before, show Claude the relevant files
2.  **Have Claude think about test cases** - Have Claude brainstorm which tests can validate your new feature
3.  **Have Claude implement these tests** - Choose the most relevant tests, and have Claude write them
4.  **Have Claude write code that passes the tests** - Claude will iterate on the implementation until all tests pass

This approach often produces more robust code, because Claude has clear success criteria to work toward.

## Practical Example

Here's what these workflows look like in practice. Suppose you want to add a document conversion tool to an existing project:

```
// First, ask Claude to read relevant files
> Read the math.py and document.py files

// Then ask for planning (not implementation)
> Plan to implement document_path_to_markdown tool:
1. Create a function that:
   - Takes a file path parameter
   - Validates the file exists  
   - Determines file type from extension
   - Reads binary data from file
   - Leverages existing binary_document_to_markdown function
   - Returns markdown string
2. Add appropriate documentation
3. Register the tool with MCP server
4. Add tests

// Finally, ask for implementation
> Implement the plan

```

Then, Claude will create the function, update the necessary files, write tests, and even run the test suite to verify everything works.

## Other Commands

Claude Code includes several useful commands:

-   `/clear` - Clears the conversation history and resets the context
-   `/init` - Scans the codebase and creates a `CLAUDE.md` document
-   `#` - Adds notes to your `CLAUDE.md` file

Claude can also handle everyday development tasks such as staging and committing changes to git, running tests, and managing dependencies. Instead of switching between your editor and terminal, you can let Claude handle these tasks while you focus on the bigger picture.

The key to using Claude Code successfully is to remember that it is designed to be a partner, not just a code generator. The more context and structure you provide, the more effectively Claude can help you build and maintain your project.

#### Video Transcript

To give you hands-on experience with Claude Code, I created a small project for us to work through together. You should be able to find the source code for this project in the zip file accompanying this lecture. I encourage you to download this zip file, extract its contents, and then launch your code editor in this new project directory. I've already opened this project in my editor.

Once you've opened the project, you might want to browse the project contents, and perhaps follow some of the setup instructions in the ReadMe file. But before you begin, I want you to understand one important thing about Claude Code. Claude Code is not just a tool that writes code for you. Of course, it can absolutely do that, but that's not the whole story of Claude Code.

Instead, you should think of Claude Code as another engineer working alongside you. Every task you would normally go through in a regular project can be fully delegated to Claude. This includes everything from the initial project setup, to new feature design, to deployment and support. Throughout the project, we'll make heavy use of Claude to assist us at each step.

We'll use Claude to set up the project for us, plan new features, write tests and code, and then later, on a slightly different project, I'll show you how we can use Claude Code to automatically discover and fix bugs in a production environment. So let's get started. Back in my editor, I'll open my terminal, then launch Claude Code inside it by executing `Claude`. Once it's open, I'll give Claude my first instruction.

I'm going to ask it to read the contents of the ReadMe file and execute any setup instructions listed in it. I'll ask Claude to read the contents of the ReadMe file and execute the setup instructions listed in it. Claude will then use a variety of different tools to read the file, and then execute a series of different commands. It will create a new virtual environment, activate that environment, and install some dependencies.

Once that's done, we'll run a command that helps Claude better understand our project. We'll do this by running the `init` command. This is a command we'll execute inside Claude Code. When you execute this command, Claude automatically scans your codebase to understand your project's overall architecture, coding style, and so on.

Once that's done, Claude writes all its findings to a special file called Claude.MD. The next time we run Claude, this file will automatically be included as context. By the way, there are three different Claude files: Project, Local, and User. We'll see references to them later.

We'll discuss what each of them is for at that point. Whenever you run this `init` command, you can actually also add some special instructions to have Claude focus on certain areas. So let's try it out now and see what gets generated for our project. I'll run the `init` command and pass in some special instructions at the same time.

I'm going to ask Claude to include some detailed instructions about defining MCP tools. Once it's done, I'll take a look at the newly generated Claude.MD file. This is Claude's summary of our codebase. At the top, it lists some important commands it might need to run in the future.

We'll see a list of the coding styles we use in this project. Then, just as I specifically requested, it also includes some information about defining MCP tools. As I just mentioned, this file will be included as context in any subsequent requests we make to Claude in the future. Now, projects change over time.

We might change our coding style or add some additional commands. If that happens, we can very easily edit this file manually, or we can choose to re-run the `init` command. If you re-run this command, Claude will update the contents of the Claude.MD file. Finally, as a very small shortcut, we can type a hash sign and then type the specific instructions we want to append to the contents of that file.

So we can use this as a tool to give Claude very specific small instructions that will be included in all subsequent requests. So for me, I might add an instruction here like "Always apply appropriate types to function parameters." If I run it, the system will ask me where I'd like to add this small instruction. This is where we see the Project, Local, and User memories appear.

In my case, this is an instruction I want to share with everyone working on this project, so I'll add it to Project memory. Once added, I can check the contents of my Claude.md file, and under code style or at the very bottom, I might see any instructions I just added. At this point, we've added a new file to the project, and this project is managed by Git. So normally, we'd open the terminal, stage this new file we just created into Git, and then commit those changes.

We could do all of this manually, but it's much faster to just have Claude do it for us. So I'll ask Claude to stage and commit all changes. Claude will then look at all the different changes we made to the codebase, write a descriptive commit message, and commit the files. Next, I want to show you some tips for making Claude more effective when writing code.

We're going to add a new feature to this project. As a reminder, this project is a very small, very simple MCP server. We're going to ask Claude to add a new tool to the server that will read a Word document or PDF file and convert its contents to Markdown. Now, we could of course just give Claude an instruction directly, like "Make a Word document + PDF file to Markdown converter tool."

But before we do that, I want you to know that with a little more thought, we can significantly improve Claude's effectiveness. Let me show you how. Think of Claude Code as a force multiplier. If you put a little more thought into how you guide Claude, you'll get significantly better results.

I'm going to show you two different workflows, two different ways of guiding Claude through a task. Both of these workflows require some effort on your part, but they enable Claude to solve more complex problems. In the first workflow, we'll go through three different steps. First, we'll identify certain areas of our codebase that are relevant to the feature we're working on.

Then, we'll ask Claude to specifically read and analyze these files. Second, we'll tell Claude about the feature we want to build, and ask it to plan a solution. That is, the steps it will actually execute to implement whatever feature or problem you're trying to solve. Finally, after Claude finishes the planning step, we'll ask Claude to actually implement these solutions.

Let me show you how to do this for our specific feature of adding a new document conversion tool. First, I'll browse my codebase and find some relevant files. You'll notice there's a Tools directory with a Math.py file inside it. This is an example of a completed tool.

So this file might be relevant to the feature we're trying to build, because it gives Claude a better idea of how to write a tool. Second, we might ask Claude to look at the document.py file, which contains a very useful function, `binary_document_to_markdown`. This function takes in some binary data and converts it to markdown. So we can tell Claude to look at these files, and Claude will have a better idea of how to write a tool, as well as how to actually do the conversion.

So I'll add some instructions to Claude to only read the contents of these files. Next, I'll ask Claude to plan the implementation of a new tool called `Document Path to Markdown`, which will take in the path to a PDF or Word document, read the file, convert the contents to Markdown, and return the result. I'll also explicitly tell Claude to only plan the feature and not write any code for now. In response, Claude will give us a fairly detailed plan that includes several steps needed to implement the entire project.

Finally, I'll ask Claude to implement this plan. It will first update the document.py file, which is absolutely correct. Then it will update the main.py file, which is also good. Finally, it will write a test for this new tool.

Then it will run the test suite and make sure the tests actually pass. You'll notice that in the summary message, Claude also tells me that it added some error handling for nonexistent or unsupported file types, which is absolutely appropriate, even though it wasn't something I explicitly asked for when describing this feature. I want to show you another way we can ask Claude to implement this feature. Before I show this alternative, I'll have Claude remove all the code it just wrote by stashing these changes.

Now I'm back to a clean state, without anything related to the new feature I just implemented. The second technique we'll explore is the test-driven development workflow. Again, this requires more effort up front, but it can greatly improve Claude's effectiveness. In this workflow, we'll once again ask Claude to look at some relevant context.

Then, before writing any code, we'll ask Claude to think about some different tests it could write for our feature. Next, we'll select the most relevant tests and ask Claude to implement them. Finally, after we have some usable tests, we'll ask Claude to write a solution and write code until the tests actually pass. Let me show you what this approach looks like for the exact same feature, namely, we're building a tool that will read a document from our hard drive and convert its contents to Markdown.

First, I'll run the `/clear` command. This will clear my conversation history with Claude, basically resetting its context. In this case, I'm doing this just so it doesn't copy the previous solution. Then I'll once again ask Claude to read those two relevant files.

Then I'll add some very explicit instructions for it to think about some tests that can be used to evaluate the new feature we want to build. Again, I'll explicitly ask it not to write any code for now. The suggestions I received are very solid. Now, I don't really want to worry about tests 6, 7, and 8, because those are a bit more specialized than what we're doing right now.

So I'll ask Claude to only implement tests one through five. Now we'll move on to the final step, where we'll ask Claude to write some code to make these tests pass. Excellent, all the tests pass. Now, before we continue, I just want to remind you once more that Claude Code really is a force multiplier.

You can give very simple instructions, and Claude will do its best. But you can significantly improve Claude's effectiveness by putting a little more thought in on your end.

---

### 81. Enhancements with MCP Servers

> Type: Video | Duration: 2:30 | Video: 10 - 004 - Enhancements with MCP Servers.mp4

Claude Code has a built-in MCP client, which means you can connect MCP servers to dramatically extend Claude's capabilities. This opens up some very powerful possibilities for customizing your development workflow.

## How MCP Extends Claude

The `Model Context Protocol` allows Claude Code to connect to external services and tools through MCP servers. Instead of being limited to Claude's built-in features, you can add custom functionality by connecting to servers that provide specific tools, resources, or integrations.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542913%2F10_-_004_-_Enhancements_with_MCP_Servers_01.1748542913123.jpg)

Each MCP server can expose different types of functionality to Claude through three main components: Tools (for performing actions), Prompts (for templates), and Resources (for accessing data).

## Setting Up an MCP Server

Adding an MCP server to Claude Code is very straightforward. You can use the command line to register your server:

```
claude mcp add [server-name] [command-to-start-server]
```

For example, if you have a document processing server whose start command is `uv run main.py`, you would run:

```
claude mcp add documents uv run main.py
```

Once registered, Claude Code automatically connects to your server when it starts up.

## Example: Document Processing

A practical example is creating a tool that lets Claude read PDF and Word documents. By building an MCP server with a "document_path_to_markdown" tool, you can ask Claude to convert document contents to markdown format.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542914%2F10_-_004_-_Enhancements_with_MCP_Servers_02.1748542913748.jpg)

When you ask Claude to "Convert the tests/fixtures/mcp_docs.docx file to markdown," it automatically uses your custom tool to read the document and return the converted content.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542914%2F10_-_004_-_Enhancements_with_MCP_Servers_13.1748542914556.jpg)

## Popular MCP Integrations

The MCP ecosystem includes servers for many common development tools and services:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542915%2F10_-_004_-_Enhancements_with_MCP_Servers_16.1748542915462.jpg)

-   **sentry-mcp** - Automatically discover and fix errors logged in Sentry
-   **playwright-mcp** - Gives Claude browser automation capabilities for testing and troubleshooting
-   **figma-context-mcp** - Exposes Figma designs to Claude
-   **mcp-atlassian** - Allows Claude to access Confluence and Jira
-   **firecrawl-mcp-server** - Adds web scraping capabilities to Claude
-   **slack-mcp** - Allows Claude to send messages or reply to specific posts

## Building Your Development Workflow

The real power comes from combining multiple MCP servers that match your specific development process. You could set up:

-   A Sentry server to fetch production error details
-   A Jira server to read ticket requirements
-   A Slack server to notify your team when work is complete
-   Custom servers for your internal tools and APIs

This creates a development environment where Claude can work seamlessly with all the tools and services you already use, making it a more powerful coding assistant tailored to your specific workflow.

#### Video Transcript

In this video, I want to show you one of the most interesting aspects of Claude Code. Claude Code has an embedded MCP client. This means we can connect MCP servers to Claude Code, thereby dramatically extending its capabilities. To demonstrate this, we're going to connect Claude Code to the MCP server we've been developing.

We just wrote a tool called `Document Path to Markdown`. We can now expose this tool to Claude Code, giving it the ability to read the contents of PDF and Word documents. So we're dynamically extending the capabilities of Claude Code. Let me show you how to set this up.

Back in my terminal, I'll end my running session with Control-C. Then I'll add an MCP server to Claude Code by executing `Claude, MCP, add`, and then we type in the name of our MCP server. Now this name can be whatever you want. In our case, we're creating a document-related server, so I'll name our server `documents`.

Finally, we'll type in the command we use to start the server, which for us is `uvrun main.py`. So it's `uvrun main.py`. That's it. I'll execute it, then restart Claude Code with `Claude`.

Now we can take advantage of the tool we just created and actually test it in our tests directory. There are two demo files in the fixtures folder, a Word document and a PDF document. Both contain a small amount of documentation about MCP itself. So I'll now ask Claude to convert the contents of either of these files to Markdown.

I expect Claude to use the tool we just wrote. If we scroll up a little, sure enough, it worked. So this is actually the content of that file. This ability to consume MCP servers adds incredible flexibility to Claude Code, and really opens the door to some very interesting development opportunities.

For example, you might decide to add a series of MCP servers relevant to your specific development process. For instance, if you use Sentry for production monitoring, you could add a Sentry MCP server to have Claude fetch details about errors occurring in production. If you use JIRA, you could add an MCP server to have Claude view the contents of specific tickets. If you're a Slack user, you could add Slack so that Claude sends you a message when it finishes working on a particular issue.

These are just a small sample of the possible enhancements you can add to Claude Code. So it's definitely worth taking the time to think about how to enhance your specific development workflow.

---

### 82. Parallelizing Claude Code

> Type: Video | Duration: 8:12 | Video: 10 - 005 - Parallelizing Claude Code.mp4

Running multiple instances of Claude Code in parallel is one of the biggest productivity gains you can achieve. Because Claude is lightweight, you can easily launch multiple copies, assign each a different task, and have them work simultaneously. This effectively gives you a virtual team of software engineers working on your project.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542981%2F10_-_005_-_Parallelizing_Claude_Code_00.1748542980996.jpg)

## The Core Challenge

The main problem with parallel instances is file conflicts. When two Claude instances try to modify the same file at the same time, they may write conflicting or invalid code, because they aren't aware of each other's changes.

The solution is simple: give each instance its own independent workspace. Each Claude instance works with its own copy of the project, making changes in an isolated environment, and then merges those changes back into your main project.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542981%2F10_-_005_-_Parallelizing_Claude_Code_02.1748542981591.jpg)

## Git Worktrees

`Git worktrees` are the perfect tool for this workflow. If your project uses Git (which it should), you can use worktrees right away. They're like an extension of Git's branching system, creating complete copies of your project in separate directories.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542982%2F10_-_005_-_Parallelizing_Claude_Code_03.1748542982035.jpg)

Each worktree corresponds to a separate branch. You can have:

-   A feature A branch in one folder
-   A feature B branch in another folder
-   Each folder containing a complete copy of your codebase
-   A separate Claude Code instance running in each folder

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542983%2F10_-_005_-_Parallelizing_Claude_Code_04.1748542982398.jpg)

When each Claude instance finishes its work, you commit the changes and merge them back into the main branch, just like any normal Git workflow.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542983%2F10_-_005_-_Parallelizing_Claude_Code_05.1748542983427.jpg)

## Automating Worktree Creation

Rather than creating worktrees manually, you can let Claude handle the entire process. Here's a prompt that creates a worktree and sets up the workspace:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542984%2F10_-_005_-_Parallelizing_Claude_Code_06.1748542984326.jpg)

This prompt tells Claude to:

1.  Check whether the worktree already exists
2.  Create a new Git worktree in the `.trees` folder
3.  Create a symlink to the `.venv` folder (since the virtual environment is not tracked by Git)
4.  Launch a new VSCode instance in that directory

## Custom Slash Commands

Repeatedly typing long prompts gets tedious. Claude Code supports custom commands that automate this process.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542985%2F10_-_005_-_Parallelizing_Claude_Code_09.1748542984846.jpg)

To create a custom command:

-   Add a `.md` file to `.claude/commands`
-   Put your prompt in the file
-   Use `$ARGUMENTS` as a placeholder for dynamic values
-   Run it with `/project:filename argument`

For example, `/project:create_worktree feature_b` will create a worktree named "feature_b".

## Parallel Development Workflow

A typical parallel development session looks like this:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542985%2F10_-_005_-_Parallelizing_Claude_Code_15.1748542985281.jpg)

1.  Create multiple worktrees for different features
2.  Launch Claude Code in each workspace
3.  Assign each instance a different task
4.  Let them work in parallel
5.  Commit changes as each task completes
6.  Merge all branches back into the main branch

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542985%2F10_-_005_-_Parallelizing_Claude_Code_16.1748542985628.jpg)

## Automating Merges

You can also use another custom command to automate the merge process. Create a merge prompt that will:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542986%2F10_-_005_-_Parallelizing_Claude_Code_17.1748542986000.jpg)

1.  Switch to the worktree directory
2.  Check the latest commit
3.  Switch back to the root directory
4.  Merge the worktree branch
5.  Automatically handle any merge conflicts
6.  Resolve conflicts based on its understanding of the changes

Claude can even handle merge conflicts intelligently, understanding the context of changes from different branches and resolving them appropriately.

## Scaling Your Development

This approach scales to as many parallel instances as you can effectively manage. Your only limits are:

- Your machine's resources
- Your ability to coordinate multiple tasks
- The complexity of potential merge conflicts

The productivity gains are enormous—instead of developing features sequentially, you can develop multiple features simultaneously, dramatically reducing development time for complex projects.

#### Video Transcript

In this video, we'll explore one of the biggest productivity gains that Claude Code offers. Because Claude is a lightweight process, you can easily run multiple copies of it. Each instance can be assigned a task, and then these independent instances will work in parallel. This technique lets a single developer command their own virtual team of software engineers.

So in the next few videos, I want to show you some of the specifics of implementing this workflow on a real project. The first big challenge to address is that two Claude instances might want to work on the same file at roughly the same time. They could end up writing conflicting code, or even invalid code, because they aren't aware that another process is modifying the same file. To solve this, we can make sure each instance has its own independent workspace.

Each instance can work on its own copy of the project, make changes, and then ultimately merge those changes back into your main project workspace. One common way to achieve this is to use Git worktrees. Git worktrees are a feature of Git, so if your project is already managed by Git, you can use worktrees right away. Worktrees are like an extension of Git's built-in branching feature.

A worktree lets you create a complete copy of your project in a new directory on your development machine. Each worktree corresponds to a separate branch. So if we have feature A on the left and feature branch B on the right, we can easily create two separate folders, each containing a complete copy of our codebase. Then, we can run a separate Claude Code instance for each worktree.

Each one will work in its own independent environment, completely isolated. Once each copy of Claude Code finishes a feature, we can commit the work for each worktree, then merge them back into our main branch, just like merging a regular branch. Now, this might sound like a very complex and hard-to-manage workflow. But remember, you can delegate a lot of the work to Claude Code, including Git-related tasks.

So we can actually have Claude Code manage this entire workflow itself. Let me show you how. First, we can write a prompt to have Claude create the worktree for us. We might also have it open our code editor in the newly created worktree folder.

That's what I'm asking for in this prompt. I'm asking Claude to create a new worktree in the `trees/feature_a` directory. Then, I'll ask it to `Symlink` some dependencies, because these dependencies aren't tracked by Git, so they won't be automatically copied into the worktree directory. Then I'll have Claude also open my code editor in that new subdirectory.

Now let me demonstrate how this all works. So, I'll take this exact prompt and run it in Claude Code. Claude will then create the worktree, create the symlink for the virtual environment, and then open a new code editor instance in this new sub-project folder.

In my original editor window, I'll see a new `trees` directory has appeared. Inside it is a `feature a` folder. Inside that `feature a` folder is a complete copy of my project. And this copy has already been automatically opened in this new editor window.

So in this new editor window, I can launch Claude Code and have it complete some tasks, like adding some new features or writing tests, or doing whatever I need to do. Now this Claude Code instance is running in a completely isolated environment. Before having Claude Code do anything here, though, I want to point out that, remember, this prompt is really long. And copying and pasting it everywhere every time we want to create a new worktree would be very tedious.

So we'll soon discuss a little aside, a small feature of Claude Code that makes creating and merging these separate worktrees very easy. So we can automate the entire prompt by taking advantage of another feature of Claude Code. That is, support for custom commands. You can add custom slash commands to Claude Code by creating a markdown file in a special directory in your project.

This special directory is `.claude/commands`. In a file in that directory, we'll write out our entire prompt, and then we can easily run this custom prompt anytime. Let me show you how to use this feature to easily create a new worktree. So in my original editor window, I'll create a new folder called `.claude`, inside it I'll create another folder called `commands`, and then inside it I'll create a file called, say, `createworktree.md`.

Then, inside it, I'll paste the prompt we just saw. Now, this prompt has a hard-coded feature name or hard-coded branch `feature_underscore_a`. I don't always want exactly the same string. So I'll replace it with a special string.

That is, `dollar sign arguments`, all uppercase. This allows us to inject some additional arguments when we actually run this custom command. So now if I save this file and quickly restart Claude Code, I can run `/project:create worktree`. It's specifically named `create work tree` because that's the name of the file I created in that `commands` directory.

Then I'll type a space, followed by the name of this new worktree. This time I'll name it `feature_underscore_B`. So now `feature_B` will be used to replace wherever I placed `dollar sign capital arguments`. So now if I run this, I should soon see a new worktree being created.

Shortly after, I have my new code editor instance. So now this one is opened in worktree `feature_underscore_B`. I can see that the additional worktree has been created in the `trees` directory. Now that we've seen how to create multiple different worktrees, I want to give you a full demonstration here.

I'm going to create four separate worktrees. Each one is intended to accomplish some different task. So I'll have four Claude Code instances running. The first will add some tests around documents.

The second will add some logging. The third will add two new tools, and the fourth will add a subtraction tool. I'll run all these tasks in parallel, and then I'll merge all of their changes back into my main branch. So for the first step, I'll create four separate worktrees.

I now have four separate editor instances, one for each feature I want to implement. I'll launch Claude Code in each instance and give each of them some instructions about the feature to add. When a worktree is finished, I'll ask Claude to commit that code. Then once they're all done, it's time to merge all these changes from the different branches back into my main branch.

We don't have to do it manually. Instead, we can have Claude do it for us. So I put together another prompt, which I'll wire up as an additional custom command in the project. This prompt asks Claude to go into one of our different feature worktrees.

Check the most recent commit, just to understand what was done, and then try to merge those changes back into the main branch. I'll add this prompt as another custom prompt to my project. So inside `Claude commands`, I'll create a new file. I'll name this one `mergeworktree`.

Paste it in. Then I'll restart Claude Code. Then I'll run the `mergeworktree` command. I'll first ask it to merge the `document test` branch I just created.

Then I can repeat this process for all the other worktrees. When I merge the Subtract feature, there's a merge conflict, but Claude automatically resolves it for me. Once everything is merged, I can have Claude clean up all these different worktrees that were created. And that's it.

So I've now implemented four separate features completely in parallel by using Git worktrees. This is clearly a huge productivity gain. And it scales to as many different instances as I, as an engineer, feel I can manage at the same time.

---

### 83. Automated Debugging

> Type: Video | Duration: 4:24 | Video: 10 - 006 - Automated Debugging.mp4

Claude Code's capabilities go far beyond writing code in your editor. It can monitor your deployed applications, detect bugs in production, and even fix them automatically. This creates a powerful workflow where Claude acts as your automated debugging assistant, catching issues that only appear in production.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542967%2F10_-_006_-_Automated_Debugging_00.1748542967081.jpg)

## Production Issues

Here's a common scenario: your application runs perfectly in your development environment, but has problems in production. You might have a chatbot that correctly answers simple questions locally, but fails when generating artifacts like spreadsheets after being deployed to AWS Amplify. The request appears to succeed, but the result is empty or incomplete.

Traditionally, you'd need to dig into CloudWatch logs, look for error messages, and manually debug the differences between your local and production environments. This process is very time-consuming and requires you to switch from development work to operational troubleshooting.

## Automated Error Detection and Fixing

Instead of debugging manually, you can set up Claude to handle the entire workflow automatically. Here's how the system works:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748542967%2F10_-_006_-_Automated_Debugging_14.1748542967617.jpg)

The automated workflow follows these steps:

1.  A GitHub Action runs daily (usually in the early morning)
2.  Claude queries CloudWatch for errors from the past 24 hours
3.  It filters and deduplicates the errors to fit within context limits
4.  Claude analyzes each error and attempts to fix it
5.  The fixed code is committed, and a pull request is automatically opened

## Setting Up the Workflow

The GitHub Action needs several components to work effectively:

-   `repository checkout` and dependency installation
-   Claude Code setup and configuration
-   Installation of the AWS CLI to access CloudWatch
-   Error filtering logic to manage context window limits
-   Automatic commits and pull request creation

When Claude finds errors in your logs, it doesn't just identify them—it understands the context. For example, if you have an invalid model identifier that only affects production (such as `us.anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20241021-v2:0` instead of the correct `us.anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20240624-v1:0`), Claude can recognize this pattern and apply the appropriate fix.

## How It Works in Practice

When the automated system runs successfully, you'll see a pull request that contains:

-   A clear description of the error in plain language
-   A `root cause analysis`
-   The specific fix that was implemented
-   Code updated with the correct model identifier or configuration

This pull request becomes a reviewable artifact, where you can clearly see what Claude found and how it fixed the problem. This lets you maintain your code review practices while staying confident in the changes.

## Customizing Your Debugging Workflow

This automated debugging approach is very flexible. You can adapt it to your specific needs:

-   Adjust the error detection frequency
-   Customize which types of errors to prioritize
-   Add application-specific debugging instructions
-   Integrate with different logging systems beyond CloudWatch
-   Set up notifications for critical issues

The key is Claude Code's ability to understand your application context, intelligently analyze production errors, and propose fixes that account for environment-specific differences. This transforms debugging from a reactive, manual process into a proactive, automated system that keeps your application running smoothly.

#### Video Transcript

Cloud Code isn't just for helping you write code in your editor. After you deploy your application, it can also help you monitor for errors and assist in fixing them when they appear. So in this video, I'll walk you through an example workflow where we'll observe a production application that's experiencing errors in production. We'll look at how to use Cloud Code to automatically discover and fix these errors.

First, let me show you a small example application I made. This is a very simple chatbot, currently running locally on my own computer. You can see the evidence, I'm currently on localhost 3000.

If I ask any question here, like "What is one plus one," no problem. I get an answer quickly. This chat application can also create simple artifacts. For example, it can display a built-in spreadsheet.

I'll ask it to create a spreadsheet with some fake data. It looks like everything works 100% as expected. Remember, this application is currently running in my local development environment. But since everything works fine when testing now, I might decide to deploy it to production.

I've already done that in advance. I've deployed this exact same application to AWS Amplify. So this is the production version of the exact same application, now hosted on Amplify. I'll repeat the exact same test I just showed you.

I'll first ask "What is one plus one." Then I'll immediately make the same request as I did just now in the local application. That is, create a spreadsheet with some fake data. We'll see the request go out.

But unfortunately, the spreadsheet itself is completely empty. No data was actually generated. So now it's clear that something in this production environment isn't working as expected. Everything in the development environment is fine.

The exact same sequence of operations is completely fine in the development environment, but specifically in production, I encounter this error. To find out what's wrong, I can look at my CloudWatch logs. I've already opened those logs, and I've searched and found the source of this error. So I have an error message here.

It says the provided model identifier is invalid. And there's a lot of debugging information included here as well. So as an engineer, I could take this error message that I painstakingly found in the logs, do some local debugging, and try to figure out why this fails in production but not locally. Or, I could delegate this whole task to Claude.

Let me demonstrate. In the GitHub repository for this project, I created a GitHub Action that runs automatically every day in the early morning. On screen now are the results of a sample run from earlier today. I'll show you some logs from this GitHub Action later.

But first, let me show you a diagram that will help you understand what's happening inside. Okay, so whenever this GitHub Action runs, it checks out my repository, installs some dependencies, and then installs and sets up Cloud Code. Then I also install an AWS CLI, which lets me access and fetch some logs from CloudWatch. Then I pass some instructions to Claude.

I have it access CloudWatch and find all the errors that occurred in the past 24 hours. I also include some logic to remove duplicate errors and reduce the total number of errors so that Claude's context window can handle them. Once Claude has the list of errors, it goes through them and tries to fix each one. Then, once Claude has hopefully successfully fixed these errors, it commits the changes and automatically opens a pull request where I can review its work.

So in this case, just like we just saw, I encountered an error in our production environment. I saw that error in the logs. If I now go here to my list of pull requests, I'd say Claude ran automatically, it automatically discovered the problem, applied a fix, and then created a pull request. So now I can very easily review this pull request.

The entire problem is explained to me in plain language, and in this case, it was just a typo of my own. I accidentally typed an invalid model ID that's only used in production. So Claude noticed this. It found the correct model ID and swapped in the correct one, then committed the change.

So now I can very easily review the change and merge the pull request. This is just one example workflow you might use to automatically monitor and fix applications in production. Remember, Cloud Code is very flexible, and you can create custom workflows like this to help with your own debugging efforts.

---

### 84. Computer Use

> Type: Video | Duration: 3:05 | Video: 10 - 007 - Computer Use.mp4

Computer Use is a powerful feature that lets Claude interact directly with a desktop environment, essentially giving the AI the ability to control a computer like a human would. This capability opens up entirely new possibilities for automation, testing, and workflow assistance.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543021%2F10_-_007_-_Computer_Use_00.1748543021569.jpg)

## What Computer Use Can Do

Instead of just generating code or providing suggestions, Claude can actually browse websites, click buttons, fill out forms, and interact with applications in real time. This makes it very useful for tasks such as:

- Automated `QA testing` of web applications
- Data entry and form filling
- Website navigation and information gathering
- `UI testing` and validation
- Repetitive desktop tasks

## Real-World Example: Automated QA Testing

Here's a practical example that shows the power of Computer Use. Imagine you built a React component with autocomplete functionality—users can type @ to mention files or resources. At first glance, everything seems to work well, but there might be some edge cases you haven't discovered yet.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543022%2F10_-_007_-_Computer_Use_05.1748543022086.jpg)

Instead of manually testing all possible interactions, you can delegate this work to Claude. You simply provide instructions like the following:

```
Your goal is to perform QA testing on a React component hosted at https://test-mentioner.vercel.app/

Testing process:
1. Open a new browser tab
2. Navigate to https://test-mentioner.vercel.app/
3. Execute the following test cases one by one
4. After completing all tests, write a concise report

Test cases:
1. Typing 'Did you read @' should display autocomplete options
2. Typing 'Did you read @' then pressing Enter should add '@document.pdf'
3. After adding '@document.pdf', pressing Backspace should display the autocomplete options directly below the text, not elsewhere on the page
```

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543022%2F10_-_007_-_Computer_Use_16.1748543022490.jpg)

## How It Works in Practice

Computer Use runs in a controlled environment—typically a `Docker` container with a browser, completely isolated from your main system. You interact with Claude through a chat interface, giving it instructions, and then watch how it navigates and interacts with the application.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543023%2F10_-_007_-_Computer_Use_17.1748543022870.jpg)

Claude operates step by step according to your instructions, taking screenshots, clicking elements, typing text, and observing the results. In the QA testing example, Claude will:

1.  Navigate to the specified URL
2.  Type the test content and observe the autocomplete behavior
3.  Test the Enter key functionality
4.  Check the Backspace behavior to ensure correct positioning
5.  Generate a detailed report explaining what passed and what failed

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543023%2F10_-_007_-_Computer_Use_18.1748543023770.jpg)

## Results

After running all the test cases, Claude provides a comprehensive report. In our example, it might find that the first two tests passed (autocomplete displays correctly, the Enter key works fine), but the third test failed because pressing Backspace caused the autocomplete dropdown to appear in the wrong location.

This kind of automated testing can save developers a lot of time, especially for repetitive QA tasks or when you need to quickly test multiple scenarios. Instead of manually clicking through every possible interaction, you can describe what you want to test and let Claude handle the execution.

## Security and Isolation

For safety, Computer Use runs in a sandboxed environment. The browser and desktop environment run inside a `Docker` container, completely isolated from your main system. This means Claude can interact with web applications and test interfaces without posing any risk to your personal files or system security.

This isolation is crucial, because it lets you give Claude broad permissions to interact with applications, while maintaining full control over what it can and cannot access.

#### Video Transcript

Let's take a look at Claude's Computer Use feature. We'll cover it in three parts. First, I'll give you a quick demonstration of Computer Use to help you understand why it's useful. Then, we'll look at some behind-the-scenes details to understand how Computer Use works. Finally, I'll show you how to set up the reference implementation so you can test Computer Use on your own computer.

So, let's get started. First, let's do a quick demonstration of Computer Use. For this demo, I prepared a very small web application. It just displays a text area that supports using the @ symbol to mention files or some kind of resource.

So if I type test and then @, I can scroll through a bunch of different options, and finally press Enter to select that particular option. Now, at first glance, this thing seems to work pretty well. I can add as many mentions as I want. But if we use it for a little longer, we start to see a lot of `janky behavior`.

For example, if I add two mentions and then press Backspace, I see a window suddenly pop up in the top-left corner of the screen. So clearly, this thing doesn't work entirely as expected. Now, I could spend time sitting here as a software engineer, figuring out where this component fails in all the different scenarios. Or, I could delegate this task to Claude's Computer Use.

Let me show you how. I've already set up the Computer Use environment in the current window. So this is a demo that we'll set up on your computer later. On the right is a browser running inside a Docker container.

So this browser is completely isolated from the rest of my system. Then on the left, I have a chat interface where I can directly give Claude instructions, telling Claude to interact with this browser in some specific way. In that chat interface, I'll type a fairly large prompt to Claude. I'll tell Claude that it will perform some `QA testing` on a React component hosted at a particular address.

Then I outline for Claude some testing process, along with some different test cases. Finally, I ask Claude to write a concise report summarizing the output of all these tests. So, once again, I'm using Claude Computer Use to automate some QA testing, just to save myself some time. I'll type this large prompt into this chat interface, and then Claude will immediately get to work.

Claude will follow the instructions I laid out, so it will try to navigate to the website where I'm hosting this application, then execute those different test cases one by one. The first test case will just verify whether the autocomplete options appear. The second test case will verify whether pressing Enter inserts a mention. The third will make sure that when Backspace is pressed, the autocomplete options appear below the mention itself, rather than in the top-left corner.

After all the test cases have run, we'll see some output in the chat window. So it tells me that the first test and the second test passed, but the third failed. So this once again reminds me that I might need to go investigate this test case myself and figure out how to fix it. In any case, Claude's Computer Use feature saved me, the developer, a lot of time on the QA process.

---

### 85. How Computer Use Works

> Type: Video | Duration: 3:26 | Video: 10 - 008 - How Computer Use Works.mp4

Computer Use in Claude works exactly the same way as regular Tool Use—it's just a special implementation of the same underlying tool system. Understanding this connection makes Computer Use much easier to grasp and implement.

## Tool Use Recap

Before diving into Computer Use, let's quickly review how regular Tool Use works with Claude:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543025%2F10_-_008_-_How_Computer_Use_Works_01.1748543025424.jpg)

1.  You send Claude a user message and a `tool schema`
2.  Claude decides it needs to use a tool to answer the question
3.  Claude responds with a `tool use request` containing the tool name and input parameters
4.  Your server runs the actual function and returns the result
5.  You send the result back to Claude as a `tool result message`

For example, if someone asks "What's the weather in San Francisco?", you'd provide a `get_weather` tool schema. Claude would call the tool with the location parameter, your code would fetch the weather data, and then you'd return "sunny" to Claude.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543026%2F10_-_008_-_How_Computer_Use_Works_07.1748543026297.jpg)

## Computer Use: Same Flow, Different Tool

Computer Use follows exactly the same pattern. The key insight is that Computer Use is implemented as a tool—just a very special tool that can interact with a desktop environment.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543026%2F10_-_008_-_How_Computer_Use_Works_08.1748543026655.jpg)

Here's what happens:

-   You include a tool schema that provides computer interaction capabilities
-   Claude decides to use the computer tool
-   Instead of running a simple function, you execute the requested action in a computing environment
-   You send the result (such as a screenshot) back to Claude

## The Computer Tool Schema

The Computer Use tool schema starts out simple, but is automatically expanded into something more comprehensive:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543027%2F10_-_008_-_How_Computer_Use_Works_09.1748543027109.jpg)

You send a basic schema, like the following:

```
{
  "type": "computer_20250124",
  "name": "computer",
  "display_width_px": 1024,
  "display_height_px": 768,
  "display_number": 1
}
```

Behind the scenes, this gets expanded into a detailed schema that tells Claude it can perform actions like:

-   `key` - Press a keyboard key
-   `mouse_move` - Move the cursor
-   `left_click` - Click at a specific coordinate
-   `screenshot` - Take a screenshot
-   `scroll` - Scroll the screen

## The Computing Environment

For Computer Use to work properly, you need an actual computing environment that can execute these actions programmatically. The most common approach is to use a `Docker` container with a desktop environment (such as Firefox).

When Claude requests an action like "click at coordinate (500, 300)", your system will:

1.  Receive the tool use request
2.  Execute the mouse click in the Docker container
3.  Take a screenshot of the result
4.  Send the screenshot back to Claude

The Docker container doesn't have to be complex—it just needs to support programmatic keyboard and mouse interaction.

## Getting Started

You don't need to build a computing environment from scratch. Anthropic provides a reference implementation that handles all the technical details.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543027%2F10_-_008_-_How_Computer_Use_Works_17.1748543027671.jpg)

The setup process is very straightforward:

1.  Install Docker (you may already have it)
2.  Run the provided Docker command with your API key
3.  Access the web interface to chat with Claude

The setup command is as follows:

```
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="your_api_key"
docker run \
  -e ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=$ANTHROPIC_API_KEY \
  -v $HOME/.anthropic:/home/computeruse/.anthropic \
  -p 5900:5900 \
  -p 8501:8501 \
  -p 6080:6080 \
  -p 8080:8080 \
  -it ghcr.io/anthropics/anthropic-quickstarts:computer-use-demo-latest
```

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543028%2F10_-_008_-_How_Computer_Use_Works_19.1748543027991.jpg)

Once it's running, you'll have access to a chat interface where you can directly test Claude's Computer Use capabilities. The complete setup guide is available at github.com/anthropics/anthropic-quickstarts.

## Key Takeaways

Computer Use is not magic—it's just the regular tool use system applied to desktop automation. Claude doesn't directly control the computer; instead, it issues tool requests, and your code fulfills those requests by executing actions in a controlled environment. This makes Computer Use both powerful and safe, because you have full control over what actually gets executed.

#### Video Transcript

Now that we've seen a demonstration of Computer Use, let's take a deeper look at how Computer Use works behind the scenes. To help you understand Computer Use, let's first quickly review how Tool Use works with Claude. So, whenever we want to use a tool, we send a request to Claude that includes a user message and some `tool schema`. This tool schema describes some additional functionality we want to expose to Claude.

So, in this example, I might send a tool schema like GetWeather, which roughly lets Claude get the weather for a particular location, like San Francisco. Claude looks at the user's message and realizes it can't answer this question on its own. So it decides to use that GetWeather tool. To use this tool, Claude returns a tool use section.

This will contain a tool use ID, a name, and some input it wants to pass into the tool. In this case, it's most likely a location of San Francisco. Then our server will have some code, written by you and me, that takes that location and returns the current weather for that location. We'll return that result to Claude in a tool result section.

So we can summarize the entire process with a diagram like this. We initially send Claude some kind of question and a series of tools, Claude decides to use a tool, we run some code or do something on Claude's behalf, and then send the result back to Claude. It turns out that Computer Use follows exactly the same flow, because Computer Use itself is actually implemented with this exact same tool system. So here's how Computer Use actually works.

We send Claude a request that contains a very special tool schema. The schema we send is very small, just like what you see on the left. And it doesn't conform to the typical structure of a tool schema.

Behind the scenes, this schema will be expanded into a much larger structure, like the one you see on the right. And it's this larger structure that actually gets fed into Claude. This large schema tells Claude that it can call an action function, and that this action function accepts parameters like `mouse move`, `left click`, `screenshot`, and so on. Claude might then decide to use that tool in some way, so it sends us back a tool use section.

Recall the git weather function we just saw. Whenever Claude decides to use a tool, it's up to us developers to fulfill Claude's tool use request in some way. So, to simulate a computing environment for Claude, we can set up a Docker container running some program that can programmatically perform key presses and mouse movements. Once we've executed the requested key press or mouse movement, we send a response back to Claude.

So all in all, that's exactly what happens behind the scenes with Computer Use. Claude doesn't actually operate the computer directly. Instead, Computer Use is implemented using the tool system. And providing the actual computing environment is up to us.

Finally, I want to tell you how you can get started with Computer Use yourself. Fortunately, you don't need to create that Docker container from scratch yourself. Anthropic has already implemented a reference implementation. This is a Docker container that already includes the code to receive tool requests from Claude and execute programmatic mouse movements and key presses inside the container.

I've provided a link to the reference implementation on the left. Setting up this implementation is very simple. You just need a Docker implementation. You probably already have one installed.

You'll execute a simple Docker command, and then you'll have access to the interface I just showed you. Then you can chat directly with Claude on the left side of the screen and test Claude's Computer Use feature.

---

### 87. Agents and Workflows

> Type: Video | Duration: 4:22 | Video: 11 - 001 - Agents and Workflows.mp4

Workflows and agents are strategies for handling user tasks that Claude can't complete in a single request. In fact, you've been creating both throughout this course—when you use tools and let Claude figure out how to complete a task, that's an agent.

## When to Use a Workflow vs. an Agent

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543103%2F11_-_001_-_Agents_and_Workflows_01.1748543103044.jpg)

This decision depends on how well you understand the task:

-   **Use a workflow** when you can envision the exact process or steps Claude should follow to solve the problem, or when your application's user experience confines the user to a series of tasks
-   **Use an agent** when you're not sure what task or task parameters to give Claude

A workflow is a series of calls to Claude designed to solve a specific problem through a predetermined sequence of steps. An agent gives Claude a goal and a set of tools, expecting Claude to figure out how to accomplish the goal on its own using the provided tools.

## Example: Image-to-CAD Workflow

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543103%2F11_-_001_-_Agents_and_Workflows_06.1748543103613.jpg)

Let's look at a practical workflow example. Imagine you're building a web application where users can drag and drop an image of a metal part, and you create a `STEP file` (the industry-standard format for 3D models) from it.

Because we have a fairly clear idea of exactly what to do after a user provides an image file, and we can easily write all of this in code as a series of predefined steps, this makes it a perfect candidate for a workflow.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543104%2F11_-_001_-_Agents_and_Workflows_07.1748543103955.jpg)

Here's the breakdown of the workflow steps:

1.  Feed the image into Claude and have it describe the object
2.  Based on the description, have Claude use the `CadQuery` library to model the object
3.  Create a rendering
4.  Have Claude compare the rendering against the original image and score it. If there are problems, fix them

## Evaluator-Optimizer Pattern

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543104%2F11_-_001_-_Agents_and_Workflows_15.1748543104283.jpg)

This modeling workflow is an example of the evaluator-optimizer pattern. Here's how it works:

-   **`Producer`**: Takes input and creates output (Claude uses CadQuery to model the part and create a rendering)
-   **`Grader`**: Evaluates the output against certain criteria
-   **`Feedback loop`**: If the grader doesn't accept the output, feedback goes back to the producer for improvement
-   **`Iteration`**: The loop repeats until the grader accepts the output

## Why Learn Workflow Patterns

The goal of identifying different workflows is to give you a set of repeatable recipes for implementing your own features. The evaluator-optimizer is a workflow pattern that other engineers have already proven effective—consider using it in your own applications!

Remember, identifying workflows by itself doesn't do anything for us—we still need to write the actual code to implement them. But these patterns have been proven successful by many engineers, so they're worth understanding and applying to your own projects.

#### Video Transcript

In this module, we'll focus on workflows and agents. Let's get started right away by understanding what these things are. Workflows and agents are strategies we use to handle user tasks that Claude can't complete in a single request. Believe it or not, you've already been creating workflows and agents throughout this course.

For example, when learning about tools, we fed tasks into Claude and relied on Claude to use the provided tools to figure out how to complete them. That's an example of an agent. Now, here's the rule of thumb we use when deciding whether to create a workflow or an agent. If we have a very precise idea of the task that needs to be completed, and we know the exact series of steps required to complete it, we'll use a workflow.

Otherwise, if we're not quite sure about the details of the task Claude needs to solve, we'll use an agent. In this video and the next few sections, we'll focus 100% on workflows. I'll show you a few examples of workflows, so let's look at our first example now. Let's imagine we're building a small web application.

The goal of this application is to let users drag and drop an image of a metal part onto the screen. Then we'll take that image and somehow build a 3D model from it. Then we'll return something called a `step file` to the user. Now, if you're not familiar with `step files`, that's okay, a `step file` is just an industry-standard way to communicate or share 3D models.

So, essentially, we're making a 3D model from an image. Now, even if you're not very familiar with 3D modeling, with a little help and a little time, I bet you could figure out a way to implement this application. We might do it like this. We might take the image the user uploaded, feed it into Claude, and ask Claude to describe the object in detail.

Then we can take that description, feed it into Claude again separately, and ask Claude to use a Python library called `CAD Query` to model the object. `CAD Query` is a Python library that lets you do 3D solid modeling, and you can output a `step file` from that process. Now, it's entirely possible that Claude won't get the model completely accurate on the first try. So once we've built this initial model, we might decide to add a small error-checking step, where we can create a rendering as a normal image, then feed that image back to Claude and ask how it performs compared to the original image the user uploaded.

If Claude thinks there's a significant problem with our rendering, we can go back to the second step and ask Claude to try rendering the part again. Then we can repeat this process over and over, until hopefully we eventually get an accurate model that matches the original part. Now, the important thing to understand here is that this is a complete sequence of steps that we can envision in advance. We can sit down and design out the entire process.

We can easily write some code to implement it. In fact, I've implemented almost exactly the same thing before. Because we can explicitly lay out and detail all these steps in advance, we call this a workflow. Remember, we define a workflow as a series of calls to Claude designed to solve a very specific problem, where we know exactly what those steps should be in advance.

This modeling workflow I described is an example of what we call the "evaluator-optimizer" pattern. The idea behind this workflow is that we push some input into something called a "producer." In our case, the producer is Claude using the `CAD Query` library to model the part and then create a rendering from it. This output, the rendering, is fed into something called a "grader."

The grader looks at the output and decides whether it meets certain criteria. If it does, then the workflow is done. Otherwise, if the output doesn't meet certain criteria, feedback is sent back to the producer, and the producer will get a chance to improve the output in some way. Then this loop repeats over and over, until the grader eventually accepts the output.

Now, by this point, you probably have a fairly reasonable understanding of this "evaluator-optimizer" pattern. But you might be wondering, okay, what's the deal with these workflows? So, here I just want to quickly clarify one point. Identifying workflows by itself doesn't do anything for us.

We still need to write down and code out the actual code that implements these things. The only reason we discuss workflows, and the reason you'll see workflows become a hot topic of discussion, is that many other engineers have implemented workflows using these exact same patterns and achieved great success. So the reason I'm showing you these different workflows is so that you can use these same patterns in your own projects, and hopefully achieve some success with them, since they work well for other engineers.

---

### 88. Parallelization Workflows

> Type: Video | Duration: 3:44 | Video: 11 - 002 - Parallelization Workflows.mp4
