When building AI applications, you'll often encounter tasks that seem simple on the surface, but become complex when you try to implement them effectively. Let's explore a powerful pattern called the parallelization workflow, which can help you break complex tasks into manageable, focused pieces.

## The Problem with a Complex Single Prompt

Imagine you're building a materials designer application where users upload an image of a part and receive a recommendation for the best material to use. Your first instinct might be to send the image to Claude with a simple prompt asking it to choose between metal, polymer, ceramic, composite, elastomer, or wood.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543069%2F11_-_002_-_Parallelization_Workflows_02.1748543069350.jpg)

While this approach might work, you're actually asking Claude to do a lot of heavy lifting in a single request. Without specific criteria for each material type, the reliability of the results will be greatly diminished.

You might think to improve this by adding detailed criteria for each material into one massive prompt. But this creates a new problem—Claude has to process all these different considerations at once, which can lead to confusion and suboptimal results.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543069%2F11_-_002_-_Parallelization_Workflows_06.1748543069782.jpg)

## A Better Approach: Parallelization

Instead of cramming everything into one request, you can split the task into multiple parallel requests. Each request focuses on evaluating the part's suitability for one specific material type using specialized criteria.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543070%2F11_-_002_-_Parallelization_Workflows_09.1748543070332.jpg)

Here's how it works:

-   Send the same image to Claude multiple times simultaneously
-   Each request contains specialized criteria for one material (metal criteria, polymer criteria, ceramic criteria, etc.)
-   Claude independently evaluates the part's suitability for each material
-   Collect all the analysis results and feed them into a final aggregation step

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543070%2F11_-_002_-_Parallelization_Workflows_11.1748543070706.jpg)

The final step returns all the independent analysis results to Claude and asks it to compare these results and make a final material recommendation.

## How Parallelization Workflows Work

The parallelization pattern follows a simple structure:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543071%2F11_-_002_-_Parallelization_Workflows_15.1748543071284.jpg)

-   **Split a single task into multiple subtasks** - Break a complex decision into focused, specialized evaluations
-   **Run subtasks in parallel** - Execute all evaluations simultaneously to speed up processing
-   **Aggregate the results** - Combine the specialized analyses into a final decision
-   **Parallelized subtasks don't have to be identical** - Each subtask can have a specialized prompt, tool set, or evaluation criteria

## The Benefits of This Approach

Parallelization workflows offer several key advantages:

Focus: Claude can focus on one specific aspect at a time, rather than trying to balance multiple competing considerations simultaneously. This makes the analysis of each material type more thorough and accurate.

Easy to optimize: You can independently improve and test the prompt for each material evaluation. If your metal analysis isn't working well, you can optimize just that prompt without affecting the others.

Better scalability: Adding a new material to evaluate is very straightforward—you just add another parallel request. You don't need to rewrite existing prompts, and you don't have to worry about how new criteria might interfere with existing ones.

Higher reliability: By breaking down complex tasks, you reduce the cognitive load on the AI model and get more consistent, reliable results.

## When to Use Parallelization

This pattern is great when your complex decision can be broken down into independent evaluations. Look for situations where you're asking the AI to consider multiple criteria, compare several options, or make a decision that involves different areas of expertise.

The key is identifying tasks that can be meaningfully separated—each parallel subtask should be able to operate independently and contribute a unique piece of analysis to the final decision.

#### Video Transcript

Thank you. Let's look at another workflow. This time we're going to change our application slightly. We'll still ask the user to drag and drop an image of a part onto the screen.

But this time, based on various criteria, we'll return an analysis report to the user telling them the best material to use to make their part. To implement this feature, we might send the image the user provided to Claude along with a short prompt. In the prompt, we might ask Claude to decide whether it's best to make this part out of metal, polymer, ceramic, and so on. Now, this might work, but in this very simple prompt, we're really asking a lot of Claude.

For example, we're not really telling Claude what actual factors to consider when deciding which material to use. So, even though this might work, we might not get the best results. So a natural improvement might be to go back to this prompt and add more detail. Maybe tell Claude in which different scenarios it should recommend metal or polymer, and so on.

So we might end up with a very, very large prompt like this. We might give some criteria for deciding when to use metal, then some criteria for deciding when to use polymer, then repeat this process for ceramic, composite, wood, and so on. We'd end up with a very, very large prompt, which might confuse Claude a bit, because it needs to do a lot of analysis and a lot of work in a single step. So this might not lead to the best results.

Let me show you a better way to implement this feature. We can decide to make a series of different requests to Claude in parallel when the user first submits the image. Each individual request can contain a specialized prompt asking Claude whether it's a good idea to make this given part out of metal, polymer, ceramic, composite, and so on. So in each individual request, we're asking Claude about the suitability of making this part out of a single material.

With this approach, we can specialize each individual prompt for the given material. Now, Claude doesn't have to worry about all these different materials. It really only needs to focus on one material at a time. Now, when we eventually get some responses back from Claude, I'll change the structure of this diagram slightly so I can fit everything on one screen.

So we'll get these individual analysis results back from Claude. Each one will tell us the suitability of making the given part out of metal, polymer, ceramic, composite, and so on. Then we can take each of these analysis results and feed them back into Claude in a subsequent request, and ask Claude to consider each different analysis result and decide on the final material to use. Now, Claude really doesn't have to worry about comparing all these different materials up front.

Instead, it can just look at the analysis results that seem most promising. This is an example of a parallelization workflow. The idea behind a parallelization workflow is that we break a task into multiple different subtasks. Each of these subtasks can run in parallel, that is, simultaneously.

Then, we'll bring all the results from these different subtasks together in a final aggregator step. In our example, the aggregator is this last step of Claude. So we feed the results of each parallel task into the aggregator, and Claude gives us this final recommendation. This workflow has several benefits.

First, it lets Claude focus on one task at a time. So, recall how I just told you we could feed the original image part into Claude along with a very large prompt listing criteria for many different material types. In that case, when Claude tries to consider all the different pros and cons of each material simultaneously, it might get a bit confused or distracted. The second benefit is that we can very easily improve and evaluate the prompt used in each subtask.

Finally, this process usually scales well. We can add additional subtasks at any time without really affecting the other subtasks being executed.

---

### 89. Chaining Workflows

> Type: Video | Duration: 4:37 | Video: 11 - 003 - Chaining Workflows.mp4

Chaining workflows might seem obvious at first, but they're actually one of the most useful patterns you'll encounter when working with Claude. This approach becomes especially valuable when you're dealing with complex tasks or long prompts that Claude has trouble handling consistently.

## What Is a Workflow Chain?

Chaining workflows break a large, complex task into smaller, sequential subtasks. Instead of having Claude do everything at once, you split the work into focused steps that depend on each other.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543144%2F11_-_003_-_Chaining_Workflows_03.1748543144730.jpg)

Here's a practical example: imagine you're building a social media marketing tool that automatically creates and publishes videos. Instead of using one massive prompt to have Claude handle everything, you can break it down like this:

-   Find relevant trending topics on Twitter
-   Select the most interesting topic (using Claude)
-   Research that topic (using Claude)
-   Write a script for a short video (using Claude)
-   Create the video using an AI avatar and text-to-speech technology
-   Publish the video to social media

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543145%2F11_-_003_-_Chaining_Workflows_08.1748543145309.jpg)

## Why Chaining Instead of One Big Prompt?

You might wonder why not combine all of Claude's tasks into one prompt. The key benefit is **focus**—when you give Claude one specific task at a time, it can concentrate on doing that one well, rather than juggling multiple demands simultaneously.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543145%2F11_-_003_-_Chaining_Workflows_09.1748543145813.jpg)

The chaining approach offers several advantages:

-   Breaks a large task into smaller, non-parallelizable subtasks
-   Allows optional non-LLM processing between each task
-   Lets Claude focus on one aspect of the overall task

## The Long Prompt Problem

This is where chaining becomes truly valuable. You'll often encounter situations where you need Claude to write content with many specific constraints. Suppose you want Claude to write a technical article, and you specify that it should:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543146%2F11_-_003_-_Chaining_Workflows_11.1748543146214.jpg)

-   Not mention that it was written by AI
-   Avoid using emojis
-   Avoid clichés or overly casual language
-   Write in a professional, technical tone

Even with all these constraints clearly stated, Claude might still generate content that violates some of your rules. You might get an article that still uses emojis, mentions AI authorship, or sounds unprofessional.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543146%2F11_-_003_-_Chaining_Workflows_13.1748543146542.jpg)

## The Chaining Solution

Instead of fighting with one massive prompt, use a two-step chaining approach:

Step one: Send your initial prompt and accept that the first result might not be perfect. Claude will generate an article, but it might violate some of your constraints.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543147%2F11_-_003_-_Chaining_Workflows_14.1748543146936.jpg)

Step two: Make a follow-up request specifically focused on fixing the problems. Provide Claude with the article it just wrote, and give it targeted revision instructions:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543148%2F11_-_003_-_Chaining_Workflows_17.1748543148012.jpg)

`Please revise the article provided below.

Rewrite the article following these steps:
1. Identify any places in the text that identify the author as AI and remove them
2. Find and remove all emojis
3. Identify any awkward phrasing and replace it with text a technical writer would use`

This approach works because Claude can fully focus on the revision task, rather than trying to balance content creation with constraint adherence.

## When to Use Chaining Workflows

Chaining workflows are especially useful in the following situations:

- You have a complex task with multiple requirements
- Claude consistently ignores certain constraints in a long prompt
- You need to process or validate output between steps
- You want to keep each interaction focused and manageable

While chaining workflows might seem like extra work, they often produce better results than trying to cram everything into a single prompt. The key is recognizing when a task is complex enough to benefit from being broken into focused, sequential steps.

#### Video Transcript

The next workflow we'll look at might seem a bit obvious and simple, but trust me, this actually ends up being one of the most useful workflows, especially in one particular situation you'll encounter often. So let's get started. Let's change our application again. We'll assume we're building a social media marketing tool.

The user will be asked to enter a topic, what the focus of their social media account is. Then the goal of our application is to generate and publish some videos for their account. So we might implement it like this. We don't actually need a complex agent with a bunch of fancy tools to accomplish this.

We can build a workflow that executes a series of predefined steps one by one. So in the first step, we might take whatever topic the user entered in the form and search Twitter for trending topics. Then we can feed the entire list of topics into Claude and ask Claude to choose the most interesting topic. Next, we can make a follow-up request to Claude asking it to do some web research on that topic.

After the research is done, we can ask Claude to write a script for a short video. Once we have the script, we can use an AI avatar and some text-to-speech program to create an actual video, and finally publish that video to social media. This is an example of a chaining workflow. In a chaining workflow, we take a large task, which initially is to generate some videos and publish them to social media, and break it into a series of separate steps.

In our example, the subtasks are the individual calls we make to Claude. We could have tried to do all three of these tasks in a single call to Claude. So we could have fed a bunch of topics into Claude, asked it to choose the most interesting topic, research that topic, and write a script all in one prompt. But by breaking it into three separate calls, we let Claude focus on just one separate task at a time.

Now, like I said, this might seem like a simple and obvious workflow, maybe not even worth discussing, since you've probably implemented something like it before. But I'm specifically pointing out this workflow for a very particular reason, because it actually ends up being one of the most important workflows for understanding how to consistently get high-quality output from Claude when using fairly long prompts. So let me walk you through a little scenario. You may not have encountered this situation before, but I can almost guarantee you'll run into it at some point.

Let's imagine you're using Claude to write an article on some topic. You might initially just send Claude a very simple prompt asking it to write an article, and you might get back some results that, while perhaps okay, have some aspects you don't like. So you might initially find that Claude might mention that it's an AI writing the article, which you might not want. It might overuse emojis, which you might also not want.

And it might use some clichéd language throughout, which you also might not want. So over time, as you begin to refine this prompt, you might end up building a long list telling Claude not to do these things at all. But inevitably, no matter how many times you repeat these items, Claude might still end up giving you a response that always seems to use emojis, mention that it was written by AI, and generally have an off-putting, unprofessional tone.

And no matter how many times you repeat these constraints or things Claude shouldn't do, Claude might still insist on writing an article like this. So to solve this problem, you can use a very simple prompt chaining workflow. You can do it like this. You might enter that initial long prompt with all these constraints, and then just accept that you'll get an initial article that doesn't completely meet your requirements. Claude might inevitably decide to violate some of the different constraints you listed.

To fix these problems, you can make a follow-up request to Claude, providing the article Claude just wrote. Below the article, you can ask Claude to rewrite the article in some specific way. So you could say, find any places where the author claims to be an AI and remove that reference, find and remove all emojis, then rewrite the text in the style of a professional technical writer. By using this chaining workflow and breaking the task into multiple steps, you let Claude focus more on each separate task presented to it.

So, even if it initially might not have met all the requirements you laid out in the long prompt, the follow-up prompt lets Claude focus only on the constraints you really care about, and hopefully rewrite the article in the style you really want. So again, even though prompt chaining seems like an obvious and simple thing, when you have a task with many constraints, and Claude doesn't always seem to adhere to those constraints as you'd expect, this really does become something you'll use often.

---

### 90. Routing Workflows

> Type: Video | Duration: 3:17 | Video: 11 - 004 - Routing Workflows.mp4

Routing workflows solve a common problem in AI applications: different types of user requests require different handling approaches. Instead of using a one-size-fits-all prompt, you can classify incoming requests and route them to specialized processing pipelines.

## The Problem with a Universal Prompt

Consider a social media marketing tool that generates video scripts based on a user's topic. Users might enter "programming" or "surfing" as their topic, but these two topics should produce very different types of content:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543142%2F11_-_004_-_Routing_Workflows_02.1748543142365.jpg)

A programming topic needs educational content with clear explanations and definitions. A surfing topic is better suited to an entertainment-focused script that emphasizes excitement and visual appeal. A single universal prompt can't handle both cases effectively.

## Setting Up Content Categories

The first step is to define the different types of content your application might need to generate. You could divide requests into the following types:

- Entertainment - Energetic, culturally relevant content that uses trendy language
- Educational - Clear, engaging explanations with relevant examples
- Comedy - Sharp, unexpected content with clever observations and timing
- Personal Vlog - Authentic, intimate content with conversational storytelling
- Review - Decisive, experience-based content that highlights pros and cons
- Storytelling - Immersive content using vivid details and emotional connection

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543143%2F11_-_004_-_Routing_Workflows_07.1748543143540.jpg)

Each category has its own specialized prompt template. For example, an educational prompt might ask Claude to "develop a clear, engaging script that turns complex information into easy-to-understand insights, using relevant examples and thought-provoking questions."

## How Routing Works in Practice

The routing process happens in two steps:

1.  **Classification** - Send the user's topic to Claude and ask it to classify it into one of your predefined types
2.  **Specialized processing** - Use the classification result to select the appropriate prompt template and generate content

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543144%2F11_-_004_-_Routing_Workflows_13.1748543144107.jpg)

For example, if the user enters "Python functions" as their topic, you'd first ask Claude to classify it:

```
Classify the video topic into one of the listed categories:
<topic>Python functions</topic>

<categories>
- Educational
- Entertainment  
- Comedy
- Personal Vlog
- Review
- Storytelling
</categories>
```

Claude responds "Educational," so you then use the educational prompt template to generate the actual script content.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543145%2F11_-_004_-_Routing_Workflows_15.1748543144938.jpg)

## Routing Workflow Architecture

The routing workflow follows this pattern:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543145%2F11_-_004_-_Routing_Workflows_17.1748543145286.jpg)

- The user input first enters a router component
- The router classifies the request through an initial Claude call
- Based on the category, the input is forwarded to a specific processing pipeline
- Each pipeline can have its own workflow, prompts, or tools optimized for that category

The key insight is that the user input only enters one specialized pipeline, not all of them. This lets each pipeline be highly optimized for its specific use case.

## When to Use Routing

Routing workflows work well in the following situations:

- Your application handles multiple request types that require different approaches
- You can clearly define categories that cover your use cases
- The classification step can be handled reliably by Claude
- The performance benefits of specialized processing outweigh the overhead of the routing step

This pattern is especially valuable for customer service bots, content generation tools, and any application where the "right" response depends largely on understanding the type of request being made.

#### Video Transcript

Our next workflow will show us a way to improve this social media marketing tool. So once again, let's imagine a user will enter a topic, and then we'll somehow produce some videos and publish them to the user's social media account. Now, regarding the script generation process, I really want you to think about something. In other words, the tone and language actually used in these different videos.

Given two different topics, like programming on the left and surfing on the right, we really expect to get video scripts of very different natures. So on the left, for programming, we want a lot of information, carefully explaining definitions, and generally probably a video intended to be educational in nature. If the user enters surfing as the topic, we probably want a very different video script. Probably something much less educational, without long definitions about what surfing is or anything like that.

So let me show you a workflow we can use to ensure that a given topic produces a video script that's very well suited to the nature of that topic. First, we'll sit down and think about all the different possible types of videos the user might ask us to create. So we might decide that the topic the user provides to us will fit into one of six different types. Entertainment, education, comedy, and so on.

So in our earlier example, programming might be educational, and surfing might be entertainment. Then for each different type, we might write out a script generation prompt. So if someone asks for a topic we classify as educational, we'd ask Claude to write a script using this prompt here. This prompt asks Claude to produce a clear, engaging script that includes some interesting examples and interesting questions, and so on.

If the user gives us a surfing topic, we might classify it as entertainment. Then we'd use this prompt here, set the topic to surfing, then feed the entire prompt into Claude and ask Claude to write a script about surfing, perhaps including some trendy language and an engaging opening, and so on. So let me show you what the entire process would look like in practice. We'd initially send Claude a request containing only the topic the user entered, like Python functions, and ask Claude to classify this topic into one of the different categories we just came up with.

In this case, Python functions is probably most closely related to the educational category. So then we'd take this response from Claude and make a follow-up request, asking Claude to write a script containing some clear, engaging information about Python functions, with thought-provoking examples. Then we'd presumably get a script with these different qualities and a tone suited to an educational video. This is an example of a routing workflow.

In a routing workflow, we take the user's original input and feed it into a routing step. This routing step is very likely a call to Claude itself, asking Claude to classify the user input or task in some way. Then, based on Claude's answer, we forward the user's input to some very specific downstream processing pipeline. So it could be this one here, or this one here, or this one here, and so on.

But probably just one of these three. Each of these different routing options might have a different workflow implemented within it, or a prompt or a custom set of tools tailored to handle the exact task the user requested.

---

### 91. Agents and Tools

> Type: Video | Duration: 5:02 | Video: 11 - 005 - Agents and Tools.mp4

Agents represent a shift away from the structured workflows we've been using. While workflows are perfect when you know exactly the steps needed to complete a task, agents shine when you're not sure what those steps should be. Instead of defining a rigid sequence, you give Claude a goal and a set of tools, and let it figure out how to combine those tools to achieve the goal on its own.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543186%2F11_-_005_-_Agents_and_Tools_00.1748543186504.jpg)

This flexibility makes agents attractive when building applications that need to handle a wide variety of unpredictable tasks. You can create an agent once, make sure it works reasonably well, and then deploy it to solve a broad range of problems. However, this flexibility also comes with trade-offs in reliability and cost that we'll explore later.

## How Tools Make Agents Possible

The real power of agents lies in their ability to combine simple tools in unexpected ways. Consider a basic set of date-time tools:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543187%2F11_-_005_-_Agents_and_Tools_04.1748543186919.jpg)

- get_current_datetime - Get the current date and time
- add_duration_to_datetime - Add time to a given date
- set_reminder - Create a reminder for a specific time

These tools look simple individually, but Claude can chain them together to handle surprisingly complex requests:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543187%2F11_-_005_-_Agents_and_Tools_05.1748543187417.jpg)

For "What time is it now?", Claude just needs to call get_current_datetime. But for "What day of the week will it be 11 days from now?", it would first call get_current_datetime, then call add_duration_to_datetime. To set a gym reminder for next Wednesday, it might use all three tools in sequence.

Claude can even recognize when it needs more information. If you ask "When does my 90-day warranty expire?", it knows it needs to ask when you purchased the item before calculating the expiration date.

## Tools Should Be Abstract

The key insight for building effective agents is to provide fairly abstract tools, rather than highly specialized ones. Claude Code perfectly demonstrates this principle.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543188%2F11_-_005_-_Agents_and_Tools_11.1748543187917.jpg)

Claude Code has access to general-purpose, flexible tools, such as:

- bash - Run any command
- read - Read any file
- write - Create any file
- edit - Modify a file
- glob - Find files
- grep - Search file contents

Notably, it doesn't have specialized tools like "refactor code" or "install dependencies." Instead, Claude figures out how to use these basic tools to accomplish those complex tasks itself. This abstraction allows it to handle countless programming scenarios that the developers never explicitly planned for.

## Best Practice: Composable Tools

When designing agents, provide tools that Claude can creatively combine. For example, a social media video agent might include:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543188%2F11_-_005_-_Agents_and_Tools_16.1748543188372.jpg)

- bash - Access FFMPEG for video processing
- generate_image - Create images from prompts
- text_to_speech - Convert text to audio
- post_media - Upload content to social platforms

This tool set enables both simple workflows (create and publish a video) and more interactive experiences, such as the agent first generating a sample image, getting user approval, and then proceeding with video creation.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543188%2F11_-_005_-_Agents_and_Tools_19.1748543188734.jpg)

The agent can adapt its approach based on the user's feedback and preferences, which is hard to achieve with rigid workflows. This flexibility is exactly what makes agents powerful for building dynamic, user-responsive applications.

#### Video Transcript

Now that we've looked at several different workflows, we'll switch over and start discussing agents. Agents are easiest to understand if you think back to workflows, specifically when we actually used them. Workflows work best when we know the exact steps needed to complete a given task. On the other hand, agents work better when we don't fully know what steps are needed.

So in these cases, we give Claude a task and a set of tools. Then we rely on Claude to come up with a plan to complete the task using the given tools. This flexibility of agents makes them very attractive when building applications. The idea is that you can make an agent, make sure it works reasonably well, and then this agent can solve a wide variety of different tasks.

However, this approach has some major drawbacks that we'll discuss later. A key aspect of agents is their ability to use tools in different combinations. So to help you understand this, I want you to think back to an example we discussed earlier in this course, where we combined three different tools. We created tools like Get Current Date Time, Add duration to Date Time, and Set Reminder.

Each of these tools is essentially quite simple, but Claude was able to combine them in different, even somewhat surprising ways, to accomplish a variety of different tasks that we might not have planned for in advance. Let me show you some examples. So on the left side of this diagram, I've listed some example tasks we could feed into Claude along with those three different tools. Then on the right side is a series of different tool calls Claude might make to accomplish a given task.

So for example, if we ask Claude what time it is now, simple, Claude just needs to call the get current date time tool by itself to answer the question. If we ask Claude what day of the week it will be 11 days from now, it can first call get current date time, then call add duration to date time. If we ask Claude to, say, set a reminder to go to the gym next Wednesday, Claude can first figure out the current day of the week, then add a duration to it, and finally set a reminder for that particular day. Finally, Claude can also figure out when it needs some additional information to successfully call a tool.

So if a user asks, when does my 90-day warranty expire? Well, there's no guarantee the user got the warranty today. So Claude might first ask the user for some additional information, specifically when they actually got the warranty. Once the user provides this information, Claude can call add duration to date time and figure out when the warranty expires.

These are all examples of how Claude can combine a set of tools in interesting ways to solve a given task. This leads us to the first important lesson or understanding about agents. That is, the set of tools we provide to an agent needs to be fairly abstract. To help you understand what I really mean by abstract, a good example is to look back at Claude Code and some of the tools provided to it.

Claude Code is given a very small set of abstract tools. When I say abstract, I mean general-purpose or a bit vague in their purpose. They're not highly specialized in any way. So Claude Code has access to tools like Bash to run commands, web fetch to fetch URLs, write to create files, and so on.

Claude Code is able to modify and add features to an existing codebase in very impressive ways by combining these different tools. Claude Code doesn't have access to highly specialized tools that complete one very specific task in just one particular scenario. So for example, on the right side of this diagram, these are all tools that Claude Code explicitly does not have. So there's no refactor tool that can magically refactor a file.

Instead, Claude Code is supposed to figure out how to use the tools on the left to refactor something. Similarly, there's no install dependencies tool. Instead, Claude Code needs to read files to understand the project configuration, then run the bash tool to execute the appropriate command to install dependencies. The lesson we can take from this is that whenever we create an agent, we want to make sure we provide fairly abstract tools that Claude can somehow figure out how to combine together to achieve some goal.

So for example, if we go back to our example of building some kind of social media video creation agent, we might provide it with different tools. One might be Bash, which would give it access to FFmpeg. This is a commonly used command-line tool you can use to generate videos from some input images, videos, text, or audio, and so on. We might also give it a generate image tool, a text to speech tool, just to enhance the video generation process, and then finally a post media tool, so it can publish the generated content, whatever it is, to a social media account.

Claude can use this set of tools in fairly surprising ways. So for example, it will be able to implement the flow you see on the left, where a user might chat with our agent and ask it to create and publish a video about Python programming. But this set of tools might also allow for a more dynamic interaction with the user. For example, on the right, the user might ask for a video, but first ask the agent to generate a sample cover image for the video.

Then our agent can first generate an image, show it to the user, get the user's approval, and then proceed with the video generation process.

---

### 92. Environment Inspection

> Type: Video | Duration: 3:05 | Video: 11 - 006 - Environment Inspection.mp4

When building AI agents, there's a crucial concept that's often overlooked: environment inspection. Claude is operating blindly—it needs to be able to observe and understand the results of its actions in order to work effectively.

## Why Environment Inspection Matters

Think about how Claude interacts with a computer. Whenever Claude performs an action, like typing text or clicking a button, it immediately receives a screenshot to understand what happened. This isn't just a nice-to-have feature—it's essential.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543181%2F11_-_006_-_Environment_Inspection_00.1748543181585.jpg)

From Claude's perspective, clicking a button might navigate to a new page, open a menu, or trigger any number of changes. Without being able to see the result, Claude can't understand whether its action succeeded, or what the new state of the environment is.

## Read Before You Write

The same principle applies to file operations. Before Claude can modify any file, it needs to understand the current contents. This might seem obvious, but it's a pattern you should always follow when building agents.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543182%2F11_-_006_-_Environment_Inspection_08.1748543182126.jpg)

In the example above, when asked to add a new route to a Python file, Claude first reads the existing code to understand the current structure. Only then can it safely make the requested changes without breaking existing functionality.

## System Prompts for Environment Inspection

You can guide Claude to inspect its environment through the system prompt. For complex tasks like video generation, this becomes especially important.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543182%2F11_-_006_-_Environment_Inspection_11.1748543182591.jpg)

Consider a video creation agent that needs to:

- Generate video content using a tool like FFmpeg
- Verify that the audio dialogue is placed correctly
- Check that visual elements appear as expected

You might include instructions in the system prompt like:

- Use the bash tool to run whisper.cpp and generate a subtitle file with timestamps, to verify dialogue placement
- Use FFmpeg to extract screenshots from the video at regular intervals, to visually inspect the output
- Compare the generated content against the original requirements

## The Benefits of Environment Inspection

When Claude can inspect its environment, several things improve:

- **Better progress tracking** - Claude can assess how close it is to completing the task
- **Error handling** - Unexpected results can be detected and corrected
- **Quality assurance** - The output can be verified before the task is considered complete
- **Adaptive behavior** - Claude can adjust its approach based on what it observes

## Implementation in Practice

When designing your own agents, always ask: "How will Claude know whether this action succeeded?" Whether you're working with files, APIs, or user interfaces, provide tools and instructions that let Claude observe the results of its actions.

This might mean:

- Reading file contents before modifying them
- Taking a screenshot after a UI interaction
- Checking an API response for expected data
- Validating generated content against requirements

Environment inspection transforms Claude from an executor that blindly carries out commands into an agent that can truly understand and adapt to its working environment.

#### Video Transcript

The next idea we'll discuss about agents is environment inspection. When we looked at Computer Use earlier, you might remember something interesting. After each action recorded on the left, like typing or moving the mouse, we always seemed to immediately see a screenshot, which is exactly what I'm showing you in this diagram. Claude tries to type some text, we see this recorded in the first panel above, and then immediately afterward, we see a screenshot appear right away.

I want you to look at Computer Use from Claude's perspective. Claude tries to type or click somewhere, the page presumably changes, but Claude doesn't really understand how it changed. Clicking a button might navigate to a new page, or it might open a menu. To understand the result of any action it takes, Claude needs a screenshot to understand the new state or environment it's in.

This same idea applies to any agent we assemble. After taking an action, and sometimes before taking an action, Claude really needs a way to assess the result of the action, which often goes beyond what the tool returns. By helping Claude understand its environment, it can better gauge its progress toward completing the task, and also better handle unexpected results or errors. We can see a very similar idea when using Claude Code.

So in this screenshot, at the very top, I'm asking Claude to update the main.py file. Now, the task I gave Claude, namely to add an additional route, is actually quite simple. But before we modify that file in any way, this seems very obvious. Well, Claude needs to first understand what the current code in the file is.

So Claude needs some way to read the contents of the file. Now, I know this seems obvious, but I encourage you to always think about this idea of reading a file before writing to it when building your own agents. This idea even applies to our social media video agent. So whenever we make a request to Claude, we might give it a task, like create a video about Python and publish it to my social media account, along with our list of tools.

Then we might provide some special instructions in the system prompt to help Claude understand how to inspect its environment after generating the video. Personally, if I'm relying on Claude to use FFmpeg to generate a video, I somewhat expect that it might sometimes make mistakes with dialogue placement. Specifically, when the audio clips it generated using some text-to-speech functionality play. To help Claude better gauge its progress toward completing the task when making these videos, I might give it some instructions to use the BASH tool, specifically to run a program called Whisper CPP.

This is a program we can use to automatically generate subtitle files from a video. These subtitle files have timestamps, so Claude can use this program to make sure dialogue is placed correctly. We might also suggest that Claude use the bash tool to run ffmpeg, which has the ability to extract screenshots from a video. We might tell Claude to extract a screenshot every second or every 10 seconds, and look at these screenshots to make sure the video looks the way it expects.

This lets Claude inspect the results of its actions, namely the actual video it created, and make sure it's completing the task as requested.

---

### 93. Workflows vs. Agents

> Type: Video | Duration: 2:00 | Video: 11 - 007 - Workflows vs Agents.mp4

When building AI-powered applications, you'll often need to choose between two different architectural approaches: workflows and agents. Each has its own distinct advantages and trade-offs, making it suitable for different scenarios.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748543217%2F11_-_007_-_Workflows_vs_Agents_00.1748543217764.jpg)

## What Is a Workflow?

A workflow is a series of predefined calls to Claude designed to solve a known problem or set of problems. When you can envision the sequence of steps in advance—basically when you know the exact order needed to complete a task—you use a workflow.

You can think of a workflow as breaking a large task down into many smaller, more specific subtasks. Each step focuses on a single area, which lets Claude work more precisely.

## What is an agent?

With an agent, Claude is given a set of foundational tools and is expected to devise a plan for using those tools to complete a task. Unlike a workflow, you don't know exactly what task will be provided, so the system needs to be more adaptable.

Agents can creatively figure out how to handle a wide variety of challenges by combining tools in unexpected ways.

## Advantages of workflows

- Claude can focus on one subtask at a time, which usually leads to higher accuracy
- They are easier to evaluate and test, because you know each exact step
- Execution is more predictable and reliable
- Better suited for solving concrete, well-defined problems

## Advantages of agents

- Allow for a more flexible user experience
- More flexible task completion — Claude can combine tools in unexpected ways to accomplish a variety of tasks
- Can handle novel situations not anticipated during development
- Can request additional input from the user when needed

## Disadvantages of workflows

- Far less flexible — specialized for solving a specific type of task
- The user experience is usually more constrained — you need to know the exact inputs to the flow
- Require more up-front planning and design work

## Disadvantages of agents

- Lower task success-completion rate compared to workflows
- Harder to instrument, test, and evaluate, since you usually don't know what series of steps the agent will perform
- Behavior is less predictable

## When to use which approach

As an engineer, your primary goal is to reliably solve the problem. Users probably don't care whether you built a fancy agent — what they want is a product that works consistently.

The general advice is to always focus on implementing a workflow when possible, and only resort to an agent when truly necessary. Workflows provide the reliability and predictability that most production applications need, while agents offer flexibility for scenarios where the exact requirements can't be determined in advance.

When you have a well-defined process, consider using a workflow; when you need to handle unpredictable, varied user requests that require creative problem-solving, consider using an agent.

#### Video transcript

Let's wrap up by comparing and contrasting some of the different aspects of workflows and agents. First, recall that a workflow is a predefined series of calls to Claude. We typically use a workflow when we have a good understanding of the exact steps needed to complete a task. With an agent, on the other hand, we don't know exactly what task will be provided, so instead we provide a solid set of foundational tools and expect Claude to combine those tools to complete the given task.

You may have noticed that a common theme in workflows is that we break a large task down into many smaller tasks. Each of these smaller tasks is more specific, allowing Claude to focus on one area at a time. This increased focus usually leads to higher accuracy in completing the task compared to an agent. Because we know the exact steps a workflow executes, they're also easier to test and evaluate.

With an agent, we're not bound to a fixed series of steps. Instead, Claude can creatively figure out how to tackle a wide variety of challenges. Along with this flexibility, we also gain flexibility in the user experience. A workflow expects to receive a very specific set of inputs, whereas an agent can create its own inputs based on the query it receives from the user, and when needed, the agent can also request more input from the user.

The downside of agents is that they usually have a lower task success-completion rate compared to workflows, because we delegate so much of the work to Claude. In addition, they're also harder to test and evaluate, because we usually don't know what series of steps the agent will perform to complete a given task. At the end of the day, agents really are fun, but remember that as an engineer, your primary goal is to reliably solve the problem. Users probably don't care that you built a fancy agent.

What they really want is just a product that works 100% of the time. So with that in mind, the general advice is to focus on implementing a workflow whenever possible, and only resort to an agent when truly necessary.

---

# Lesson 9: Claude with Google Vertex AI

> Using Claude on the Google Cloud Platform
> Course link: [Claude with Google Vertex AI](https://anthropic.skilljar.com/claude-with-google-vertex) | 93 lessons total

---

### Claude Model Overview

Claude models come in 3 families, each optimized for different priorities.

**Opus** = The most intelligent model. Suited for complex, multi-step tasks requiring deep reasoning and planning. Can work independently for hours. Supports reasoning capability. The trade-off is higher cost and moderate latency.

**Sonnet** = The balanced model. Performs well across intelligence, speed, and cost-efficiency. Has strong coding capabilities, able to edit code precisely without breaking functionality. Fast text generation. The best choice for most use cases.

**Haiku** = The fastest model. Optimized for speed and cost-efficiency. Does not have reasoning capability. Best suited for real-time user interactions and high-throughput processing.

**Selection framework**: Intelligence first → Opus. Speed first → Haiku. Need balance → Sonnet.

**Key insight**: Teams often use multiple models within the same application. Haiku for user interactions, Sonnet for business logic, and Opus for complex reasoning tasks.

All models share core capabilities = text generation, coding, image analysis.

---

### Accessing the API

API Access Flow = a 5-step flow from user input to displaying the response.

Step 1: The client sends the user text to the developer's server (never access Vertex directly from the client; credentials must be kept secret).

Step 2: The server uses an SDK (Python/TypeScript/Go/Ruby) to make a request to Vertex AI. Required parameters: model name, messages list, max_tokens limit.

Step 3: Claude's text generation process = 4 stages:
- Tokenization = the input is broken into tokens (words/parts/symbols).
- Embedding = tokens are converted into lists of numbers representing all possible meanings.
- Contextualization = the embeddings are adjusted based on neighboring tokens to determine the precise meaning.
- Generation = the output layer produces vocabulary probabilities, the model selects based on probability and randomness, adds the chosen word, and repeats the process.

Step 4: Generation stops when max_tokens is reached or the model generates an end-of-sequence token.

Step 5: The API returns a response containing the generated text, usage counts, and stop reason. The server forwards this to the client for display.

Key components:
- Token = a chunk of text (word/part/symbol).
- Embedding = a numerical representation of a token's meaning.
- Contextualization = meaning refinement using surrounding context.
- Max tokens = a limit on the generation length.
- Stop reason = the reason generation ended.
- Usage = token counts for input/output.

Security: Always route API calls through the server, and never expose credentials in the client application.

---

### Making a Request

**Making a request to Vertex AI Claude**

**Setup steps:**
1. Install the anthropic SDK: `%pip install "anthropic[vertex]"`
2. Import and create the client: `from anthropic import vertex` then instantiate with `region="global"` and `project_id`.
3. Define a model version variable for reuse.

**Required parameters for client.messages.create():**
- model = the specific Claude model version string.
- max_tokens = a safety limit on response length (not a target, just a maximum).
- messages = a list of message dictionaries.

**Message types:**
- User message = `{role: "user", content: "text written by humans"}`
- Assistant message = `{role: "assistant", content: "text generated by Claude"}`

**Response access:**
The raw response contains metadata. Extract the generated text using: `message.content[0].text`

**Key concepts:**
Messages = the conversational exchange between the user and Claude.
Max tokens = a safety mechanism to prevent over-generation.
Project ID = the unique identifier from the Google Cloud console.

---

### Multi-Turn Conversations

Multi-Turn Conversations = maintaining context across multiple exchanges with Claude.

Key limitation: The Anthropic API does not store any messages. Each request is stateless - no memory of previous exchanges.

The problem: Sending a follow-up request without context produces an irrelevant response, because Claude has no memory of the previous conversation.

Solution requirements:
1. Manually maintain the message list in your code.
2. Send the entire conversation history with every request.

Implementation pattern:
- Create an empty message list.
- Add the user message to the list.
- Send it to Claude and get a response.
- Add the assistant response to the list.
- Add the next user message to the list.
- Send the entire updated list to Claude.
- Repeat.

Necessary helper functions:
- `add_user_message(messages, text)` = appends a user message to the conversation history.
- `add_assistant_message(messages, text)` = appends an assistant response to the conversation history.
- `chat(messages)` = sends the message list to the Claude API and returns the response.

Message structure = a list of dictionaries containing "role" (user/assistant) and "content" fields.

Context retention = including the full conversation history in each API call enables Claude to understand references and maintain a coherent conversational flow.

---

### System Prompts

System Prompts = instructions that guide Claude to adjust its tone, style, and behavior for a specific use case.

Purpose = controlling how Claude responds, not just what it responds. For example: making Claude play the role of a patient math tutor who provides hints rather than directly giving answers.

Implementation = passing the system prompt as a plain string to the create function via the `system` keyword argument.

Structure = the first line usually assigns Claude a role ("You are a patient math tutor"), followed by specific behavioral instructions.

Key principle = the system prompt guides Claude's approach — the tutor provides guidance and asks leading questions rather than directly solving the problem.

Technical consideration = the `system` parameter cannot be `None`, and must only be conditionally included in the API call when a prompt is present.

Best practice = make the system prompt configurable rather than hard-coded, so it can be reused across different use cases.

Result = transforming a direct question-and-answer interaction into a guided, interactive experience tailored to a specific role and context.

---

### Temperature

Temperature = a parameter that controls the randomness in Claude's text generation, expressed as a decimal value between 0 and 1.

Text generation process = tokenization → probability assignment → selecting tokens based on probability.

The effect of Temperature:
- Temperature 0 = deterministic output, always selecting the highest-probability token.
- Higher Temperature = increases the chance of selecting lower-probability tokens, producing more creative/unexpected output.

Usage guidelines:
- Low Temperature (close to 0) = data extraction, factual tasks, consistent output.
- High Temperature (close to 1) = creative writing, brainstorming, jokes, marketing.

Implementation = add a `temperature` parameter to the model call function, defaulting to 1.0 to maintain creativity.

Key insight = a higher temperature increases the likelihood of variation but does not guarantee it; it may take multiple attempts to see the creative differences.

---

### Response Streaming

Response Streaming = a technique for providing users with immediate feedback during the AI's lengthy response generation, rather than making them watch a 10-30 second loading animation.

How it works: The server sends the user message to Claude → Claude immediately sends an initial response (no text content, just an acknowledgment) → Claude sends a stream of events containing text chunks → the server receives the events and sends the text chunks to the frontend → the user sees the text appear progressively, chunk by chunk.

Event types: `message_start`, `content_block_start`, `content_block_delta` (contains the actual text), `content_block_stop`, `message_delta`, `message_stop`.

Implementation:
- Basic streaming: `client.messages.create(model, max_tokens, messages, stream=True)` returns an iterator of events.
- Text-focused streaming: `client.messages.stream()` used with a context manager provides cleaner access to text content via `stream.text_stream`.
- Final message assembly: `stream.get_final_message()` collects all chunks into a complete message for database storage.

Key benefit: Users see an immediate, progressive response instead of waiting for the full generation, improving the user experience in chat interfaces.

---

### Controlling Model Output

Model output control = two main techniques beyond modifying the prompt: pre-filling assistant messages and stop sequences.

Pre-filling assistant messages = manually adding an assistant message to the end of the message list to guide the direction of the response. Claude treats the pre-filled content as its own and continues from that point. For example: adding "Coffee is better because" forces Claude to argue in favor of a preference for coffee. The response continues from the end of the pre-filled text rather than replacing it entirely.

Implementation: After the user message in the message list, add an assistant message containing partial content. Claude treats it as its own prior response and builds upon it.

Stop sequences = forcing Claude to stop generating when a specific string appears. You provide a list of strings, and the response is terminated immediately when those strings are generated. The text of the generated stop sequence is not included in the final output.

Implementation: Add the `stop_sequences` parameter to the API call with a list of trigger strings. Once any listed sequence is encountered, generation stops immediately.

Use cases: Pre-filling controls the direction/stance of the response. Stop sequences control the length/format of the response by terminating it at a specific point.

---

### Structured Data

Structured data generation = combining stop sequences and assistant message pre-filling to get raw output with no annotations.

The problem: When generating structured data (JSON, Python, lists), Claude naturally adds headers/footers/explanations. Users often just want the raw content for easy copy/paste.

Solution pattern:
1. Pre-fill the assistant message with an opening delimiter (e.g., `` ```json ``).
2. Set the stop sequence to the matching closing delimiter (e.g., `` ``` ``).
3. Claude assumes it has already written the opening part, generates only the content, and stops at the closing delimiter.

Result: You get raw structured data without any extra annotations.

Example flow:
- User: "Generate an EventBridge rule in JSON format"
- Assistant pre-fill: `` ```json ``
- Stop sequence: `` ``` ``
- Output: only the JSON content between the delimiters.

Key benefits:
- Eliminates unwanted markdown formatting.
- Removes explanatory text.
- Enables direct copy/paste functionality.
- Works for any structured data type (JSON, code, lists).

Implementation: `text.strip()` or `JSON.loads()` can clean up any remaining newlines/formatting.

Use case: Any scenario requiring precise structured output without the helpful annotations Claude naturally provides.

---

### Prompt Evaluation

Prompt Engineering = the technique of writing/editing prompts to help Claude understand the request and the expected response format.

Prompt Evaluation = automatically testing prompts using objective metrics to measure their effectiveness.

Three paths after writing a prompt:
1. Test it once or twice and deploy to production (a pitfall).
2. Test with custom inputs and fine-tune for edge cases (a pitfall).
3. Score it objectively through an evaluation pipeline (recommended).

Options 1 and 2 are common pitfalls — engineers don't test their prompts adequately before using them in production.

Best practice = use an evaluation pipeline to get an objective performance score, then iterate on the prompt based on the results before deploying.

In the learning sequence, evaluation comes before engineering — first measure effectiveness, then learn the techniques to improve it.

---

### A Typical Evaluation Workflow

A typical Eval Workflow = a 5-step iterative process for prompt optimization.

Step 1: Initial prompt draft = write a basic prompt with input variables (e.g., "Please answer the user's question [user_input]").

Step 2: Evaluation dataset = create a collection of test inputs (a minimum of 3 examples; in real applications you'd use hundreds/thousands). These can be hand-crafted or AI-generated.

Step 3: Prompt execution = fill each dataset input into the prompt template to create a complete prompt, then send it to Claude to get a response.

Step 4: Scoring = use a grader system to score each question-answer pair (usually 1-10). Average all the scores to get an overall prompt performance metric.

Step 5: Iteration = modify the prompt based on the results, repeat the entire process, and compare scores to determine the better version.

Key principles:
- There is no standardized methodology in the industry.
- There are multiple implementation options (open-source packages, paid solutions, custom builds).
- You can start simple in a Jupyter notebook, then scale up.
- Objective scoring makes data-driven prompt improvement possible.
- The process repeats until satisfactory performance is achieved.

This workflow enables systematic prompt optimization through measurable performance comparison rather than subjective evaluation.

---

### Generating a Test Dataset

Custom prompt evaluation workflow = building a system to evaluate an AWS code-generation prompt.

Goal = help users write AWS-specific code by outputting one of three formats: Python, JSON config, or raw regex.

Prompt structure = "Please provide a solution to the following task: [user task]", without any additional explanation/header/footer.

Test Dataset = an array of JSON objects, where each object has a "task" property describing the desired AWS task.

Dataset generation approach = manually assemble it or auto-generate it using Claude Haiku (using a faster model is recommended).

Implementation process:
1. Create a `generate_dataset()` function with a large prompt asking Claude to generate test cases.
2. Use the pre-filling technique, with the assistant message starting with `` ```json ``.
3. Set the stop sequence to `` ``` `` to ensure clean JSON output.
4. Parse the response using `JSON.loads()`.
5. Save the dataset to a file using `JSON.dump()` for later evaluation use.

Dataset structure = `[{task: "description"}, {task: "description"}, ...]`

Key technique = pre-filling + stop sequences for reliably parsing JSON from LLM responses.

Next step = use the saved dataset file to evaluate the prompt's performance across all test cases.

---

### Running the Evaluation

Test case = a single record from the generated dataset, merged with the prompt and fed to Claude.

The `run_prompt` function = takes a test case, merges it with the prompt (a simple "Please solve the following task" + test case), sends it to Claude, and returns the output. It currently lacks formatting instructions, so the returned output is verbose.

The `run_test_case` function = takes a single case, calls `run_prompt`, scores the result (currently hard-coded to 10), and returns a dictionary containing the output/test case/score.

The `run_eval` function = loads the dataset, iterates over each test case calling `run_test_case`, and assembles all results into a list.

Evaluation pipeline workflow = dataset → merge with prompt → send to Claude → score the result → collect outputs.

Current limitations = no scoring logic is implemented (hard-coded scores), the prompt has no output formatting instructions, and execution time is relatively slow (31 seconds using the Haiku model).

Result format = an array of objects, where each object contains Claude's output, the original test case, and the score for each test case.

Next step = implement an actual grader to replace the hard-coded scoring system.

---

### Model-Based Grading

Model Based Grading = using an AI model to evaluate the output of other AI models by providing an objective scoring signal.

Three grader types:
- Code graders = programmatic checks (length, syntax, readability, word presence).
- Model graders = additional API calls to evaluate quality, instruction-following, completeness.
- Human graders = manual evaluation (flexible but time-consuming).

Key requirements:
- All graders must return an objective signal (usually a 1-10 score).
- Define the evaluation criteria in advance.
- For model graders: use a detailed prompt asking for strengths/weaknesses/reasoning to avoid getting middle-of-the-road scores (the model defaults to around 6 without a reasoning requirement).

Implementation pattern:
1. Create a grading function that takes the test case and model output.
2. Use a structured prompt with role definitions and explicit evaluation instructions.
3. Extract the JSON response containing the score/reasoning.
4. Compute the average score across all test cases as the final metric.

Common evaluation criteria = format compliance, syntax validation, task completion accuracy.

Model graders provide high flexibility for evaluating subjective quality, while code graders handle objective validation checks.

---

### Code-Based Grading

Code-based grading = a system for verifying that model output contains only valid code (Python/JSON/RegEx) with no explanations.

Core components:
- `validate_json()` = attempts to parse JSON, returning 10 if valid and 0 on error.
- `validate_python()` = attempts AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) parsing, returning 10 if valid and 0 on error.
- `validate_regex()` = attempts to compile the regex, returning 10 if valid and 0 on error.
- `grade_syntax()` = a dispatch function that calls the corresponding validator based on the test case's format.

Implementation steps:
1. Add validator functions that attempt to parse/compile.
2. Update the dataset to include a "format" key specifying the expected output type (JSON/Python/RegEx).
3. Update the prompt template to explicitly require only code output, with no annotations.
4. Use a pre-filled assistant message with code blocks and stop sequences.
5. Merge the syntax score with the model score by averaging.

Dataset format = must include a `format` field to indicate the expected code type for each test case.

Prompt engineering = use `` ```code `` as the pre-filled assistant message, since the exact format is not known in advance.

Score calculation = `(model_score + syntax_score) / 2`

Key insight = validate syntax via `try/catch` parsing rather than complex rule-based validation.

---

### Prompt Engineering

Prompt Engineering = techniques for refining prompts to get more reliable, higher-quality output from a language model.

Goal: Generate a three-meal-per-day meal plan for an athlete based on height, weight, body goals, and dietary restrictions.

Process:
1. Write an initial prompt (a poor first draft).
2. Evaluate the prompt's performance.
3. Iteratively apply prompt engineering techniques.
4. Re-evaluate after each improvement.
5. Monitor performance improvements.

Initial prompt example: "What should this person eat?" + basic parameter interpolation (height, weight, goals, restrictions).

The evaluation setup uses a `Prompt Evaluator` class with configurable concurrency (set it low to start, such as 3, and drop to 1 if you hit rate limits). You can specify additional criteria such as calorie totals, macronutrient breakdowns, and specific output requirements like foods/portions/timing.

Expected pattern: The initial score is poor, and is improved by systematically applying techniques. The output evaluation creates an HTML report showing test case results, scores, and reasoning.

Key insight: Start simple, measure performance, then progressively apply engineering techniques while continuously evaluating the improvements.

---

### Be Clear and Direct

Be clear and direct = a technique for improving prompt effectiveness by focusing on the first line of the prompt.

The importance of the first line = the most critical part of the prompt, which sets the foundation for the AI's response.

Structure = use a verb + simple, direct language + a clear task description.

Verbs = write, generate, create, identify, analyze - tell the AI clearly what to do.

Task description = include details about the expected output format and content requirements.

Examples:
- "Write three paragraphs about how solar panels work" = verb + output format + topic.
- "Identify three countries that use geothermal energy and for each include generation stats" = verb + quantity + specific requirement.

Implementation = replace vague openings with direct commands that specify both the action and the expected deliverable.

Result = significantly improved prompt performance (the example shows the score rising from 2.32 to 3.92).

---

### Be Specific

Be specific = adding guidelines or steps to steer the model's output in a particular direction.

Two types of guidelines:
- Type A: Controls the output's attributes (length, structure, qualities).
- Type B: Provides the model with steps to follow (forces it to consider specific elements, improving reasoning quality).

The two types are often combined in professional prompts.

When to use:
- Type A: Almost always used - list the desired output qualities.
- Type B: For complex problems requiring the model to go beyond its natural scope and consider perspectives/data more broadly.

Improvement example: After adding guidelines, the evaluation score rose from 3.92 to 7.86, demonstrating a significant impact on output quality.

---

### Structuring with XML Tags

XML tags for prompt structure = a technique that uses custom XML-style tags to organize and clarify the different content sections within a prompt.

Purpose = helps the language model distinguish between different types of interpolated content when large amounts of text are inserted into a prompt.

Implementation = wrap content sections in descriptive tags (such as `<sales_records>` or `<athlete_info>`) rather than piling raw text directly together.

Tag naming = use specific, descriptive names (`sales_records` > `records` > `data`) to provide context about the nature of the content.

Use cases = separating code and documentation, marking athlete information, organizing sales data, and any scenario with multiple content types in a single prompt.

Benefits = reduces ambiguity about the purpose of the text and improves the model's understanding of the prompt structure, especially boosting the quality of simpler models.

Best practice = even for shorter content sections, XML tags can clarify that the content represents external input or a specific category of information.

---

### Providing Examples

one-shot/multi-shot prompting = providing examples in the prompt to guide model behavior. One-shot = a single example, multi-shot = multiple examples.

Implementation = wrap the examples in XML tags and include both the example input and the ideal output sections. This structure cleanly separates the example content from the main prompt.

corner cases = use multi-shot prompting to handle edge cases such as sarcasm detection. Add context explaining why a particular scenario requires special attention.

Complex output = examples are especially effective for demonstrating complex JSON structures or formatting requirements.

Integration with prompt evaluation = pull high-scoring test cases from evaluation results to use as examples. Include reasoning explaining why the output is ideal to reinforce the desired behavioral patterns.

Effectiveness = consistently improves model performance by providing concrete behavioral templates and output format guidance.

---

### Introduction to Tool Use

Tool use = a mechanism that allows Claude to access external information beyond its training data.

Core limitation: Claude only knows information from its training data, and lacks real-time/current information.

Tool use flow:
1. Send an initial request to Claude + instructions for accessing external data.
2. Claude judges whether external data is needed and requests specific information.
3. The server runs code to fetch the requested data from an external source.
4. Send a follow-up request to Claude containing the retrieved data.
5. Claude generates a final response using the original prompt + the external data.

Weather example:
- The user asks about the current weather in San Francisco.
- Without tools: Claude responds "I cannot access current weather data."
- With tools: Claude requests the weather data → the server calls a weather API → Claude receives the data → provides a current-weather response.

Key concept: Tools enable Claude to augment its responses with real-time external information through a structured data-retrieval process.

---

### Project Overview

Project goal = teaching Claude to set up time-based reminders through tools in a Jupyter notebook.

Target interaction = the user says "Set a reminder for my doctor's appointment next Thursday" → Claude responds "I'll remind you at that time."

Three core problems were identified:
1. Claude lacks knowledge of the precise current time (it knows the date, but not the exact time).
2. Claude sometimes fails at time-based calculations (e.g., "379 days from January 13, 1973").
3. Claude has no mechanism to actually set reminders (it understands the concept but cannot execute it).

Solution approach = three specialized tools:
1. A get-current-datetime tool (date + time).
2. An add-duration-to-datetime tool (handles time calculations).
3. A reminder-setting tool (executes the actual reminder creation).

Implementation = implement one tool at a time, ultimately combining multiple tools for the complete functionality.

---

### Tool Functions

Tool Functions = Python functions that execute automatically when Claude needs additional information to help the user.

Key components:
- Tool function = an ordinary Python function that retrieves specific data (e.g., the current datetime, the weather).
- Executed when Claude judges that additional information is needed to answer the user's query.

Best practices:
1. Use descriptive function/parameter names.
2. Validate inputs and raise errors for invalid data.
3. Include meaningful error messages to help Claude correct mistakes.

Benefits of error handling:
- When a tool call fails, Claude sees the exact error message.
- It can retry the tool call with corrected parameters based on the error feedback.
- Example: "location cannot be empty" helps Claude retry with a valid location.

Implementation pattern:
```python
def get_current_datetime(date_format="%Y%m%d %H:%M:%S"):
    if not date_format:
        raise ValueError("date format cannot be empty")
    return datetime.now().strftime(date_format)
```

Tool functions should focus on a single responsibility (getting the datetime, the weather, etc.), with proper input validation and clear error messaging so Claude's retry logic can work.

---

### Tool Schemas

Tool Schemas = JSON configuration objects that describe the tool functions available to the language model.

JSON Schema = a data validation specification (not specific to language models) used to validate JSON data. The language model community adopted it for tool use because it is well-known and convenient.

Tool schema structure:
- `name`: the name of the tool function.
- `description`: 3-4 sentences explaining what the tool does, when to use it, and what data it returns.
- `input_schema`: the actual JSON schema describing the function's parameters (the type and description of each parameter).

Best practice: Use 3-4 sentence descriptions for both the tool and the individual parameters to help Claude understand their purpose and usage.

Schema generation tip: Have Claude write the JSON schema for your function, along with Anthropic's tool-use documentation for best practices. This produces high-quality schemas.

Naming convention: `function_name` + `function_name_schema` for easy tracking.

Type safety: Wrap the schema in `ToolParam` imported from `anthropic.types` to prevent type errors during implementation.

---

### Handling Message Blocks

**Step 3: Calling Claude with the tool schema**

API request structure = include a `tools` parameter with a list of JSON schemas to tell Claude which tools are available.

**Multi-block message responses**

Message content types = Text blocks (for user display) + Tool use blocks (for function calls).

Tool use block content = the function name + the input parameters for execution.

**Critical message history management**

Conversation persistence = needs to be tracked manually - Claude does not store any history.

Multi-block handling = when appending to the message history, you must preserve the entire content list, including all blocks (text + tool use).

Implementation = `messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})`

**Helper functions need updating**

Current limitation = `add_user_message` and `add_assistant_message` only support a single text block.

Needed fix = update the helper functions to handle multiple content blocks per message.

---

### Sending Tool Results

Step 4: Execute the tool function Claude requested. Extract the parameters from the tool use block using `response.content[1].input`, then call the function using `**kwargs` syntax to convert the dictionary into keyword arguments.

Step 5: Send a follow-up request to Claude containing the full conversation history, plus a new user message containing the tool result block.

Tool result block structure:
- `tool_use_id` = matches the ID in the original tool use block (enabling Claude to match requests to results when multiple tools are called).
- `content` = the tool function's output converted to a string.
- `is_error` = false (the default value; set to true if the tool execution failed).

Tool use ID system: When Claude makes multiple tool calls, each call gets a unique ID. The tool result must reference the matching ID so Claude can correlate which result belongs to which request.

Complete flow: original user message → assistant message with tool use block → execute the tool function → user message with tool result block → final assistant response using the tool output.

Once tools are introduced, the tool schemas must be included in all requests, even on subsequent calls.

---

### Multi-Turn Conversations with Tools

Multi-turn conversations with tools = a system that allows the AI to make multiple sequential tool calls within a single conversation.
