### 7. Making changes

> Type: Video | Duration: 4:04 | Video: 005 - Making Changes.mp4

When working with Claude in your development environment, you'll often need to make changes to an existing project. This guide covers practical techniques for effectively implementing changes, including using screenshots for visual communication and leveraging Claude's advanced reasoning capabilities.

## Using Screenshots for Precise Communication

One of the most effective ways to communicate with Claude is through screenshots. When you want to modify a specific part of the interface, taking a screenshot helps Claude understand exactly what you're referring to.

To paste a screenshot into Claude, use `Ctrl+V` (not `Cmd+V` on macOS). This keyboard shortcut is specifically for pasting screenshots into the chat interface. After pasting an image, you can ask Claude to make specific changes to that area of the application.

## Planning Mode

For more complex tasks that require extensive research of the codebase, you can enable Planning Mode. This feature makes Claude thoroughly explore the project before implementing changes.

Enable Planning Mode by pressing `Shift + Tab` twice (once if you've already enabled auto-accept edits). In this mode, Claude will:

- Read more files in the project
- Create a detailed implementation plan
- Show you exactly what it intends to do
- Wait for your approval before proceeding

This gives you a chance to review the plan and redirect Claude if it misses something important or fails to consider a particular scenario.

## Thinking Modes

Claude offers different levels of reasoning through "thinking" modes. These modes allow Claude to spend more time thinking about complex problems before providing a solution.

The available Thinking Modes include:

- "Think" - Basic reasoning
- "Think more" - Extended reasoning
- "Think a lot" - Comprehensive reasoning
- "Think longer" - Prolonged reasoning time
- "Ultrathink" - Maximum reasoning capability

Each mode progressively gives Claude more tokens to work with, enabling deeper analysis of challenging problems.

## When to Use Planning vs Thinking

These two features address different types of complexity:

Planning Mode is best for:

- Tasks that require a broad understanding of the codebase
- Multi-step implementations
- Changes that affect multiple files or components

Thinking Mode is best for:

- Complex logical problems
- Debugging tricky issues
- Algorithmic challenges

You can combine these two modes to handle tasks that require both breadth and depth. Remember, both features consume additional tokens, so using them has cost considerations.

#### Video Transcript

Let's try making some changes to this project. Along the way, I'll show you some useful features of Claude Code. The first thing I want to do is move the placeholder text on the left to the center of this panel. To help Claude understand exactly what I want to move, I'll take a screenshot of that area, then use Ctrl V to paste it into Claude Code.

Note that it's Ctrl-V, not Command-V, which you might be used to on macOS. Ctrl-V is specifically for pasting screenshots. Then I can ask Claude to center that placeholder. After some searching, Claude successfully makes the styling update.

Then back in the browser, yes, it looks great. Let me show you the next thing I want to change in this application. I'm going to ask for a card component that displays a title and some description. The card is generated without any issue, but there's one awkward thing.

On the left side of the chat interface, there's a string replace editor. That little panel is meant to indicate to the user that a file is being created. But right now it's using a very technical term, "String Replace Editor," for the tool being used behind the scenes. I want to show the user some friendlier text and tell the user that a file is being created, along with the file's name.

Of course, we should also handle cases like when the chatbot is editing a file or deleting a file, and so on. To direct Claude's attention, I'll take a screenshot again so it understands exactly what I'm talking about. Then, back here, I'll paste that image and ask Claude to replace that specific text with some friendlier messages. Now, this is a bit of a tricky task, and Claude needs to do a lot of research in this project to complete it.

Whenever you give Claude a harder task, there are two ways to easily boost Claude's intelligence. The first way is to enable Plan mode. Plan mode is enabled by pressing Shift-Tab twice, or just once if you've already auto-accepted file edits. In Plan mode, Claude will do more research into the contents of your project, read more files, and develop a detailed plan for completing your task.

Once the plan is done, Claude will tell you exactly how it intends to complete your task. At that point, you can choose to accept the plan, and Claude will implement it, or you can redirect Claude in some way, perhaps it missed some files, or didn't consider a certain scenario. The second way we can boost Claude's intelligence is to enable Thinking Mode. This turns on Claude's extended thinking feature, allowing it to think more deeply about a particular task.

To enable thinking, there are several different trigger phrases. Each phrase progressively gives Claude a larger token budget to think with. Given that this is a tricky task, I might ask Claude to "ultra-think" about how best to implement it. The last thing to understand is that planning and thinking can be used together.

So in addition to this ultra-thinking, I'll also turn on Plan mode. Now I'll run it and see how Claude implements this feature. You might be wondering when you should use planning and when you should use thinking. Think of these two as addressing breadth versus depth.

Planning mode is useful when you have a task that requires a broad understanding of the codebase and needs to look at different areas. It's also useful when handling tasks that require several steps to complete. Thinking mode, on the other hand, is useful when you're focusing on a particularly tricky piece of logic or troubleshooting a difficult bug. A second question you might have is whether you should always enable thinking and planning.

Of course you can, but remember, planning and thinking consume additional tokens, so using them carries an associated cost. After a few minutes of work, this feature seems to be done. So I'll go back into my editor to test it. So soon, we can see we've got some better status messages here than before.

The user is now told that a file is being created. If I send a follow-up request, maybe to change the title. Hopefully now, in the follow-up action, we'll see some information about editing that file. So there we see it.

Now we're editing the app.jsx file. Well, I'd say Claude definitely succeeded in implementing this feature. Now that we've made some changes to this project, we should probably commit our changes. Claude Code is a reliable Git assistant.

We can ask it to stage and commit our changes, and it will write a descriptive commit message for us.

---

### 9. Controlling context

> Type: Video | Duration: 3:37 | Video: 006 - Controlling Context.mp4

When using Claude for complex tasks, you'll often need to steer the conversation to keep it focused and efficient. There are several techniques you can use to control the flow of the conversation and help Claude stay on the right track.

## Interrupting Claude with Escape

Sometimes Claude will start heading in the wrong direction or try to handle too many tasks at once. You can press the `Escape` key to stop Claude's response and thereby redirect the conversation.

This is especially useful when you want Claude to focus on one specific task rather than handling multiple at once. For example, if you ask Claude to write tests for several functions and it starts creating a comprehensive plan for all of them, you can interrupt and ask it to focus on just one function at a time.

## Combining Escape with Memories

One of the most powerful applications of the Escape technique is fixing recurring errors. When Claude repeatedly makes the same mistake across different conversations, you can:

- Press the `Escape` key to stop the current response
- Use the `#` shortcut to add a memory about the correct approach
- Continue the conversation with the corrected information

This prevents Claude from making the same mistake in future conversations about your project.

## Rewinding Conversations

In long conversations, you may accumulate context that becomes irrelevant or distracting. For example, if Claude encounters an error and spends time debugging it, that back-and-forth discussion may not be useful for the next task.

You can press the `Escape` key twice to rewind the conversation. This shows all the messages you've sent, allowing you to jump back to an earlier point and continue from there. This technique helps you:

- Maintain valuable context (such as Claude's understanding of your codebase)
- Remove distracting or irrelevant conversation history
- Keep Claude focused on the current task

## Context Management Commands

Claude provides several commands to help manage conversation context effectively:

### /compact

The `/compact` command summarizes your entire conversation history while preserving the key information Claude has learned. This is ideal when:

- Claude has gained valuable knowledge about your project
- You want to continue working on related tasks
- The conversation has become lengthy but contains important context

Use compact when Claude knows a lot about the current task and you want to retain that knowledge as it moves on to the next related task.

### /clear

The `/clear` command completely clears the conversation history, letting you start fresh. This is most useful when:

- You're switching to a completely different and unrelated task
- The current conversation context might confuse Claude on the new task
- You want to start over without any prior context

## When to Use These Techniques

These conversation control techniques are particularly valuable in the following situations:

- Long conversations where context can become cluttered
- Task transitions where prior context might be distracting
- Situations where Claude repeatedly makes the same mistake
- Complex projects where you need to focus on a specific component

By strategically using escape, double-tapping escape, `/compact`, and `/clear`, you can keep Claude focused and efficient throughout your development workflow. These aren't just convenience features—they're essential tools for maintaining an effective AI-assisted development session.

#### Video Transcript

In this video, I want to show you several different techniques for controlling and steering the flow of the conversation. Here's a basic example. I'm going to ask Claude to write tests for some functions written in the authentication file. Claude quickly forms a plan to write several different tests.

However, I know that testing this file is a bit difficult, and I want Claude to test only one thing at a time. To interrupt Claude, I can press the Escape key. This stops Claude from continuing and lets me suggest a different path. Combining the Escape key with memories is a very powerful way to fix mistakes that Claude makes repeatedly.

Here's an example. I'm going to again ask Claude to write tests for the same file. This time, it will try to read a test configuration file that doesn't actually exist. Now, this is a mistake I've seen Claude make on this project before.

So, to stop this mistake from happening again, I'll press the Escape key very quickly. Then I'll use the pound shortcut to add a memory about the correct name of this test configuration file. Now, I may not have to see this mistake again. Some of these conversation control shortcuts may seem like just conveniences.

But when used well, they can really improve Claude's productivity and keep it focused. So let me show you a more practical example. In the auth.ts file, there are four different functions. I want Claude to write tests for each of them, one at a time, starting with a function called create session.

Claude will certainly try to write the tests, but while running them, it runs into an error and spends some time debugging it. It turns out I forgot to install a package. Eventually, the tests are completed and working, and it's time to start the next set of tests. But here's the problem.

Now my conversation history has a lot of back-and-forth about that broken package. Now, this pile of context is completely irrelevant to writing the next set of tests. Ideally, we should be able to go back in time, back to the last message we sent, and update it to "write tests for git session." The benefit of doing this is that we retain the context where Claude has already looked at the contents of the auth.ts file.

And when we mention git session, it already knows what we're talking about. And because we removed all those extra messages that were just about debugging, we won't have so much distraction here. So Claude can really focus on the task. To go back in the conversation history, press the Escape key twice.

This will show all the different messages you've set, so you can rewind to a previous point in time and skip over some of the intermediate conversation. Claude will now start working on the next set of tests. This time, Claude stays highly focused, but unfortunately, it runs into a number of problems. It eventually resolves them and makes the tests pass.

Now, Claude has worked on its own for a few minutes and has a good understanding of how to write tests for this file. At the same time, once again, we have a large amount of context in this conversation history. When it's time to write tests for the next function, I want to use a command called `compact`. The `compact` command takes all the messages in the current conversation and summarizes them.

The `compact` command is very useful when Claude knows a lot about the current task and you want to retain that knowledge as it moves on to the next task. The last context-related command to be aware of is the `Clear` command. The `Clear` command clears the entire conversation history, letting you start from scratch. The `Clear` command is most useful when you're about to start a task that's completely different from the current one.

I recommend using these shortcuts often, especially when you're switching between tasks, or when you're in a long conversation with Claude. We'll use them many times throughout the rest of this course to ensure Claude stays focused and on task.

---

### 10. Custom commands

> Type: Video | Duration: 1:44 | Video: 007 - Custom Commands.mp4

Claude Code comes with built-in commands that you can access by typing a slash, but you can also create your own Custom Commands to automate repetitive tasks you run frequently.

## Creating Custom Commands

To create a custom command, you need to set up a specific folder structure in your project:

1. Find the `.claude` folder in your project directory
2. Create a new directory inside it called `commands`
3. Create a new Markdown file with the name you want for your command (for example, `audit.md`)

The file name becomes your command name—so `audit.md` creates the `/audit` command.

## Example: Audit Command

Here's a practical example of a custom command that audits project dependencies for vulnerabilities:

This `audit` command does three things:

1. Runs `npm audit` to find vulnerable installed packages
2. Runs `npm audit fix` to apply updates
3. Runs the tests to verify the updates didn't break anything

After creating the command file, you must restart Claude Code for it to recognize the new command.

## Commands with Arguments

Custom commands can accept arguments using the `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder. This makes them more flexible and reusable.

For example, a `write_tests.md` command might contain:

```
Write comprehensive tests for: $ARGUMENTS

Testing conventions:
* Use Vitests with React Testing Library
* Place test files in a __tests__ directory in the same folder as the source file
* Name test files as [filename].test.ts(x)
* Use @/ prefix for imports

Coverage:
* Test happy paths
* Test edge cases
* Test error states
```

You can then run this command with a file path:

`/write_tests the use-auth.ts file in the hooks directory`

The arguments don't have to be file paths—they can be any string you want to pass to provide Claude with context and direction for the task.

## Key Benefits

- **Automation** - Turn repetitive workflows into a single command
- **Consistency** - Ensure the same steps are followed every time
- **Context** - Give Claude specific instructions and conventions for your project
- **Flexibility** - Use arguments to make commands work with different inputs

Custom commands are especially useful for project-specific workflows, such as running test suites, deploying code, or generating boilerplate according to team conventions.

#### Video Transcript

When you run Claude Code, you can type a slash and see the list of commands built into Claude Code by default. In addition to these default commands, you can also easily add your own custom commands. Custom commands are useful for automating repetitive tasks you run frequently. Let me show you how to create one.

In my project directory, I'll find the .Claude folder. Inside it, I'll create a new directory called commands. Then inside that, I'll create a new file called audit.md. The name of the file we create, in this case audit, will be the name of the command we ultimately run.

The goal of this command is to audit all the different dependencies installed in the project, update them if there are any vulnerabilities, and then run the tests to make sure nothing is broken. After creating the command file, you need to restart Claude Code. Don't forget to restart it. When you reopen Claude Code, type slash audit.

This will show the command you just created. Then you can run it, and in this case, it will do exactly what we asked Claude to do. It will run a command to see if there are any vulnerable packages, fix them if necessary, and then run the tests. Commands can also take arguments.

Let me show you an example. I'll create another command called write test. Whenever I run this command, I want to create some tests for a specific file in the project. In the command text, I'll put a dollar sign plus arguments placeholder.

Whenever I run that command, if I pass in a path to a file, that path will be inserted where the dollar sign arguments are. So now I can restart Claude Code again, and then execute the write test command. Now, to be clear, the argument we pass in doesn't have to be a file path. It can be any string we want to pass in.

So I might casually request tests for files in a specific folder, giving Claude a bit of direction about where to look.

---

### 11. MCP servers with Claude Code

> Type: Video | Duration: 2:53 | Video: 008 - Extending Claude Code with MCP Servers.mp4

You can extend Claude Code's capabilities by adding MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers. These servers can run on a remote or local machine and provide Claude with new Tools and capabilities it doesn't normally have.

One of the most popular MCP servers is Playwright, which gives Claude the ability to control a web browser. This opens up powerful possibilities for web development workflows.

## Installing the Playwright MCP Server

To add the Playwright server to Claude Code, run this command in your terminal (not inside Claude Code):

```
claude mcp add playwright npx @playwright/mcp@latest
```

This command does two things:

- Names the MCP server "playwright"
- Provides the command to launch the server on your local machine

## Managing Permissions

The first time you use an MCP server tool, Claude will request permission every time. If you get tired of these permission prompts, you can pre-approve the server by editing the settings.

Open the `.claude/settings.local.json` file and add the server to the `allow` array:

```
{
  "permissions": {
    "allow": ["mcp__playwright"],
    "deny": []
  }
}
```

Note the double underscore in `mcp__playwright`. This allows Claude to use the Playwright tools without requesting permission every time.

## Practical Example: Improving Component Generation

Here's a practical example of how the Playwright MCP server can improve your development workflow. Instead of manually testing and tweaking prompts, you can have Claude:

1. Open a browser and navigate to your application
2. Generate a test component
3. Analyze the visual styling and code quality
4. Update the generation prompt based on its observations
5. Test a new component with the improved prompt

For example, you could ask Claude:

> "Navigate to localhost:3000, generate a basic component, review the styling, and update the generation prompt at @src/lib/prompts/generation.tsx to produce better components going forward."

Claude will use the browser Tools to interact with your application, inspect the generated output, and then modify your prompt file to encourage more original and creative designs.

## Results and Benefits

In practice, this approach can lead to significantly better results. Instead of generating generic purple-to-blue gradients and standard Tailwind patterns, Claude might update the prompt to encourage:

- Warm sunset gradients (orange to pink to purple)
- Ocean dark themes (teal to emerald to cyan)
- Asymmetric designs and overlapping elements
- Creative spacing and unconventional layouts

The main advantage is that Claude can see the actual visual output, not just the code, which enables it to make more informed decisions about styling improvements.

## Exploring Other MCP Servers

Playwright is just one example of what MCP servers can do. The ecosystem includes servers for:

- Database interactions
- API testing and monitoring
- File system operations
- Cloud service integration
- Development tool automation

Consider exploring MCP servers that fit your specific development needs. They can transform Claude from a code assistant into a comprehensive development partner that can interact with your entire toolchain.

#### Video Transcript

You can add new Tools and capabilities to Claude Code by using MCP servers. These MCP servers can run either remotely or locally on your machine. One very popular MCP server is called Playwright, which gives Claude Code the ability to control a browser. Let me show you how to add it to Claude Code, and then we'll use it to further develop our application.

To install the server in your terminal, rather than inside Claude Code, we'll run `Claude MCP Add`. Then give this MCP server a name; I'll name it Playwright. Then after this name, we'll add a command that will launch the server locally on your machine. Then we can start Claude Code and ask it to open a browser and navigate to our application at localhost 3000.

Before the browser opens, you might notice that you need to grant the tool permission to run. If you get tired of all these permission pop-ups, you can open the Claude directory, in settings.local.json. Then in the allow array, you can add a string `mcp_underscore_underscore`, note there are two underscores here, `playwright`. This allows Claude Code to use this MCP server and the tools inside it in any way, without you having to provide permission every time.

If I restart Claude Code and then ask it again to open a browser, it will do so without me providing permission. There are many ways you can use the Playwright MCP server. Let me show you an example that's very applicable to the project we're working on right now. Back in my editor, I'll find the SRC, Lib, prompts, generation.tsx file.

This is the prompt used to actually generate the components you request in our application. So I want to allow Claude Code to use the browser, generate a component on its own, and then adjust this prompt on its own based on the generated component. Hopefully, in the end, we'll generate better-looking components from our application. So let me show you how we do that.

Back in Claude Code, I'll ask it to navigate to localos3000. Try to generate a component, look at the generated source code and evaluate the styling, then update the prompt in the generation.tsx file, so that hopefully our generated components will end up with better styling. So let's see how it does. Claude will first open the browser.

It will try to generate a component. From some of Claude's comments, it seems it's not very satisfied. You might notice that it complains about a styling that's very common in similar applications, namely the purple-to-blue gradient. Claude will then update our prompt and try to generate a new component.

Honestly, this is actually a much better result than I expected. This recommendation card looks really great. From these results alone, you can immediately get a sense that MCP servers really do open the door to many interesting use cases. I strongly recommend you look into some MCP servers that might help Claude develop whatever kind of project you're personally working on.

### 12. Github integration

> Type: Video | Duration: 3:40 | Video: 009 - Github Integration.mp4

Claude Code offers an official GitHub integration that allows Claude to run in GitHub Actions. This integration provides two main workflows: mention support for issues and pull requests, and automatic pull request reviews.

## Setting Up the Integration

To get started, run `/install-github-app` in Claude. This command walks you through the setup process:

- Install the Claude Code app on GitHub
- Add your API key
- Automatically generate a pull request containing the workflow files

The generated pull request adds two GitHub Actions to your repository. Once merged, you'll have the workflow files in the `.github/workflows` directory.

## Default GitHub Actions

This integration provides two main workflows:

### Mention Action

You can mention Claude in any issue or pull request using `@claude`. When mentioned, Claude will:

- Analyze the request and create a task plan
- Have full codebase access to execute the task
- Reply with the results directly in the issue or PR

### Pull Request Action

Whenever you create a pull request, Claude automatically:

- Reviews the proposed changes
- Analyzes the impact of the modifications
- Posts a detailed report on the pull request

## Customizing the Workflows

After merging the initial pull request, you can customize the workflow files to fit your project's needs. Here's how to enhance the mention workflow:

### Adding Project Setup

Before Claude runs, you can add steps to prepare your environment:

```
- name: Project Setup
  run: |
    npm run setup
    npm run dev:daemon
```

### Custom Instructions

Provide Claude with context about your project setup:

```
custom_instructions: |
  The project is already set up with all dependencies installed.
  The server is already running at localhost:3000. Logs from it
  are being written to logs.txt. If needed, you can query the
  db with the 'sqlite3' cli. If needed, use the mcp__playwright
  set of tools to launch a browser and interact with the app.
```

### MCP Server Configuration

You can configure MCP servers to give Claude additional capabilities:

```
mcp_config: |
  {
    "mcpServers": {
      "playwright": {
        "command": "npx",
        "args": [
          "@playwright/mcp@latest",
          "--allowed-origins",
          "localhost:3000;cdn.tailwindcss.com;esm.sh"
        ]
      }
    }
  }
```

## Tool Permissions

When running Claude in GitHub Actions, you must explicitly list all allowed Tools. This is especially important when using MCP servers.

```
allowed_tools: "Bash(npm:*),Bash(sqlite3:*),mcp__playwright__browser_snapshot,mcp__playwright__browser_click,..."
```

Unlike local development, there are no permission shortcuts in GitHub Actions. Every Tool from every MCP server must be listed individually.

## Best Practices

When setting up Claude's GitHub integration:

- Start with the default workflows and customize incrementally
- Use Custom Instructions to provide project-specific context
- Be explicit about Tool Permissions when using MCP servers
- Test your workflows with simple tasks before tackling complex ones
- Consider your project's specific needs when configuring additional steps

The GitHub integration transforms Claude from a development assistant into an automated team member that can handle tasks, review code, and provide insights directly within your GitHub workflow.

#### Video Transcript

Claude Code has an official GitHub integration that allows Claude Code to run in GitHub Actions. You can set up this integration by running the slash install GitHub app. This will guide you through several steps. First, you'll need to install the Claude Code app on GitHub.

Next, you'll need to add an API key. Then, a pull request will be automatically generated. This pull request adds two different GitHub Actions. The first action adds mentioning support.

So you can mention Claude from within an issue or pull request using @Claude and give Claude some tasks to run. The second action adds support for reviewing pull requests. So whenever you create a pull request, Claude Code will automatically run and review the proposed changes. Both of these actions can be customized, and you can also add additional actions to trigger based on other types of events.

Let me show you how to customize the mention feature. First, we've merged these two action config files into the repository on GitHub. So I need to pull these changes down to my local machine. Then, in the newly created GitHub workflows directory, I'll see these two action config files.

One adds support for pull request reviews, and the other adds support for handling mentions. Now, here's how I want to customize the mention feature. Whenever I mention Claude in an issue or pull request, I want it to be able to run the project and use the Playwright MCP server to access the application in a web browser, all within a GitHub Action. To make this happen, I'll first add a step before Claude Code runs in this workflow.

I'll run the setup command, then start the development server. Then I'll update the Claude Code configuration. I'll add some custom instructions. These instructions are passed directly to Claude, and they allow us to provide some additional directions or context.

In this case, I'll tell Claude that the development server is already running, and that if needed, I can use the Playwright MCP server to access the application in a browser. Then I'll add some configuration to set up the Playwright MCP server itself. There's one more thing to note here. When you run Claude Code in an action, we must explicitly list all the permissions we want to grant Claude Code.

There's a tricky part to this. If you're using an MCP server, you must individually list every tool from every MCP server that you want to allow. Unlike the permission shortcuts we saw earlier. Unfortunately, the Playwright MCP server has many different tools, so they all need to be listed individually.

After completing this configuration update, I'll make sure to commit these changes and push them. Now it's time to test this updated workflow. I'll give Claude a small task. In our actual application, look at these two buttons above.

Now they work correctly. I can switch between the preview panel and the code panel without any issues. But I'll pretend they aren't working as expected. I'll take a screenshot of that button.

I'll create an issue. I'll paste the screenshot. Then I'll mention Claude using @Claude and ask it to verify whether these two buttons are working as expected. Then I'll create the issue and wait.

Now, it may take a minute or two for the action to actually start up and wait for Claude to respond. Remember, as we just saw in the action, we're now setting up the entire application and starting it running before Claude Code even begins to run. But eventually, Claude will respond. It will typically create a checklist of steps to complete the given task.

In this case, it will try to access the application, manually test the buttons, and fix any issues it finds. Claude will notice that the buttons actually work fine, so it will terminate early with a message documenting its findings. Now, this is just a small example of how to use Claude Code's GitHub integration. I recommend you spend some time thinking about how to tailor it to your specific project.

## Part 3: Hooks and the SDK

### 13. Introducing hooks

> Type: Video | Duration: 3:37 | Video: 010 - Introducing Hooks.mp4

Hooks allow you to run commands before or after Claude attempts to run a Tool. They are very useful for implementing automated workflows, such as running a code formatter after a file edit, executing tests when files change, or blocking access to specific files.

## How Hooks Work

To understand Hooks, let's first review the normal flow when interacting with Claude Code. When you ask Claude a question, your query is sent to the Claude model along with the Tool definitions. Claude may decide to use a Tool by providing a formatted response, and then Claude Code executes that Tool and returns the result.

Hooks insert themselves into this process, allowing you to execute code before or after Tool execution happens.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1752618158%2F010_-_Introducing_Hooks_06.1752618158162.png)

There are two types of Hooks:

- **PreToolUse hooks** - Run before a Tool is invoked.
- **PostToolUse hooks** - Run after a Tool is invoked.

## Hook Configuration

Hooks are defined in the Claude settings file. You can add them to:

- **Global** - `~/.claude/settings.json` (affects all projects)
- **Project** - `.claude/settings.json` (shared with the team)
- **Project (not committed)** - `.claude/settings.local.json` (personal settings)

You can write Hooks manually in these files, or use the `/hooks` command inside Claude Code.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1752618158%2F010_-_Introducing_Hooks_07.1752618158600.png)

The configuration structure contains two main parts:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1752618159%2F010_-_Introducing_Hooks_10.1752618159645.png)

## PreToolUse Hooks

`PreToolUse` Hooks run before a Tool executes. They contain a `matcher` that specifies which Tool types to target:

```
"PreToolUse": [
  {
    "matcher": "Read",
    "hooks": [
      {
        "type": "command",
        "command": "node /home/hooks/read_hook.ts"
      }
    ]
  }
]
```

Before the "Read" tool executes, this configuration runs the specified command. Your command receives the details of the Tool Call that Claude wants to make, and you can:

- Allow the operation to proceed normally
- Block the Tool Call and send an error message to Claude

## PostToolUse Hooks

`PostToolUse` Hooks run after a Tool executes. Here's an example that triggers after write, edit, or multi-edit operations:

```
"PostToolUse": [
  {
    "matcher": "Write|Edit|MultiEdit",
    "hooks": [
      {
        "type": "command", 
        "command": "node /home/hooks/edit_hook.ts"
      }
    ]
  }
]
```

Since the Tool Call has already happened, `PostToolUse` Hooks cannot block the operation. However, they can:

- Run follow-up operations (for example, formatting the file that was just edited)
- Provide additional feedback to Claude about the Tool Use

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1752618160%2F010_-_Introducing_Hooks_15.1752618160073.png)

## Practical Applications

Here are some common ways to use Hooks:

- **Code formatting** - Automatically format a file after Claude edits it
- **Testing** - Automatically run tests when files change
- **Access control** - Block Claude from reading or editing specific files
- **Code quality** - Run a linter or type checker and provide feedback to Claude
- **Logging** - Track which files Claude accesses or modifies
- **Validation** - Check naming conventions or coding standards

The key insight is that Hooks allow you to extend Claude Code's capabilities by integrating your own Tools and processes into the workflow. `PreToolUse` Hooks let you control what Claude can do, while `PostToolUse` Hooks let you enhance what Claude has done.

#### Video Transcript

In this video, we're going to learn about Hooks. They allow you to run commands before or after Claude attempts to run a Tool. Hooks can be used to implement some very interesting and very useful functionality. For example, after Claude decides to write a file, you can automatically run a code formatter on the created file, or run tests after a file edit, or block Claude from reading a specific file.

The possibilities are endless, and I've prepared a few good examples to show you how to use Hooks in your specific project. However, first, let me help you understand exactly how Hooks work. As a reminder, when you ask Claude Code something, your query is sent to the Claude model along with some Tool definitions. The Claude model may decide to run a Tool by providing a carefully formatted response.

At this point, Claude Code is responsible for running the requested Tool, perhaps in this case reading a file, and then responding with the result of that Tool Call. Now, Hooks enable us to execute code before or after Tool execution. Hooks that run before a Tool are called `PreToolUse hooks`, because they run before the Tool. Hooks that run after a Tool are called `PostToolUse hooks`, for the same reason.

To define Hooks, we add the configuration to a Claude settings file. Remember, there are several different settings files: one for global use across all projects on your machine, one for your specific project that is shared with other engineers, and one just for your files in a specific project. You can add Hooks manually in these files, or by using the built-in `/hooks` command inside Claude Code itself. The configuration itself looks just like what you see on the right side of the screen.

Let me walk you through this example file so you can get a better sense of what's going on. So, first, notice that this file contains two different sections. One section lists all the commands that should be executed before Tool Use. Remember, these are called `PreToolUse hooks`.

The other section lists all the different commands that should be executed after Tool Use. Again, these are `PostToolUse hooks`. Within each section, we provide a matcher. This indicates which Tool Use Types we're looking for.

So in this example, I want to find usage of the `read` tool. Whenever Claude Code attempts to read a file, I want to run the command listed there. Similarly, in the `PostToolUse` section, after the `write`, `edit`, or `multi-edit` tool is used, I want to run a different command. Now, here's the important part.

This is what Hooks really want to do. The commands you see will receive the details of the Tool Call that Claude wants to run. For a `PreToolUse hook`, you can inspect what Claude wants to do. If for any reason you don't want to allow it, you can block the Tool Use operation and send an error message to Claude.

In the case of a `PostToolUse hook`, the Tool Call has already happened, so it's too late to block it. But you can perform some follow-up operations based on the Tool Call, such as formatting the file that was just edited. You can also provide some message to Claude about that Tool Use. For example, you might decide to run a separate program to check the quality of the edited code, or do type checking, and then provide that feedback to Claude.

Claude may take that feedback and update the file it just wrote. If you're still confused about Hooks or what they're used for, that's totally fine. Understanding Hooks can be really challenging. So let's come back later and use Hooks on an example project to understand how all these steps work together.

---

### 14. Defining hooks

> Type: Video | Duration: 3:44 | Video: 011 - Defining Hooks.mp4

Hooks in Claude Code allow you to intercept and control before or after a Tool Call executes. This gives you fine-grained control over what Claude can and cannot do in your development environment.

## Building a Hook

Creating a Hook involves four main steps:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1752618153%2F011_-_Defining_Hooks_05.1752618152864.png)

1. **Decide on a PreToolUse or PostToolUse hook** - `PreToolUse` hooks can block the execution of a Tool Call, while `PostToolUse` hooks run after a Tool has been used.
2. **Determine which type of tool calls you want to watch for** - You need to specify exactly which tools should trigger your Hook.
3. **Write a command that will receive the tool call** - This command receives data about the proposed Tool Call in JSON format via standard input.
4. **If needed, command should provide feedback to Claude** - Your command's exit code tells Claude whether to allow or block the operation.

## Available Tools

Claude Code provides several built-in Tools that you can monitor with Hooks:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1752618153%2F011_-_Defining_Hooks_07.1752618153492.png)

To know exactly which Tools are available in your current setup, you can ask Claude directly for a list. This is especially useful because the available tools can change when you add Custom MCP servers.

## Tool Call Data Structure

When your Hook command executes, Claude sends JSON data via standard input containing details about the proposed Tool Call:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1752618154%2F011_-_Defining_Hooks_11.1752618154320.png)

```
{
  "session_id": "2d6a1e4d-6...",
  "transcript_path": "/Users/sg/...",
  "hook_event_name": "PreToolUse",
  "tool_name": "Read",
  "tool_input": {
    "file_path": "/code/queries/.env"
  }
}
```

Your command reads this JSON from standard input, parses it, and then decides whether to allow or block the operation based on the Tool Name and Input Parameters.

## Exit Codes and Control Flow

Your Hook command communicates with Claude through Exit Codes:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1752618154%2F011_-_Defining_Hooks_16.1752618154725.png)

- **Exit Code 0** - Everything is fine, allow the Tool Call to proceed.
- **Exit Code 2** - Block the Tool Call (`PreToolUse` hooks only).

When you exit with code 2 in a `PreToolUse` hook, any error message you write to standard error is sent to Claude as feedback, explaining why the operation was blocked.

## Example Use Case

A common use case is to block Claude from reading sensitive files, such as the `.env` file. Since both the `Read` and `Grep` Tools can access file contents, you need to monitor both tool types and check whether they are trying to access a restricted file path.

This approach gives you complete control over Claude's file system access, while providing clear feedback about why certain operations are restricted.

#### Video Transcript

To better understand how Hooks work, we're going to look at a new example project. The file accompanying this lecture is called Query.zip. I encourage you to download this project and open your code editor in it. Once the editor is open, run `NPM Run Setup` in the terminal.

This will install some dependencies and get some Hooks ready to use. To better understand Hooks, we're going to create our own Hook in this project. So here's what I want our Hook to do. In the project root, there's a file called `.env`.

This file contains some sensitive information. Out of an abundance of caution, I want to completely block Claude from reading this file directly. Let me show you a few diagrams to help you understand how we're going to build this Hook. The first step is to decide whether we need a `PreToolUse` or `PostToolUse` hook.

In this case, we want to block Claude from reading a specific file. If we create a `PostToolUse` block, then we would execute our Hook or run our command after Claude has already read the file. So in this case, we definitely need a `PreToolUse hook` to ensure we can block the read operation from happening. The next thing we need to do is decide exactly which type of Tool Call we want to watch for.

I've listed all the current different Tool Names on the right side of this diagram. Now, remembering all the different Tool Names included in Claude Code can be very challenging, especially when you can add your own Custom Tools by using MCP servers. So let me show you a little trick you can use here. If I go back and open Claude Code, I can ask Claude directly for a list of all the different Tool Names it currently has access to.

Among all these different Tools, there are two that can read file contents very easily. First is the `read` tool, and then one that's easily overlooked, but this one can also read file contents, which is the `grep` tool. `grep` can search file contents. So we do need to monitor Tool Calls for both the `read` tool and the `grep` tool.

Next, we need to write a command that will receive some information about the Tool Call that Claude wants to make. Here's how that part works. We'll write a command, and Claude will automatically execute it. Then, on the standard input of that process, Claude will provide some Tool Call Data in JSON format.

I have an example of Tool Call Data in the upper right corner. So it will be a large JSON object that contains some information about the Tool Name and the input to that tool. In this case, the Tool Name is `read`, so Claude is trying to call the `read` tool, and it may be trying to specifically read a file path that points to that `.env` file. Again, this is the file we want to block the read operation on.

So, in our program or command, we need to receive this information via standard input, parse that JSON, then read the Tool Name, Tool Input Arguments, and so on, and decide how we want to handle this Tool Call. Then comes the fourth step. In the fourth step, after our command receives the proposed Tool Call Data, we'll exit. Our Exit Code will provide a signal to Claude Code.

`Exit Code 0` means everything is fine and we want to allow this Tool Call to happen. However, `Exit Code 2` is a signal to Claude Code that we want to block this Tool Call. This applies specifically to `PreToolUse hooks`. Because remember, only in a `PreToolUse hook` can we actually block a Tool Call.

If we exit with code 2, then any standard error logs we generate in the command during that time will also be sent to Claude as feedback. So we can both reject the Tool Call and give Claude a reason at the same time. So that's the whole process. Again, I know there's a lot going on here.

So let's go through the whole process of wiring up everything needed for this Hook, to understand how all these steps work together.

---

### 15. Implementing a hook

> Type: Video | Duration: 4:14 | Video: 012 - Implementing a Hook.mp4

Let's build a Custom hook to prevent Claude from reading sensitive files (such as `.env`). This is a practical example that demonstrates how Hooks can protect your environment variables and other secret data during a development session.

## Setting Up the Hook Configuration

First, we need to configure our Hook in the settings file. Open your `.claude/settings.local.json` file and find the `hooks` section. We'll create a `PreToolUse` hook, since we want to intercept Tool Calls before they execute.

The configuration requires two key parts:

- **Matcher** - Specifies which tools to watch
- **Command** - The script to run when those tools are invoked

For the matcher, we want to capture `read` and `grep` operations that might access the `.env` file:

```
"matcher": "Read|Grep"
```

The pipe symbol (`|`) acts as an OR operator, so this will trigger on either tool. For the command, we'll point to a Node.js script:

```
"command": "node ./hooks/read_hook.js"
```

## Understanding Tool Call Data

When Claude attempts to use a Tool, your Hook receives details about that call in JSON format via standard input. This data includes:

- Session ID and Transcript path
- Hook event name (in our case, `PreToolUse`)
- Tool name (Read, Grep, etc.)
- Tool input parameters, including the file path

Your Hook Script processes this data and can then allow the operation to proceed or block it by exiting with a specific code.

## Implementing the Hook Script

The Hook Script needs to read the Tool Call Data from standard input and check whether Claude is trying to access the `.env` file. Here's the core logic:

```
async function main() {
  const chunks = [];
  for await (const chunk of process.stdin) {
    chunks.push(chunk);
  }
  
  const toolArgs = JSON.parse(Buffer.concat(chunks).toString());
  
  // Extract the file path Claude is trying to read
  const readPath = 
    toolArgs.tool_input?.file_path || toolArgs.tool_input?.path || "";
  
  // Check if Claude is trying to read the .env file
  if (readPath.includes('.env')) {
    console.error("You cannot read the .env file");
    process.exit(2);
  }
}
```

The script checks whether the file path contains `.env`, and blocks the operation if found. When you exit with code 2, Claude receives the error message and understands that the operation was blocked by the Hook.

## Testing Your Hook

After saving the configuration and Hook Script, restart Claude Code for the changes to take effect. Then test it by asking Claude to read your `.env` file.

When Claude attempts the read operation, your Hook will intercept it and return the error message. Claude will recognize that the operation was blocked and explain why to you, often mentioning that a read Hook blocked access to the file.

The same protection also applies to `grep` operations—if Claude tries to search within the `.env` file, the Hook will also block that operation.

## Key Benefits

This approach provides several advantages:

- **Proactive protection** - Blocks access before sensitive data is read
- **Transparent operation** - Claude understands why the operation failed
- **Flexible matching** - Works across multiple tools (read, grep, etc.)
- **Clear feedback** - Provides meaningful error messages

While this particular example focuses on the `.env` file, the same pattern can protect any sensitive file or directory in your project. You can extend the logic to check multiple file patterns, or implement more sophisticated access control based on your security requirements.

#### Video Transcript

Let's walk through building a custom Hook together. Remember, the entire goal here is to block Claude from reading the contents of the `.env` file. In the previous video, we discussed the many different configuration options we need to set up, so in this video, we'll focus mainly on the implementation. First, in the `.Claude` directory, I'll open the `settings.local.json` file.

Remember, inside this, we have a list of `PreToolUse hooks` and `PostToolUse hooks`. As we just discussed, we want to create a `PreToolUse hook` so that we can block Claude from reading the contents of that specific file. I've already added a small configuration section for us, just to save some typing time. We only need to fill in the matcher and the command.

First is the matcher. The matcher will be the tools we want to watch. As we discussed, we want to watch for calls to the `read` and `grep` tools. I'll separate these two tool names with a pipe symbol.

So it's not the letter L or a capital letter I. It's a symbol above the return key on the keyboard. Then, next, we need to provide a command to run when Claude attempts to call these two tools. We can put any command you want here, so it could be a CLI, or a call to a shell script, absolutely anything. To follow the pattern I've already established in the rest of this file, I'll call a Node.js script that I've placed ahead of time in this project's hooks directory.

So, in the hooks directory, I've prepared a `read_hook.js` file for us. This is the file I want to run whenever Claude attempts to call one of these two tools. So to call it, I'll replace the `true` here (which is just a placeholder) with `node ./hooks/read_hook.js`. I'll save this file, and that's all we need to do in this file. Next, we need to actually implement the command that will run when Claude attempts to call the `read` or `grep` tool.

So this will be the `read_hook.js` file. At the top of this file, I have some code that will read data from standard input and parse it into JSON. So the `toolArgs` object here will be the large JSON object I showed you in this diagram. So it will have properties like `session ID`, `tool name`, `tool input`, and so on. So really all we need to do is look at that file path and decide whether it's trying to read the `.env` file.

If it is, then we want to make sure we exit our program or this command here with exit code 2. And hopefully also log some information to Claude saying, sorry, but you cannot read that file. So you'll notice that here, I have some code that will read that file path. You'll also notice that there's a fallback here that looks at `toolInput.path`.

I'll tell you why I added that later. So now let's implement the to-do statement. We'll say if `readPath` includes `.env`, that means Claude is definitely trying to read the `.env` file. If that's the case, then I want to make sure we block the operation and provide some log feedback to Claude.

So I'll first add a `console.error`, specifically a `console.error`, because we want to log to standard error. Remember, this is how we provide some feedback to Claude. I'll say something like "You cannot read the .env file." Then I'll do `process.exit(2)`. So now to test this, I'll save the file.

I'll open Claude Code. If you already have it open, make sure to restart Claude Code. You must restart it for any changes to your hooks to take effect. I'll ask Claude to read the `.env` file.

It may try to read it, but when it tries, we'll return an error saying "You cannot read the .env file." Claude will hopefully recognize that, sorry, you actually cannot read this file. In fact, it can even recognize that it was blocked by a read hook. Now, our hook should also work for grep operations. So if I ask Claude to try the grep tool, this should hopefully also be blocked.

So let's see how it does. Yes, again, it's now blocked. So this is a working Hook that we've completed. Now, this Hook isn't particularly useful, and I'll show you a more useful Hook later.

### 16. Gotchas around hooks

> Please visit the online course platform to view the full content of this lesson.

### 17. Useful hooks!

> Type: Video | Duration: 11:33 | Video: 013 - Useful Hooks!.mp4

Claude Code Hooks can help solve common weaknesses in AI-assisted development, especially on large projects. These Hooks run automatically when Claude makes changes to your code, providing immediate feedback and preventing common problems.

## TypeScript Type Checking Hook

One of the most useful Hooks addresses a fundamental problem: when Claude modifies a function signature, it often doesn't update all the locations throughout the project that call that function.

For example, if you ask Claude to add a `verbose` parameter to a function in `schema.ts`, it will successfully update the function definition, but will miss the call site in `main.ts`. This produces a type error that Claude doesn't immediately catch.

The solution is a `post-tool-use hook` that runs the TypeScript compiler after every file edit:

- Runs `tsc --noEmit` to check for type errors
- Catches any errors found
- Immediately feeds the errors back to Claude
- Prompts Claude to fix the problems in other files

This Hook works for any language that can be type-checked by running a Type Checker. For untyped languages, you can achieve similar functionality using automated tests instead of a Type Checker.

## Query Duplication Prevention Hook

In large projects with many database queries, Claude sometimes creates duplicate functionality instead of reusing existing code. This is especially problematic when you give Claude complex, multi-step tasks where database operations are just one component.

Consider a project structure that contains multiple query files, each containing many SQL functions. When you ask Claude to "create a Slack integration that alerts on orders pending for more than 3 days," it may write a new query instead of using the existing `getPendingOrders()` function.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1752618172%2F013_-_Useful_Hooks%21_09.1752618172075.png)

The Query Duplication Hook solves this by implementing a review process:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1752618172%2F013_-_Useful_Hooks%21_14.1752618172611.png)

Here's how it works:

- Triggers when Claude modifies a file in the `./queries` directory
- Programmatically launches another instance of Claude Code
- Asks the second instance to review the changes and check for similar existing queries
- If a duplicate is found, provides feedback to the original Claude instance
- Prompts Claude to remove the duplicate and use the existing functionality

## Implementation Considerations

Both of these Hooks use the `pre-tool-use` or `post-tool-use hook` system. The TypeScript hook is relatively lightweight and runs quickly. The query duplication hook requires more resources because it launches a separate Claude instance for each review.

For the query Hook, consider the following trade-offs:

- **Benefits**: A cleaner codebase with less duplication
- **Costs**: Each query directory edit adds time and API usage
- **Recommendation**: Only monitor critical directories to minimize overhead

These Hooks use Claude's TypeScript SDK to interact with the AI programmatically. This allows you to create sophisticated workflows where one Claude instance can review and provide feedback on another instance's work.

## Extending These Concepts

These Hooks demonstrate broader principles you can apply to your own projects:

- Use compiler/linter output to provide immediate feedback
- Use a separate AI instance to implement a code review process
- Focus monitoring on valuable directories where consistency matters most
- Balance automation gains against performance costs

The key is identifying specific pain points in your development workflow and creating targeted Hooks to automatically address those problems.

#### Video Transcript

In this video, I want to show you some very useful Hooks that you might want to use in your own projects. These Hooks are designed to address some common weaknesses in Claude Code. To help you understand how the first Hook works, let me quickly demonstrate a problem that Claude Code sometimes runs into, especially on large projects. So in the SRC directory, I'll find schema.ts.

Inside, there's just one function called `create schema`. This function is called from the `main.ts` file, specifically right here. Now, I'll go back to the `schema.ts` file and update the function definition. I'll declare that if you want to call this function, you must also pass in a `verbose` parameter, which must be of type Boolean.

Now, once I add this change, if I go back to the `main.ts` file, I'll get a type error, because I just updated this function's definition, but I didn't actually add a value for `verbose`. So the error here clearly states that no argument was provided for `verbose`. Now, I'll undo that change very quickly. I'll close the `main.ts` file.

Then I'll open Claude Code and ask it to make the exact same change. Now, if I run it, Claude Code will absolutely have no problem making this edit. But it will update this file, and then unfortunately, after making that change, the new `verbose` is right there. Unfortunately, Claude won't go through the project and try to find where that function is actually called, and try to update any different call sites.

So if I now open `main.ts`, we'll see that, in fact, we do have an error here. Unfortunately, Claude didn't really catch this. So the first Hook I want to show you will solve this problem very easily. If you're not familiar with TypeScript, and if you're not, that's okay too.

If I close Claude Code and run the command `TSC-dash-noemit`, it will type-check my entire project. In this type check, we can see the error is very obvious right here. So it's complaining that we're calling `create schema` from the `main.ts` file. So the idea for my Hook is very simple.

I think, anytime we edit a TypeScript file, we should run the TypeScript type checker to see if there are any obvious errors. If there are, we should try to immediately feed those errors back to Claude via a `post-tool-use hook`. Hopefully this gives Claude a signal telling it that it just introduced a type error, and it may need to fix it somewhere else in our project. Now, I've already prepared this Hook for us, fortunately, just to save some time, in the `hooks`, `TSC.js` file.

So in this file, I have a bunch of logic to run the TypeScript type checker, catch any errors found, and feed them back to Claude. Right now, I've disabled this Hook, just to demonstrate what you just saw. So I disabled it by adding `process exit 0` there. I'll remove it.

Now this Hook should work fine. So if I now go back to the `schema.ts` file, remove that redundant flag, restart Claude Code, and then ask it to make the same change again. It will make the change. Then hopefully this time, it will immediately get feedback from the TypeScript type checker, saying, hey, you just introduced an error somewhere else in the project.

Hopefully Claude will go fix it. So we can see here, this is the edit that was made. We got some edit operation feedback from the Hook we wrote together. So it found a problem in one of our different files.

Claude now says, okay, I see. I introduced an error. I need to fix the call to `create schema` in `main.ts`. Then the next update it makes will try to go into that file and update that function call to add the one missing argument.

So this is a Hook you might want to try implementing on your own personal projects. Now, even though this Hook is implemented specifically for TypeScript, it still works for any other type of language, as long as you can run a type checker very easily. Even if you're using an untyped language, you could even achieve the same thing using tests instead of running a type checker. So every time you make an edit, you could run tests to make sure the edit is correct.

Now, the next Hook I want to show you is a bit more challenging to explain, but once you understand the idea behind it, I think you'll definitely find the next Hook really helpful, especially in large projects. To help you understand this Hook, I want to give you some background on this project. In the SRC queries directory, there are many different files. Each different file contains many SQL queries written in different functions.

In particular, in the `orderqueries.ts` file, I want to point out that there's a function in there called `git pending orders`. This query iterates over a database containing some e-commerce-related data. In theory, it will find all the different orders that have been created and are in a pending state. So keep this function in mind for a moment.

Okay, so I'll quickly show you a few diagrams to help you understand a common problem that starts to emerge in large projects. So in this diagram, on my left, I've listed the different query files, and as we can see, each different query file contains many different queries. In particular, the `order queries` file has a `git pending orders` function. So we already have a query that will try to find some different pending orders.

Now, if I ask Claude a question and ask it to update the `main.ts` file to print out all orders that have been pending for more than three days, in a perfect world, Claude would find the `order queries.ts` file. It would find that existing query, and would use it instead of writing a new query. This is what we want. We'll see that if we now use Claude and ask it to do exactly that, we'll get the result we want.

So I'm going to ask Claude in the `main.ts` file to print out the orders that have been pending. To give Claude credit, it will look at the different query files that exist. It will find the `order queries` file. Then, within it, it will recognize that there's already a query called `get pending orders`.

It will try to use that function instead of creating a new query. We don't want a new query. We want Claude to use the existing function. So when we give Claude a very focused and direct task, it's able to understand, yes, I probably shouldn't write a new query. It should at least look at some queries that already exist. This is, of course, good. Now I'm going to give Claude a small challenge.

I'm deliberately making this task a bit harder. First, I'll run slash clear to clear all the context we've accumulated. Then, I want you to look at the task.md file. In this file, I've written a prompt that still asks Claude to find orders that have been pending for a while, but I've also wrapped it in some larger project. I'm asking Claude to write a Slack integration that sends a message to a specific channel every day, containing all the orders that have been pending for too long.

So in this scenario, we still want to find orders pending for too long, but now I've wrapped it in this larger task. If I take this task and feed it into Claude, again, after doing that slash clear operation, we'll find that this time, unfortunately, it won't stay as focused, and it will end up trying to write a brand new Git pending orders query, which again is not what we want, because this leads to duplicate code in our project. If I let it run for a while, I'll eventually see that, yes, it did create a brand new query called Git orders pending too long. So this is an example of Claude losing focus a bit and deciding to write a brand new query instead of reusing the existing one.

Again, we have some duplicate code here, which is probably not what we want. In addition, it not only created the new query, but also created a brand new file, which is probably also not what we want. We probably wanted to add this order-related query to the order queries file. Now that we understand the problem here, let me show you how we can potentially fix it by using a Hook.

Okay, so whenever Claude attempts to modify something in the `queries` directory using the write, edit, or `multi-edit` tool, I'll run the following Hook. First, in this Hook, I'll programmatically launch a new, separate copy of Claude Code. I'll ask this new copy to look at the changes that were just made, and look at some of the existing code in the `queries` directory, to see if a similar query already exists. Then, if an existing query exists, I'll take that feedback and send it back to the original copy of Claude, and I'll ask Claude to maybe decide to fix this situation.

So remove the added query and use the one that already exists. So this will allow us to ensure the queries folder generally stays clean, and that there isn't a bunch of duplicate code in it. So let me show you how this will actually work. First, I'll flip back here.

I'll delete the brand new `order alerts.queries.ts` file and `slack.ts` file that were just created. Then I'll find the `query hook` file in the `hooks` directory. So I've already prepared this Hook for us. Right now it's currently disabled, because I have a `process.exit` at the top.

So let's quickly walk through this Hook. First, I'll tell it that it only reviews changes to the `SRC queries` directory. Then, a bit lower, I'll check and see whether the changes that were just made were made in the `queries` directory. After that, I have a long prompt that asks Claude to review the changes that were just made. And then after that is where I programmatically launch Claude Code.

Specifically, these lines here. This is using the Claude Code TypeScript SDK. I can give you more information later. For now, just understand that this is essentially the same as us using Claude Code in the terminal. Once Claude Code runs and I get a response from it, I check and see whether Claude decided, yes, the changes look fine, or we might have a duplicate query. If there is, then we'll exit early with exit code 2, which will pass this feedback back to Claude, and hopefully tell it that it needs to make a change.

So now that I have this additional Hook, and have enabled it by removing the `process exit zero` at the top, I'll restart Claude Code again, and then run the same query again. Hopefully this time it might initially put in that duplicate query, but then our Hook will run, and hopefully tell it, hey, we don't want that duplicate code. You should use some existing query to implement this functionality. Now, Claude Code will again try to create a brand new, completely separate query file, instead of using the old, existing query.

However, when it tries to create that file, our Hook will run. It will launch that separate copy of Claude Code, which will do some research, and discover that there is indeed an existing query that can be reused. It will provide some suggestions, and say, hey, you can probably update this existing query to fully serve your purpose. We'll see some feedback from Claude (the main instance we're interacting with), saying, ah, yes, this existing query exists.

Let's modify that existing query instead of trying to write a new one. Now, the downside of this Hook is that every time I want to edit something in the `queries` directory, it costs extra time and money to run. But the upside is that there will be much less duplicate code in my `queries` directory. So it really comes down to a set of trade-offs for you, whether you want to implement something like this in your own project.

If you do, I'd at least recommend you do what I showed you in the `query hook`. So that's it, and only monitor a small handful of very important folders in your project, to minimize the amount of extra work being done.

### 18. Another useful hook

> Please visit the online course platform to view the full content of this lesson.

### 19. The Claude Code SDK

> Type: Video | Duration: 2:46 | Video: 014 - The Claude Code SDK.mp4

The Claude Code SDK allows you to run Claude Code programmatically in your own applications and scripts. It's available for TypeScript, Python, and CLI, giving you the same capabilities as the Claude Code you use in the terminal, but integrated into larger workflows.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1752618201%2F014_-_The_Claude_Code_SDK_00.1752618201045.png)

The SDK runs the exact same code as the Claude Code you're already familiar with. It has access to all the same Tools, and will use them to complete whatever task you give it. This makes it especially powerful in automation and integration scenarios.

## Key Features

- Run Claude Code programmatically
- The same Claude Code capabilities as the terminal version
- Inherits all settings from Claude Code instances in the same directory
- Read-only permissions by default
- Most useful as part of a larger pipeline or tool

## Basic Usage

Here's a simple TypeScript example that asks Claude to analyze code to find duplicate queries:

```
import { query } from "@anthropic-ai/claude-code";

const prompt = "Look for duplicate queries in the ./src/queries dir";

for await (const message of query({
  prompt,
})) {
  console.log(JSON.stringify(message, null, 2));
}
```

When you run this code, you'll see the raw conversation between local Claude Code and the Claude language model, with messages displayed one by one. The last message contains Claude's complete response.

## Permissions and Tools

By default, the SDK only has read-only permissions. It can read files, search directories, and perform grep operations, but it cannot write, edit, or create files.

To enable write permissions, you can add the `allowedTools` option to the query:

```
for await (const message of query({
  prompt,
  options: {
    allowedTools: ["Edit"]
  }
})) {
  console.log(JSON.stringify(message, null, 2));
}
```

Alternatively, you can configure permissions in a settings file in the `.claude` directory for project-wide access.

## Practical Applications

The Claude Code SDK excels when integrated into larger development workflows. Consider using it for:

- Git hooks that automatically review code changes
- Build scripts that analyze and optimize code
- Helper commands for code maintenance tasks
- Automated documentation generation
- Code quality checks in CI/CD pipelines

The SDK essentially allows you to add AI-powered intelligence to any part of your development process where programmatic access is valuable.

#### Video Transcript

Just now when we looked at the query review hook, we got a brief glimpse of the Claude Code SDK. The SDK allows you to use Claude Code programmatically. You can use the SDK through the CLI, the TypeScript library, or the Python library. This is exactly the same as the Claude Code you use in the terminal.

It has all the same tools, and will use them to complete the given task. The SDK is most useful as part of a larger pipeline or tool, as we just saw in that hook. You can easily make Claude Code part of a larger process, adding a lot of intelligence to some given workflow. I want to quickly demonstrate the TypeScript SDK by adding it to our existing project.

Back in my editor, I'll find the `SDK.ts` file in the root project directory. Inside, I've written some code to get us started using the SDK. I'll update the prompt at the top, and ask Claude to look for duplicate queries in the SRC queries directory. Then I'll save this file, and open the terminal to run `NPM Run SDK` to run it.

Now, this isn't a built-in command, just so you know, but behind the scenes, it just executes this file as a normal TypeScript file. I just created this little shortcut for us to make it easier to run TypeScript files. When we run it, we'll see the raw conversation between local Claude Code and the Claude Language model, with messages displayed one by one. Eventually, we'll get back to the command line.

The last message printed out will contain Claude's final response. Now there's a small gotcha about the SDK. That is, by default, it only has read capabilities. In other words, it can only read files, directories, perform grep operations, and so on.

It doesn't have the ability to write, edit, or create files, etc. To grant write permissions, you can manually add write permissions in the query call here, or, you can add some permission settings to a settings file in your .Claude directory. Let me show you how we can allow the SDK to use the edit tool in this project. I'll find the `prompt` argument here.

After it, I'll add `options`, put in an object, `allow tools`, which will be an array, and I'll put in `edit`. I'll update the prompt at the top, and ask it to add a description to the `package.json` file. Now I'll save and run `npmrunsdk` again. Then once it finishes, I can open the `package.json` file, and I'll see that it did add a description.

So now it definitely has the ability to edit files. As I mentioned earlier, the Claude Code SDK is most useful as part of other tools. So I encourage you to consider opportunities to use it in your own projects in helper commands, scripts, or most notably, hooks.
