# Lesson 11: Claude and Amazon Bedrock

> Using Claude on AWS Bedrock
> Course link: [Claude and Amazon Bedrock](https://anthropic.skilljar.com/claude-in-amazon-bedrock) | 83 lessons total

---

## Part 1: Course introduction

### 1. Introduction to the course

> This lesson is a course introduction/summary page. Please visit the online platform to view it.

### 2. Overview of Claude Models

> Type: Video | Duration: 3:56 | Video: 02 - 001 - Overview of Claude Models.mp4

Claude offers three distinct model families, each optimized for different priorities. All three models share Claude's core capabilities—they can handle text generation, coding, image analysis, and other tasks. The key difference is how they balance intelligence, speed, and cost.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1750971508%2F02_-_001_-_Overview_of_Claude_Models_02.1750971508481.png)

## Claude Opus

Opus provides Claude's highest level of intelligence. It's designed for complex scenarios that require sophisticated reasoning and planning capabilities.

Opus excels at independently completing complex projects over extended periods. It can manage multi-step processes and handle varying requirements without significant human intervention. The model supports reasoning, which means it can provide quick responses for simple tasks and take time to think through more complex problems.

The trade-off is moderate latency and higher cost. You'll pay more and wait longer for this additional intelligence.

## Claude Sonnet

Sonnet sits in the sweet spot of Claude's lineup, offering a balanced combination of intelligence, speed, and cost suitable for most practical applications.

What makes Sonnet especially valuable is its strong coding ability combined with fast text generation. Many developers appreciate its ability to make precise edits to complex codebases without breaking existing functionality.

## Claude Haiku

Haiku is Claude's fastest model, built specifically for applications where response time is critical. It's optimized for speed and cost efficiency, rather than pursuing maximum intelligence.

One important limitation: Haiku doesn't support the reasoning capabilities offered by Opus and Sonnet. This makes it ideal for user-facing applications that require real-time interaction, but less suitable for complex problem-solving tasks.

## Choosing the Right Model

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1750971509%2F02_-_001_-_Overview_of_Claude_Models_12.1750971509142.png)

Model selection comes down to understanding the trade-off between intelligence and cost/speed. Here's how to decide:

- **Choose Opus** when intelligence is your top priority. If you have complex tasks that require strong reasoning capabilities, you'll be choosing quality over speed and cost.
- **Choose Haiku** when speed matters most. Suitable for real-time user interactions or high-throughput processing that needs the fastest response.
- **Choose Sonnet** when you need balance. Most applications benefit from Sonnet's combination of intelligence, speed, and reasonable cost.

## Using Multiple Models

Many teams don't use just one model. Instead, they use different models for different parts of the same application:

- Haiku for user-facing interactions where speed is critical
- Sonnet for the main business logic
- Opus for complex tasks that require deeper reasoning

This approach lets you optimize each part for your application's specific needs, while managing overall cost and performance.

#### Video Transcript

In this video, we'll explore Claude's three model families and figure out which one is best for your specific use case. To help you understand the differences between these models, I'll walk you through the key characteristics of each one, then show you a simple framework for choosing the right model. Before we dive into the details, let me clarify one thing. All three models share Claude's core capabilities, so they can all handle text generation, coding, image analysis, and many other tasks.

The real difference between them lies in their optimization direction. One focuses on intelligence, one focuses on speed and cost efficiency, and the other strikes a better balance between intelligence and speed. The first is Opus. Opus is Claude's most powerful model.

When I say powerful, I mean it provides the highest level of intelligence you can get from Claude. In practical terms, this means Opus is designed for scenarios with complex requirements that need a high level of intelligence and planning to complete. It can independently handle complex projects over extended periods, like a task that might run for hours, where the model needs to manage a multi-step process and handle many different requirements on its own, without too much human intervention. Opus supports what we call reasoning, which means it can provide quick responses for simple tasks, and take time to think through more complex tasks.

The downside is that Opus has moderate latency and higher cost, which is exactly the trade-off you need to make. While you get extremely high intelligence, each request also costs more time and money. Next is Sonnet. Sonnet occupies a "sweet spot" position in Claude's lineup.

It strikes a good balance between intelligence, speed, and cost, which makes it very useful for most practical use cases. Sonnet's strength lies in its outstanding coding ability along with fast text generation. Many developers love its ability to make precise edits to complex codebases, which means it can make changes to a project without breaking existing functionality. Finally, we have Haiku.

Haiku is Claude's fastest model, designed specifically for applications where response time is critical. One thing to note about Haiku is that it doesn't support the reasoning capabilities offered by Opus and Sonnet. Instead, Haiku is optimized for speed and cost efficiency. This makes Haiku a great choice for user-facing applications that require real-time interaction.

Now, let's talk about how to choose which of these three models for your specific application. The key to model selection is understanding the trade-offs between these different models. On one hand, you have extremely high intelligence; on the other hand, you have to weigh cost and speed. Opus leans toward intelligence.

It's very intelligent, more expensive, and has higher latency. Haiku leans toward cost and speed. It has moderate intelligence, low cost, and the fastest speed. And Sonnet is in the middle, striking a good balance between these different qualities.

So how do you decide which model to use? You really need to determine what matters most for your specific use case. If intelligence is your top priority, meaning you have a complex task that requires strong reasoning capabilities, then you'll probably want to use Opus. You're choosing quality over speed and cost.

If speed is your priority, meaning you have real-time user interactions, or you have high-throughput processing needs that require getting a response as quickly as possible, then you should choose Haiku. If you need a balance between intelligence, speed, and cost, which is usually the case for most applications, then Sonnet is probably your best choice. One thing to note here is that many teams don't just pick one model and stick with it. Instead, you might use multiple different models for different parts of the same application.

You might use Haiku for user-facing interactions where speed is critical, Sonnet for the main business logic, and Opus for the truly complex tasks that require deeper reasoning. That covers Claude's three model families and how to choose between them. By the way, we'll generally use Claude Sonnet in this course, because it offers a very nice balance between these three different qualities.

---

## Part 2: Working with the API

### 3. Accessing the API

> Type: Video | Duration: 2:31 | Video: 001 - Accessing the API.mp4

When building applications with AI models, you need to understand the flow of data from user input to the AI-generated response. Let's look at how AWS Bedrock works, and understand what happens behind the scenes in a typical chat application.

## How a Chat Application Works

Imagine you're building a web application with a simple chat interface. A user types "Define quantum computing" and clicks send. Here's what actually happens:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557570%2F05_-_001_-_Accessing_the_API_02.1748557570066.png)

The user sees a clean interface, but behind the scenes there's an entire system at work to generate that response.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557570%2F05_-_001_-_Accessing_the_API_03.1748557570599.png)

## The Request Flow

When a user submits text, the message goes through the following process:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557571%2F05_-_001_-_Accessing_the_API_04.1748557571285.png)

1. The user submits their message through your web interface
2. Your server receives the request containing that text
3. Your server uses the Bedrock client to send a request to AWS Bedrock
4. The request includes the user message and the model ID (e.g., Claude Haiku or Claude Sonnet)
5. The selected model processes the request and generates text
6. AWS Bedrock sends back an assistant message containing the generated response
7. Your server forwards this response back to the user's browser

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557572%2F05_-_001_-_Accessing_the_API_08.1748557571907.png)

#### Video Transcript

In this module, we'll learn how to access Claude models through AWS Bedrock and use them to generate a certain amount of text. First, I want to walk you through a small example application—a straightforward, standard chatbot application. So let's imagine you're making a web application, and you want to show users a chat interface where they can type in some messages and click send. Whenever they do, their expectation is that some response will magically appear.

But let's examine what happens behind the scenes to actually make this happen. Whenever a user submits some text, a request containing that text is sent to your server. Your server will use the Bedrock client to send a request to the AWS Bedrock service, where the Anthropic models are actually hosted. This request sent to the Bedrock service will contain something called a user message, which contains the text the user submitted, along with a model ID, which specifies which model we want to run.

The selected model will run, it will generate some text, and send it back to your server through something called an assistant message. Then, your server can take the generated text and send it back to the browser, where it can be rendered on the screen for the user to view. Now, in this part of the course, we'll focus entirely on the communication layer between the Bedrock client and the Bedrock service. We'll look at how to make API requests, how to access the generated text, and discover some common design patterns along the way.

Before we continue, I want to clarify a common point of confusion, specifically the difference between the Bedrock API and the Anthropic API. First, a very quick recap. Earlier in this course, we discussed the Claude Sonnet and Claude Haiku models. These models are responsible for the actual computational work of generating a certain amount of text.

The Claude family of models is currently hosted through two different services: AWS Bedrock and the official Anthropic API. If you're taking this course, you'll be using the Claude family of models from the AWS Bedrock service. Now, the important point you need to be aware of. These really are different services.

They're accessed through different SDKs. They have different documentation sources. So whenever you consult documentation or try to understand how to use the models, be sure to make sure you're looking at resources specifically for AWS Bedrock. Now that we've cleared up this point of confusion, let's go ahead and make our first request to Bedrock in the next video.

---

### 4. Making a request

> Type: Video | Duration: 7:34 | Video: 05 - 002 - Making a Request.mp4

Making your first API request to AWS Bedrock requires three essential components: a Bedrock Runtime Client to connect to the service, a Model ID to specify which model to run, and a User Message containing the text to feed into the model.

## Setting Up the Bedrock Client

Start by creating a client to connect to the Bedrock runtime service using boto3:

```
import boto3

client = boto3.client("bedrock-runtime", region_name="us-west-2")
```

## Understanding Model IDs and Regional Availability

This is where things get a bit tricky. Not every model is available in every AWS region. If you try to run a model that doesn't exist in your selected region, you'll receive a cryptic error message stating that the model doesn't exist.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557580%2F05_-_002_-_Making_a_Request_04.1748557580294.png)

For example, if Claude Sonnet is available in us-west-2, but you make a request from us-east-1, your request will fail.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557581%2F05_-_002_-_Making_a_Request_05.1748557580814.png)

## Using Inference Profiles

Inference profiles solve the regional availability problem by automatically routing your request to a region that actually hosts your selected model.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557581%2F05_-_002_-_Making_a_Request_06.1748557581635.png)

Instead of tracking which models are in which regions, you can use an inference profile that knows the model is available in multiple regions, such as us-west-2 and us-east-2.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557582%2F05_-_002_-_Making_a_Request_07.1748557582083.png)

When you make a request using an inference profile, AWS automatically routes it to the correct region where your model exists, even if you're connecting from a different region.

To find the inference profile ID, go to the AWS Bedrock console and look under "Cross-region inference," rather than using the model ID from the main model catalog page.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557583%2F05_-_002_-_Making_a_Request_10.1748557583183.png)

Copy the inference profile ID for your selected model.

## Creating User Messages

User messages have a specific structure that may seem overly complex at first glance, but there's a good reason for it:

```
user_message = {
    "role": "user",
    "content": [
        {"text": "What's 1+1?"}
    ]
}
```

The content is a list because a single message can contain different types of content—text, images, or other media types. This structure allows you to send multimodal requests.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557584%2F05_-_002_-_Making_a_Request_18.1748557583888.png)

## Making the Request

Now you can make the API call using the `converse` method:

```
response = client.converse(
    modelId=model_id,
    messages=[user_message]
)
```

The response contains a lot of metadata, but to get just the generated text, you need to navigate through the response structure:

```
response["output"]["message"]["content"][0]["text"]
```

## Understanding Message Types

You'll primarily use two message types:

- **User messages** – The content you want to feed into the model (role: "user")
- **Assistant messages** – The content the model generates (role: "assistant")

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557584%2F05_-_002_-_Making_a_Request_17.1748557584391.png)

Both message types follow the same structure, with a `role` and a `content` list. This consistency makes it easy to build a conversation by alternating user messages and assistant messages.

The assistant message you receive from Bedrock has the exact same format as your user message, except for a different `role`. This standardized structure makes it straightforward to chain multiple requests together for longer conversations.

#### Video Transcript

Let's get our hands dirty and write some code. In this video, we'll focus on making our first API request to AWS Bedrock. We'll run a model and generate a small amount of text. Now you'll notice that I've already created a Python notebook.

I encourage you to pause the video here and create your own notebook, so you can follow along with me and get some experience making these API requests. To make our first request, we need three things. First, we need a client to actually access Bedrock. We'll create this client using the Bodo3 Python module.

Second, we need a Model ID. This will be the ID of the exact model hosted in Bedrock that we want to run. Finally, we also need something called a User Message. This will be a specially formatted object that contains the text we want to feed into the model.

Back in my notebook, let me show you how we'll create that client, get the model ID, and create the user message. First, I'll create a client using the Bodo3 module. I'll specifically connect to the Bedrock runtime and specify a region name. In my case, I'll use US West 2.

Next, we need to specify our model ID, which will be a string. Unfortunately, the model ID will actually be a bit more complex than it first appears. So let me show you a few diagrams to help you understand what's going on. Now, as a reminder, whenever we make a request to Bedrock, we need to include the ID of the actual model we want to run.

We'll send that request to a very specific region. In my case, I entered US-S2. But here's a very big gotcha that isn't very clearly stated in the documentation, but that you really need to be aware of. It turns out that not every model is hosted in every region.

So, for example, US East 1 is another region in AWS. And US East 1 may not actually be running the exact model I want to execute. So if I send this request to US East 1, I might end up getting an error message stating that the model simply isn't available. To resolve that error, I could try to figure out which region actually hosts the model I'm looking for.

Or, I could also try to keep track of which model is hosted in which region. But, there's an easier way to solve this problem using something called an Inference Profile. Inference profiles are used to route requests to a different region that actually hosts your selected model. So, for example, we might have an inference profile that says this specific model is guaranteed to be hosted in U.S.

West 2 and U.S. East 2. Here's what the inference profile does behind the scenes. We'll still make a request to some specific region hosted in AWS.

But, instead of specifying a specific model ID, we'll specify the ID of an inference profile. Then, when we send that request, AWS automatically reroutes our request to another region where the model is guaranteed to be hosted. So the reason I mentioned that the model ID here is a bit hard to understand is because it's called a model ID everywhere. But in reality, we usually don't actually enter a model ID.

Instead, we provide the ID of an inference profile, so that we get automatic request routing. Let me very quickly show you an example in the Bedrock console, so the concept of an inference profile is very clear. So in my Bedrock console, I've gone to the model catalog on the left. Then, I'll look at one of the Claude family models.

Then on this page, if I scroll down a bit, I'll see here that, yes, I have a model ID. But again, this model ID is probably not what we actually want to add to our request. Instead, what we probably want to use is "Cross-region inference" on the left. If I click on it, I'll find the model I'm looking for.

Here we go. Then on this page, I'll find my Inference Profile ID. And this inference profile ID is exactly what we want, because it will automatically route our request to some region where the model is guaranteed to be hosted. So I'll grab that inference profile ID, copy it, and then put it back in my notebook and assign it to the model ID.

The last thing we need to create before making a request is our User Message. Remember, this is a specially formatted object that contains the text we want to feed into the model. I'll create a variable below called `user_message`. It will be a Python dictionary with a `role` of `user`, and the `content` will be a list containing another nested dictionary that contains some text.

The text value will be what I want to feed into the model. So in my case, I'll ask the model: "What's 1+1?" Now, I'll walk you through the structure of user messages in detail later. But for now, we're just focused on making a request to make sure we can actually generate some text.

To make our actual request, I'll scroll down a bit and add a call to `client.converse`. We'll input our `modelId`, and notice that the keyword argument here is `modelId`, where the `I` is capitalized. I'll also add `messages`. This will be our list of messages.

In this case, we only have one. It will be the user message we just created. Then I'll make sure I run both cells. Then I'll add a new cell below it and print out the `response`.

This response contains a lot of information, but to get just the generated text, you need to navigate the response structure:

`response["output"]["message"]["content"][0]["text"]`

So this will look at the output property, look at the message inside it, look at the content inside that, look at the first element in this list, and then grab our text, which is where our generated text actually lives. So if I now run this cell again, I'll see just the generated text "1 + 1 = 2". Now, I'll make a change to this line very quickly. I'll remove the content, remove the zero lookup in the text, and then run that cell again.

Now I'll get a dictionary, which has a `role` of `assistant` and some `content` assigned to it. If you look at this dictionary, it may look a bit familiar. It looks very similar to the structure we just created, specifically the user message here. So let me help you understand what these messages are and what they do for us.

Messages contain the text we want to feed into the model, and they also contain the text that the model ultimately generates. There are two different types of messages: user messages and assistant messages. So a user message will always contain the text we want to feed into the model. And an assistant message will contain the text generated by the model itself.

The structure of these two different message types is always very similar. The only biggest difference between them is that the `role` of a user message is always `user`, while the `role` of an assistant message is always `assistant`. Both message types will always have a `content` property, which we'll call a list. You might be a bit curious why there's a list there, why it isn't just a simple text property that tells us what we fed into the model or what we got back.

The `content` property is a list because, ultimately, we'll see that a single message can contain many different parts. For example, we might send a message to the model that contains an image and some text. We would encode the image and text as separate parts in the list of content items. We won't worry about messages containing multiple content parts for now.

I just mention it to help you understand why there's that extra syntax. Okay, now that we've successfully made our first request, let's come back later and discuss it in more detail.

### 5. Multi-Turn conversations

> Type: Video | Duration: 9:05 | Video: 05 - 003 - Multi-Turn Conversations.mp4

The code we've written so far simulates a very simple exchange with Claude. But what happens when you want to continue the conversation? When you ask a follow-up question after asking "What's 1+1?", such as "And 3 more added to that?", you might expect Claude to understand that you're asking to add 3 to the previous result of 2.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557630%2F05_-_003_-_Multi-Turn_Conversations_01.1748557630812.png)

However, there's critical information you need to understand about the Bedrock API and Claude itself.

## No Message Storage

Bedrock and Claude don't store any messages. Any message you send isn't stored, and any response you receive isn't stored either. Every API call is completely independent.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557631%2F05_-_003_-_Multi-Turn_Conversations_02.1748557631276.png)

To have a multi-message conversation that maintains context, you need to:

- Manually maintain a list of all messages in your code
- Provide the complete list of messages in each subsequent request

## Why Context Matters

Let's see what happens without proper context. If you only send "And 3 more added to that?" as a standalone message, Claude doesn't know what you're referring to. It will do its best to respond, but the answer won't make sense because it lacks the context of your previous conversation.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557631%2F05_-_003_-_Multi-Turn_Conversations_05.1748557631762.png)

When you only send the follow-up question, Claude only sees that isolated message and tries to respond without knowing about the previous "What's 1+1?" exchange.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557632%2F05_-_003_-_Multi-Turn_Conversations_06.1748557632283.png)

## Building Conversation Context

To maintain context, you need to include the complete conversation history in each request. Here's how it works:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557633%2F05_-_003_-_Multi-Turn_Conversations_07.1748557632856.png)

Your message list should include all previous exchanges—both user messages and assistant responses. When you send this complete context, Claude can understand that "And 3 more added to that?" refers to adding 3 to the previous result of 2.

## Helper Functions for Message Management

To make conversation management easier, you can create helper functions:

```
def add_user_message(messages, text):
    user_message = {
        "role": "user",
        "content": [
            {"text": text}
        ]
    }
    messages.append(user_message)

def add_assistant_message(messages, text):
    assistant_message = {
        "role": "assistant", 
        "content": [
            {"text": text}
        ]
    }
    messages.append(assistant_message)

def chat(messages):
    response = client.converse(
        modelId=model_id,
        messages=messages
    )
    return response["output"]["message"]["content"][0]["text"]
```

## Implementing Multi-Turn Conversations

Here's how to build a conversation step by step:

```
# Make a starting list of messages
messages = []

# Add in the initial user question of "What's 1+1?"
add_user_message(messages, "What's 1+1?")

# Pass the list of messages into chat to get an answer
answer = chat(messages)

# Take the answer and add it as an assistant message into our list
add_assistant_message(messages, answer)

# Add in the user's followup question
add_user_message(messages, "And 3 more added to that?")

# Call chat again with the list of messages to get a final answer
answer = chat(messages)
print(answer)
```

This approach ensures Claude has the full context and can respond appropriately: "Starting from the result of 1+1 = 2, if we add 3 more, we get: 2 + 3 = 5"

## Message Role Alternation

When building a message list, be sure to ensure the message roles alternate correctly:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557633%2F05_-_003_-_Multi-Turn_Conversations_19.1748557633396.png)

Your conversation should follow this pattern: user → assistant → user → assistant. You must never have two user messages or two assistant messages in a row. The API requires this alternating pattern, which reflects the natural flow of conversation.

While this manual message management might seem tedious at first, you'll quickly get used to it. This pattern is the foundation for building any application that needs to maintain conversation context with Claude.

#### Video Transcript

The code we've written so far simulates a very simple exchange with our model. We can visualize this conversation with a chat box like this. So we send a request asking "What's 1 plus 1?" We got a very simple reply.

Like the answer is 2. Naturally, at some point we might want to continue this conversation. So we might want to send a follow-up question, like "And 3 more?" Then we expect to get a reply saying "2 plus 3 more gives you 5".

To have this kind of multi-message conversation, you need to understand a very critical issue with the Bedrock API and Claude itself. That is, Bedrock and Claude don't store any messages. Any message you send isn't stored in any way, and any reply you receive isn't stored in any way either. So if you want to have some kind of conversation where multiple messages can maintain context or flow, then you need to do two things.

You need to manually maintain a list of all the messages you're exchanging in your code. Second, you need to make sure that you provide that complete list of messages in every subsequent request you make. So let's go through the whole concept in detail, so you're really clear on what's happening. The first thing I want to do is run some example code.

So I still have the code we wrote in the last video, where we asked what 1 plus 1 is, and we got that 1 plus 1 is 2. Now I'll paste in some additional code snippets that I wrote ahead of time. So in this additional code snippet I just pasted, I'm making another call to Bedrock, this time providing a message that's just "And 3 more?" This kind of simulates the screenshot I just showed you.

Now, if I run this cell, I'll end up getting a reply that makes no sense at all. So let me show you a diagram to make sure it's clear why we got this very strange text. Okay. So on the left, I have the messages I included in the request sent to Bedrock.

I initially sent just one message, "What's 1 plus 1?" We sent it, and we got the assistant message "1 plus 1 is 2", which is exactly what we expected. Then, when I executed the code you just saw (that additional code snippet), I sent a request that contained only one message, which said "And 3 more?" Now, when we send this "And 3 more?", Claude will do its best to give you a reply.

In our case, the reply we actually got doesn't make much sense, but it's doing its best, because it only knows that we said "And 3 more?". So to solve this problem, here's what we'll do. As I just mentioned, we'll manually maintain a list of these messages in our code, and make sure to provide that list in each subsequent request. So as a demonstration of what we'll do next, whenever we want to have a multi-turn conversation, we'll still send this original user message.

We'll receive the assistant reply, and then we'll include that assistant message in our list of messages. So we move it to the left. Then, whenever we want to continue the conversation, we'll add another user message to the end of the list, where we ask "And 3 more?" Now, when we take the complete list of messages and make a request, Claude will have the complete context of the conversation.

It will see all three messages, and when we say "And 3 more?", it will know exactly what we're talking about. So we should expect to get a reply, like 3 plus 2 is 5. Let's go back to our notebook and try to simulate this multi-turn conversation. Okay, back here, I'll very quickly delete that little code snippet I pasted.

Then in this earlier code cell, I'll write three helper functions. These helper functions just make it easier for us to maintain the context of the entire conversation and maintain the message list. So above the user message, I'll define an `add_user_message` function. I'll assume I'll call this function with a message list and some text.

I'll indent the user message like this. I'll replace the "What's 1 plus 1" here with the text parameter we'll pass in. Then after we define the user message, I'll add it to the message list. Next, I'll copy this function entirely.

I'll paste it here. I'll rename it to `add_assistant_message`, change the name of this variable to `assistant_message`, change the role to `assistant`, and finally update the `messages.append` here to `assistant_message`. So now we have two helper functions that can very easily create message types for us, whether a user message or an assistant message, and append it to an existing message list.

Then there's one more helper function. Here, I'll make it easier for us to call the model, get a reply, and then get the actual text we care about. So I'll also define a function here called `chat`. It will receive a list of messages.

I'll indent `response`. I'll pass in the message list like this. Then I'll return `response`, `output`, `message` from this function. Do you remember all the things to type?

Then `content`. Right. Then we get zero and text. So now, with these three functions, we can very easily simulate a complete conversation.

Let me show you how we'll do this. In this cell here, I'll paste in some comments I wrote ahead of time, just to guide myself and make sure I conduct the entire conversation correctly. So here's my list of comments. We'll put in one line of code for each comment I added.

But don't worry, each line of code is very short and simple. First, I'll create an initial list of messages. So initially, I don't have any communication. Then I'll add the initial user question, which is the initial user message "What's 1 plus 1?".

To do this, I'll call `add_user_message`, passing in the message list, and then the second argument will be the actual text I want to include in this message. So in this example, it will be "What's 1 plus 1?" Then immediately after, I'll print out the messages very quickly to make sure we're on the right track. So I'll run it.

It looks like, yes, we correctly added our user message. So I have a `user` role, and the `content` is a list with a Python dictionary inside. So I'll move on to the next step. I'll pass the message list to the `chat` function we created, to get an answer.

So when we call that `chat` function, it will contact Bedrock and call our API. So we'll say `answer` is `chat` with the message list passed in. Then again, to make sure I'm on the right track, I'll print out `answer`. I'll run it.

After a brief pause, I see that, yes, we got it correctly. We got the answer "1 plus 1 equals 2". So now we got this answer as a plain string. We need to take it and add it as an assistant message to our list.

Again, we'll use the helper function we just made to do this. So we'll call `add_assistant_message`, passing in our list and the answer we just got. Then again, print out the messages, to make sure we're doing this correctly. So now we'll see that, yes, we are indeed building our conversation.

We're building our message list. We start with the user, then the assistant. We got "What's 1 plus 1", and then the answer we got. So now we can address the user's additional question, which is `add_user_message`.

We'll say, "And 3 more?" We won't check the messages again. Let's call our API again, passing in the complete message list, to get the follow-up answer. I'll print out the final answer, and let's see how it turns out.

Sure enough, starting from the result of 1+1=2, we add 3 more, and end up with 5. So now it's clear that by including all the previous messages in subsequent requests, we are maintaining the context of this conversation. Now, this might seem a bit tedious at first, and even the code here might look a bit confusing. If it's confusing, I won't blame you.

Don't worry, you'll quickly get used to this kind of thing. I just want to mention one more thing about the message list, like the one we assembled here. That is, whenever we send a message list to the API, we need to make sure each message's role alternates correctly. In other words, we should always be user, then assistant, then user, then assistant.

We should never send two user messages in a row, nor two assistant messages in a row, or anything like that. So we always want to make sure we're changing or alternating these roles.

### 7. System prompts

> Type: Video | Duration: 7:20 | Video: 05 - 005 - System Prompts.mp4

When building an AI chatbot for a specific use case, you need a way to control how the AI responds. System prompts are what transform a general-purpose AI into an assistant that follows specific guidelines and stays professionally on-topic.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557628%2F05_-_005_-_System_Prompts_00.1748557628015.png)

## The Problem with User-Level Instructions

You might think the solution is to include all the requirements in the user message itself. For example, telling the AI in every conversation to "mention AWS services" and "don't mention competitors." This approach has serious limitations:

- You need to anticipate all possible questions and edge cases
- The list of instructions becomes unwieldy and repetitive
- The user sees all the internal instructions, cluttering the conversation
- The requirements change depending on the specific question asked

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557629%2F05_-_005_-_System_Prompts_05.1748557629207.png)

## System Prompts: A Better Approach

System prompts solve this problem by giving Claude a role. Instead of listing specific "dos" and "don'ts," you tell Claude to play a specific type of professional. Then the AI naturally responds like that person would.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557629%2F05_-_005_-_System_Prompts_07.1748557629765.png)

System prompts provide several key advantages:

- Claude gets guidance on how to respond consistently
- The AI adopts the mindset and constraints of the specified role
- Responses automatically stay focused and on-brand
- You don't need to anticipate every possible situation

## Implementing System Prompts

To add a system prompt to your Claude conversation, you can pass it as a parameter to the `converse` function:

```
system_prompt = """
You are an AWS cloud support specialist. Your job is to answer user queries related 
to cloud hosting services on AWS.
"""

response = client.converse(
    modelId=model_id, 
    messages=messages, 
    system=[{"text": system_prompt}]
)
```

The system prompt is passed as a list containing a dictionary with a "text" key. This tells Claude what role it should play before it sees any user messages.

## Building a Flexible Chat Function

Here's a reusable chat function that handles system prompts gracefully:

```
def chat(messages, system=None):
    params = {"modelId": model_id, "messages": messages}
    
    if system:
        params["system"] = [{"text": system}]
    
    response = client.converse(**params)
    
    return response["output"]["message"]["content"][0]["text"]
```

This approach allows you to optionally include a system prompt. When no system prompt is provided, Claude responds in its default state. When you include one, Claude plays that specific role.

## System Prompts in Action

When you test the same question with and without a system prompt, the difference is immediately apparent. Asking "How do I host a Postgres database?" without a system prompt gives you a comprehensive answer covering multiple cloud providers and self-hosting options.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557631%2F05_-_005_-_System_Prompts_11.1748557630855.png)

With the AWS support expert system prompt, the response focuses exclusively on AWS solutions such as RDS, Aurora, and EC2-based deployments. There is no mention of competitors, and the answer includes AWS-specific setup steps.

Even more impressive is how the system prompt handles off-topic questions. When you ask for a bread recipe using the AWS expert prompt, Claude politely declines while staying in character:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557631%2F05_-_005_-_System_Prompts_15.1748557631341.png)

## Important Technical Details

Keep the following requirements in mind when using system prompts:

- System prompts cannot be empty strings—they must contain at least one character
- The `system` parameter expects a list of dictionaries with a "text" key
- The system prompt is processed before any user message in the conversation
- You can update the system prompt between conversations, but not mid-conversation

System prompts give you powerful control over AI behavior without a complex rule system. By assigning Claude a specific professional role, you get consistent, appropriate responses that naturally follow that role's constraints and expertise.

#### Video Transcript

Earlier in this module, we discussed how to build a web-based chat interface. Now, I want to revisit that example, but change it slightly. I want to imagine how we could turn that example into a kind of AWS support expert chatbot. So maybe a user would ask a question like "How do I host a Postgres database?" Then the answer we get back should satisfy a few requirements.

For example, the response we get and present to the user should directly answer the user's question and list several different ways to host Postgres on AWS. It might even want to walk the user through some initial steps. Finally, it might also want to mention some related AWS services that the user might be interested in. Now, just as we want the response to contain certain things, our response should also definitely not contain certain types of content.

For example, we don't want to see any answer mentioning how to host Postgres using a competitor's solution. We also don't want to see an answer to any question unrelated to cloud services. So if a user asks "How do I fix my car?", we'd probably want our chatbot to very politely decline to answer that question. Now, we'll explore two possible approaches to building a chatbot like this.

This way, a user can ask a question and then get an answer that meets specific requirements. The first option, which may not be the best, but we'll look at it anyway, is that we could write a user message that lists all of these different requirements. So in the user message, we could say, "Make sure you mention how to host things on AWS." We could also say, "Don't mention how to host services outside of AWS." And don't answer questions that have nothing to do with AWS. Now, using the first option would be very tedious, because we'd have to come up with a very comprehensive list of "dos" and "don'ts" to address every different question a user might ask.

So this probably isn't the best solution. Maybe we have a better way to handle this. With that in mind, let me show you the second option, which will be a much better way to solve this problem. In the second option, we'll provide a system prompt to the `converse` function call.

The system prompt will give Claude guidance on exactly how to respond. It does this by assigning Claude a role. By role, I mean we're going to essentially tell Claude to pretend it has a real profession. So in this example, our system prompt might say, "You are an AWS cloud support expert." This will force Claude to pretend it's a real cloud support expert.

It will try to respond the way a real support expert would respond. So, presumably, a real expert would never try to answer with a competitor's solution and would probably very politely decline to answer questions unrelated to cloud services. By simply assigning this system prompt, Claude will try to do the same thing. It will try to respond the way a real support expert would respond.

Now, the best way to master system prompts is to write some code. So let's go back to Jupyter Notebooks and get some experience using system prompts. Back here, I've already created a new notebook, but it's almost identical to the code we had before. So I'm still creating a client, a model ID, and then defining these three helper functions.

I've also put together another cell here, where I just asked a very simple question: How do I host a Postgres database? Now, at this point, I don't have any system message. So if I run it, I'd expect to see a response that's kind of like a generic Claude answer. It will respond the way Claude normally does, giving a very comprehensive answer.

And it won't satisfy some of our chatbot's main requirements. That is, if I look at the response here, it might mention some competitors, which isn't really what we want. So we can fix this by adding a system message. Now, I'm just going to hardcode a system message.

Then I'll show you how to make this chat function more reusable afterward. So first, in the `chat` function, I'm going to add a system prompt here. To save some time, I'm going to copy and paste a system prompt. It's the same one you just saw in the diagram.

So it just says you are a cloud support expert, and you should only answer questions related to cloud hosting. Then to pass it to the `converse` call, we'll add a `system` keyword here. It'll be a list containing a dictionary with that system prompt text, like this. So now I'll re-run this cell to redefine the `chat` function.

Then I'll try re-running my conversation here, and we'll see how the response updates. Hopefully it will respond with some AWS-specific Postgres solutions, without mentioning any competitors. As it turns out, that's exactly what we see here. We'll see different hosting solutions, some self-hosting options, and some initial setup.

Then, notably, it won't mention any competitors or anything like that. Similarly, we can also try updating our query to something completely unrelated to cloud hosting. Now, this doesn't always work, but it usually does. So I'll try asking something like "Give me a bread recipe."

We'll see what kind of response we get now. So, naturally, without the system prompt, we'd expect to get an actual recipe. But with the system prompt, we get a very polite refusal. It says, okay, I understand what you mean, but what I'm really good at is AWS cloud support.

So if you have any related questions, I can help you. But other than that, I probably can't answer your question about bread. Let's go back to that chat function. As I mentioned, I want to refactor this thing and make it easier to pass in a system prompt, rather than always hardcoding it like it is now.

Note that if we pass in this `system` keyword here, we have to pass in a system prompt with at least one character. So if we try to pass in an empty string, like this, and I run this cell and the next cell, I'll end up with a big error message. It will tell me I provided a system prompt of length zero, and that it must contain at least one character. So to refactor this thing and make it easier to specify the system prompt, I'm going to copy and paste some code here to save us some time.

I'll replace the chat function, like this. So now I'll receive a system prompt by default. It will be `None`. Then I create the `params` object ahead of time.

Then, if a system prompt is passed in, I add it to the `system` keyword and pass all these parameters to the `converse` function. So now, as a quick demonstration of how to use this updated `chat` function, I'll re-run that cell. I'll scroll down. Now when I call `chat`, I can pass in a `system` that says "You are an AWS support expert."

Of course, we could flesh this out. But for now, let's give it a try and make sure we still get some appropriate output. That is, for the question "Give me a bread recipe," it shouldn't give any good output. Looks like, yes, we do indeed get good output.

### 9. Temperature

> Type: Video | Duration: 7:55 | Video: 05 - 007 - Temperature.mp4

Temperature is a powerful parameter that controls how creative or deterministic Claude's responses are. Understanding how to use it effectively can significantly improve your AI applications.

## How Claude Generates Text

Before diving into temperature, it's helpful to understand Claude's text generation process. When you send Claude a prompt like "What do you think?", it goes through three stages:

- **Tokenization**: Breaking your input into smaller chunks
- **Prediction**: Calculating probabilities for the possible next tokens
- **Sampling**: Selecting a token based on those probabilities

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557675%2F05_-_007_-_Temperature_00.1748557675436.png)

In the diagram above, you can see how Claude assigns different probabilities to potential next tokens. The word "about" has a 30% chance, "would" has a 20% chance, and so on. This process repeats for every token until the response is complete.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557676%2F05_-_007_-_Temperature_03.1748557675981.png)

## What Temperature Does

Temperature is a decimal value between 0 and 1 that directly affects these token-selection probabilities. Think of it as a creativity dial:

- **Low temperature (close to 0)**: Makes the highest-probability tokens even more likely to be selected
- **High temperature (close to 1)**: Distributes the probabilities of all possible tokens more evenly

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557676%2F05_-_007_-_Temperature_05.1748557676662.png)

At a temperature of 0, Claude becomes deterministic—it will always choose the most likely token. At a temperature of 1, lower-probability tokens have a greater chance of being selected, producing more creative and diverse output.

## Temperature Ranges and Use Cases

Different tasks call for different temperature settings:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557677%2F05_-_007_-_Temperature_08.1748557677098.png)

### Low Temperature (0.0 - 0.3)

- Factual answers
- Coding assistance
- Data extraction
- Content moderation

### Medium Temperature (0.4 - 0.7)

- Summarization
- Educational content
- Problem solving
- Constrained creative writing

### High Temperature (0.8 - 1.0)

- Brainstorming
- Creative writing
- Marketing content
- Joke generation

## Setting Temperature in Code

By default, Claude's temperature is set to 1.0, which means maximum creativity. You can override this by adding temperature to the inference configuration:

```
def chat(messages, system=None, temperature=1.0):
    params = {
        "modelId": model_id,
        "messages": messages,
        "inferenceConfig": {"temperature": temperature}
    }
    
    if system:
        params["system"] = [{"text": system}]
    
    response = client.converse(**params)
    return response["output"]["message"]["content"][0]["text"]
```

## Temperature in Practice

Here's a practical example using movie idea generation. When the temperature is set to the default value (1.0), you might get a creative response like:

"A reclusive origami master discovers that her intricate paper creations come to life at night, leading her on a fantastical journey to save their miniature world from a mysterious shadow creature that threatens to unfold their very existence."

But when you set the temperature to 0.0 for the same prompt, you'll consistently get a more predictable response:

"A time-traveling archaeologist must stop a tech billionaire from stealing ancient artifacts that the billionaire is using to build a doomsday device that harnesses their forgotten power."

Running the low-temperature version multiple times will produce very similar responses, often with recurring themes like "time-traveling historian" or "time-traveling archaeologist."

## Key Takeaways

Temperature gives you direct control over Claude's level of creativity. Use a lower temperature when you need consistent, factual responses; use a higher temperature when you want creative, diverse output. The default temperature of 1.0 maximizes creativity, so for tasks that require precision and consistency, consider lowering it.

#### Video Transcript

Earlier in this course, we briefly talked about how Claude actually generates text. Remember, we feed some text into Claude, such as "What do you think?" Claude will then tokenize this text, or break it into smaller chunks. Claude will then enter the prediction phase, where it decides what words might come next and assigns a probability to each option. Finally, in the sampling phase, a token is actually selected based on those probabilities.

So, in this diagram on my screen, given the input "What do you think?", the possible next tokens might be `about`, `would`, `of`, and so on, everything you see on the right side here. Each token is assigned a probability, and then, in this case, Claude might choose `about` as the best next token. So we'd end up with the phrase "What do you think about?" Then the whole process repeats to finish the sentence or finish the entire message. Now, to make sure things are really clear, the numbers I'm showing here are probabilities, the percentage chance that each token is selected.

To make these probabilities clearer, I'll display them as a chart for the rest of this video. So they're still the same probabilities, just in a format that's easier for us to understand. You'll also notice I've sorted them from left to right. There's no actual internal ordering here.

I just sorted them from largest to smallest to make this chart easier to understand. So now that we've reviewed how Claude generates text, I want to show you a way we can directly influence these probabilities and control which token Claude actually decides to select. So we can use a parameter called "temperature" to control these probabilities. Temperature is a decimal value between 0 and 1 that we provide when making a model call, that is, whenever we call the `converse` function. Temperature affects the exact distribution of probabilities.

This is a bit hard to grasp, so you can look at my chart here, or, I made a quick demo using Claude itself to give you a better sense of what's going on. So let me show you that demo. Okay, this is the same chart we just saw in the diagram. Whenever we provide a temperature value close to 0, you'll see I have a temperature here, the highest-probability event becomes more likely to occur.

So our highest probability is `about`, and it increases all the way to 100%. So at a temperature of 0, we start getting what we call deterministic output, where we always select the token with the highest initial probability. Then, as we start increasing the temperature, it increases the chance that we select tokens with lower initial probabilities. So we go from a 0% chance of selecting `we` as the next token, all the way up to 9%.

That's the theory behind temperature, but what does this actually mean in the real world? Well, we'll start using different temperature values depending on the actual task we're trying to accomplish. These are some example ranges and the tasks that might be appropriate for each sample range. For a task like data extraction, we really don't want a lot of randomness or creativity.

If we give Claude a big block of text and ask it to extract a very specific piece of information, there's really no need for creativity there. We just want Claude to look at the exact text we provided and extract the most relevant information. Then, on the higher-temperature end, we'd become more creative and would start seeing less common tokens used. You might want to use a higher temperature for any task that needs a real creative focus, such as brainstorming, writing, maybe some very creative marketing, or a task like jokes, where many jokes really depend on using words in ways that aren't always entirely expected.

Now that we have a general sense of temperature, let's look at how to play with it using the Bedrock API. So the first thing I want to show you is in the Bedrock documentation, or specifically the Bedrock User Guide. Here, on the left, I've navigated to the Anthropic Claude models and am looking at some documentation about Claude Sonnet. If I search this page for "temperature" and scroll down a bit, I'll eventually find temperature here, which is a description of a parameter, and you'll see that by default, temperature is set to 1.

This means that by default, whenever we access Claude through Bedrock, we'll usually get some very creative responses, which may or may not be good. So we might want to control this temperature value, sometimes to make sure we get more deterministic output, such as for data extraction tasks, and then maybe we can adjust the temperature depending on whether we need a more creative task (for example, writing a screenplay for a movie). So now back in Jupyter, I have the same notebook I've been using in the previous video. I've added a new cell here where I created a new message list.

I added a user message where I ask Claude to generate a movie idea in one sentence. Then I call `chat`, get some text, and print it out. Before running it, let's update our `chat` function. So it will receive an optional temperature parameter, which we'll pass through the `converse` call.

So we can adjust our model's creativity a bit. I'll go back to the `chat` function here. I'll add an extra temperature parameter, and I'll match the model's own default temperature value, which is 1.0. Then I'll pass it through this `params` dictionary.

So I'll add a new key `inferenceConfig` for this. Note that it's the word `inference`. It's not `interface`. So make sure to spell it correctly.

Then I'll set the temperature value that's passed in. That's it. So now if I re-run that cell to redefine `chat`, I'll go back to the cell I added here for generating movie ideas. Remember, now we have a default temperature of 1, which I'll leave as is for now.

If I run it once, I should get a movie idea that's at least somewhat creative. If I read it carefully, a reclusive origami master, that's a very creative movie idea, you can tell right away, and I'll run it again. We'll definitely get another very creative movie idea. Okay, this one looks reasonable too, one more.

Yeah, pretty creative. Now I'm going to crank the temperature down to zero. In theory, we should get some movie ideas that may not be as creative. So if I run it, I'll see a time-traveling archaeologist.

Now, it's hard for you and me to decide whether it's creative just by running this prompt a few times. But let's run it one or two more times, and I think you'll quickly notice a theme. Note that this is a time-traveling archaeologist.

So if I run it once more myself, I'll probably see another one about, yes, here we go, a time-traveling history-related thing. I'll do it one more time. I'll probably see, again, a time-traveling historian. So you can start to see right away that the responses we start getting are slightly less creative, and they tend to be very similar to all the other responses we got when we fed in this same initial prompt, "Generate a movie idea in one sentence."

Okay, so that's temperature. Now remember, there are some general guidelines here. Whenever we do a task that needs less creativity or where we want very deterministic output, we'll use a lower temperature. Then, whenever we do a task that needs more creativity, we'll start thinking about bumping up the creativity a bit.

---

### 10. Streaming

> Type: Video | Duration: 9:05 | Video: 05 - 008 - Streaming.mp4

When building a chat interface with an AI model, users want to see a response immediately, rather than waiting 10-30 seconds for a complete response. The `converse_stream` function solves this by streaming the generated text, creating a better user experience.

## How Streaming Works

Instead of waiting for the entire response to be generated, streaming sends pieces of text as soon as they become available. Here's how the flow changes:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557673%2F05_-_008_-_Streaming_03.1748557673079.png)

When you call `converse_stream`, you immediately get an initial response containing a stream object. This stream is a generator that yields events as the model generates text. Each event contains a small portion of the overall response.

## Basic Implementation

Here's how to use `converse_stream` in code:

```
messages = []
add_user_message(messages, "Write a 1 sentence description of a fake database")
response = client.converse_stream(messages=messages, modelId=model_id)

for event in response["stream"]:
    print(event)
```

This will print out all the different events as they arrive one after another. You'll see the response come in chunks, rather than all at once.

## Understanding Stream Events

The stream yields several types of events, each serving a different purpose:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557673%2F05_-_008_-_Streaming_12.1748557673618.png)

For basic text generation, you only need to care about the `contentBlockDelta` events. These events contain the actual generated chunks of text that you want to display to the user.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1748557674%2F05_-_008_-_Streaming_15.1748557674330.png)

Events always arrive in a predictable order: `messageStart`, multiple `contentBlockDelta` events containing text, then `contentBlockStop`, `messageStop`, and finally `metadata`.

## Extracting the Text

To get just the generated text from each chunk, filter for `contentBlockDelta` events and extract the text:

```
text = ""
for event in response["stream"]:
    if "contentBlockDelta" in event:
        chunk = event["contentBlockDelta"]["delta"]["text"]
        print(chunk, end="")
        text += chunk

print("\n\nTotal Message:\n" + text)
```

The `end=""` argument removes the newline that Python's `print` function adds automatically, making the streaming text display more naturally.

## Practical Applications

In a real application, you usually won't print each chunk, but instead:

- Send each chunk to your frontend via WebSockets or Server-Sent Events
- Update the UI to display the growing response in real time
- Store the complete message after streaming finishes
- Handle any errors that may occur during streaming

This streaming approach transforms the user experience from "submit and wait" to "submit and watch the response appear," making your AI-powered application feel more responsive and engaging.

#### Video Transcript

I want to revisit our original chat interface example again. Remember, we have a chat interface running on a website or mobile app. A user will submit a question, or they'll enter text into our server, which puts it into a user message and then sends it to Bedrock, and Bedrock will return an assistant message to us, from which we'll extract some text and then maybe send it to the browser, where the actual generated text can be displayed to the user. Now, all of this would work, but it has one big drawback.

That is, the time between sending the initial request with the user message and receiving the response can sometimes be very long, depending on the length of the response being generated. So it might take three seconds, but it might also take 30 seconds. Most users' expectation is that whenever they enter some kind of initial message, such as "How do I host a Postgres database," they want to see something being generated on the screen immediately. But they don't want to wait 30 seconds.

So, to achieve this immediate feedback, we can use a function that's kind of like `converse`, except it works slightly differently. That's the `converse_stream` function. `converse_stream` lets us stream the response. It streams the text the model is generating.

Let me show you a few diagrams to make sure how it works is clear. So again, we'll have a user submit an initial question to our server. Then we'll send a request to Bedrock, this time using the `converse_stream` function. Then the response we receive will be somewhat different in nature.

Immediately, we'll receive an initial response. This initial response won't actually contain any generated text. It's really just a message from Bedrock saying, "Hey, we received your request, we're now generating some text." Then we'll start receiving a stream of events. We'll talk in more detail about what exactly these events are.

But for now, just understand that they contain the pieces of the generated response that we want to eventually send back to the user. We'll receive many different events. The number we receive depends on how much text we're generating. Each event will contain a small portion of the overall message being generated.

So maybe the first event's text is "You can," then the next is "host a," then the next is "Postgres." In theory, we might have some additional events after that to really flesh out the message. As we receive each event, we'll grab the generated text, send it to the browser, and then hopefully our browser or mobile app will display that part of the generated message on the screen. Then we'll repeat this process for each additional event. So we'll grab the text, send it to the browser, and display it on the screen.

Ultimately, we'll stream each chunk so the user can start reading the response as soon as possible. Now, I want to spend a lot of time discussing the precise nature of these events, because that's really the challenging part of understanding how `converse_stream` works. But first, let's write some code so we can get hands-on with `Converse Stream` and really understand it. So back in the notebook, I again have an initial set of messages that start out empty.

I added a message that just says, write a one-sentence description of a fake database. Now I'll get the response by calling `clientConverseStream`. I'll pass in my message list. Of course, I still need to pass in my model ID.

Remember, our `modelId` keyword is actually `model` capital `ID`, but the `modelId` we specified earlier was `model_id`. Now I'll print out the current response. When I run this cell, you'll notice it only takes me about a second to get the initial response. So again, this is the initial response we saw in the diagram.

It's right here. In this initial response, there's actually no generated text. Instead, we'll have a new `stream` key added to it. `stream` is an event stream object.

Essentially, it's a generator that we can iterate over. And each value that generator emits or yields will be one of these different event objects. So let's update the code a bit here. I'll change it to `for event in response`.

Remember, that content is specifically located at the `stream` key in the response object. Then I'll print out that event. Then I'll run it again. Let's see what we end up getting.

So now we start to see a series of different events being printed out. Although it's a little hard to see, you might notice that when you run that cell, you first see one or two printouts, such as the first event print. Then you'll see the other events start to appear gradually, with a small delay between each event. So let me show you a diagram that can help you understand what exactly these events are.

So again, whenever we make a request to bedrock, we'll receive a series of different events. The generator will yield several different types of events. When we run that cell, we can see these events all printed out. So we might see an event of type `message start`.

Then we might see events of type `content block start` and `content block delta`, and so on. If I go back to Jupyter, I'll see these events here. So this is `message start`. Then I have some `content block deltas`.

If I scroll down, I'll eventually receive `message stop` and `metadata` events. Now, these different events have different meanings in the context of our response. For now, the only event we really care about is `content block delta`. Each `content block delta` event contains a small piece of text generated by the model.

This is the actual text we might ultimately want to display to the user or utilize in some way. So if I look at the logs here again, I'll see that each `content block delta` has a `delta` nested inside it, and then a `text`. That's the actual text I care about. So for me, I'll end up with a generated text that says "The Xenonlin Q7 database is a revolutionary, but completely blah blah, you see, etc."

You'll always see these different events listed in the same order. So we'll always see the `message start` event first. Then we'll see some `content block delta` events, then `content block stop`, `message stop`, and `metadata`. Now, when we start looking at some of the model's more advanced features, we might end up seeing multiple `content block start` and `stop`.

But for now, when we're just generating text, we'll only receive one set of `content block deltas` and one `content block stop`. We won't even actually see `content block start`, even though that's an event. So that's an event, but if we're just generating text, we won't actually see it. It only becomes relevant when we start using tools, which we'll cover later.

So again, for now, when we only care about text, the only event we really care about is `content block delta`. With that in mind, let's update the `for` loop in the code. I want to collect all the different `content block delta` events. Whenever we get one of these events, I want to try to print out the text in that event.

So we'll do this. I'll update the contents of the for loop, and then I'll say `if "contentBlockDelta" in event`. So, I'm just checking whether that string exists in this event dictionary. Then I want to grab the chunk text, which I'll name `chunk`, and it will be `event["contentBlockDelta"]["delta"]["text"]`.

Then I'll just print out that chunk. Whenever we print a chunk using Python's `print` function, it automatically adds a newline at the end of the print statement. So this would be a bit hard to read. So I'd probably suggest adding the `end` keyword.
