### 9. Defining Resources

> Type: Video | Duration: 9:45 | Video: 09 - 007 - Defining Resources.mp4

`Resources` in an MCP server let you expose data to the client, similar to `GET request handlers` in a typical HTTP server. They're perfect for scenarios where you need to fetch information rather than perform an action.

## Understanding Resources Through an Example

Suppose you want to build a document mention feature, where the user can type `@document_name` to reference a file. This requires two operations:

- Get the list of all available documents (for autocomplete)
- Get the contents of a specific document (when mentioned)

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749849287%2F09_-_007_-_Defining_Resources_01.1749849287240.png)

When a user mentions a document, your system automatically injects the document's contents into the prompt sent to Claude, eliminating the need for Claude to use a tool to fetch the information.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749849287%2F09_-_007_-_Defining_Resources_02.1749849287776.png)

## How Resources Work

Resources follow a request-response pattern. When your client needs data, it sends a `ReadResourceRequest` with a `URI (Uniform Resource Identifier)` to identify the resource it wants. The MCP server handles this request and returns the data in a `ReadResourceResult`.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749849288%2F09_-_007_-_Defining_Resources_05.1749849288268.png)

Here's the flow: your code requests a resource from the MCP client, and the client forwards the request to the MCP server. The server processes the URI, runs the appropriate function, and returns the result.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749849289%2F09_-_007_-_Defining_Resources_06.1749849288783.png)

## Resource Types

There are two types of resources:

### Direct Resources

Direct resources have a static URI that never changes. They're perfect for operations that don't require parameters.

```
@mcp.resource(
    "docs://documents",
    mime_type="application/json"
)
def list_docs() -> list[str]:
    return list(docs.keys())
```

### Templated Resources

Templated resources include parameters in their URI. The Python SDK automatically parses these parameters and passes them as keyword arguments to your function.

```
@mcp.resource(
    "docs://documents/{doc_id}",
    mime_type="text/plain"
)
def fetch_doc(doc_id: str) -> str:
    if doc_id not in docs:
        raise ValueError(f"Doc with id {doc_id} not found")
    return docs[doc_id]
```

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749849289%2F09_-_007_-_Defining_Resources_07.1749849289476.png)

## Implementation Details

Resources can return any type of data—strings, JSON, binary data, etc. Use the `mime_type` parameter to hint to the client what kind of data you're returning:

- `"application/json"` for structured data
- `"text/plain"` for plain text
- `"application/pdf"` for binary files

The MCP Python SDK automatically serializes your return value. You don't need to manually convert objects to JSON strings—just return the data structure and let the SDK handle serialization.

## Testing Your Resources

You can use the MCP Inspector to test resources. Start your server with the following command:

```
uv run mcp dev mcp_server.py
```

Then connect to the inspector in your browser. You'll see two sections:

- **Resources** - lists your direct/static resources
- **Resource Templates** - lists your templated resources

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749849290%2F09_-_007_-_Defining_Resources_18.1749849290021.png)

Click any resource to test it. For templated resources, you'll need to provide values for the parameters. The Inspector shows you the exact response structure the client will receive, including the MIME type and the serialized data.

Resources provide a clean way to expose read-only data from your MCP server, making it easy for clients to fetch information without the complexity of tool calls.

#### Video Transcript

> In this video, we're going to continue exploring the next major feature of MCP servers—Resources. To help you understand resources, we're going to implement another feature in our project. We're going to add the following: I want to allow the user to mention a document by typing the @ symbol followed by the document name.
> 
> Whenever the user does this, I want to automatically get the contents of that document and insert it into the prompt we send to Claude. Overall, this feature has two sides. When the user types the @ symbol in a message, we'll automatically show a list of all the different documents they can mention in a small autocomplete window. Then, when the user submits a message containing a mention, we'll automatically get the contents of that document and insert it into the prompt we send to Claude.
> 
> For example, if the user says "what's in @report.pdf", I want to build a prompt like the following and send it to Claude. So we'll put the user's query in there, and then we'll also tell Claude that the user may have referenced a certain document, and here are the contents of that document. So the idea here is that we don't have to rely on Claude to use some tool to find out what's in report.pdf. Instead, the user can mention the file ahead of time, and we'll automatically insert some context in advance.
> 
> Now, I want to clarify one point, that we're actually talking about two different features. The first feature is that when the user types the @ symbol, we do need the MCP server to give us a list of all the documents the user might mention. The second aspect is that when the user submits a message containing a mention, we need the MCP server to give us the contents of a single document. To get this information from our MCP server, we're going to use resources.
> 
> Resources allow our MCP server to expose some data to the client. We usually define a resource for each different read operation. So in our case, we need to get a list of documents and read the contents of a single document. Therefore, we'll probably end up creating two separate resources.
> 
> One resource will be responsible for returning the list of document names, so we can put them into the autocomplete list. Then, we'll probably create another resource that will expose the contents of a single document based on its document ID. When we define these resources, they'll be accessed through our MCP client. So the whole flow we're ultimately going to build is, when the user types something like "@...", once they type the @ character, we need to display a list of document names for autocomplete. So our code contacts the MCP client, which sends a Read Resource Request to the MCP server.
> 
> In that Read Resource Request, we'll include something called a URI. This is essentially the address of the resource we want to read. This URI is defined when we initially build the resource. So the URI is that thing.
> 
> When we send this Read Resource Request, the MCP server looks at the exact URI we put in it, and then runs the function we wrote earlier. It gets the result and returns it to us in a Read Resource Result Message. Then we can take the data in it and display it in our autocomplete window, or do whatever we need to do. There are two different types of resources: direct and templated.
> 
> You'll sometimes see direct resources called static resources too. A direct resource has just a static URI, so it's always exactly the same thing, like `docs:://documents`. A templated resource includes one or more parameters in its URI. For example, we might have `documents/`, and then a wildcard here.
> 
> So we can put in any document ID we want. When we request this resource, that document ID in the URI will be automatically parsed by the Python MCP SDK and provided to our function as a keyword argument. The name of that keyword argument will be exactly the same as the string you type here. So this `doc_id` becomes that `doc_id` there.
> 
> As you might have guessed, we'll use templated resources whenever we want to allow more choice, variety, or customization in what someone requests from our MCP server. Implementing resources is very straightforward. So let's go back to our editor and add some resources to our server right away. Okay, back in my editor, I'll find the `MCP_server.py` file.
> 
> Then I'll scroll down a bit and find the comments about writing a resource to return all document IDs and writing a resource to return the contents of a specific document. For the first one, I wrote the comment "document IDs". Keep in mind that, for us, a document ID is essentially the name of the document. So for us, we're really just returning these IDs.
> 
> They'll function as names. This means we can put them directly into that autocomplete element. Okay, to create our resource, I'll delete that to-do, and then I'll add an `@mcp.resource`.
> 
> The first argument will be the URI for accessing this thing. Again, it's somewhat equivalent to a route handler. So I'll use `docs:://documents`, and I'll also add an `application/json` MIME type. Resources can return any type of data, so it can be plain text, JSON, binary data, anything.
> 
> It's up to us to give the client a hint about what kind of data we're returning. To do this, we'll define this MIME type. The `application/json` MIME type is a hint to our client, which will eventually request this resource, telling it that we're going to return a string containing structured JSON data. So our client will be responsible for deserializing that data, or converting it into a usable data structure.
> 
> Below that decorator, I'll write out my function `list_docs`, which will return a list of strings. Then in it, I'll return `list(docs.keys())`. So just take all the keys from that dictionary and convert it to a list, and return it. Now, you'll notice that what we're returning here isn't standalone JSON.
> 
- In other words, we're not actually returning a string. The MCP Python SDK will automatically convert whatever we return into a string. Okay, let's handle our second resource. So I'll delete that comment and replace it with `@mcp.resource('docs:://documents/{doc_id}')`.
> 
> This time I want a templated resource, because I've put this wildcard in here. Then this time my MIME type, just to add a bit of variety, I'll return plain text, since it's going to just be the contents of the document. I won't wrap it in any kind of structure. Now, just so you know, in a real application, for an operation like reading a document, I'd probably return the entire document record.
> 
> That is, some kind of dictionary containing the ID, content, author name, author ID, and so on. But just for the sake of example, I'll just return the text of the document, to show you how we typically return plain text. So in this case, my MIME type will be `text/plain`, then I'll create `fetch_doc`. I'll receive a `doc_id`, which is a string, and I'll return a string.
> 
> Again, whatever word you type here will show up as a keyword argument in your function. If I add some extra parameters here, like `doc_type` or something, it would show up as an extra keyword argument, like this. Then in here, the first thing I want to do is make sure the ID this person requests actually exists. So if `doc_id` is not in `docs`, I'll raise a `ValueError` with an f-string saying "Doc with id ... not found".
> 
> Then if we pass that check, I'll return `docs[doc_id]`. That's it. Now let's test these things in our MCP Inspector again. Remember, in our terminal, we can run the `uvicorn mcptest.mcpserver:app --reload` command.
> 
> This will start a web server on port 6277 or the default 6274. So I'll make sure to open it in my browser. Okay. I click connect.
> 
> Then I find resources. Then I should be able to list all the available resources. Now when I list resources, this is specifically referring to static or direct resources. So I'll only see `docs/documents`.
> 
> Then I can separately list all the different resource templates. So I see I have a resource template for `fetch_doc`. I can first try to run the `slash documents` here. Let's see what we get.
> 
> This is the actual message, the exact structure returned from our MCP server. You'll notice it has a `text` property, with all the data we returned, serialized as a JSON string. So again, it will be up to us, in our CLI application, to take this text and deserialize it from this JSON string into a usable list of strings. Then we can also test `fetch_doc`.
> 
> So I click it. I have to enter a document ID. So I'll enter, I want to read the `report.pdf` file.
> 
> I'll read the resource. Now I should be able to see the contents of that particular document. You'll notice this time, I get a `text/plain` again.
> 
> So this hints to me that this is plain text. I shouldn't try to deserialize it from JSON in any way.

---

### 10. Accessing Resources

> Type: Video | Duration: 4:38 | Video: 09 - 008 - Accessing Resources.mp4

Resources in MCP allow your server to expose information that can be directly included in prompts, rather than requiring data to be accessed through a tool call. This creates a more efficient way to provide context to AI models.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749849281%2F09_-_008_-_Accessing_Resources_00.1749849281584.png)

The diagram above shows how resources work: when the user types something like "What's in the @...", our code recognizes it as a resource request, sends a `ReadResourceRequest` to the MCP server, and gets back a `ReadResourceResult` with the actual content.

## Implementing Resource Reading

To enable resource access in your MCP client, you need to implement a `read_resource` function. First, add the necessary imports:

```
import json
from pydantic import AnyUrl
```

The core function makes a request to the MCP server and processes the response based on its MIME type:

```
async def read_resource(self, uri: str) -> Any:
    result = await self.session().read_resource(AnyUrl(uri))
    resource = result.contents[0]
    
    if isinstance(resource, types.TextResourceContents):
        if resource.mimeType == "application/json":
            return json.loads(resource.text)
    
    return resource.text
```

## Understanding the Response Structure

When you request a resource, the server returns a result with a `contents` list. We access the first element, because we usually only need one resource at a time. The response includes:

- The actual content (text or data)
- A MIME type that tells us how to parse the content
- Additional metadata about the resource

## Content Type Handling

The function checks the MIME type to determine how to process the content:

- If it's `application/json`, parse the text as JSON and return the parsed object
- Otherwise, return the raw text content

This approach seamlessly handles both structured data (like JSON) and plain text documents.

## Testing Resource Access

After implementing it, you can test the resource functionality through your CLI application. When you type "@" followed by a resource name, the system will:

1. Display available resources in an autocomplete list
2. Let you select a resource using the arrow keys and space bar
3. Include the resource content directly in your prompt
4. Send everything to the AI model, without an additional tool call

Compared to having the AI model make a separate tool call to access document content, this creates a smoother user experience. The resource content becomes part of the initial context, enabling an immediate response to the data.

#### Video Transcript

> We've already defined two separate resources in our MCP server. So now our client needs the ability to request these resources. To do this, we're going to add a function to the MCP client. Keep in mind, this MCP client will contain some functionality we're building that will be used by the rest of our application.
> 
> I've already done that part of the code. So elsewhere in this project, there's something that will try to use this function we're about to add to the MCP client. First, I'll open the MCP client file again. I'll scroll down and find `read_resource` here.
> 
> So our goal here is to read a specific resource by making a request to our MCP server, then parse the returned content based on its MIME type, and return whatever data we get. You'll notice one of its parameters is the URI. This will be the URI of the resource we want to get from the server. To make the request, and to make all our types work nicely, we'll add two imports at the top of the file.
> 
> I'll add an import for the JSON module. And import `AnyUrl` from PyDantic. Then I'll go back to our `read_resource` function. I'll clear out the comment and the return statement.
> 
> Then I'll get a result from calling `await self.session().read_resource()`. Again, this is really just to make the types work properly. We'll put in an `AnyUrl` with the input URI. Then I'll pull from that result's `result.contents[0]`. I want to be explicit here about why we're adding this.

> 
> We really only care about the first one. So I want the first dictionary. I want to access the `type` property and the `mime type`. I particularly want the MIME type, because it will help me understand what kind of data we got.
> 
> If it's JSON, then I need to make sure to parse the text as JSON and return that result. Let me show you how we're going to do this. I'll add an `if isinstance(resource, types.TextResourceContents)`. Inside it, if `resource.mime_type` equals `application/json`.
> 
> So this is our usage hint. If the server tells us it returned some JSON, we need to make sure to parse the text content as JSON. So I'll return `json.loads(resource.text)` in this case. Then, if we didn't enter that if statement and return early, I just want to return `resource.text`.
>

The code we're writing in the MCP client is being used by several other places in this codebase. So elsewhere in this codebase, we'll call that function we just wrote, to get a list of document names, and eventually get document contents to put into the prompt. So at this point, everything should basically work, because the rest of the work has already been done for us.
> 
> With that in mind, let's go back to our terminal and test our CLI application again, to see whether this mention feature works. Okay, back here, I'll execute `uvrun main.py`, and now I should be able to say something like "what's in the at", and lo and behold, I see my list of resources, and I can scroll through them using the arrow keys. Once I find a resource I like, I just press the space bar, and it inserts that resource. So what's in the report.pdf document? Now, I can tell you, everything here works as expected.
> 
> In other words, the contents of this document are being sent into Claude's prompt. So if I submit this, I should see an immediate response telling me what's inside the report PDF. So this time, Claude doesn't have to use a tool to read the document's contents. Okay, that's resources.
> 
> Again, we leverage resources to expose some information from our MCP server.

---

### 11. Defining Prompts

> Type: Video | Duration: 7:44 | Video: 09 - 009 - Defining Prompts.mp4

Prompts in an MCP server let you define pre-built, high-quality instructions that clients can use, without having to write their own prompts from scratch. Think of them as carefully crafted templates that can deliver better results than what a user might come up with on the fly.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749849287%2F09_-_009_-_Defining_Prompts_00.1749849286857.png)

## Why Use Prompts?

The key insight here is: users can already ask Claude to do most tasks directly. For example, a user could type "reformat report.pdf in markdown format" and get a decent result. But if you provide a well-tested prompt, designed specifically to handle edge cases and follow best practices, they'll get a much better result.

As the author of an MCP server, you can spend time carefully designing, testing, and evaluating prompts that work reliably across different scenarios. Users don't need to become prompt engineering experts themselves to benefit from this expertise.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749849289%2F09_-_009_-_Defining_Prompts_07.1749849288094.png)

## Building a Format Command

Let's implement a real example: a `format` command that converts a document to markdown. The user will type `/format doc_id`, and get a professionally formatted markdown version of the document.

The workflow is as follows:

- The user types `/` to see available commands
- They select `format` and specify a document ID
- Claude uses your pre-built prompt to read and reformat the document
- The result is clean markdown with proper headers, lists, and formatting

## Defining Prompts

Prompts use a decorator pattern similar to tools and resources:

```
@mcp.prompt(
    name="format",
    description="Rewrites the contents of the document in Markdown format."
)
def format_document(
    doc_id: str = Field(description="Id of the document to format")
) -> list[base.Message]:
    prompt = f"""
Your goal is to reformat a document to be written with markdown syntax.

The id of the document you need to reformat is:
<document_id>
{doc_id}
</document_id>

Add in headers, bullet points, tables, etc as necessary. Feel free to add in structure.
Use the 'edit_document' tool to edit the document. After the document has been reformatted...
"""
    
    return [
        base.UserMessage(prompt)
    ]
```

The function returns a list of messages that will be sent directly to Claude. You can include multiple user and assistant messages to create more complex conversational flows.

## Testing Your Prompts

Before deploying, test your prompts using the MCP Inspector:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749849289%2F09_-_009_-_Defining_Prompts_18.1749849289799.png)

The Inspector shows you exactly the messages that will be sent to Claude, including how variables are inserted into your prompt template. This lets you verify the prompt looks correct before users start relying on it.

## Key Advantages

- **Consistency** - Users get reliable results every time
- **Professionalism** - You can encode domain knowledge into the prompt
- **Reusability** - Multiple client applications can use the same prompt
- **Maintainability** - Update the prompt in one place to improve all clients

Prompts work best when they're specialized for the domain of your MCP server. A document management server might have prompts for formatting, summarizing, or analyzing documents. A data analysis server might have prompts for generating reports or visualizations.

The goal is to provide prompts that are so well-designed and well-tested that users prefer using them over writing their own instructions from scratch.

#### Video Transcript

> Our last major focus in the MCP server will be prompts. Again, just like we did with resources, we're going to implement a small feature in our project. We're going to use this feature to understand what prompts really are, just like we did with resources. Let me tell you the feature we're going to add to the program.
> 
> We're going to add support for slash commands. For example, I want a format command. I have some screenshots here about how it works. When the user types a slash, we'll list some commands our application supports.
> 
> For now, we only have one command called `format`. So if I just type a slash, I should see a small autocomplete window, and the only option should be `format`. If I select `format`, I should be prompted to add a document ID after it. Like one of our document names, such as `report.pdf` or something.
> 
> Then when the user runs this command, the goal is to have Claude reformat this document using Markdown syntax. In other words, take the plain string without any special formatting that's currently in each of our documents. Remember, in our MCP server, our current document content is just plain text. We want to feed this into Claude, and somehow get Claude to rewrite it using Markdown syntax.

> So I'd expect to see output like this, something saying, I'll help you reformat the document. Claude then uses a tool to read the contents of the document. >

So, if, as the authors of the MCP server, we sit down and write, test, evaluate, and go through the whole process of evaluating our very thorough, excellent prompts (like the one you see on the right), the user will probably be more satisfied.

---

### 12. Prompts in the Client

> Type: Video | Duration: 3:01 | Video: 09 - 010 - Prompts in the Client.mp4

The final step in building the MCP client is implementing prompt functionality. This allows us to list all available prompts on the server, and retrieve a specific prompt populated with variables.

## Implementing List Prompts

The `list_prompts` method is simple. It calls the session's `list_prompts` function and returns the prompts:

```
async def list_prompts(self) -> list[types.Prompt]:
    result = await self.session().list_prompts()
    return result.prompts
```

## Getting a Single Prompt

The `get_prompt` method is more interesting, because it handles variable interpolation. When you request a prompt, the arguments you provide are passed as keyword arguments to the prompt function:

```
async def get_prompt(self, prompt_name, args: dict[str, str]):
    result = await self.session().get_prompt(prompt_name, args)
    return result.messages
```

For example, if your server has a `format_document` prompt that requires a `doc_id` parameter, then the args dictionary would contain `{"doc_id": "plan.md"}`. This value gets inserted into the prompt template.

## Testing Prompts in Action

After implementing it, you can test prompts through the CLI. When you type a slash (/), the available prompts appear as commands. Selecting a prompt like "format" prompts you to choose from the available documents.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1751999840%2F011_-_Prompts_in_the_Client_11.1751999840520.png)

After selecting a document, the system sends the complete prompt to Claude. The AI receives the formatting instructions and the document ID, then uses the available tools to fetch and process the content.

## How Prompts Work

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1751999841%2F011_-_Prompts_in_the_Client_17.1751999841663.png)

A prompt defines a set of user and assistant messages for the client to use. They should be high-quality, well-tested, and relevant to the purpose of your MCP server. The workflow is:

- Write and evaluate prompts relevant to your server's functionality
- Define prompts in your MCP server using the `@mcp.prompt` decorator
- The client can request the prompt at any time
- The arguments provided by the client become keyword arguments in your prompt function
- The function returns formatted messages ready for the AI model

This system creates reusable, parameterized prompts that maintain consistency while allowing customization through variables. This is especially useful for complex workflows where you want to ensure the AI receives correctly structured instructions every time.

#### Video Transcript

> Our last major task is to implement some functionality in our MCP client that lets us list all the different prompts defined in the MCP server, and get a specific prompt with some variables inserted. So let's first implement `list_prompts`. I'll delete the comment and replace it with `result = await self.session().list_prompts()`, then I'll return `result.prompts`. That's basically it.
> 
> Then `get_prompt`. To be clear, when we get a single prompt, we get some arguments. These arguments will eventually show up in our prompt function. For example, in `format_document`, we expect to receive a document ID.
> 
> In this `args` dictionary, we expect there to be a `document ID` key. It will be passed to the corresponding function over here. Then we'll get that value interpolated in the prompt itself. So in the `get_prompt` function, I'll get a result from `self.session().get_prompt()`.
> 
> I'll pass in `prompt_name`. This is the name of the prompt I want to retrieve. Then I'll pass in the arguments. Then I'll return `result.messages`.
> 
> So these are the messages returned. They make up some kind of conversation we want to feed directly into Claude. That's it. That's all we need to do for the client.
> 
> So now we can test this in the CLI itself. I'll switch back and run the project again. Now if I type a slash here, I'll see that I have access to this `format` command. Now `format` is actually just the name of the prompt we're going to call.
> 
> So if I select it, then press the space bar, I'll be asked to choose a document, like `plan.md`. I press enter. Then we feed the whole prompt, which is really just that single user message, directly into Claude. So Claude now has the instructions to reformat the document using Markdown syntax.
> 
> It also gets the ID of the document we want to reformat. So the first thing it needs to do is go get the contents of that document. It'll do this by using the `get_document` tool. Then finally, Claude will respond with the Markdown version of this document.
> 
> So this is the document with lots of Markdown syntax. Okay, now that this seems to be working well, let's quickly review prompts and make sure we understand what they really are. We start by writing and evaluating a prompt relevant to the purpose of our MCP server. In our case, we're making a document server.
> 
> So some functionality or something about rewriting documents in different styles, I think that makes some sense. Once we've put the prompt together, we define a prompt in the MCP server. Then our client can request that prompt at any time. When we request the prompt, we type in some arguments, which will be provided as keyword arguments to this prompt function.
> 
> Then our function can use those keyword arguments in the prompt itself.

---

## Part 4: Evaluation and Summary

### 14. MCP Review

> Type: Video | Duration: 4:12 | Video: 09 - 011 - MCP Review.mp4

Now that we've built our MCP server, let's review the three core server primitives, and understand when to use each one. The key insight is that each primitive is controlled by a different part of your application stack.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749849326%2F09_-_011_-_MCP_Review_00.1749849326738.png)

## Tools: Model Controlled

Tools are entirely controlled by Claude. The AI model decides when to call these functions, and their results are used directly by Claude to complete tasks.

Tools are perfect when you need to give Claude additional capabilities it can use autonomously. When you ask Claude to "compute the square root of 3 in JavaScript", it's Claude that decides to use a JavaScript execution tool to run the computation.

## Resources: App Controlled

Resources are controlled by your application code. Your app decides when to fetch resource data and how to use it—typically for UI elements or to add context to a conversation.

In our project, we used resources in two ways:

- Fetching data to populate the autocomplete options in the UI
- Retrieving content to augment a prompt with additional context

Think about the "Add from Google Drive" feature in Claude's interface—it's the application code that decides which documents to display, and handles the process of injecting their content into the chat context.

## Prompts: User Controlled

Prompts are triggered by user actions. The user decides when to run these predefined workflows through UI interactions like button clicks, menu selections, or slash commands.

Prompts are ideal when you need to implement workflows that users can trigger on demand. In Claude's interface, the workflow buttons below the chat input box are examples of prompts—predefined, optimized workflows that users can launch with a single click.

## Choosing the Right Primitive

Here's a quick decision guide:

- **Need to give Claude a new capability?** Use a tool
- **Need to fetch data into your app for UI or context?** Use a resource
- **Want to create a predefined workflow for users?** Use a prompt

You can see all three primitives in action in Claude's official interface. The workflow buttons demonstrate prompts, the Google Drive integration shows resources in action, and when Claude executes code or performs a computation, it's using tools behind the scenes.

These are high-level guidelines to help you choose the right primitive for a particular use case. Each primitive serves a different part of your application stack—tools serve the model, resources serve your app, and prompts serve your users.

#### Video Transcript

> Our project is all done, but before we move on, I want to quickly review the three server primitives we learned: tools, resources, and prompts. In particular, I want to highlight what's interesting about each of them. That is, which part of an application is really responsible for running each one?
> 
> In other words, in a typical application, who really runs these things, and who benefits from them? Well, we'd say tools are model controlled. This means Claude alone is responsible for deciding when to run a given tool. Resources are app controlled.
> 
> In other words, some code running in your app decides it needs some data provided by a resource. It'll be up to your app's code to decide to execute a resource and use the returned data in some way, perhaps by using that data in the UI or something similar. In our case, we fetch a resource, then use that data in the UI to provide a list of autocomplete options. We also fetch a resource to augment a prompt.
> 
> Both of these things are actually done by the application-related code that you and I write. Finally, prompts are actually user-controlled. So the user decides when a prompt runs. The user might launch a prompt invocation by clicking on some UI element like a button or menu option, or they might use a slash command, just like we did.
> 
> The reason I highlight what controls each of these things is to give you a sense of their purpose. So, if you need to add functionality for Claude, you might want to consider implementing some tools in your MCP server, or using some server's tools through your MCP client. If you want to get some data into your app, to display something in the UI or for a similar purpose, then you might want to use resources. And if you want to implement some kind of predefined workflow, you might want to consider prompts.
> 
> Now you can see examples of all these ideas in the official Claude interface at `claude.ai`. So this is what I'm seeing right now. You'll notice there are some buttons below the main chat input box. If I click one of them, then click one of these examples, you'll see I immediately enter a chat.
> 
> So this is a user-controlled action. I, as the user, decided to start this particular workflow, and I'm using a prompt that may have been written ahead of time and may have been optimized in some way. So to implement that list of buttons there, we'd probably need to build a series of different prompts in the MCP server. Similarly, if I go back, maybe click this little tab, the plus button, you'll notice I have an Add from Google Drive button.
> 
> Now, I won't click it, because it would show some of my internal files. But if I clicked that button, I'd see some files I can add as context to this chat. Knowing which files to actually render in that list, and then automatically injecting their content into this chat's context when I click one of them, all of this is related to the application code. So it's entirely the application that needs to know the list of documents to render here.
> 
> And again, this is specifically related to a UI element. So to implement the functionality of listing documents from Google Drive, I'd probably consider implementing a resource in the MCP server. Then, finally, if I type a message into this chat, like, "what's the square root of 3, compute the value in JavaScript" and send it off, I obviously expect Claude to somehow execute some JavaScript code, which is most likely done by using a tool. In this case, the decision to use a tool is 100% model controlled.
> 
> It's the model that decides to use some JavaScript tool to execute. To implement something like this in an MCP server, we'd most likely need to, you guessed it, provide a tool. So overall, these are our three different server primitives. Each one is really intended to be used by a different part of your overall application.
> 
> So we have tools, which typically serve your model; resources, which typically serve your app; and prompts, which will serve your users. Again, these are all high-level guidelines. The reason I mention them is just to give you a feel for when you should use these primitives, depending on what you're trying to build.

# Lesson 13: MCP Advanced Topics

> Advanced development with the Model Context Protocol
> Course link: [MCP Advanced Topics](https://anthropic.skilljar.com/model-context-protocol-advanced-topics) | 15 lessons total

---

## Part 1: Introduction

### 1. Let's get started!

> This lesson is a course introduction/summary page, please visit the online platform to view it.

### 2. Sampling

> Type: Video | Duration: 5:17 | Video: 002 - Sampling.mp4

Sampling allows a server to access a language model like Claude through the connected MCP client. Instead of calling Claude directly, the server asks the client to make the call on its behalf. This shifts the responsibility and cost of text generation from the server to the client.

## The Problem Sampling Solves

Suppose you have an MCP server with a research tool that fetches information from Wikipedia. After collecting all the data, you need to synthesize it into a coherent report. You have two options:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747890%2F002_-_Sampling_07.1749747889898.png)

Option 1: Have the MCP server access Claude directly. The server needs its own API key, handles authentication, manages costs, and implements all the Claude integration code. This works, but it adds significant complexity.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747890%2F002_-_Sampling_09.1749747890532.png)

Option 2: Use sampling. The server generates a prompt, then asks the client, "Can you call Claude for me?". The client already has a connection to Claude, so it makes the call and returns the result.

## How Sampling Works

The flow is simple:

- The server does its work (such as fetching Wikipedia articles)
- The server creates a prompt requesting text generation
- The server sends a sampling request to the client
- The client calls Claude with the provided prompt
- The client returns the generated text to the server
- The server uses the generated text in its response

## Benefits of Sampling

- **Reduced server complexity:** The server doesn't need to integrate directly with a language model
- **Shifts the cost burden:** The client pays for token usage, not the server
- **No API key needed:** The server doesn't need Claude credentials
- **Great for public servers:** You don't want a public server bearing AI costs for every user

## Implementation

Setting up sampling requires writing code on both the client and server sides:

### Server Side

In your tool function, use the `create_message` function to request text generation:

```
@mcp.tool()
async def summarize(text_to_summarize: str, ctx: Context):
    prompt = f"""
    Please summarize the following text:
    {text_to_summarize}
    """
    
    result = await ctx.session.create_message(
        messages=[
            SamplingMessage(
                role="user",
                content=TextContent(
                    type="text",
                    text=prompt
                )
            )
        ],
        max_tokens=4000,
        system_prompt="You are a helpful research assistant",
    )
    
    if result.content.type == "text":
        return result.content.text
    else:
        raise ValueError("Sampling failed")
```

### Client Side

Create a `sampling callback` to handle the server's requests:

```
async def sampling_callback(
    context: RequestContext, params: CreateMessageRequestParams
):
    # Call Claude using the Anthropic SDK
    text = await chat(params.messages)
    
    return CreateMessageResult(
        role="assistant",
        model=model,
        content=TextContent(type="text", text=text),
    )
```

Then pass this callback when initializing the client session:

```
async with ClientSession(
    read,
    write,
    sampling_callback=sampling_callback
) as session:
    await session.initialize()
```

## When to Use Sampling

Sampling is most valuable when building a publicly accessible MCP server. You don't want random users using your resources without limit and incurring charges. By using sampling, each client pays for its own AI usage while benefiting from your server's functionality.

This technique essentially shifts the complexity of AI integration from your server to the client, which typically already has the necessary connections and credentials.

#### Video Transcript

Sampling allows a server to access a language model like Claude through the connected MCP client. To help you understand what this means and what sampling is all about, I want to show you a quick demo application. Here it is. This is a chat application powered by Claude, so I can ask it what one plus one equals, and I'll get an answer.

This chat application is also connected to a single MCP server that implements a tool called the "research tool". I can use this tool to ask Claude to write a report about archaeology. When I do this, Claude decides to use that research tool, indicating it will do some research, synthesize all the results, and then present a report to me. Now that you've seen this research tool in action, I want to show you what's going on behind this application, and how this research tool actually works.

Okay, so here's a high-level overview of the whole process. First, in the top left, I have a Next.js application, which actually renders some content in the browser, and has an MCP client integrated inside it. At some point, a user asks us to research a topic, like archaeology. We send this query to Claude, and Claude decides to run the research tool it was provided with ahead of time.

The MCP client on our Next.js application converts this into a tool call request and sends it to the MCP server. Then on the MCP server, the research tool runs. It makes multiple requests to Wikipedia, trying to find some articles about archaeology and some related content, and so on. Once the research tool gets some pages from Wikipedia, we need to synthesize all this content into a final report to display to the user.

There's more than one way we can do this. Let me show you two different options. In the first option, we can have the MCP server contact Claude or some other similar LLM directly, and ask it to summarize all the results found from the Wikipedia search. So in this case, we'd give our MCP server direct access to a language model.

This is definitely doable. This is definitely something we could do. However, this adds a lot of complexity to our MCP server. We'd have to write some code to let the MCP server make a request to Claude, get the response, extract the generated text, and so on.

We'd also have to make sure the MCP server has the API key to access Claude or whatever other language model we use. So, again, option one is doable, but it adds quite a bit of complexity. So let's look at option two. In option two, we'll have our MCP server use a technique called sampling.

With sampling, we'll have our MCP server ask the client to run a prompt on its behalf. In short, the MCP server writes a prompt, sends it to the MCP client, and then says, hey, can you feed this into Claude for me? The MCP client we built receives that prompt and sends it to Claude. It gets a response, then sends the result of the text generation back to the MCP server, which can do whatever it wants with the generated text.

This approach shifts the complexity to the MCP client. In this case, that's okay. Because as you can see, our MCP client, or that Next.js application, already has an existing connection to Claude. It's already making requests to Claude.

So running just one more request on behalf of the MCP server doesn't add much work. Another obvious benefit is that the MCP server doesn't need an API key to access Claude at all. If this is a publicly accessible MCP server, now it doesn't have to worry about paying for the tokens generated for everyone else using the server. So this technique is called sampling.

It allows an MCP server to ask the client to run Claude or any other language model to generate some text. You can think of sampling as shifting the responsibility of calling Claude from the server to the client. Sampling is most useful when you're building a publicly accessible MCP server. You don't want a publicly available MCP server that's open to everyone, where anyone can come and generate some text.

For your publicly accessible server, you'll want to use sampling, and shift the responsibility of generating text from your server to whatever client connects to it. Using sampling requires some setup on both the server and client sides. First, on the server, inside a tool function, you'd call the Create Message function. Then you'd pass in a list of messages you want to eventually hand to Claude.

This list of messages will be formatted into a request and sent to the client. Then on the client, you have to write some more code, specifically something called a sampling callback. In the top right of this code snippet, I have an example of a sampling callback. Your sampling callback will receive the messages sent from the server.

Then inside this callback function, it's up to you to decide how to call Claude or whatever other language model you're using, generate some text, and then return a create message result. Okay, that's sampling. As you can see, it's not particularly complex. It's really just this concept or idea of shifting the burden of generating some text from the server back to the client.

Again, this is a technique you'll definitely want to look into when building a publicly accessible MCP server.

### 4. Log and progress notifications

> Type: Video | Duration: 2:41 | Video: 003 - Log and Progress Notitications.mp4

Log and progress notifications are simple to implement, but they can greatly improve the user experience when working with an MCP server. They help users understand what's happening during long-running operations, instead of leaving them wondering whether something has gone wrong.

When Claude calls a tool that takes time to complete—like researching a topic or processing data—the user typically sees nothing until the operation finishes. This can be frustrating, because they don't know whether the tool is working or has gotten stuck.

With log and progress notifications enabled, users get real-time feedback that shows exactly what's happening behind the scenes. They can see progress bars, status messages, and detailed logs while the operation runs.

## How It Works

In the Python MCP `SDK (Software Development Kit)`, log and progress notifications work through the Context parameter, which is automatically provided to your tool function. This context object provides methods to communicate with the client during execution.

```
@mcp.tool(
    name="research",
    description="Research a given topic"
)
async def research(
    topic: str = Field(description="Topic to research"),
    *,
    context: Context
):
    await context.info("About to do research...")
    await context.report_progress(20, 100)
    sources = await do_research(topic)
    
    await context.info("Writing report...")
    await context.report_progress(70, 100)
    results = await generate_report(sources)
    
    return results
```

The key methods you'll use are:

- `context.info()` - send a log message to the client
- `context.report_progress()` - update progress with a current value and a total value

## Client Implementation

On the client side, you need to set up `callback` functions to handle these notifications. The server emits these messages, but it's up to your client application to decide how to present them to the user.

```
async def logging_callback(params: LoggingMessageNotificationParams):
    print(params.data)

async def print_progress_callback(
    progress: float, total: float | None, message: str | None
):
    if total is not None:
        percentage = (progress / total) * 100
        print(f"Progress: {progress}/{total} ({percentage:.1f}%)")
    else:
        print(f"Progress: {progress}")

async def run():
    async with stdio_client(server_params) as (read, write):
        async with ClientSession(
            read,
            write,
            logging_callback=logging_callback
        ) as session:
            await session.initialize()
            
            await session.call_tool(
                name="add",
                arguments={"a": 1, "b": 3},
                progress_callback=print_progress_callback,
            )
```

You provide the logging callback when creating the client session, and the progress callback when making an individual tool call. This gives you the flexibility to handle different types of notifications appropriately.

## Presentation Options

How you present these notifications depends on the type of your application:

- **CLI applications** - simply print messages and progress to the terminal
- **Web applications** - use WebSockets, server-sent events, or polling to push updates to the browser
- **Desktop applications** - update progress bars and status displays in your UI

Remember, implementing these notifications is entirely optional. You can choose to ignore them completely, display only certain types, or present them in whatever way makes sense for your application. They are purely a user-experience enhancement to help users understand what is happening during long-running operations.

#### Video transcript

Next up are logging and progress notifications. These notifications are very simple to set up, and they can greatly improve the user experience of your MCP server. So let me give you another quick demo. You might remember that research app example I showed you earlier.

If I ask Claude to generate a report about archaeology, it will make a tool call through the MCP server we just discussed. It will specifically call that research function. Now, this research function takes a while to complete. While it's running, the user actually gets no feedback at all.

So as far as the user is concerned, this tool call might actually have failed, or might just be stuck or something like that. So it would be great if we could give the user more insight into what's happening behind the scenes. To do this, we can use logging and notifications. Let me show you what the same query would look like if I turn on logging and notifications, which I already turned on when I refreshed the page just now.

So I'm going to issue the same query again, and now I'll get the same research tool call, but now I'll get a progress bar and some log statements. The progress and log bars are not fake data. These are log and progress statements emitted inside the tool call, inside the research tool. So let me show you some code to help you understand how this works.

To start using logging and progress notifications inside a tool function on our server, we receive the `context` argument, which is automatically included as the last argument of our tool function. On that `context` object, there are various different methods that allow us to log information or report this tool's progress to the client. So we have methods like `info` and `report_progress`. Whenever you call these functions, a message is automatically sent back to the client.

To use the log statements and progress updates on the client side, we'd write some code similar to what we just saw with sampling. So we'd put together a callback function. When it receives a log statement from the server, it gets called. We'd also make a separate callback that will receive progress updates from the server.

Then we'd pass the log callback to our client session, and the progress callback to the `call_tool` function. Inside these callback functions, it's up to you to decide how to report these log statements and progress to your users. If you're building a CLI application, you could just print them directly to the terminal. If you're building a web app, you'd need to come up with a cleverer solution to get this information to the browser.

Of course, you also don't have to actually include any of these log statements or progress statements. If you don't want to, you can absolutely not show them to the user. These are just user-experience enhancements. Again, just to give your users a better understanding of what's happening.

### 6. Roots

> Type: Video | Duration: 7:55 | Video: 004 - Roots.mp4

Roots are a way to authorize an MCP server to access specific files and folders on your local machine. You can think of them as a permission system that says "hey, MCP server, you can access these files" — but they do far more than that.

## The problem Roots solve

Without roots, you run into a common problem. Imagine you have an MCP server with a video conversion tool that takes a file path and converts MP4 format to MOV format.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747894%2F004_-_Roots_01.1749747894124.png)

When the user asks Claude to "convert biking.mp4 to mov format", Claude will call this tool with only the file name. But the problem is — Claude can't search your entire file system to find where that file actually lives.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747894%2F004_-_Roots_04.1749747894579.png)

Your file system can be complex, with files scattered across different directories. The user knows that the biking.mp4 file is in their Movies folder, but Claude doesn't have this context.

You could solve this problem by requiring the user to always provide the full path, but that isn't friendly. No one wants to type out a full file path every time.

## Roots in practice

Here's how the workflow changes once you use roots:

1. The user requests to convert a video file
2. Claude calls `list_roots` to see which directories it can access
3. Claude calls `read_dir` on the accessible directories to find the file
4. Once the file is found, Claude calls the conversion tool with the full path

This all happens automatically — the user can still just say "convert biking.mp4" without providing the full path.

## Security and boundaries

Roots also provide security by restricting access. If you only grant access to the Desktop folder, the MCP server can't access files in other locations like Documents or Downloads.

When Claude tries to access a file outside the approved roots, it receives an error and can notify the user that the file isn't accessible under the current server configuration.

## Implementation details

The MCP SDK doesn't automatically enforce root restrictions — you need to implement this yourself. A typical pattern is to create a helper function like `is_path_allowed()` that:

- Takes a requested file path
- Gets the list of approved roots
- Checks whether the requested path falls within one of these roots
- Returns true/false as the access permission

Then, in any tool that accesses a file or directory, call this function before performing the actual file operation.

## Key advantages

- **User-friendly** - users don't need to provide full file paths
- **Focused search** - Claude only looks in approved directories, making file discovery faster
- **Security** - prevents accidental access to sensitive files outside the approved areas
- **Flexibility** - you can provide roots through a tool, or inject them directly into the prompt

Roots make MCP servers both more powerful and more secure by giving Claude the context it needs to find files, while maintaining clear boundaries on what it can access.

#### Video transcript

Our next topic is the root system. Roots allow users to grant a server access to certain specific files and folders. You can think of roots as a codified way of saying, hey, MCP server, you can access these files. But roots do more than just grant permissions.

Let me help you understand how they work with a quick example. In this example, I'll first show you a common problem you'd run into without Roots. Once we understand the problem, we'll look at how Roots solve it. So in this example, we'll have a very simple MCP server that has just one tool called `Convert Video`.

This tool takes a path to a video file on the user's local machine and then converts that video file into another file type, like from MP4 to MOV. Now let's imagine how the user would actually use this feature. The user might type something into some CLI application or similar, asking Claude to convert a biking.mp4 video file into MOV format. Claude would then look at the list of tools provided by this MCP server and discover the `convert video` tool is available.

So Claude might decide, great. I'm going to call that tool, and I'll pass in a path `Viking.mp4`. Because that's exactly what the user asked for. The user asked to convert a video file called `Viking.mp4`.

This is the response I might expect to get from that tool. I might expect to see an error returned saying something like "no Viking.mp4 file found". So why would I expect to see that error? Well, remember, the user's file system can be very complex.

They might have all kinds of files and folders, with tons of different documents scattered everywhere. So the user might know that the `Viking.mp4` file is in this movies directory, but Claude has no real way of knowing that. So if a user simply requests `Viking.mp4`, Claude doesn't have the ability to search the user's file system and figure out where exactly that particular document is.

Now, one way we could solve this problem is by requiring the user to always pass in the full path of a video file. So we might say, you know what, if you want to use this tool, you have to pass in a fully qualified path, like `movies/Viking.mp4`. Now, this might work, but it isn't particularly convenient.

The user doesn't really want to sit there and type out the full path for every single file they're trying to access. They probably just want to type something like "convert this video", and just type its name, into another format. So now that we understand the problem, let me show you how we can use the concept of roots to solve it.

So we'll solve this problem by adding some extra tools to our MCP server. We'll still have the same `convert video` tool, but we'll also add a `read directory` tool. I bet you can guess what it does. It just lists all the files and folders under a certain particular directory.

Then the third tool we'll have is called `List Roots`. It returns a list of roots. Our root is a file or folder that the user has pre-granted read access to. In other words, when they first launch this MCP server, they might pass in, as a command-line argument, a list of files and folders the server is allowed to read.

On top of that, we'll also add a small requirement to our other tools. We'll add some code to our `read directory` tool and `convert video` tool. We'll make sure that whenever they try to access a certain directory or a certain video file, they can only access files and folders contained within one of these different roots.

To help you understand how these three different tools work together, I built a very small example application. So let me give you a quick demo right away. To run this program, I run `uvrunmay.py`. Then this program is set up to accept a list of additional command-line arguments.

So if I want, I can type `desktop`, like this. This will be set up as a root inside the MCP server. So I'm basically saying, I want the server to be able to access the desktop folder and all the files within it. Then over here on the right, you can see my desktop directory, which has a file called `Viking.mp4` in it.

So now I'm going to run this, and then I'll ask Claude to convert the `Viking.mp4` file into a MOV file. Now I'll run it, and we'll see some tool calls get printed out. First, Claude will look at all the different files and folders it can access by calling the `List Roots` tool. Then it'll see that there's only one directory it can access, the desktop folder, because that's the only one I granted access to in this program.

Then it'll try to read that directory, and then it'll discover the `Viking.mp4` file inside. So at that point, it now has everything it needs to actually run the `convert video` tool successfully. So it can run that tool with the fully qualified path of my desktop folder, `Viking.mp4`. Now remember, the purpose of roots is to restrict which files and folders the MCP server can access.

So besides that desktop folder, I also have a documents folder here. Inside that directory, there's a `swimming.mp4` file. So now I'm going to ask Claude to convert the `swimming.mp4` file into a MOV file as well. This time, I'll provide the full path of the MP4 file.

So now, theoretically, Claude doesn't need to look at the roots list. It can directly call the `convert video` tool, because it already has the fully qualified path of the specific file it wants to convert. So I can run this, and let's see what happens. So it'll immediately try to call `convert video`, but it'll return early, because Claude will say, sorry, but I ran into an error when running that tool.

It couldn't find that particular file. Claude will then try to look at the available roots. Read the only available root, which in this case is the desktop. Then Claude will realize, hey, it looks like I can't access that file at all.

The only directory I can access is the desktop. If you want me to convert that swimming file, well, I just can't access it. So try to give me access, or move the file, or something. Now, based on that demo, you might notice that roots actually serve two distinct purposes.

On one hand, they allow the user to grant access to specific files and folders. But on the other hand, their other really nice benefit is that they allow Claude to focus only on specific areas of your file system. So as we saw, we don't have to type out the fully qualified path for a certain directory or file we might want Claude to access. Instead, Claude can autonomously decide to look at the available roots, and then search within those roots to find a certain file.

One thing I haven't fully mentioned here, but is very key for you to understand, is that the concept of roots is a bit loose. And there isn't much implementation around them. In other words, in any Anthropic SDK, there's nothing that automatically restricts access to specific files and folders. Instead, it's up to you to make sure, in your Anthropic server, that whenever a tool tries to access a file or folder, it's listed or contained within one of these different roots.

You might do this by implementing a function like the one you see in the top right corner here, like `is path allowed`. Inside that function, it takes a requested path, then it gets a list of roots from the client, and then sees whether the path the tool wants to use is contained within one of those routes. If it is, great, we allow access. Otherwise, no, we don't allow access at all.

Now, the last thing I want to mention is that I showed implementing a tool like `list roots`, and allowing Claude to call that tool at any time. This isn't strictly required. You could also just get the list of roots and manually throw them all into a prompt. So you don't have to build that tool.

This is just a pattern I've found helpful for getting Claude to look at the list of roots whenever it decides it needs to figure out which files and folders it can access.
