### 9. JSON message types

> Type: Video | Duration: 6:45 | Video: 005 - JSON Message Types.mp4

MCP (Model Context Protocol) uses JSON messages to handle communication between clients and servers. Understanding these message types is essential to working with MCP, especially when dealing with different `transport` methods (like streamable HTTP transport).

## Message format

All MCP communication happens through JSON messages. Each message type has a specific purpose — whether it's calling a tool, listing available resources, or sending a notification about a system event.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747889%2F005_-_JSON_Message_Types_04.1749747889371.png)

Here's a typical example: when Claude needs to call a tool provided by an MCP server, the client sends a "Call Tool Request" message. The server processes this request, runs the tool, then responds with a "Call Tool Result" message containing the output.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747889%2F005_-_JSON_Message_Types_05.1749747889768.png)

## The MCP specification

The complete list of message types is defined in the official MCP specification repository on GitHub. This specification is independent of the various SDK repositories (like the Python or TypeScript SDKs), and serves as the authoritative source for how MCP should work.

The message types are written in TypeScript, purely for convenience — not because they execute as TypeScript code, but because TypeScript provides a clean way to describe data structures and types.

## Message categories

MCP messages fall into two broad categories:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747891%2F005_-_JSON_Message_Types_13.1749747891508.png)

### Request-Result messages

These messages always come in pairs. You send a request, and you expect a result:

- **Call Tool Request** → Call Tool Result
- **List Prompts Request → List Prompts Result**
- **Read Resource Request → Read Resource Result**
- **Initialize Request → Initialize Result**

### Notification messages

These are one-way messages used to notify about events, but they don't require a response:

- **Progress Notification** - updates about long-running operations
- **Logging Message Notification** - system log messages
- **Tool List Changed Notification** - when the available tools change
- **Resource Updated Notification** - when a resource is modified

## Client and server messages

The MCP specification organizes messages by who sends them:

Client messages include requests sent from the client to the server (like tool calls) and notifications the client might send.

Server messages include requests the server sends to the client and notifications the server broadcasts.

## Why this matters

When dealing with different transport methods, it's especially important to understand that the server can send messages to the client. Some transports, like the streamable HTTP transport, have restrictions on which types of messages can flow in which direction.

The key insight is that MCP is designed as a bidirectional protocol — both the client and the server can initiate communication. This becomes critical when you need to choose the right transport method for your particular use case.

#### Video transcript

We've discussed several topics, and before we move on, I just want to give you a small heads-up. You see, the next two or three videos will focus on MCP messages and the standard IO transport. These videos might seem a bit dry and boring, but there's a very important reason we're discussing these topics. One of the main goals of this course is to help you understand the streamable HTTP transport, which allows clients to connect to remotely hosted MCP servers.

The thing is, in some cases, when an MCP server uses HTTP transport, its functionality is significantly limited. Understanding these limitations can be quite challenging, but if you have a solid foundation in MCP messages and the standard IO transport, it'll be much easier to understand. So that's why we're going to spend some time on the first two topics. With all that in mind, let's look at the format MCP uses to communicate, specifically the message format.

The client and server communicate using JSON. We call these JSON snippets messages. There are many different types of messages defined in the MCP specification, each designed for a different purpose. So let me show you an example right away.

For example, if a language model connected to an MCP client decides to call a tool provided by an MCP server, the client sends a message to the server. This type of message is named `call tool request`. At the top of this diagram, I have an example of a `call tool request`. The MCP server would then run the tool, and then put the result of the tool's run into another message type called `call tool result`.

At the bottom of this diagram, I also have an example. Now, as I mentioned, the MCP specification defines a complete list of all the different message types. You can find the complete list in the MCP specification repository hosted on GitHub, which we'll look at shortly. But first, to be clear about the repository we're going to look at, this specification repository is separate from all the different SDK repositories, like the Python or TypeScript MCP SDKs.

This specification repository has a few different documents that describe how the MCP specification works. So in this repository, the complete list of message types is written in a TypeScript file. To be clear again, this TypeScript file is never executed by anything. It's not included in any of the different SDKs.

These types are just written in TypeScript because TypeScript is a very convenient language for describing typed data. So I want to show you this schema file, which lists all the different message types, just because there are some very interesting insights to be gained from it. So I'm going to navigate to this address at the top of your screen. Then in this repository, you'll find a file called `schema.ts` under a certain path.

The `schema.ts` file contains all the different message types available. So in this file, I can search for `call tool request`. Right here. This is an example of a `call tool request`.

So a `call tool request` must have a method of `tool/call`, and then a params object containing the name of the tool we want to call and some arguments to pass in. You might notice that in the diagram I showed you earlier, back here, there's also a `JSON RPC` and an `ID`. Those are actually defined on a separate object in the schema file called `JSON RPC request`. So if I look it up, I'll see, okay, here's the `JSON RPC request`, which must also have an `ID`.

Now, throughout this whole file, what I really want you to focus on are some different types defined at the very bottom. So I'm going to scroll to the bottom, the very very bottom of the file. Then I'll scroll up a little bit, and I'll find a comment that says `server messages`, and a comment that says `client messages`. You'll notice that inside both of these sections, there are some similarly named types.

So in client messages, there's `client request`, `client notification`, and `client result`. Then in the server section, there's `server request`, `server notification`, and `server result`. So let me show you a diagram. It can help you understand what these different types in this file, specifically the client and server sections, are actually telling us.

Okay, here we go. So in this types file, we can divide all these different types of messages into two or three different categories. First, on the left side of this diagram, are the request-result messages. These message types always come in pairs.

Their names are always `something-something-request`, and then the paired message type is `something-something-result`. For example, we have `call-tool-request`, which is paired with `call-tool-result`. We have `initialized request`, which is paired with `initialized result`. All these different message types, I bet you can guess what they do.

They describe the messages we send to the client or server, and the type of message we expect to get in response. So if I sent a `call to request`, I expect to get a `call tool result`. The other kind of message we'll deal with is notification messages. These are more like events.

They tell the client or server that something just happened, but they don't really need a response. So we have types like `progress notification`, `logging message notification`, `tool change notification`, and so on. Now, back to the types file we just looked at, you might have noticed we have the `server messages` and `client messages` headings. These comments, and the types inside each one, are meant to indicate whether these types of messages are sent by the client or the server.

For example, this `client request` type describes all the different types of requests expected to be sent from the client. Similarly, these here are requests expected to be sent from the server. The same goes for server notifications, these are notifications issued by the server. And these are notifications issued by the client.

So we can summarize everything with a diagram like this. Now this doesn't show every type of message. I just wanted to show that there are some requests meant to be issued from the MCP client, and I have examples here. There are some results meant to be issued from the server.

There are some results meant to be sent from the client. There are some requests meant to be sent from the server, and so on. Okay, so here's the key point I want you to understand. The focus of this video, which will become very important when we start discussing the streamable HTTP transport.

The key point to understand here is that there are many different messages meant to be sent from the server to the client. So the server request types and server notification types here. These are all messages that will be sent from the server to the client. I know this might seem completely irrelevant right now, why is it important to know this?

Again, when we start looking at the streamable HTTP transport, this will become very important. So keep this in mind for now.

---

### 10. The STDIO transport

> Type: Video | Duration: 9:28 | Video: 006 - The Stdio Transport.mp4

MCP clients and servers communicate by exchanging JSON objects that we call messages. Now, JSON can generally be transmitted between a client and server in many different ways. For example, we could make HTTP requests. We could use WebSockets.

We could even write some JSON on a postcard, mail it to someone, and have them manually type that JSON into the server. So there are actually a huge number of ways to send and receive JSON. In the MCP specification, the thing we use to actually move JSON between the client and server is called a transport. When you initially develop an MCP server or client, a very commonly used transport is the `stdio transport`.

The idea of this transport is that our client will launch a server as a separate `subprocess`. By doing so, the client has a handle to that process, and the client can send messages to the server by writing messages to the server's `standard in` channel. And it can receive messages by watching the server's `standard out` channel.

One really nice thing about the `stdio` transport is that communication between the client or server can be very easily initiated by either side. In other words, at any time, the client can communicate with the server or send a message to the server by writing to standard in. And the server can communicate with the client by writing to standard out. But the `stdio` transport also has a big drawback, which is that we can only use this transport when the client and server are running on the same physical machine.

To help you better understand the `stdio` transport, let me give you a quick demo. I built a very small MCP server. I have some print statements up here at the top, just to help you understand what's happening. But besides those print statements, there isn't much to this server.

I'm creating a server, I've defined a single tool, and then I launch the server with the `stdio` transport. Now I could create a client that connects to this server using the `stdio` transport, but I can also connect to the server directly from my terminal without creating any separate client. Let me show you what I mean. If I run `UVRunServer.py` here, it'll launch that server.

Now that server will listen to standard in, and write any outgoing messages to standard out. Well, in my terminal, in case you don't know, whenever I run a program, if I type anything down here, I'm writing to the standard in of this running program. So I can actually paste JSON messages down here, execute them, and they'll be received as input by the server. So let me give you a quick demo.

I'm going to quickly restart the server. Then I'm going to copy the first example message here. I'm going to paste it here. When I press enter, it'll happen almost instantly.

I'll immediately get a response. So I wrote one message to standard in. I saw an output message on standard out. Then I'll send another message.

Here we go. This time, I won't see any response. Finally, I'll run the last message down here.

When I run this, I'll immediately get a response, then another response, then another response. Three objects total. This is one, this is two, this is three. So let me show you a few diagrams to help you understand what exactly I was doing when I pasted these different messages.

Okay, back here. On the left-hand side, I labeled it as my MCP client, but really it's just me pasting messages in the terminal. By pasting those messages, I'm writing to the MCP server's standard in. The server then processes these given messages, and then might send a result by simply printing it to standard out, which I see directly in my terminal.

The first message I sent is something called an `initialize request`. Let me give you a little background. Whenever a client first connects to a server, the MCP specification states that we must send a sequence of three different messages back and forth. The first message must always be an `initialize request`.

Again, that's what I initially pasted in. That was the `initialize request`. Because I sent a request-type message, I expect to get a result. And that's exactly what happened.

I got something called an `initialized result`. That was that text. Then the NCP specification also says that after exchanging these initial messages, we must send an `initialized notification` from the client to the server. As a reminder, notifications don't require a response.

So I did send that `initialized notification`. As we noticed, we didn't get any immediate response. Again, this is just because notifications don't require a response. Once we've exchanged these three messages, our connection to the MCP server is considered initialized.

From then on, we can run `call tool` requests, or run list prompts requests, or anything else we want to do. So in my example, as you saw, I sent a `call tool` request. That was the last one. I tried to call the `add` tool with arguments 5 and 3.

The way I wrote the `add` tool, it's set up to send back a few different logs, and then finally a `called tool result`. So if I look at the logs, I actually changed it from what you saw in that diagram. First, I received a message notification. This is a logging statement from there.

That was my logging statement. Then I got a progress statement. So that was the progress update there. Those are all notifications sent from the server to the client.

Finally, I got my `call tool` response, with the result of adding 3 plus 5 inside, which I see is 8. So now that we've seen an example of the `stdio` transport, there are some very key ideas about this particular transport that I want to point out, ideas that will be very relevant or very important when we start looking at the streamable HTTP transport. First, I just showed you a diagram. I showed you this diagram to help you understand that there are some message types meant to be sent from the client to the server, and some types meant to be sent from the server to the client.

The other thing I really want you to understand from this diagram is that, in some cases, specifically the messages in the top left, we can imagine the client initiating communication with the server. In other words, the client is saying, I'm issuing a request, and I expect to get a response. Similarly, in some cases, the MCP server is sending an initial request and expecting to get a response. This is like the `create message` request, which is used to kick off sampling.

So the server needs to send this request to the client, and expect to get some kind of result. Now, in slightly clearer terms, what I really want to convey with this is. Actually, for different transports like the `stdio` transport, we have four different cases to handle. We need to be able to handle an initial request, or some kind of initial request, from the client to the server, and the transport needs to be able to handle a response from the server to the client.

In addition, the server needs to be able to send an initial request to the client, and likewise, the client needs to be able to respond. So I want to go through all four of these different cases, and think about how we would implement them with the `stdio` transport. So first is the initial request from the client to the server. So this case would be any time the client wants to send something to the server, like a call request or something similar.

To implement this with the `stdio` transport, all we have to do is write to standard in. The MCP server will receive that message, process it, and then hopefully form a response. Once it has a response, it responds by writing a message to standard out. Now the third case is, we have an initial request to send from the client to the server.

So this would be the server perhaps wanting to do some sampling, which requires the server to send some kind of initial message to the client. Whenever the server needs to send an initial request to the client, all it has to do is write to standard out. Then likewise, to respond, all the client has to do is write to standard in. Okay, I know these few minutes of video and the series of diagrams I just walked through might be a bit confusing.

You might be wondering, what exactly is all this about initial requests and responses and so on? Well, the point is. The point of all this is to help you understand that the `stdio` transport is really great, because at any time, either the client or the server can initiate communication. Either side can send a request at any time, and expect to get a response.

Here's the key. This is not the case for the streamable HTTP transport. In some cases, the streamable HTTP transport doesn't allow this. In some cases, the server can't send an initial request to the client.

This is where the streamable HTTP transport gets hard to understand. So let's pause here for now. When we come back, we'll start looking at the HTTP transport.

We'll see this particular scenario and understand, yes, there are some tricky things about this that you'll definitely need to understand when developing your own MCP server.

---

### 11. The StreamableHTTP transport

> Type: Video | Duration: 6:26 | Video: 007 - The StreamableHTTP Transport.mp4

The `streamable HTTP transport` enables MCP clients to connect to remotely hosted servers over HTTP. Unlike the `standard I/O transport`, which requires the client and server to be on the same machine, this transport opens up the possibility of public MCP servers that anyone can access.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747948%2F007_-_The_StreamableHTTP_Transport_00.1749747948797.png)

However, there's an important caveat: certain configuration settings will severely limit your MCP server's functionality. If your application works great locally with the standard I/O transport, but breaks after you deploy it with the HTTP transport, this is very likely the culprit.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747949%2F007_-_The_StreamableHTTP_Transport_02.1749747949391.png)

## Important configuration settings

Two key settings control the behavior of the streamable HTTP transport:

- `stateless_http` - controls connection state management
- `json_response` - controls response format handling

By default, both of these settings are false, but certain deployment scenarios may force you to set them to true. When enabled, these settings can break core functionality like progress notifications, logging, and server-initiated requests.

## The challenge of HTTP communication

To understand why these limitations exist, we need to review how HTTP communication works. In standard HTTP:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747951%2F007_-_The_StreamableHTTP_Transport_13.1749747951318.png)

- The client can easily initiate a request to the server (the server has a known URL)
- The server can easily respond to these requests
- The server cannot easily initiate a request to the client (the client doesn't have a known URL)
- The pattern of responses going from the client back to the server becomes problematic

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747952%2F007_-_The_StreamableHTTP_Transport_15.1749747951861.png)

## Affected MCP message types

This HTTP limitation affects specific MCP communication patterns. The following message types become difficult to implement when using plain HTTP:

- **Server-initiated requests:** Create Message request, List Roots request
- **Notifications:** Progress notification, Logging notification, Initialized notification, Cancelled notification

These are exactly the features that break when you enable the restrictive HTTP settings. Progress bars disappear, logging stops working, and server-initiated sampling requests fail.

## The streamable HTTP solution

The streamable HTTP transport does offer a clever solution to HTTP's limitations, but it comes with trade-offs. When you're forced to use `stateless_http=True` or `json_response=True`, you're essentially telling the transport to operate within HTTP's constraints, rather than working around them.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747953%2F007_-_The_StreamableHTTP_Transport_19.1749747952895.png)

Understanding these limitations helps you make informed decisions:

-   Which transport method to use for different deployment scenarios
-   How to design your MCP server to gracefully handle HTTP restrictions
-   When to accept reduced functionality for the benefits of remote hosting

The key is to understand that these limitations exist, and to plan your MCP server architecture accordingly. If your application relies heavily on server-initiated requests or real-time notifications, you may need to reconsider your transport choice or implement alternative communication patterns.

#### Video transcript

Now to discuss the Streamable HTTP transport. This transport allows us to send messages between a client and server over an HTTP connection. The advantage of it is that it truly enables remotely hosted MCP servers. By contrast, with the Standard IO transport, we always had to run the client and server on the same machine.

With the streamable HTTP transport, we can host our server remotely. So our remote server might be hosted on MCPServer.com or a similar domain. This really does open up broader possibilities for MCP servers, because you can create a public server that anyone can connect to. However, as I mentioned in the previous few videos, you may need to apply some settings to this transport that limit your MCP server's functionality, specifically limiting the types of messages you can send back from the server to the client.

So this is a big problem I want you to notice. When you use this particular transport, there are some settings that limit the server's functionality. So if you develop an application on your local machine, everything works fine with the standard IO transport, and then you deploy and start using the HTTP transport and find things don't go as smoothly, then you'll need to come back and look at this video to understand what exactly is happening. First, I want to quickly demo an application I showed you earlier in this course.

You might remember this Wikipedia research assistant. It uses a Next.js application to connect to an MCP server that implements a research tool. This server is using the streamable HTTP transport, so I can use this application to show you a few scenarios where the streamable HTTP transport doesn't work as expected. First, I'll send a request here, just asking for a report about archaeology, just to remind you what this application looks like.

Normally, when I send a query like this, I'll see a call tool request here, then there'll be a nice progress bar, I'll get status updates, and finally, if I wait a moment, I'll get the complete response. Now, over on the MCP server's source code side, you'll notice I commented out two settings: Stateless HTTP and JSON response. By default, both of these settings are set to false, but in some cases, you really need to enable them or set them to true, which I'll explain in more detail later.

Changing these settings to true affects your MCP server's functionality, and in some cases, may even break the way your client works. So let me show you an example right away. I want to change `stateless HTTP` to true. I'll save the file.

Then I'll reload the page and run the exact same query again. Now you won't see a big difference here, but you'll immediately notice something. You'll notice that now I don't have a progress bar. So there's no progress bar at all above the log statements.

In addition, if I let it sit here for a moment, I'll speed up the video, and we'll see that the final request fails completely without generating any text. Now I'll refresh the page, set `JSON response` to true, save the file, and then run the exact same query again. Now we'll see that when both settings are set to true, we get some more surprising results. Now I don't see any progress bar, and I don't get any log statements, and trust me, if we just let it sit there, the request will fail again.

So we can immediately see that these two seemingly harmless settings have a big impact on our MCP server. So the rest of this video is meant to help you understand what these settings mean, why we might set them to true, and what exactly is happening here. First, I want to quickly remind you of a diagram I showed in the previous video. I emphasized that the standard IO transport is able to initiate a request from the client to the server, and then get a response.

Similarly, the server can also initiate a request to the client at any time, and get a response. So keep this in mind for now, and let me give you a quick review of HTTP communication. This is some basic knowledge about HTTP, which applies to all HTTP communication, not just MCP-related stuff. As a reminder, if we have a client and a server, the client can very easily make a request to the server at any time.

For example, on the right, the client can make a POST request to the server, and expect to get some kind of response. There's nothing wrong with this setup. So translating this into the world of MCP, this means that if we want to make an initial request from the client to the server, no problem, it works. If we want to get a response from the server to the client, that's also completely fine, it works normally.

However, if we consider the opposite scenario, if the server wants to initiate a request to the client, this isn't so easy in HTTP requests. On a very basic level, the server doesn't know the client's address, and the client might not even be publicly accessible. Therefore, in traditional HTTP, it's very challenging for the server to initiate a request to the client. This means that in our MCP world, it's really hard for the server to send that initial request down, and it also means it's hard to imagine how you could expect to get a response from the client back to the server.

So, if we look at this diagram we just looked at, where I told you some requests are sent from the server to the client, this means that in this HTTP world, there are some message types that are quite difficult to implement with plain HTTP requests. Specifically, sampling requests, listing routes, progress notifications, and logging notifications, as well as some other message types I didn't show on this diagram, are harder to implement in the HTTP world. Sure enough, this is pretty much what you just saw in this demo. As soon as I start switching these properties from false to true, you'll see different parts of the application start to break and not work as expected.

So what exactly broke? That's right. Progress notifications broke, logging broke, and sampling, or the `create message` request, also broke. Sampling was being used to write the research report.

But I have some good news. Even though it's challenging to make requests from the server to the client in the pure HTTP world, the streamable HTTP transport does have a clever solution. But there are some caveats you need to be aware of. Let's look at how this transport actually works, and what exactly those caveats are, shortly.

### 12. StreamableHTTP in Depth

> Type: Video | Duration: 10:28 | Video: 008 - StreamableHTTP in Depth.mp4

StreamableHTTP is MCP's solution to a fundamental problem: some MCP functionality requires the server to make requests to the client, but HTTP makes this challenging. Let's explore how StreamableHTTP works around this limitation, and the circumstances under which you might need to break this workaround.

## The core problem

Some MCP functionality, like sampling, notifications, and logging, depends on the server initiating requests to the client. However, HTTP is designed for clients to make requests to servers, not the other way around. StreamableHTTP solves this problem using a clever workaround: Server-Sent Events (SSE).

## How StreamableHTTP works

This magic process establishes a persistent connection between the client and server through a multi-step process.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747952%2F008_-_StreamableHTTP_in_Depth_04.1749747952200.png)

### Initial connection setup

The process begins like any MCP connection:

-   The client sends an `Initialize Request` to the server
-   The server responds with an `Initialize Result` containing a special `mcp-session-id` header
-   The client sends an `Initialized Notification` with the session ID

This session ID is critical — it uniquely identifies the client, and must be included in all future requests.

### The SSE workaround

After initialization, the client can make a GET request to establish a server-sent events connection. This creates a long-lived HTTP response that the server can use to stream messages to the client at any time.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747953%2F008_-_StreamableHTTP_in_Depth_06.1749747952963.png)

This SSE connection is the key to enabling server-to-client communication. The server can now send requests, notifications, and other messages through this persistent channel.

## Tool Calls and dual SSE connections

When the client makes a tool call, things get more complex. The system creates two separate SSE connections:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747953%2F008_-_StreamableHTTP_in_Depth_08.1749747953358.png)

-   **Main SSE connection:** used for server-initiated requests, and kept open indefinitely
-   **Tool-specific SSE connection:** created for each tool call, and automatically closed after the tool result is sent

### Message routing

Different types of messages are routed through different connections:

-   **Progress notifications:** sent through the main SSE connection
-   **Logging messages and tool results:** sent through the tool-specific SSE connection

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747953%2F008_-_StreamableHTTP_in_Depth_11.1749747953846.png)

## Configuration flags that break the workaround

StreamableHTTP includes two important configuration options:

-   `stateless_http`
-   `json_response`

Setting these to `True` breaks the SSE workaround mechanism. In some cases, you may want to enable these flags, but doing so limits the full MCP functionality that relies on server-to-client communication.

## Key takeaways

StreamableHTTP is more complex than other MCP transports because it has to work around HTTP's limitations. The SSE-based workaround enables full MCP functionality over HTTP, but understanding the dual-connection model is essential for debugging and optimization.

When building MCP applications with StreamableHTTP, remember that all requests after initialization require the session ID, and the system automatically manages multiple SSE connections to handle different types of server-to-client communication.

#### Video transcript

Let's quickly review the content of the previous few videos to make sure we're on the same page. First we learned that some MCP functionality, namely sampling, notifications, logging, and so on, depends on the server making requests to the client. Shortly after, we learned that when we use HTTP generally, it's quite difficult to get the server to make requests to the client. So these first two points are somewhat contradictory.

They conflict with each other. We need the server to build and make requests to the client for full functionality, but at the same time, this is hard to do when using HTTP. Now, as I mentioned, Streamable HTTP has a workaround to solve this problem. I'll show you in this video exactly what this workaround is, and how this transport generally works.

So we're finally going to figure out exactly what's happening. Finally, remember that in some cases, we'll set these two flags to true, and we'll discuss why we'd do that. When you do this, it breaks this workaround. This is also why Streamable HTTP, as a transport, is a bit hard to understand, because we have this limitation around HTTP.

We have the workaround, but in some cases, we don't want to use that workaround, which is why this is a tricky topic. Okay, enough chitchat, let's get started. Let's understand how this transport actually works. I'll first show you a few different diagrams to help you understand the behind-the-scenes process.

I'll also show you a short demo, where you'll see all these requests actually being made, and how the entire system works together in a very small demo application. So, let's get started. First, I want you to recall what happens when a client first connects to a server. According to the MCP specification, the client must send an initialize request to the server, the server replies with a result, and then the client must make a follow-up request with an initialized notification.

At that point, the server considers the client connected, and everything is ready. Now, once we start using the HTTP transport, this flow changes slightly. So once you start using this transport, the initialize result sent back from the server will include a header in the HTTP response. This header is the MCP session ID.

This is a random string of numbers and letters, assigned to our client, that serves as the identifier for our connection to the server. Once we receive that header, we must include it in all subsequent requests we make to the server. This allows the server to identify our client. Now, after we finish this initialization and get that session ID, the magic part begins.

This is the important workaround that allows the server to make requests to the client. Once we finish initialization, the client can optionally (it doesn't have to do this) make a GET request to the MCP server, and include that session ID in the request. The response we get is very special. It's an SSE response.

SSE stands for Server-Sent Events. This is a response that can be kept open for any amount of time. Once this response is established with our client, the server can stream small chunks of information, basically individual messages, to our client. So once this connection is established, now, at this point, this is the really key part.

This now allows the server to send messages down to the client at any time. This connection can be used, essentially, to allow the server to make requests to the client. So that's the trick. That's the workaround the HTTP transport uses.

It takes advantage of this long-lived SSE response, and streams down the messages it wants to send to the client. So, now let's go a step further, and walk through what happens when the client might want to call a tool, because the complexity isn't over yet. There's another key part you need to understand. Now at the top of this diagram, I have that SSE response we just created.

Remember, that response, that open connection, has a session ID tied to it. So the MCP server knows exactly which connector, which connection belongs to which client. So later at some future point in time, while that response is still running, our client might decide to make a call tool request to the server. When it makes this request, it includes the session ID as a header.

Then the MCP server opens a second SSE response. So now, at this point, we have two separate SSE responses. The first one at the top, meant for requests from the server to the client. The brand-new second SSE response is meant for messages related to this call tool request.

Importantly, and this is the key part, this second SSE response automatically closes immediately after the call tool result message is sent. So the response at the bottom automatically closes quickly after we get the result, while the response above is meant to stay open for any length of time. Then, remember, our ultimate goal here is actually to call a tool. So maybe we call the add tool we've seen many times.

That implementation I've shown you a few times has some log messages and some progress notifications in it. Eventually, it returns a result, which is our call tool result. Now here's another somewhat tricky part. Technically, the log messages and progress notifications are both closely related to the call tool request.

So you might think both of these primary messages should be sent back in the response at the bottom, because they're both really related to this call tool request. Well, that's not exactly how many SDKs currently work. The progress notification ends up being considered separate from the incoming call tool request. So the progress notification is actually sent in the first SSE response.

So that's the response meant to be kept open for a long time to allow the server to make requests to the client. Then the log messages and the actual call tool result are sent back in the response to the POST request or GET tool request that was made. So that's the entire flow. Now to make sure all this is clear, I want to walk you through a very quick demo, where you can see all these different requests being made in a very intuitive way.

So I prepared a small demo. First, I again prepared a simple MCP server with a simple add tool. The add tool will log some information, wait two seconds, emit a progress report, and then return a result. I use this client running in the browser to connect to this MCP server.

This client will allow us to make some different requests to the server, and then display the specific responses we get. Now, in general, this client will go through the flow you see on the screen. I know there's a lot here, but essentially, we first go through the entire initialization process, with these things at the top. Then we'll form the GET SSE connection, which allows the server to send requests to the client.

Then we'll make a call tool request, which will form the second SSE connection, responsible for handling messages related to this particular call tool request. So let's run it and see what happens. First, I'll make the initialize request. So I'll send it.

This will give me my session ID, which uniquely identifies my client to the server in any future request. Remember, the session ID is only provided when you use the streamable HTTP transport. It's not used at all for the standard IO transport. You'll notice it starts with EAA.

This header is automatically captured and applied to all future requests. So if I scroll down to the next request, you'll notice the MCP session ID has already automatically applied the correct session ID, the one I got from the request above. So now we've sent the initialize request, that one, and our second request is the initialized notification. So I'll send it.

Okay, now at this point, we've handled the first three boxes here. So now we'll make our GET request to the server. This GET request will stay open for a long time. This is what allows the server to send requests to our client whenever it wishes.

It'll be used for things like sampling or any kind of server-initiated request. To make that request, I'll use this little box in the bottom right corner. So I'll click Start GET SSE. This request will also include that session ID.

Now I've formed that connection. So now, theoretically, at any time, the server can send a message to my client, for sampling, logging, or progress notifications, etc. If I receive any of these messages, they should appear inside this box. So now it's the last step.

I'll call the Add function. I again include the correct session ID in it. Now, as a reminder, when I click this, theoretically, we hope to see the log statement, progress statement, and the final call to a result all appear in this box, which represents that part of the response. But in reality, just because of the way Python's MCP SDK is set up, the progress notification will actually be sent as part of the response above this.

So we should see a log statement and a call tool result here. We should see the progress statement in that box in the bottom right corner. So let's run it and see what happens. Okay, I'll run it.

Then after a small delay, because remember there's a two-second pause in it, we'll then get our result. So sure enough, in the bottom right corner, I got a notification/progress. So that's the progress statement. I completed 80% out of 100%.

So that was sent in that response channel. Then the actual response to my call tool request has a log statement in the form of notification/message. Then after that, I got the actual result of the call tool request. So this is my call tool result, the answer to adding those two numbers is eight.

Then remember, once we get that call tool result, this connection automatically closes. So I see there, yes, the connection is closed, and I can no longer receive any messages along this SSE response we got. Okay, that's it. That's the entire flow.

So now you understand what's happening behind the scenes with the streamable HTTP transport. But we're not done yet. Remember, I said many times that in some cases, you'll want to set these flags to true, and it'll break some parts of this flow. So this is the last thing we need to understand.

Why would we set them to true? Because this might be something you want to do at some point, and what exactly does it break? So this is the last thing we really need to figure out.

---

### 13. State and the StreamableHTTP transport

> Type: Video | Duration: 6:29 | Video: 009 - Stateless HTTP.mp4

The `stateless_http` and `json_response` flags in an MCP server control fundamental aspects of your server's behavior. Understanding when and why to use them is critical, especially if you plan to scale your server or deploy it in a production environment.

## When you need stateless HTTP

Imagine you build a wildly popular MCP server. Initially, there might be just a few clients connecting to a single server instance:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747958%2F009_-_Stateless_HTTP_01.1749747958582.png)

As your server grows, you might have thousands of clients trying to connect. Running a single server instance won't scale to handle all that traffic:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747959%2F009_-_Stateless_HTTP_02.1749747959000.png)

The typical solution is to scale horizontally — running multiple server instances behind a load balancer:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747959%2F009_-_Stateless_HTTP_03.1749747959503.png)

But here's where things get complicated. Remember, an MCP client needs two separate connections:

-   A GET SSE connection, for receiving server-to-client requests
-   POST requests, for calling tools and receiving responses

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747960%2F009_-_Stateless_HTTP_05.1749747960146.png)

With a load balancer, these requests might be routed to different server instances. If your tool needs to use Claude (via sampling), the server handling the POST request will need to coordinate with the server handling the GET SSE connection. This creates a complex coordination problem between servers.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747960%2F009_-_Stateless_HTTP_06.1749747960668.png)

## How stateless HTTP solves this

Setting `stateless_http=True` eliminates this coordination problem, but comes with significant trade-offs:

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747961%2F009_-_Stateless_HTTP_09.1749747961056.png)

When stateless HTTP is enabled:

-   **The client doesn't get a session ID** - the server can't track individual clients
-   **No server-to-client requests** - the GET SSE channel becomes unavailable
-   **No sampling** - you can't use Claude or other AI models
-   **No progress reporting** - you can't send progress updates during long operations
-   **No subscriptions** - you can't notify clients about resource updates

However, there's one benefit: client initialization is no longer required. The client can make requests directly, without the initial handshake process.

![](https://everpath-course-content.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com/instructor%2Fa46l9irobhg0f5webscixp0bs%2Fpublic%2F1749747961%2F009_-_Stateless_HTTP_10.1749747961577.png)

## Understanding JSON response

The `json_response=True` flag is simpler — it just disables streaming of POST request responses. Instead of getting multiple SSE messages as the tool executes, you only get the final result as plain JSON.

With streaming disabled:

-   No intermediate progress messages
-   No log statements during execution
-   Only the final tool result

## When to use these flags

Use stateless HTTP when:

-   You need to scale horizontally with a load balancer
-   You don't need server-to-client communication
-   Your tools don't need AI model sampling
-   You want to minimize connection overhead

Use JSON response when:

-   You don't need streaming responses
-   You prefer simpler, non-streaming HTTP responses
-   You're integrating with systems that expect plain JSON

## Development vs. production

If you develop locally with the standard I/O transport, but plan to deploy with the HTTP transport, test with the same transport you'll use in production. The behavioral differences between stateful and stateless modes can be significant, and it's best to discover any issues during development rather than after deployment.

These flags fundamentally change how your MCP server operates, so choose them based on your specific scaling and functionality needs.

#### Video transcript

The last thing we need to do is understand what the stateless and JSON response flags mean. How do they affect our server, and when would we set them to true? I'll first walk you through a scenario where we might want to set these flags to true, because it really explains why these flags exist and how they affect our server. Let's imagine that you and I build an MCP server, and publish it publicly online, where anyone can connect to it.

So, maybe we connect to it with our own client, and then some other people connect to it with their own clients too. Over time, our server might become very, very popular, and besides the three clients we initially connected, there might be many, many other people connecting to it too. At some point, running a single instance of our server on one machine might not scale to handle the incoming traffic. So one way we can solve this problem is through some horizontal scaling.

With horizontal scaling, we might run multiple copies of our server, and then control access to them through a load balancer. So any incoming request gets randomly routed to one of these different servers. This will allow us to scale to meet higher traffic demands. Let's think about what happens if we put a load balancer here, especially keeping in mind the fact that we want two separate connections from any given client to our MCP server.

First, we want that running GET request to always be running, so that we can receive server-to-client requests. Our client also needs to make POST requests, and then receive an SSE response containing several messages. So, let's just consider one client connecting to perhaps two different servers. This client makes an initial request, establishing that initial GET SSC response pipeline.

So, we can then imagine that we have this response pending or continuously running to the client. Again remember, this is all so that the server can send requests back down to the client. Then, perhaps at some future point in time, the client decides to run a tool. So it might make a call tool request using a POST request.

Maybe this request is routed to the second MCP server. The second server then responds by establishing its own separate SSC response. Now, at this point, for clarity, I won't show the load balancer. When the tool runs inside that server, let's imagine for a moment that it needs to use Claude, it might try to use Claude by creating a create message request.

Remember, this represents an attempt to use sampling. Now, a sampling request always needs to go through a GET SSE response. But that connection was established by a completely separate server. So, we need to figure out some way to send this request from here to the other server, have this server send the request down through this GET SSC response, have the MCP client run Claude, generate some text, send it back to this server, and then somehow pass the generated text back to this server.

As you can probably tell right away, coordinating all this would be very, very challenging. Now, this is definitely doable, but it represents a lot of difficulty and a lot of extra configuration and infrastructure. So, if we're building an MCP server that we expect to scale horizontally, and we don't want to go through all this extra coordination and infrastructure setup, we might decide to set that stateless HTTP flag to true. Setting this flag to true has a straightforward but very important effect.

It means the client doesn't get a session ID, which means the server can never track a client. Right away, this produces some very big knock-on effects. Without a session ID, the MCP server can no longer use the GET SSC response channel to send requests to the client. To understand why that response channel can no longer be used, I suggest you imagine a bank without any account IDs.

So, a bank might receive money, but it wouldn't really know who gave them the money, nor would it really know who to give money to, because it doesn't know who's owed what. It's the same situation here without an identifying token. Now, without that GET SSC response channel, this means that because our server can't send any requests to the client, we also can't use features like sampling, or progress logging, or subscriptions around things like resource changes. However, this also has a benefit.

In stateless mode, you no longer need to do client initialization. So, this means, back in this application, remember that whenever we connect to the server, we had to make that initialize request, then the follow-up initialized notification. When you're in stateless mode, you don't need to make those two requests, which definitely reduces the traffic your server receives. So, there's a pretty nice trade-off here.

Now, the other flag we've been discussing is JSON response. Whenever you set JSON response to true, this just means the POST request you send down to the client won't have streaming enabled. I can give you a very simple, easy-to-understand demo so you can see what that looks like. So, here, let me quickly remind you how this application normally works.

I'll send the initialize request, the initialized notification, and then when I call the add tool function, I'll open that SSC connection, and I'll first get a message, then the call tool result. Now, I'll very quickly flip the JSON response flag to true. Again, this means all the responses I get will just be the final result as plain JSON. No streaming at all.

So, now I'll go through the same process again. I'll run initialize, I'll run the notification, and now this time when I call the add function, I won't get those intermediate messages about some logging or anything else. Instead, I'll get the final tool call result, and nothing else. So, now if I run this, we'll see that I'm not streaming the response back.

Instead, I'm waiting until the tool call completes, and I just get the result, absolutely nothing else. No log statements at all. So you can immediately see that these two flags have a huge impact on your server's behavior. But, depending on how you're trying to develop and deploy your application, setting these flags to true might be completely appropriate.

Okay, with a lot of patience and many different diagrams, I think we finally have a reasonable understanding of how the streamable HTTP transport works. So, remember these two flags and how exactly they affect your server. If you're developing your server on your local machine using standard IO as the transport, when you deploy to production, if you plan to use the streamable HTTP transport, remember that your server might behave a bit differently. So, while you develop your server, I recommend you use the transport you plan to use in production, because that will save you a lot of trouble later.
